"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ee3447b.190433844@news.optonline.net...
| riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
|
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
| Fine, I'll delete any reference to CLC.

You could run a poll, vote yes or no.

Maybe you could add a few more symbols.
Especially for the longer words, or constructions.
We could use double symbols for some reserved words, like >> for PERFORM.

Instead of:

PERFORM  0100-PROCESS-TRAN
       THRU  0100-PROCESS-TRAN-EXIT.

WE could use:

>> 0100-PROCESS-TRAN  0100-PROCESS-TRAN-EXIT <<

OR

PERFORM  0100-PROCESS-TRAN
       THRU  0100-PROCESS-TRAN-EXIT.
                   VARYING  I-BUBBL  FROM 2  BY  10
                         UNTIL  I-BUBBL  >  50
                                OR IND-EOF-TRAN

>> 0100-PROCESS-TRAN  0100-PROCESS-TRAN-EXIT <<
(I-BUBBL,2,10,>,50,!,IND-EOF-TRAN)


Only kidding. I think.
```

I don't see what all the fuss is about.

It is very easy to write edit macros to substitute symbols for words, and
vice versa.
As a matter of fact, I plan to do just that.
Code in shorthand, expand, and compile.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T16:34:21+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc2cud$cc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb865h$156q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net> <3ED8EDE3.50507@Knology.net> <bbfu3m$frk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3edc0a96.366765655@news.optonline.net> <bbif0v$2ae1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3edda21b.471106765@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041216.f2bad8a@posting.google.com> <3edefdb6.560106826@news.optonline.net> <bbq9nc$qgk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ee23fa0.123645106@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071658.9d74df@posting.google.com> <bc288l$a9d$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Pb2Fa.195938$ja4.10382205@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On  9-Jun-2003, "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> I don't see what all the fuss is about.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Code in shorthand, expand, and compile.

One could write macros to allow one to convert non-code to code all over the
place.   But why?   If you make the real code your native language, you avoid
problems when looking at someone else's code.

My fingers are already on the keyboard, typing in a word is easier than calling
a macro - and it looks right.   We don't have to rely on the expansion not
working correctly - formatting is WYSIWYG, and everything is simple.

I like simple.

(And most everybody these days realizes that the best thing they can do here is
to learn to type well).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<173Fa.196038$ja4.10388893@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bc2cud$cc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
|
| On  9-Jun-2003, "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| > It is very easy to write edit macros to substitute symbols for words,
and
| > vice versa.
| > As a matter of fact, I plan to do just that.
| > Code in shorthand, expand, and compile.
|
| One could write macros to allow one to convert non-code to code all over
the
| place.   But why?   If you make the real code your native language, you
avoid
| problems when looking at someone else's code.
|
| My fingers are already on the keyboard, typing in a word is easier than
calling
| a macro - and it looks right.   We don't have to rely on the expansion not
| working correctly - formatting is WYSIWYG, and everything is simple.
|
| I like simple.

Me too.

|
| (And most everybody these days realizes that the best thing they can do
here is
| to learn to type well).

You could assign the macro to a pf key.

I have all sorts of edit macros. (ISPF Rexx).
I have one to convert a PROC to JCL, comes in handy.
If I'm editing a program, and want to see a copy book, it's a few key
strokes away.
I have one that shows the FD's, 01 levels, Divisions, and Paragraphs.
I collapse a program, then do a "find all" to show me where something is
used.

If you want to do a COBOL interview, sit the person down at a terminal, edit
a COBOL program, and see what they do with the text editor.
Chance are, if they can't use the editor, they probably won't be a
productive asset.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T17:38:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc2gnh$du8$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb865h$156q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net> <3ED8EDE3.50507@Knology.net> <bbfu3m$frk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3edc0a96.366765655@news.optonline.net> <bbif0v$2ae1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3edda21b.471106765@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041216.f2bad8a@posting.google.com> <3edefdb6.560106826@news.optonline.net> <bbq9nc$qgk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ee23fa0.123645106@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071658.9d74df@posting.google.com> <bc288l$a9d$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Pb2Fa.195938$ja4.10382205@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bc2cud$cc7$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <173Fa.196038$ja4.10388893@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On  9-Jun-2003, "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> If you want to do a COBOL interview, sit the person down at a terminal, edit
> a COBOL program, and see what they do with the text editor.
> Chance are, if they can't use the editor, they probably won't be a
> productive asset.

I make sure I only change the Fkey settings in a minimalist manner - so that
others can use my keyboard.

I have to change F1 to recall, as that function needs to be available.   I 
changed F2 and F9 to Start and Swap Next, as these work about the way the
defaults do, with more screens.   And in SDSF I have F19 & F20 as PREV and NEXT,
working in a similar way as F7 & F8.   F13-24 are pretty much changeable though,
but since they are another keystroke, I save them for complicated tasks.

Not for expanding out abbreviations.

When someone wants to know how to do something in TSO around here, he goes to
me.  It's no great skill, but I am lazy and found learning the commands to be
less work than not knowing them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T19:37:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc2nkv$g61$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0306071658.9d74df@posting.google.com> <bc288l$a9d$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Pb2Fa.195938$ja4.10382205@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:

> It is very easy to write edit macros to substitute symbols for words, and
> vice versa.

I use an editor that has keystroke macros so that most keywords are 
available as two keystrokes f9-P -> PERFORM, f9-V -> VARYING etc. 

It saves time and keystroke errors and also puts them in my preferred 
'Modula-2' style of upper case keywords with mixed case names. 

I don't see much point in doing this with OR, or even AND and NOT. Using 
symbols to save one or two keystrokes is hardly a great leap in 
productivity.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T22:47:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<q88Fa.116854$cO3.8594545@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0306071658.9d74df@posting.google.com> <bc288l$a9d$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Pb2Fa.195938$ja4.10382205@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bc2nkv$g61$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:bc2nkv$g61$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
| Harley wrote:
|
| > It is very easy to write edit macros to substitute symbols for words,
and
| > vice versa.
|
| I use an editor that has keystroke macros so that most keywords are
| available as two keystrokes f9-P -> PERFORM, f9-V -> VARYING etc.

It's a nice function.
|
| It saves time and keystroke errors and also puts them in my preferred
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| productivity.

It wasn't to save 1 or 2 keystrokes.
It might be handy for things like perform or compute.
I agree with you, it seems silly to me.
One of the things I like about COBOL it that it not C.
When a co-worker suggested I learn C, I got the K&R, took a look, and
decided it wasn't for me.
 I had worked in PL/1 , and imagined the nightmares some of the PL/1
programmers could create in C.

The point I was trying to, and failed, to make is that if someone is more
comfortable reading symbols, they could replace words with symbols.
Depending on the text editor, changing ' and ' (spaces are needed) to ' & '
shouldn't be that difficult.
```

If all you want to do is READ, then an edit  macro will translate things
nicely.

No need to change the COBOL language.

BTW: I worked on a COBOL project with a C programmer who had no prior
experience with COBOL. He had no trouble.

You talk about making things easier for people who work in languages other
than COBOL. What about the COBOL programmers who will have to maintain  the
code they produce.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T13:00:44-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306101200.1f680e05@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bbif0v$2ae1$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3edda21b.471106765@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306041216.f2bad8a@posting.google.com> <3edefdb6.560106826@news.optonline.net> <bbq9nc$qgk$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ee23fa0.123645106@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071658.9d74df@posting.google.com> <bc288l$a9d$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <Pb2Fa.195938$ja4.10382205@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3ee5a9c9.126864553@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >It is very easy to write edit macros to substitute symbols for words, and
> >vice versa.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That misses the point. I want to READ the symbols, not just write them.

Then all you need do is to have an edit macro or preprocessor that
goes through the program and changes all the ' and ' etc. to '
squiggle d'jour '. Then you can change the program as you wish and
possibly use REPLACE or just reprocess to reverse.

Just put it in your 'beautifier'.

How hard is this ?  Why do you think he said 'and vice versa' ?

You can do that today, not have to wait several years and then buy a
new compiler (or did you think it would be retro-actively added to
your existing ones).

Or perhaps you really meant: "I want to READ them in everyone else's
programs", or perhaps "I want everyone else to write them and convert
existing programs to them - compulsory."

Front up Robert, your proposal is not about 'choice', it is about
_you_ choosing what _we_ should be doing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T20:19:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eda4576.250748157@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed6b0bf.16033634@news.optonline.net> <bb865h$156q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311216.5f67fb9b@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

>Actually you didn't, I know that you did understand the real issue,
>but you have either forgotten it or ignore it.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>   What is 'better' for one is what one is used to.

I don't have difficulty getting comfortable with a new convention. I fail to see
why that's such a compelling argument, especially in the programming business.

>You are used to words so they are better _for_you_.

I can use either. That's not the point.

>No one except you (RW) has expressed any problem with 'switching
>paradigms' between words and symbols.

What's better for me is consistency. Whether I use = or 'equal to' doesn't annoy
me as much as 'not ='. If one is a symbol, they should both be symbols. 

>Some have argued that >= is more readily understood (_for_them_) than 
>the equivalent NOT <.  This is _NOT_ altered by making it '! <'  it is
>the concept of NOT that creates the need to make a reversal, not the
>change between words and symbols.

I agree. 

>> Now that I'm converted (saved?), 

>Others only expressed what _they_ found better for themselves, their
>preferences.  There was no intent to make you change, just to stop
>your attempts at changing us.
>
>Now you are _still_ trying to change us by coercing us with 'symbols'.

I asked for symbols as an alternative to AND and OR. I'm not shoving them down
anyone's throat. 

>No one wants symbols for and, or, not. 

Nobody said that when I mentioned it ... several times.

>If you _really_ want to write code using symbols for and, or, not then
>please do so and write a pre-processor.  

I have a preprocessor, but I don't maintain raw code; I maintain its output
Cobol.

Micro Focus has a feature like #define. Hmmm, I wonder if that can be used.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T10:28:07+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbdum0$gcg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311216.5f67fb9b@posting.google.com> <3eda4576.250748157@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I don't have difficulty getting comfortable with a new convention. 

>>You are used to words so they are better _for_you_.
> 
> I can use either. That's not the point.
 
> What's better for me is consistency. Whether I use = or 'equal to' doesn't
> annoy me as much as 'not ='. If one is a symbol, they should both be
> symbols.

You obviously don't see the conflict in your arguments here.  On the one 
hand you claim you don't have difficulty with conventions, even new one, 
you reinforce this with an ability to use word and symbols.

You then claim difficulty with 'not ='.  This is a convention, not even a 
new one, and is a symbol and a word where you say you cope with both.

Having '<>' or '!=' is a completely different issue from changing 'and, ot, 
not' into something no one cares about.


>>No one wants symbols for and, or, not.
> 
> Nobody said that when I mentioned it ... several times.

They mostly said they were happy with using a mix of words and symbols.  
They said that having 'all symbols' wasn't necessary.

Certainly _no_one_ agreed that 'and, or, not' needed to have symbols '^, |, 
!', that 'majority' claim was a complete fabrication.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T15:46:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbehb4$o43$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3eda4576.250748157@news.optonline.net> <bbdum0$gcg$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eda98ca.272083404@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Having '<>' or '!=' is a completely different issue from changing 'and,
>>ot, not' into something no one cares about.
> 
> It's not a completely different issue. Both are operators describing
> logical relationships.

Having '<>' or '!=' is a completely different issue form 'not' because many 
languages have '<>' or '!=' for unequal, while having and, or, not.

Thus the argument in your proposal that other programmers would prefer 
<some obscure sqiggle d'jour> is quite untrue.

>>They mostly said they were happy with using a mix of words and symbols.
>>They said that having 'all symbols' wasn't necessary.
> 
> Quote one posting which said that. You can't because there weren't any.

Well, _I_ said that. You claimed that if maths used a mixture of words and 
symbols :

RW> Suppose COBOL offered the - symbol for subtraction (that's what
RW> comparison is) while making you use words for addition and
RW> multiplication. Would you use such an incomplete system? 

HB> So the answer is, yes, I would use such an incomplete system.

Other replied in a similar manner.

WMK may provide a more complete set but:

""" You haven't shown that one of the two is a "complete" set and the other 
is an "incomplete set".  However, even if you had, I don't think that would 
(necessarily) mean that simply not being a "complete" set means that it is 
"less good" to use one or the  other option - where both exists. """

> What they said was they're happy to use whatever the language provides.

Yes, they are happy to use whatever the language provides _now_.  What it 
provides is 'and or not', they are happy with this, symbols are not 
necessary.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T16:56:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0306011556.4b3cfa63@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed6b0bf.16033634@news.optonline.net> <bb865h$156q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net>...

> This began with me arguing that words are better than symbols. As expected, most
> took the opposite side while a few, such as Howard, remained neutral. We even
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> asked for our opinion. Gimme a break.

"Even had a visit from jallan"?

Am I famous or something?

Well to speak for myself, yes I would prefer symbols to "AND", "OR",
and "NOT" in COBOL.

I prefer symbols for operators.

There's lots else I find wrong with COBOL also, mostly the same old
things a lot of people find wrong with it. I find things wrong with c
also, and Perl and Xbase and so forth, mostly the same old things most
people find wrong with them.

Replacing "AND", "OR" and "NOT" in COBOL with symbols, while to my
mind a desireable change, is hardly a crusade. I'm used to them in
COBOL and various forms of BASIC and in xBase and so forth and they
are reasonably short.

But if symbols were to be introduced, well with Unicode and all, why
not go with the actual real and true and honest logical symbols that
(golly! gosh!) real logicians use?

For AND use U+2227, the actual LOGICAL AND symbol, allowing /\ as a
fallback for those stuck with ASCII/EBCDIC.

For OR use U+2228, the actual LOGICAL OR symbol, allowing \/ as a
fallback for those stuck with ASCII/EBCDIC.

For NOT use U+00AC, (ï¿½), the actual NOT SIGN which is available in the
standard Latin-1 alphabet and available in EBDCIC ever since there was
an EBCDIC, even on the keyboards(!) of EBCDIC terminals, but allowing
also ~ as a fallback, this being the older NOT symbol used by
logicians and also used in c (for bit-not).

My suggestion to use "/\" and "\/" is not original. According to Bob
Bemer at http://www.bobbemer.com/BACSLASH.HTM:

<< I had called a joint meeting of IBM, SHARE, and GUIDE, to
regularize the IBM 6-bit set to become the standard BCD Interchange
Code [76]. Frequency studies of symbol occurrence had been prepared,
particularly from ALGOL programs. The meeting of 1961 July 6 produced
general agreement on a basic 60-64-character set, which included the
two square brackets and the reverse slant, which was chosen in
conjunction with "/" to yield 2-character representations for the AND
and OR of early ALGOL. This is reflected in the set I proposed to ANSI
X3.2 on 1961 September 18. >>

Maybe at long last the backslash may be used for the purpose for which
it was invented.

But probably not. Using the correct signs would be too simple.


Jim Allan
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T09:30:46-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbfu3m$frk$2@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bb64k0$2n5e$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed6b0bf.16033634@news.optonline.net> <bb865h$156q$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed8ad55.146250961@news.optonline.net...
> >> Pascal *may* still be used in classrooms, but not in production code.

I responded:

> >Much of the MCP/AS intersystem communication software is written in
Pascal,
> >as, I believe, is some of the imaging support software for financial
> >applications.  Our COBOL85, C and Pascal83 compilers are written in
Pascal
> >compiler is written in Pascal as well.  And I'm very much surprised to
learn
> >that none of these products are used in production.
>
> Thank you for the substantiation.

Let me clarify for you.  The statement
    >Pascal *may* still be used in classroms, but not in production code.
is patently, and provably, false.   I have cited the examples that prove it
false.

I had thought my comment that I was surprised to learn otherwise would be
taken as obvious sarcasm.  The management and staff involved in MCP/AS
compiler development are completely unaware of the demise of Pascal in any
production environment, me included.  So are the customers who continue to
submit queries, potential bug reports, and requests to new features to our
support organization, both for the compilers themselves and for the Unisys
products that are dependent on them.

The Unisys MCP/AS COBOL85 compiler is the "flagship" COBOL compiler for that
system, it continues to be the target of significant enhancements, it is
*heavily* used in production, its customer base is growing, other products
(e.g., LINC aka EAE) are dependent on it.  And it's written in Pascal83.

The COBOL74 compiler for that system is written in an ALGOL dialect (NEWP;
it was converted from ALGOL itself about ten years ago), and is also heavily
used, though it no longer benefits from significant enhancements.   The only
language compilers that I'm aware of that were once used in production on
Burroughs Large Systems but are no longer supported are Basic, FORTRAN (66),
PL/I, APL/B, ESPOL (another ALGOL derivative replaced by NEWP) and
COBOL(68).  No dialect of Pascal is numbered among them.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T20:19:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eda4e5d.253027810@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305291802.784eb5f9@posting.google.com> <3ED6C9E9.8AC0BBCC@shaw.ca> <3ed858a5.124568155@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311147.6f8bb0e@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>Your proposal was to have symbols '^ | !' for 'and or not'.

>I cannot find _anyone_ who agreed that they needed to mentally 'switch
>gears' when using the words and, or, not.

You did, when you said you negated = to !=, then had to switch to Cobol's not =.

>I cannot find _anyone_ who agreed that symbols would be _better_ (some
>may have said no difference).

jallan did:

"Computer languages must of necessity handle very clearly and precisely
with the same kind of things as mathematics and logic, with values
that must be computed and tested. This kind of notation bypasses
spoken language altogether, going  directly from symbol to meaning."

and

"> I dispute that.The symbol > is no more (or less) precise than the words
>'greater than'.

Ah, but word take longer to process mentally than a single symbol."

and 

"Many, including myself, find that use of symbols adds clarity, that
">=" is clearer than "ge" even though it involves more typing, and far
clearer than a long phrase of words in the same symbols used for
variables.

The ideographic symbols are easier to parse mentally when considering
relation concepts than are words."

>I cannot find _anyone_ who agreed that those particular codes were
>suitable (actually even you now see they weren't).

We did not discuss particular codes.

>So where does this 'unanimous opinion' come from ?
>You fabricated it from what people said about completely different
>issues.

The issue was symbols in logic statements.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T10:41:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbdven$gnd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed858a5.124568155@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311147.6f8bb0e@posting.google.com> <3eda4e5d.253027810@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>Your proposal was to have symbols '^ | !' for 'and or not'.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> not =.

That is not a 'symbol for not' that is to have an operator for 'unequal'.

You have made a distinction between the logical operators and the 
comparison operators, you want consistency then live by it.


>>I cannot find _anyone_ who agreed that symbols would be _better_ (some
>>may have said no difference).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ah, but word take longer to process mentally than a single symbol."

That is about _two_ words 'greater than' compared to a single symbol.


> "Many, including myself, find that use of symbols adds clarity, that
> ">=" is clearer than "ge" even though it involves more typing, and far
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> relation concepts than are words."

That is a single symbol '>=" compared to several words 'greater than or 
equal'.

You have incompetently extrapolated this into 'random squiggle I am not 
used to' is better than 'and'.  

No one has made a statement about the logic operators and, or or not and 
their ability to understand these being worse than any particular symbol.

 
>>I cannot find _anyone_ who agreed that those particular codes were
>>suitable (actually even you now see they weren't).
> 
> We did not discuss particular codes.

If you had then you may have got specific opinions about them.  You may 
also have learnt something about what others use _before_ making a proposal 
that even you don't want.
 
>>So where does this 'unanimous opinion' come from ?
>>You fabricated it from what people said about completely different
>>issues.
> 
> The issue was symbols in logic statements.

And, I ask again, where did you get any opinions on the _logic_ operators ?
```

>>That is a single symbol '>=" compared to several words 'greater than or

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T08:48:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbfrkt$e1n$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed858a5.124568155@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311147.6f8bb0e@posting.google.com> <3eda4e5d.253027810@news.optonline.net> <bbdven$gnd$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eda9a0a.272403047@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3eda9a0a.272403047@news.optonline.net...

> The question is moot now. I'll delete CLC from the proposal.

Your proposal has already been formally submitted and published via the J4
web site.  Once that happens, such proposals aren't changed "in place",
though updated versions can be submitted to replace them.  Those who wish to
comment on your formal proposal through the formal J4 process should not be
expected to have to deal with a moving target.

Thus, if you wish to change anything in the proposal you have made to J4,
I'd suggest writing a replacement and submitting it to the J4 chair with the
request that the original be replaced by it.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T16:03:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed97b67_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305291802.784eb5f9@posting.google.com> <3ED6C9E9.8AC0BBCC@shaw.ca> <3ed858a5.124568155@news.optonline.net> <bbbchv$5eh$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bbbchv$5eh$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net...
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> news:3ed858a5.124568155@news.optonline.net...
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> > peers. It's much faster to make final decisions than to do the grunt
work
> > yourself.
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bill,

I have a lot of sympathy with this and I can understand your frustration.

The fact is, I, for one, would have gladly volunteered if I was satisfied
there was no profit involved.

I was not then (and am STILL not) satisfied that this "non-profit"
organization can sell the report for around $200 a copy, charge people (and,
especially, Corporations), for the dubious privelege of sitting on the
committee, and then EXPECT that lovers of COBOL should volunteer their time
and expertise for NOTHING. I would have done as much as they wanted if they
paid me.

I make a living by using the couple of pounds of grey slush that floats
around in my head. It has value. I am not persuaded by the "prestige" of
joining an exclusive club, as some of the members obviously were...

I work free for Charities of my own choosing, and they are Organizations
that I am satisfied need some help and support, and can't afford to pay the
going rate. ANSI/ISO/J4 doesn't come anywhere near that category. Hence, I
didn't get involved.

Obviously, I can't speak for others, but the above is an honest statement of
the reasons leading to one person's failure to get involved. (It may also
help to explain why my position was so vehement when I saw the lack of
achievement that came out of it...).

Pete.





> --
> Bill Klein
>  wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T17:17:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDA31AE.EBD2AC9A@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305291802.784eb5f9@posting.google.com> <3ED6C9E9.8AC0BBCC@shaw.ca> <3ed858a5.124568155@news.optonline.net> <bbbchv$5eh$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

>
> I an GUARANTEE that *every* "volunteer" who was willing to do ANYTHING for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> includes Mr. Wagner) who "volunteered" to help.

I read Pete's reply before I read this Bill. Guarantee or not - that's just not
the case. Go back in your memory, and something you are always doing, prompting
people here to get involved.

At one stage your made such a suggestion - can't quite recall the topic, but it
finished up as OO and Collections, (Standard Classes). BOTH Ken Foskey and I,
as a result of your suggestion, offered our services to J4, as interested
parties.

Messages sent - finito. (I think our notes got duly stamped and finished up
with document numbers. The bureaucracy is efficient if nothing else.). No
acknowledgement and neither of us had any clue that all that work had been done
on Standard Classes until you resurrected Russell's paper on Collections
written in '96. As I said previously, Russell is now off coding in the Java
World.

Meanwhile, when I had 'located' his current e-mail address, his name now
appears on the J4 list of 'outsiders' to be advised. I'm quite sure Russell is
'thrilled' to get junk on COBOL now he's solely into Java !

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T20:19:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305291802.784eb5f9@posting.google.com> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>not ?  What has it to do with 'switch gears' ?

The part about != supports my proposal for not. The last sentence supports
switching gears.

>If you knew anything about C then you would know that '!=' is a single
>not-equal operator and is not built from separate '!' and '='
>operators.

It is the same in COBOL. I posted a program demonstrating that GREATER OR EQUAL
is treated as a single operator. The OR is not a real OR. 

>A change to having '<>' or '!"' as an inequality comparator has noting
>to do with having a symbol for 'not'.

C uses ! to mean not, as well as using it in != (which gives lie to it being
context-free). My proposal for ! is exactly the same as the C rules. 

>Perhaps you are so unused to have anybody agree with anything you say
>that you were overwealmed.

I was convinced that your argument was valid. 

>> The proposed symbols for OR and NOT are the same as C++ and Java.
>
>The symbol for not is the same as Java and C.  The symbols for and or
>or are _NOT_ the same.  In Java and C they are '&&' and '||'.  You
>chose '^' and '|' which means something quite different.

In comments posted here, there has been opposition to carat. I like it because
it looks like the symbol used in mathematical logic. Evidently many programmers
have never seen Boolean Algebra; they have only seen it in other programming
languages where it means something else. 

>> On second thought, && and || might be better choices. COBOL has no problem
>> with * for multiplication and ** for exponentiation. 
>
>Well, duh.  You finally get a clue.

Double asterisk was designed in the 60s. I doubt the current J4 would be as
recepetive to double characters. 

>You may one day realise why I say that your arguments are shallow. 
>Now that you have dug a little deeper with the material supplied here
>you realise that your original choices were wrong.

Not wrong, a matter of style. After repeating that for months, you violate your
own precept when someone else's ox is being gored. 

>Far from have 'majority' support for your codes, now that you have
>changed your mind that leaves _zero_ support for them.

I said the majority supported the use of symbols. I did not say it supported my
choice of symbols. We didn't discuss specific symbols. If J4 _did_ approve the
suggestion, it would  probably change the symbols anyway.

>> I knew enough to borrow from current languages -- C++ and Java --
>
>I very much doubt that you knew what symbols Algol or PL/I used at
>all. 

I worked on both briefly, thirty years ago. 

>I also doubt that you knew enough about many other languages to
>know what was generally used by other current programmers.  You may
>recognise what C looks like, but you didn't know enough to know which
>codes to borrow - you probably never heard of the bit-wise operators.

COBOL's bit-wise operators are B-NOT, B-OR, B-AND and B-XOR. I don't propose
changing them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T11:19:36+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> switching gears.

No it doesn't.  '!=' and '<>' are comparison operators, not logic.  Python, 
VB, Pascal derivitives (and many other languages) use and, or, not for 
logic operators so Cobol provides a 'single method' except for lack of '<>' 
for unequal.

If I was concerned about C/C++ (I am not, changing to using &&, ||, and ! 
for C is trivial), then I would use #define and && and #define or || and 
use those.
 
>>If you knew anything about C then you would know that '!=' is a single
>>not-equal operator and is not built from separate '!' and '='
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> EQUAL is treated as a single operator. The OR is not a real OR.

Which is change from your whining about the 'implicit OR'.


>>A change to having '<>' or '!"' as an inequality comparator has noting
>>to do with having a symbol for 'not'.
> 
> C uses ! to mean not, as well as using it in != (which gives lie to it
> being context-free). My proposal for ! is exactly the same as the C rules.

So why did you use my note on '<>' as 'support' for your use of '!' as 
logical not ? and how did you extrapolate that to '^' and '|' ?

Consistency is not a strong point with you is it.


> I was convinced that your argument was valid.

Thank you, but it was about something else.

 
> Double asterisk was designed in the 60s. I doubt the current J4 would be
> as recepetive to double characters.

Perhaps you may understand why '^' was not part of the Cobol character set 
when you finally 'get' the national replacement characters.

The evidence is that they are more likely to accept double characters (**) 
than a character not in the set (^).

After you get the character set extended, then you can propose what they 
may do with those characters.
 
>>You may one day realise why I say that your arguments are shallow.
>>Now that you have dug a little deeper with the material supplied here
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> your own precept when someone else's ox is being gored.

No, your choices were wrong because you claimed they would be easier for 
other language programmers when the opposite is true _for_those_symbols_.

For many languages and, or, not, would be easier as that is what many 
languages use.  For some languages those symbols are used for something 
quite different.

However, in general, C++ and Java programmers do not want to use Cobol and 
no amount of making it look more like those languages would persuade them.

> I said the majority supported the use of symbols. 

 ... for some completely different operators.

> If J4 _did_ approve the
> suggestion, it would  probably change the symbols anyway.

No. If they have one proposal they may accept it (or reject it).  What 
basis you have for them arbitrarily changing proposals to something 
different ?

It obviously isn't important to you what symbols are used, so what is wrong 
with one built from at ASCCI hex codes x'3F52' ?

> COBOL's bit-wise operators are B-NOT, B-OR, B-AND and B-XOR. I don't
> propose changing them.

Why not ?  Your claimed need was consistency of 'all words' or 'all 
symbols' (which no one else supported), now you don't seem to care. 

Not only will your proposal confuse C programmers but it will also obstruct 
using the C operators for bitwise symbols.

You give every sign of never having considered these issues at all.  That 
is: I would expect some naieve newbie without two clues to rub together and 
without having done any research at to come up with the wrong symbols 
because they didn't know any better.  What's your excuse ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T02:42:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:

>Robert Wagner wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>for unequal.

You argue on the one hand that comparison operators are not logic, then on the
other hand the LOGICAL truth of a statement can (or should) be negated by
reversing the comparison operators. You are contradicting yourself. 

When I argued the logical operators are the ones that should be changed, you
said that's not what you are used to. 

Conclusion: you are used to illogic. 

>>>A change to having '<>' or '!"' as an inequality comparator has noting
>>>to do with having a symbol for 'not'.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>logical not ? and how did you extrapolate that to '^' and '|' ?

Your note said "I also do = to !=". It did not say 'I also do = to <>'.

If ! is read not in !=, some would think ! meant not elsewhere. That's why C and
Python both use ! for two purposes. Seems reasonable to me, especially in COBOL,
where it isn't made clear that 'not =' is a single operator. 

If we're going to create a symbol for not, in the interest of regularity we
should also have symbols for and/orl. 

>Consistency is not a strong point with you is it.

As I showed above -- using logic rather than name calling -- you are
inconsistent. 

>Perhaps you may understand why '^' was not part of the Cobol character set 
>when you finally 'get' the national replacement characters.

The 7-bit national replacement characters apply to 7-bit terminals, not
computers. All modern computers are 8 bit or better. Perhaps users in some
technical backwater are still using 7-bit terminals, but I think it implausible
anyone is still reading or writing source code on them. 

Worst case, they would revert to the spelled out words we have today.

>>>You may one day realise why I say that your arguments are shallow.
>>>Now that you have dug a little deeper with the material supplied here
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>other language programmers when the opposite is true _for_those_symbols_.

That's why I'll propose the C/Java symbols. 

>For many languages and, or, not, would be easier as that is what many 
>languages use.  For some languages those symbols are used for something 
>quite different.

Programmers comfortable with those languages can use words. 

>However, in general, C++ and Java programmers do not want to use Cobol and 
>no amount of making it look more like those languages would persuade them.

You and I use both. Quite a few posters to CLC appear to know both. 

>> If J4 _did_ approve the
>> suggestion, it would  probably change the symbols anyway.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>different ?

It's just speculation. 

>> COBOL's bit-wise operators are B-NOT, B-OR, B-AND and B-XOR. I don't
>> propose changing them.
>
>Why not ?  Your claimed need was consistency of 'all words' or 'all 
>symbols' (which no one else supported), now you don't seem to care. 

Bit-wise operators (written in function form) aren't used by 'average'
programmers. 

>Not only will your proposal confuse C programmers but it will also obstruct 
>using the C operators for bitwise symbols.

Good point. I'll feature the C/Java symbols first.

>You give every sign of never having considered these issues at all.  That 
>is: I would expect some naieve newbie without two clues to rub together and 
>without having done any research at to come up with the wrong symbols 
>because they didn't know any better.  What's your excuse ?

I'm no longer answering ad hominem. If there are legitimate points in your
message, I'll answer them and label the ad hominem as such for the benefit of
others. If the message contains nothing but ad hominem, I simply won't answer. 

We each decide how to spend our time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T00:44:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306022344.24fe26aa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >No it doesn't.  '!=' and '<>' are comparison operators, not logic.  Python, 
> >VB, Pascal derivitives (and many other languages) use and, or, not for 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> You argue on the one hand that comparison operators are not logic, 

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote 
>
> As I see it in COBOL, there are SIX classes of operators in '02-standard
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> (f) Logical (AND, OR and NOT).

Note groups (e) and (f).

> then on the
> other hand the LOGICAL truth of a statement can (or should) be negated by
> reversing the comparison operators. You are contradicting yourself. 

The logical truth of two statements may be opposite.  How those two
statements are contructed is irrelevant.  It happens that if the two
statements have different comparison operators then their logical
truth may be opposite.

There is _nothing_ to contradict.  I don't _have_ to arrive at the 2nd
statement by doing something specific, I can do it anyway that _I_
please, there is nothing 'magic' that means that one incantation works
and another doesn't.

In a similar vein:

   '<>'    ->  unequal
   '!='    ->  unequal
   'NOT =' -> unequal

In Cobol NOT is found as part of the syntax of many verbs and
conditional expressions.  For example:

        READ ...
            AT END imperitive statement
            NOT AT END inperitive statement
        END-READ

In this context NOT is part of the syntax of the READ statement and is
_NOT_ a logical operator.

In many condition statements NOT forms part of the syntax - which is
why 'NOT >=' is invalid, it is not part of the syntax of the >=
condition.

  'a NOT = b' is 'a unequal b' - a simple relation condition

  this is not the same as 'NOT ( a = b )' a negated simple condition.

> When I argued the logical operators are the ones that should be changed, you
> said that's not what you are used to. 

I am certainly not used to ^|! for anything that you proposed for
them.

> Conclusion: you are used to illogic. 

You manage to draw silly conclusions about many things that you don't
understand.

> Your note said "I also do = to !=". It did not say 'I also do = to <>'.

So ?  I don't have a problem with either.  Take your hang ups
elsewhere.

> If ! is read not in !=, some would think ! meant not elsewhere. That's why C
> and > Python both use ! for two purposes. 

You are _way_ beyond your actual knowledge.  Python uses 'not' (as do
many others).  In both C and Python (and many others) '!=' is a single
operator that means 'unequal'.

> Seems reasonable to me, especially
> in COBOL, where it isn't made clear that 'not =' is a single operator. 

Well it is, the NOT is part of the syntax of '='.  I doubt that being
'clear to you' is in any way significant.
 
> If we're going to create a symbol for not, 

Why would we create a 'symbol for not' ?  Unequal is '<>'.  I like
'not'.

> in the interest of regularity we should also have symbols for and/orl. 

Given that I see no point in a symbol for 'not' then I propose we
maintain the existing regularity by leaving 'and' and 'or' alone.

> >Consistency is not a strong point with you is it.
> 
> As I showed above -- using logic rather than name calling -- you are
> inconsistent. 

Except you didn't, you merely contrived some stretch based on what ? -
not realising that NOT is part of the syntax of '='.

> Worst case, they would revert to the spelled out words we have today.

Best case: we all continue to use the nice set we are all used to. If
that includes spelling out GREATER THAN then that is OK too.
 
> That's why I'll propose the C/Java symbols. 

And those are still foreign to Cobol, SQL, VB, Python, PERL, and a
host of other  languages.

> Programmers comfortable with those languages can use words. 

So, you don't care if they are the 'next programmer' for your code ?
 
> >However, in general, C++ and Java programmers do not want to use Cobol and 
> >no amount of making it look more like those languages would persuade them.
> 
> You and I use both. Quite a few posters to CLC appear to know both. 

They are Cobol programmers who latterly use C/C++ and Java (in the
main at least).  For them it would be more useful if you campaigned
C/C++ and Java to also use and, or, not.
 
> It's just speculation. 

As usual, yet elsewhere you claim to use 'facts'.
 
> Bit-wise operators (written in function form) aren't used by 'average'
> programmers. 

I've used them (or actually the MF ones).
 
> >You give every sign of never having considered these issues at all.  That 
> >is: I would expect some naieve newbie without two clues to rub together and 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm no longer answering ad hominem. 

It isn't, it is a serious observation, based on facts.  The fact that
you seem unaware of what trigraphs were and why, unaware of why the
Cobol set is as it is (for much the same reason).  Unaware of the C
bitwise operators, unaware that many other languages use 'and, or,
not' (and still so on Python even though it has been stated several
times).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T20:21:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee20970.109770318@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306022344.24fe26aa@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>and another doesn't.

Your method works only on relationals. A more generic approach works on all
conditionals. Of instance:

(not a or b) and (c or not d) 

negates to 

(a or not b) or (not c and d)

If b happens to be a relational such as 'x=y', you could write 'not b' as
'x<>y'. But if b is an 88 or user-defined function, it must be written 'not b'.
My way -- 'x not = y' is visually closer to the generic solution. 

Yes, I realize 'not =' is technically a single condition equivalent to <>. But
it's two COBOL words. 

>In Cobol NOT is found as part of the syntax of many verbs and
>conditional expressions.  For example:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>_NOT_ a logical operator.

We all know COBOL is context-sensitive. What does that prove in re NOT as a
logical operator?

>In many condition statements NOT forms part of the syntax - which is
>why 'NOT >=' is invalid, it is not part of the syntax of the >=
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>  this is not the same as 'NOT ( a = b )' a negated simple condition.

The COBOL standard doesn't make that very clear. It says you can put NOT in
front of =, but fails to make a distinction between that use of NOT and a
logical operator. On the contrary, it suggests they are the same when it forbids
the double negative NOT NOT. You cannot say 'if not not a' (logical operator)
nor can you say 'if x = y and not not > z'. Why? If the second NOT is part of a
relational, as you say, the statement should be interpreted 'if x = y and not <=
z'. If the second compiles -- it does under Micro Focus -- then the first should
compile as well. They are logically the same. In this case, COBOL is treating
the second NOT as though it is a logical operator.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T23:22:51-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306072222.74be4070@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306022344.24fe26aa@posting.google.com> <3ee20970.109770318@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >There is _nothing_ to contradict.  I don't _have_ to arrive at the 2nd
> >statement by doing something specific, I can do it anyway that _I_
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> conditionals. Of instance:

'My method' ?  Did I specify a method ?  Didn't I just say that I can
do it any way that I please ?
 
As it happens _I_ don't do it several times a day, I very rarely do it
at all, but I can see why you would need to mechanise it.

> If b happens to be a relational such as 'x=y', you could write 'not b' as
> 'x<>y'. But if b is an 88 or user-defined function, it must be written 'not
> b'.  My way -- 'x not = y' is visually closer to the generic solution. 

<Yawn>.  You always seem to wnat people to do it _your_ way, regardles
of whether we care or not.

> Yes, I realize 'not =' is technically a single condition equivalent to <>. But
> it's two COBOL words. 

Oh good.
 
> We all know COBOL is context-sensitive. What does that prove in re NOT as a
> logical operator?

If you want to change NOT to <symbol de jour> then it will be
confusing if sometimes it can be a symbol and sometimes it must be
NOT.  Your proposal only changed the logical operators and not the
syntax of the the various statemnets, and also did not change how SQL
must be written.

While claiming that you want to make things _consistent_ your proposal
is _inconsistent_ because NOT will still be compulsory in places.

The only _consistent_ way is to leave AND/OR/NOT alone to be used with
<>+ or the words GREATER/LESS/EQUAL as people wish.  No one cares
about 'symmetry' or other nonsense that you made up, nor what the
Goths did.

> The COBOL standard doesn't make that very clear. It says you can put NOT in
> front of =, but fails to make a distinction between that use of NOT and a
> logical operator. 

It makes it perfectly clear. Look at the syntax diagrams for the
relation conditions. Where [NOT] is optional it can be used.

> On the contrary, it suggests they are the same when it forbids
> the double negative NOT NOT. You cannot say 'if not not a' (logical operator)

Yes you can.  You can have as many 'negated simple conditions' as you
want.

      IF NOT NOT NOT NOT A = 1

Is perfectly valid, but not useful.

> nor can you say 'if x = y and not not > z'. Why? If the second NOT is part of
> a > relational, as you say, the statement should be interpreted 'if x = y and
> not <=  z'. 

Can you show where this is not valid ?  The standard is quite clear
about how NOT is interpreted in abbreviated conditions.

> If the second compiles -- it does under Micro Focus -- then the first should
> compile as well.

It does.  Not only that the manual explains it specifically.
 
> They are logically the same. In this case, COBOL is treating
> the second NOT as though it is a logical operator.

Read (from MF manual) for abbreviated conditions: "" If NOT is
immediately followed by GREATER, >, LESS, < ...""
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T19:12:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee37364.202444547@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306022344.24fe26aa@posting.google.com> <3ee20970.109770318@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306072222.74be4070@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>If you want to change NOT to <symbol de jour> then it will be
>confusing if sometimes it can be a symbol and sometimes it must be
>NOT.  Your proposal only changed the logical operators and not the
>syntax of the the various statemnets, and also did not change how SQL
>must be written.

>While claiming that you want to make things _consistent_ your proposal
>is _inconsistent_ because NOT will still be compulsory in places.

I'd be happy with making ! and NOT synonymous everywhere, just like THRU and
THROUGH. Why is this a major problem?

>> The COBOL standard doesn't make that very clear. It says you can put NOT in
>> front of =, but fails to make a distinction between that use of NOT and a
>> logical operator. 

Correcting myself -- the quote below shows the standard DOES make a distinction,
but it is not in the syntax rules for relational operators, where one would
expect to find it.

>> On the contrary, it suggests they are the same when it forbids
>> the double negative NOT NOT. You cannot say 'if not not a' (logical operator)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Is perfectly valid, but not useful.

Nope. See below.

>> nor can you say 'if x = y and not not > z'. Why? If the second NOT is part of
>> a > relational, as you say, the statement should be interpreted 'if x = y and
>> not <=  z'. 
>
>Can you show where this is not valid ?  

From the 2002 standard (85 standard contains similar):

8.6.4.2.3
Table 6   Combinations of conditions, logical operators, and parentheses
NOTE The element pair 'OR NOT' is permissible while the pair 'NOT OR' is not
permissible; the pair 'NOT (' is permissible while the pair 'NOT NOT' is not
permissible

Also:

8.8.4.2.4.2 Syntax Rules for Abbreviated combined relation conditions

2) The result of implied insertion shall comply with the rules of table 5,
Combinations of conditions, logical
operators, and parentheses. [They meant to say table 6]
3) The word NOT shall not be followed immediately by the word NOT or the words
IS NOT.

>> If the second compiles -- it does under Micro Focus -- then the first should
>> compile as well.
>
>It does.  Not only that the manual explains it specifically.

It doesn't compile. Micro Focus rejects NOT NOT as an error. 
 
>> They are logically the same. In this case, COBOL is treating
>> the second NOT as though it is a logical operator.
>
>Read (from MF manual) for abbreviated conditions: "" If NOT is
>immediately followed by GREATER, >, LESS, < ...""

The standard says:

b) If the relational operator following the word NOT is a simple relational
operator, then the NOT participates as part of the simple relational operator; 
[if that were true,  'x = y and not not > z' would compile.]

otherwise,

c) The NOT is interpreted as a logical operator and, therefore, the implied
insertion of subject or relational operator results in a negated relation
condition.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T21:06:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306082006.6b934af4@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306022344.24fe26aa@posting.google.com> <3ee20970.109770318@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306072222.74be4070@posting.google.com> <3ee37364.202444547@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> I'd be happy with making ! and NOT synonymous everywhere, just like THRU and
> THROUGH. Why is this a major problem?

It is not a problem at all to me if you do whatever you wish through
REPLACE or similar.
 
> but it is not in the syntax rules for relational operators, where one would
> expect to find it.

Where I may expect to find things may be different to you.
 
> >Yes you can.  You can have as many 'negated simple conditions' as you
> >want.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Nope. See below.

Yes, you can have multiple 'negated single conditions', but you may
need to put parantheses around the inner ones.

       IF NOT ( NOT ( NOT ( NOT A = 1 )))

> Table 6   Combinations of conditions, logical operators, and parentheses
> NOTE The element pair 'OR NOT' is permissible while the pair 'NOT OR' is not
> permissible; the pair 'NOT (' is permissible while the pair 'NOT NOT' is not
> permissible

> It doesn't compile. Micro Focus rejects NOT NOT as an error. 

My version compiled it OK.  Fujitsu 7 compiled it OK.  Not that this
is any indication that it complies with the standard,  But it does
leave your claim about what NOT is rather precarious.

> [if that were true,  'x = y and not not > z' would compile.]

It does.

Howver 'NOT NOT <=' would not as the syntaxx for <= does not allow 'IS
NOT <='.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T00:22:59+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee513f1.88498649@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306022344.24fe26aa@posting.google.com> <3ee20970.109770318@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306072222.74be4070@posting.google.com> <3ee37364.202444547@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306082006.6b934af4@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

>> >Yes you can.  You can have as many 'negated simple conditions' as you
>> >want.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>       IF NOT ( NOT ( NOT ( NOT A = 1 )))

Changing your premises or 'clarifying'?  When I do that, I'm crucified. 

You guessed NOT NOT would work and you were simply WRONG. There is no "MAY need
to put parentheses", they are required by the standard. 

>> Table 6   Combinations of conditions, logical operators, and parentheses
>> NOTE The element pair 'OR NOT' is permissible while the pair 'NOT OR' is not
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>leave your claim about what NOT is rather precarious.

Try turning on Standards Compliance and see how far you get. 

>> [if that were true,  'x = y and not not > z' would compile.]
>
>It does.

It should, but it doesn't under Micro Focus because the standard told them to
reject NOT NOT. 

>Howver 'NOT NOT <=' would not as the syntax for <= does not allow 'IS
>NOT <='.

The 85 standard is ambiguous. In simple relationals,  NOT <= is verboten. But in
Abbreviated compound conditionals, NOT (anything) is permissible. 

What I see here is people having trouble with logic, just like Joe in the next
cubicle .. trying to cope with English language skills. That's why nobody
supports my proposal for symbols.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T22:46:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306092146.75ef6459@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306022344.24fe26aa@posting.google.com> <3ee20970.109770318@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306072222.74be4070@posting.google.com> <3ee37364.202444547@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306082006.6b934af4@posting.google.com> <3ee513f1.88498649@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >> >Yes you can.  You can have as many 'negated simple conditions' as you
> >> >want.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Changing your premises or 'clarifying'?  When I do that, I'm crucified. 

You _can_ have as many 'negated simple conditions' as you want.

Standards compliance would require the parentheses, but MF (on my
machine) _does_ compile it without.

> You guessed NOT NOT would work and you were simply WRONG. There is no "MAY
> need to put parentheses", they are required by the standard. 

Actually it _did_ work.  You may need to put parenthese depending on
compiler od settings of standards compliance flags.

Of course your use of pointers which depends on non-standard features
also may need changing depending on compiler or settings of standards
compliance.

> >Howver 'NOT NOT <=' would not as the syntax for <= does not allow 'IS
> >NOT <='.
> 
> The 85 standard is ambiguous. In simple relationals,  NOT <= is verboten. But
> in Abbreviated compound conditionals, NOT (anything) is permissible. 

It is not ambiguous at all.  There is no optional [NOT] in the stntax
of 'IS <=', while there is an optional 'NOT' in the syntax of 'IS
[NOT] <'.

In abbreviated conditions it quite clearly states how NOT is
interpreted.

> What I see here is people having trouble with logic, just like Joe in the next
> cubicle .. trying to cope with English language skills. 

What I see here is somebody having trouble with Cobol.

> That's why nobody supports my proposal for symbols.

First of all I am pleased to see that you have changed your claim of
'unanimous support'.  But now it is 'nobody'.  If is ain't white it
must be black ?

Your proposals are not well thought through in any way.  Half-baked is
a fair commentary on them.

Your proposal is for 'logical operators', it does not cover the
relational operators and the use of optional NOT in these.  It also
makes no mention of the syntax of many statements where NOT is used,
such as NOT AT END.

With your proposal one may say:

         IF ! A = 1

But may not say:

         IF A ! = 1  
or:      IF A != 1

The NOT is question is part of the syntax of the 'IS [NOT] =' relation
condition and not a logical operator.

In an abbreviated condition this may result in having to use NOT
rather than !:

       IF A = 1 OR NOT < 6 OR = 3      

  ie   IF ( A = 1 ) OR ( A IS NOT < 6 ) OR ( A = 3 )

If, however it was written with ! this would change its meaning:

       IF A = 1 OR ! < 6 OR = 3

Your new proposal is even worse because it doesn't specify who should
choose the characters to use:

       J4 ?
       Vendor ?
       Programmer ?

If the proposal is for all of these then it will be the programmer
that can choose to use '^' or '/\' or '&&' for AND.  Can they use any
and all of these in one program ?

If it is for J4 to choose and they choose, say, '/\' and '|' and 'ï¿½'
then how will this be 'welcomed by C/Java programmers' ?  Or by anyone
else ?

Half-baked is a generous appraisal.
```

>

(depending on compiler).
```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>"Richard" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
>news:217e491a.0306081241.66ef127a@posting.google.com...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Anybody remember Helen Shapiro?

The scene: hotel ballroom
Lighting: multi-mirror globe slowly rotating
The year: 1960-63
The event: senior prom
Coming-of-age music: Don't Treat Me Like A Child, Helen Shapiro
	                   Walking Back to Happiness, Helen Shapiro
Warming-up music:    You Don't Have to Say You Love Me, Dusty Springfield

You don't have to say you love me, just be close at hand 
You don't have to stay forever, I will understand
Believe me, believe me, I can't help but love you 
But believe me, I'll never tie you down 

Preclimax music:	       When a Man Loves a Woman, Percy Sledge
 
When a man loves a woman
Cant keep his mind on nothin else
Hed trade the world
For a good thing hes found
If she is bad, he cant see it
She can do no wrong
Turn his back on his best friend
If he puts her down

Last-call music:  A Whiter Shade of Pale, Procol Harum 
(DIM THE LIGHTS, PLEASE)

We skipped the light fandango
And turned cartwheels across the floor
I was feeling kind of seasick
But the crowd called out for more
The room was humming harder
As the ceiling flew away
When we called out for another drink
The waiter brought a tray

She said "There is no reason,
And the truth is plain to see"
But I wandered through my playing cards
And would not let her be
One of sixteen vestal virgins
Who were leaving for the coast
And although my eyes were open
They might just as well have been closed

Iambic quadrimeter. Just the ticket -- hard driving, sexy. 

Who said, "Too bad youth is wasted on the young?"

----------------------
I'll be at the Cher concert at Madison Square Garden 6/12, this coming Thursday.
If anyone wants to meet, send me a note. 

Cher spent a lifetime giving the world the finger and having fun with it. She
wasn't a bitch perfectionist like Martha Stewart or Streisand. She got the job
done with good humor and she liked bikers and musicians. My kinda woman. 

Do you know any 59 year-old women who look or talk as sexy? I thought not.
```

It is when one strikes the 'a' key a second time with requisite force, 
aye.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: J4 paper on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T22:33:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc0v66$jal$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bb63av$e7v$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed850da.122572452@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311134.7dccfc1d@posting.google.com> <3eda4e4d.253011869@news.optonline.net> <bbe1mj$hjc$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3edbe6e0.357621858@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306022344.24fe26aa@posting.google.com> <3ee20970.109770318@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306072222.74be4070@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0306081241.66ef127a@posting.google.com>`

```
In case anyone questions Richard's statement, the '85 Standard, page VI-60,
in the section, "6.3.2.3 Precedence of Logical Operators and the Use of
Parentheses" clearly indicates that a "NOT" may *not* be followed
immediately by another "NOT" in a valid (conforming) statement.  In fact,
immediately after "Table 1: Combinations of Conditions, Logical Operators,
and Parentheses" is the explicit statement,

"Thus, the element pair 'OR NOT' is permissible while the pair 'NOT OR' is
not lermissible; the pair 'NOT (' is permissible while the pair 'NOT NOT' is
not ,ermissible."
```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed8de07.158719541@news.optonline.net...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> Your point is well taken, though. I should have posted the exact wording
of the
> email  here and waited a day or two.
>
Yes, that would have much better for all concerned. Nevermind, count it a
learning experience...

> >Finally, my offer stands. If this gets implemented, dinner is on me <G>.
>
> How gracious. After Charlie Trotter and Greens, the best vegetarian
cooking
> comes from my kitchen. You buy the food and I'll cook. For beverage, I was
> considering McEwan's Scotch Ale, Pilsner Urquell or Chimay. Do you have a
> preference? <G>
>

Very cold Pilsner Urquell is excellent. It reminds me of happy times in
Germany drinking "Pils" brewed according to the "Reinheitsgebot", and a
couple of days I spent in Pilsen (now part of The Czech Republich, but
originally part of Germany when they invented "Pils"...where I consumed
large quantities of this delicious brew.)

By an odd coincidence I have some people coming for dinner in about 2 hours,
one of whom is a vegetarian. I am baking vegetables with fresh herbs from my
garden (rosemary, garlic, thyme, and parsley), crushed  into extra virgin
olive oil and coated onto the vegetables (Zucchini, kumara (a native NZ
sweet potato), large sliced potatoes, and fresh pumpkin. For the meat eaters
I have three perfect rib steaks (one each), which I'll grill, and for the
vege guy a couple of fresh salmon fillets (I checked he eats seafood) which
I'll poach in a local chardonnay with some red onions, and I'll make a lemon
marmalade with some of the fresh lemons which are dropping off my tree. I
really appreciate how good it is to be back in NZ where all this stuff is
freely available (at around half the price in Europe) and of very high
quality. (It isn't Paradise here; we have problems like everybody else, but,
thankfully, food isn't one of them <G>).

I never thought I'd see the day when I would cook for a vegetarian, but it
just shows how unexpected life can be...<G>

Pete.
```
