###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 73)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T08:40:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
> >Most RPG shops, in my experience, seem dedicated to the language; some
have
> >asserted it's actually the first 4GL.
> >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

No, it's written in ALGOL.  It was not written in RPG because the group in
charge of developing the compiler had significant experience writing
recursive-descent compilers in ALGOL, including COBOL (68), the original
version of COBOL74 (later revised to use a different ALGOL dialect called
NEWP), Pascal, SORT, PL/1, Basic, two versions of FORTRAN, APL, the Network
Definition Language, and probably several others that I've forgotten. And,
of course, ALGOL itself which had its genesis in a machine-language
cross-compiler running on, I believe, the Burroughs B263 back in the 1950's.

Why do you ask?   Is there a particular reason you feel, or suspect, that
the authors of the RPG compiler decades ago should have written it in RPG?

       -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 74)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T12:05:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net...
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>the authors of the RPG compiler decades ago should have written it in RPG?

I think every compiler should be self-compiled, for two reasons:

.. It shows the compiler writers have their heart in it, and really understand
the language.

.. It's a reasonably good test of the language's robustness. 

In 1984 I reviewed half a dozen PC COBOL compilers. They were all mediocre,
produced slow-running code, and were written in other languages .. mostly C. I
predicted in a magazine article that when a good COBOL compiler comes along, it
will be written in COBOL. A few months later Realia was released. It blew the
doors off not just the competition but every compiler available at the time,
including hotshots such as Lattice C and Turbo Pascal. It was written in COBOL
. by two people who really knew COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 75)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T13:21:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306071221.1d518826@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> In 1984 I reviewed half a dozen PC COBOL compilers. They were all mediocre,
> produced slow-running code, and were written in other languages .. mostly C. I
> predicted in a magazine article that when a good COBOL compiler comes along, it
> will be written in COBOL. 

Was one of those MicroFocus ?

> A few months later Realia was released. It blew the
> doors off not just the competition but every compiler available at the time,
> including hotshots such as Lattice C and Turbo Pascal. 

It would do, Lattice C and Turbo Pascal are _very_ poor COBOL
compilers.


But you appear to focus on just one aspect - speed.  MF, MS, RM, Accu
had compilers that produced byte code.  They did this for several
reasons.  Some of these originated when 64Kb was the usual memory
size.  For large applications using byte code meant that more could be
fitted into the machine.

Even in 1984 it would have been unusal to find machines with more than
256Kb, possibly power users had 512Kb.

At that time I was running multi-user Cobol applications with 3 users
in 256Kb under MP/M or using networked 8 bit machines.

Byte code also allowed systems to be distributed to dozens of
different machines without even having to recompile.  With RM or Accu
I could develop on an 8 bit machine and distribute to a 68k Unix
machine.  I didn't need to buy a machine or a compiler for the target
system.

Interactive systems spend 95% or the time waiting for keystrokes and
most of the rest waiting for disk.  Code speed is irrelevant for most
purposes in business systems.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 76)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T03:17:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee289a8.142599732@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071221.1d518826@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
>> In 1984 I reviewed half a dozen PC COBOL compilers. They were all mediocre,
>> produced slow-running code, and were written in other languages .. mostly C.
I
>> predicted in a magazine article that when a good COBOL compiler comes along,
it
>> will be written in COBOL. 
>
>Was one of those MicroFocus ?

Yes. At the time it was called CIS/Micro Focus. It's speed was exactly the same
as interpreted Basic. The only native code Cobol compiler available was mbp,
which executed as fast as Turbo Pascal. But its compiler, written in CDL2
(C-like), was p-code. It took 5-10 minutes to compile an average size program. 

>> A few months later Realia was released. It blew the
>> doors off not just the competition but every compiler available at the time,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>compilers.

I discovered that. My workaround was to rewrite the Sieve of Erotosthenes in C
and Pascal (and APL and Fortran and PL/I and several assembly languages). It was
stomach-turning work but somebody had to do it. 

>But you appear to focus on just one aspect - speed.  MF, MS, RM, Accu
>had compilers that produced byte code.  They did this for several
>reasons.  

They did it for one reason -- to maximize their market coverage while minimizing
cost. They saved money by not having to write a back-end; the user paid every
time he executed a program. 

>Some of these originated when 64Kb was the usual memory
>size.  For large applications using byte code meant that more could be
>fitted into the machine.

This is true, in theory. In practice, the difference is often slight, especially
on a CISC machine. It's easy to verify today. Compare the size of some Micro
Focus .int files (p-code, byte code) to corresponding .gnt files (native code).
I have no idea how they compare. On a guess: less than twice as big. 

p-code was 'invented' (well, popularized) by the Allen-Babcock company in the
50s and early 60s. At the time, new mainframes were appearing monthly. They all
needed compilers RIGHT NOW. Allen-Babcock (named after a husband-wife team)
could provide that within a month by writing a p-code interpreter. The
motivation was time-to-market, not memory issues. 

I almost went to work for Allen-Babcock. It later morphed into Ryan-McFarland,
which carried the p-code flame through the 70s.

>Even in 1984 it would have been unusal to find machines with more than
>256Kb, possibly power users had 512Kb.

True. But running out of memory was not a common problem. 

>At that time I was running multi-user Cobol applications with 3 users
>in 256Kb under MP/M or using networked 8 bit machines.

By coincidence, I was supporting 3 telephone users in a multi-user BASCOM app in
256K under MS-DOS (all native code).  

>Byte code also allowed systems to be distributed to dozens of
>different machines without even having to recompile.  With RM or Accu
>I could develop on an 8 bit machine and distribute to a 68k Unix
>machine.  I didn't need to buy a machine or a compiler for the target
>system.

That is a benefit to the small developer. 

>Interactive systems spend 95% or the time waiting for keystrokes and
>most of the rest waiting for disk.  Code speed is irrelevant for most
>purposes in business systems.

Yeahbut, when the user hits the Enter key, he wants a response within 100 ms. 
My interactive apps would do 20 START/READ NEXT, yet the screen would appear
almost instanatly. I'd think 'That's impossible.How could it do 20 random IOs so
quickly?'  Then I'd remember -- 'Oh yeah, Realia is managing buffers.'

I was running a lot of batch jobs where speed IS important. With Realia's
superior caching (since replaced by Windows' even better caching), programs
weren't spending much time waiting for IO.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 77)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T23:21:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306092221.79837316@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <bc25s8$9a1$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ee51a48.90121338@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >> I think every compiler should be self-compiled, for two reasons:
> >>
> >> .. It shows the compiler writers have their heart in it, and really
>  understand
> >> the language.

It only shows that the compiler writers can use the features of the
language appropriate to writing a compiler.  It does not show that the
compiler writers understand what the users will want to do with the
language.

For example a compiler written in Cobol does not show they understand
ISAM file access or embedded SQL or multi-user, or GUI access or many
other things vital or useful for Cobol usage.

> >> .. It's a reasonably good test of the language's robustness.

How 'robust' does it need to be for a compile.  Compiles don't run
24/7 like a CICS service does.  Compiles are generally 'single-shot',
such things as memory leakage are not an issue.  Going belly-up on
garbage input is not an issue.  A compiler only need produce good
output when the input is clean.

I don't see any assurance from being a compiler that the language is
'robust'.

> Whatever tool is appropriate is an euphemism for whatever tool I prefer to use.
> It doesn't 'prove' the tool is more capable, it's just rationalization.

Having a compiler in the language doesn't 'prove' that the language is
useful for the purpose it was designed for, it only proves it can
compile itself.

I can show you J&W Pascal compilers for K&R Pascal. What does this
show about J&W Pascal as a business language ?

What would a Cobol compiler in Cobol show about Cobol as a business
language ?
```

```
I disagree.   Some tools are better for some tasks than others.   You're right
that when the only tool you have is a hammer, all problems look like nails - but
if other tools are available, you pick the best tool.

RPG is not a tool designed to create compilers.   Hammers aren't designed to cut
wood.  Shotguns aren't designed to sink battleships.

> It doesn't 'prove' the tool is more capable, it's just rationalization.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 75)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T13:32:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc2qso$1noe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net...

> I think every compiler should be self-compiled, for two reasons:
>
> .. It shows the compiler writers have their heart in it, and really
understand
> the language.

The idea of self-compilation is a useful one -- for those contexts in which
the language itself is well-suited to implementing compilers in the first
place.

If you want to write an RPG compiler in RPG, and demonstrate to Unisys that
it would be cost-effective both in terms of purchase price and maintenance
costs, and will provide a compile-time and execution-time performance
benefit to Unisys' user base,  for Unisys to scrap the compiler they've been
marketing for decades now and use yours, feel free.

Having been exposed to a number of languages in my time, I would contend
that whatever environments RPG might be well-suited for, writing compilers
isn't one of them, and the shenanigans that an RPG programmer has to go
through to overcome the basic capacity limitations defined into the
language, along with the defeat (if necessary) of the PLC, for extremely
large programs like compilers (and operating systems and message control
systems) mean that, no matter how much the programmer's "heart" is in RPG,
he's going to be spending a lot of his time, and the machine's time, saving
state for the indicators (L0-L9, UO-U9, H0-H9, LR, MR, 01-99, OA-OG and
OV -- is that the complete list?).

That's just one example.  We *could* write a COBOL compiler in COBOL if we
wanted.  We've already written a skeleton of a parser -- the utility we
supply to recognize COBOL(68) constructs and translate them into COBOL85 is
in COBOL.  We've found that the string-handling mechanisms in other
languages are more useful and more flexible for the purpose of writing
compilers than those to be found in COBOL, and do not wish to saddle our
maintenance programmers with the need to overcome the relative limitations,
nor do we wish to saddle our users with the inevitable execution-time costs
of programmatic circumvention of limitations inherent to the language in
which the compiler is written.

From the fact that one *can* use a language to write a compiler it is
inappropriate to conclude that a compiler written in that language would be
useful, efficient, maintainable, or reliable.  By the way, I personally
wouldn't choose RPG to write applications like mathematical models of the
chemical equations in the human retina, either.  That doesn't mean I
couldn't, and it also doesn't mean that RPG isn't used for, or useful for,
say, inventory control or payroll.

As it happens, our company has found ALGOL and its dialects (e.g., NEWP) and
its descendants (e.g., Pascal83) useful languages for compiler development,
and well-suited in terms of efficiency and clarity to that task.  Our users
have agreed with our choice, and have found our compilers efficient on the
whole.

Also, in the case of a language that has a standard or a language that's
intended for compatibility with another implementation, having a programmer
who fully understands the language can be a liability -- he might be tempted
to presume he knows what it's supposed to do rather than doing all the
research to ensure that the change being made is appropriate.  Being humble
enough to double-check the manual is a virtue.

I think it's arguably (and demonstrably!) more important that the body of
language support personnel all are familiar with the same language, and are
all familiar with how to look up what languages are supposed to do in the
relevant books.  Any ALGOL programmer worth his salt could have figured out
what was going wrong with the *DATE mechanism in our RPG compiler recently;
it took a lot less time to find the problem in a compiler written in ALGOL
(which is indeed a language well suited to writing compilers) than it would
have had the compiler been written in RPG, no matter how much the
programmer's heart was in it.  And in this particular milieu it's a lot
easier to get enough expertise in RPG to fix a reported problem in a
compiler written in ALGOL, particular

> .. It's a reasonably good test of the language's robustness.

At least partly true. Our ALGOL compiles have been triple-compiled before
being used elsewhere for somewhere in the region of four decades now, since
the first introduction of an ALGOL compiler on the Burroughs B5000.
Pascal83 and NEWP have been triple-compiled since their introduction, and
ESPOL was triple-compiled for its entire lifetime.  A triple compilation has
been the first test any of these compilers encounters for a very long time.

Building a compiler in RPG might -- *might* -- be a useful stress test of
the RPG compiler, but it's not clear that such a compiler would meet all the
criteria of a successful product.  It is clear that writing compilers in
ALGOL has led to successful compilers, in ALGOL and in other languages as
well.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 76)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T00:21:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee7aec9.33391375@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <bc2qso$1noe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net...

>As it happens, our company has found ALGOL and its dialects (e.g., NEWP) and
>its descendants (e.g., Pascal83) useful languages for compiler development,
>and well-suited in terms of efficiency and clarity to that task.  Our users
>have agreed with our choice, and have found our compilers efficient on the
>whole.

Before Realia, all PC COBOL compilers were written in C-like (or possibly
ALGOL-like) languages. They were all mediocre. Some would argue, and I might
even agree, that C and ALGOL are 'better tools' for compiler development. But
the FACTS belie the argument. In FACT, Realia was the best compiler by a wide
margin. 

Before Realia, competitors would have said "Our users have agreed with our
choice." That begs the question. It is a circular argument which uses its
conclusion as one of its premises. 

A byproduct of writing the Realia compiler in COBOL is that we application
programmers got extensions such as dynamic memory allocation, bit-wise inline
functions and calls to the operating system API. If the compiler had been
written in another language, we wouldn't have gotten them. (Remember, it was
written prior to '85). 


>
>Also, in the case of a language that has a standard or a language that's
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 79)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T12:24:31-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306121124.3c9c05d7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <bc2qso$1noe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee7aec9.33391375@news.optonline.net> <bc8uud$5m7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ee8383c.68583851@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >As for "mediocre" - I assume you have some justification for that claim?
> 
> They produced pseudo-code that ran as slow as the Basic interpreter.

We have had the argument about what the term 'pseudo-code' means
before.  I had hoped that once something has been explained to you,
and you agree, then it actually sinks in, but no:

""" Pseudocode (pronounced SOO-doh-kohd) is a detailed yet readable
description of what a computer program or algorithm must do, expressed
in a formally-styled natural language rather than in a programming
language."""

Several compilers generated p-code or byte-code or intermediate-code
('int').

MicroFocus, for example, produced .int code from CIS-Cobol in 1978 on
CP/M and other 8bit systems.  This allowed large applications to be
built in small memories (64Kb total RAM) and for the code to be
relocated for CALL/CANCEL optimisation.

For interactive applications code performance is not a requirement as
disk access times is very much larger than any code execution time. 
Having run 3 users on a 2.5 MHz 8085 using .int code and finding 60%
free CPU time (but disk was running at 80% utilisation) I found that
pcode was not an issue.

With Level II Cobol (1981) there was an optional 'generator' that
produce native Intel code in .gnt files.  This code was much larger
but also quite faster. However in typical real world programs this was
insignificant.

MF Cobol/2 in 1984 could produce .int or .gnt or native .exe with
optimisation.

It comes down to 'suitable tools for the job'.  If you actually want
to write code to do ADD 1 10 million times then something that
optimises it all away is what you want.  For business systems, code
speed is most likely irrelevant and many other factors have a more
beneficial seffect.

For example the Realia that you found 'best' had no file sharing and
locking (until 1995 apparently).  This made the ISAM disk access very
fast (so does making MF use EXCLUSIVE). It also made it almost useless
for simple intercative multi-user systems.
Realia was the 'best' for developing IBM mainframe code.  It may even
have been the 'best' for what others would do in C if they knew it.

For multi-user interactive business systems on a variety of machines
it wasn't even close.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 80)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-16T23:31:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eee473e.180878547@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <bc2qso$1noe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee7aec9.33391375@news.optonline.net> <bc8uud$5m7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ee8383c.68583851@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306121124.3c9c05d7@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>('int').

The term pseudocode has two unrelated meanings: the one you quoted and
intermediate code. Nearly ALL compilers produce intermediate code out of their
front-end. The ones with a back-end translate it to native code during the
compilation process (or JIT in the case of .NET and JVM). The ones lacking a
back-end interpret intermediate code at execution time. 

>MicroFocus, for example, produced .int code from CIS-Cobol in 1978 on
>CP/M and other 8bit systems.  This allowed large applications to be
>built in small memories (64Kb total RAM) and for the code to be
>relocated for CALL/CANCEL optimisation.

Micro Focus still produces .int code by default. You must tell it to run the
back-end to produce a .gnt (machine language) file. 

>For interactive applications code performance is not a requirement as
>disk access times is very much larger than any code execution time. 

I disagree. Interactive applications are the most sensitive to execution speed.
Whereas batch programs can chug away all night, interactive are judged by the
interval between hitting the Enter key (or mouseclick) and the results appearing
on the screen. If it is longer than 100 ms, it's too slow. 

>Having run 3 users on a 2.5 MHz 8085 using .int code and finding 60%
>free CPU time (but disk was running at 80% utilisation) I found that
>pcode was not an issue.

That was in the early '80s. Follow Mr. Peabody through the time machine to 2003,
when users expect a GUI interface and responsiveness like Google. If they could
get it from interpreters, .NET and JVM wouldn't 'waste' their time on JIT
compilers. 

>With Level II Cobol (1981) there was an optional 'generator' that
>produce native Intel code in .gnt files.  This code was much larger
>but also quite faster. However in typical real world programs this was
>insignificant.

That's discrepent with my experience, 

>MF Cobol/2 in 1984 could produce .int or .gnt or native .exe with
>optimisation.

They were so married to 'run-time' that native .exe's were bigger than 1M. They
linked the whole 'run-time' into the .exe. Realia didn't do that. It produced
clean .exe's containing only the necessary functions. 

>It comes down to 'suitable tools for the job'.  If you actually want
>to write code to do ADD 1 10 million times then something that
>optimises it all away is what you want.  For business systems, code
>speed is most likely irrelevant and many other factors have a more
>beneficial effect.

You should pronounce it 'bidness', as they do in Texas. 

>For example the Realia that you found 'best' had no file sharing and
>locking (until 1995 apparently).  This made the ISAM disk access very
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>it wasn't even close.

<slap on the forehead> I wasted fifteen years writing multi-user systems on
Realia without realizing it was inadequate. Silly me, I should have tuned into
CLC.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 82)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-17T01:26:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vet9k37fh5ejf6@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee8383c.68583851@news.optonline.net> <3eee473e.180878547@news.optonline.net> <bcln6k$pb2$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bcln6k$pb2$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <3eee473e.180878547@news.optonline.net>,
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >>> >As for "mediocre" - I assume you have some justification for that
claim?
> >>>
> >>> They produced pseudo-code that ran as slow as the Basic interpreter.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> code'; please be so kind as to supply a source for this.

Thomas C. McIntire, "Software Interpreters for Microcomputers,"
John Wiley & Sons, 1978.

The inside flap of the book cover states: "About the author.
Thomas C. McIntire is a senior systems analyst for the NCR
Corporation in Dayton, Ohio."

Mr McIntire uses the terms 'pseudo-code', 'pseudo-language',
'pseudo-object code', 'pseudo-object program', and 'pseudo-
operation (pseudo-op)' at various places in his book. He also
uses COBOL for some examples.

Page 1, "Interpreter . . . a software routine that, as processing
progresses, translates a stored program expressed in pseudo-
code into machine code and executes the intended operations."

Page 9, "Not only can language selection influence performance
but, more important, the pseudo-code object representation of
the programming statements can have a significant impact. The
closer the syntactical form of the intermediate object is to that
of the processor machine code, the less severe are the overhead
burdens."

Page 51, "In a software interpretive system using as its driving
force a pseudo-code instruction form, three languages are
involved: (1) the source programming language, (2) the pseudo-
object, or interim language, and (3) the machine language of
the processor."

In the quote from page 9, an equivalence between 'pseudo-code'
and 'intermediate [code]' may be seen. If one allows, from page
51, that 'pseudo-object' should read 'pseudo-code' and that
'interim' was used where 'intermediate' was intended, (Can a
'language', in this context, be temporary?), one may see an
equivalence between 'pseudo-code [language]' and 'intermediate
[code language]'.

It is possible, that about 25 years ago, Mr Wagner read this,
or another, text in which 'pseudocode' and 'intermediate code'
may have been seen to share the same definition. And having
been about 25 years ago, may have difficulty finding a current
reference on the internet.

What I suggest is the dual usage of 'pseudocode', in 1978, changed
to singular usage, while the memory of its prior dual usage remains
or, to Mr Wagner, there is no difference, and, to those unaware of
such texts, there is no equivalence. Such conditions can lead to
disagreement.

My apologies to all who may be offended by my support of
Mr Wagner on this point. <g>
```

_(thread depth: 83)_

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306170105.2b736859@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee8383c.68583851@news.optonline.net> <3eee473e.180878547@news.optonline.net> <bcln6k$pb2$1@panix1.panix.com> <vet9k37fh5ejf6@corp.supernews.com>`

```
"Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote

> Page 1, "Interpreter . . . a software routine that, as processing
> progresses, translates a stored program expressed in pseudo-
> code into machine code and executes the intended operations."

Hmmm. Typically an interpreter may translate a _source_ program into
some intermediate form which then used as input to a byte executor.

'Translates .. into machine code' sounds like a JIT compiler.
```

- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee8383c.68583851@news.optonline.net> <3eee473e.180878547@news.optonline.net> <bcln6k$pb2$1@panix1.panix.com> <vet9k37fh5ejf6@corp.supernews.com> <217e491a.0306170105.2b736859@posting.google.com>`

```
news:217e491a.0306170105.2b736859@posting.google.com...
> "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 'Translates .. into machine code' sounds like a JIT compiler.

I agree. Having been published in 1978, near the beginning
of the 'microcomputer revolution', the book bears some of
the qualities of other books of the time. That is, it served to
fill an information gap by using 'Microcomputer' in its title.
Since then a bit more precision has been added to reach
the understandings of today.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcn513$6kv$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…

Mr Wagner, the quotations above refer to a variety of things; in support 
of your assertion that the compilers were 'mediocre' you stated that they 
produced 'psuedo-code' which ran slowly.  When I was taught such things 
lo, those many moons ago, 'psuedo-code' was 'false code', the way that 
processing was explained while designing a program... usually used before 
and during the construction of a flowchart.  

Mr Smith cited a text from 1978 which appears to support your equating of
the two.

('p-code', as I recall it, was the portable code (or 'phony code')  
produced by the Pascal compiler that ETH put out... but my memory is, 
admittedly, porous)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 81)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-17T14:30:46+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bclujp$q7f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee8383c.68583851@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306121124.3c9c05d7@posting.google.com> <3eee473e.180878547@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> The term pseudocode has two unrelated meanings: the one you quoted and
> intermediate code. 

The term 'p-code' is usually used for intermediate code in order to avoid 
any confusion.

> Nearly ALL compilers produce intermediate code out of
> their front-end. 

Is that right. But then many intermediate code aren't 'pseudo' at all.

Or will you modify this with 'I meant COBOL compilers' or 'I meant the ones 
that do output byte-code'.

> Micro Focus still produces .int code by default. You must tell it to run
> the back-end to produce a .gnt (machine language) file.

We've been through that before. While you use a Unix MF, which may well do 
.int as default, that is not necessarily true of all compilers from MF.

For Cobol/2 it is the OPT and OMF directives which control the code being 
produced. The defaults for these are "2" and "OBJ" respectively which means 
that it will output object code for linking.
 
>>For interactive applications code performance is not a requirement as
>>disk access times is very much larger than any code execution time.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> slow.

You may well disagree, but 'normal' interactive business applications 
access disk files, that 100ms may well represent 95ms waiting for READs and 
WRITEs to complete.  Making a 300% difference to 5% matters not a lot.

> 2003, when users expect a GUI interface and responsiveness like Google. 

Google isn't a GUI, its a web server.  

GUIs are the _least_ sensitive to running intermediate code, which is why 
VB can do that.  Most of the time is spent in the CALLs amde to the OS (or 
other) waiting for it to draw on the screen.  You do realise that it is the 
GUI library or the OS that is constructing the fonts, not the application, 
don't you ?

>>MF Cobol/2 in 1984 could produce .int or .gnt or native .exe with
>>optimisation.
> 
> They were so married to 'run-time' that native .exe's were bigger than 1M.
> They linked the whole 'run-time' into the .exe. 

Geez, Robert, I thought that you knew _something_, but obviously not.

Cobol/2 has 3 run times: static, dynamic and shared.  The shared run-time 
is just a DLL that can be used by any Cobol .EXE.  My .EXEs from Cobol/2 
range from 4Kb to a couple of hundred Kb.

> Realia didn't do that. It
> produced clean .exe's containing only the necessary functions.

<shrug> MF's _could_ be linked to contain the functions, or just use the 
shared run-time and shared ADIS and EXTFH modules.  Even on DOS MF did this.
```

```
>> Realia didn't do that. It
>> produced clean .exe's containing only the necessary functions.
>
><shrug> MF's _could_ be linked to contain the functions, or just use the 
>shared run-time and shared ADIS and EXTFH modules.  Even on DOS MF did this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 83)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-18T13:47:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcogeo$sc2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eee473e.180878547@news.optonline.net> <bclujp$q7f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eefa8f5.271443759@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:


>>Google isn't a GUI, its a web server.
> 
> Its results are presented graphically. My point was it's amazingly fast.

Google does a database search using MySQL (IIRC) and pushes out the results 
via Apache, the page is sent using the internet, the actual displaying is 
done using your browser.

Given that trail it wouldn't matter one jot whether the code that built the 
SQL from the CGI enquire parameters was written in Perl, BASIC or hand 
crafted assembler.

As I said the actual application code has very little effect on the speed 
of the GUI when 95+% of the time is spent in database search, transmition 
time and display time.

If you have a web page that is 'active' with mouse-overs and such then that 
is all interpreted source code JavaScript - fast isn't it.

>>GUIs are the _least_ sensitive to running intermediate code, which is why
>>VB can do that.
> 
> Then why did VB make such fanfare about their native code generation ten
> years ago? As it turned out, it wasn't much faster than interpreted.

There yer go.  VB was criticised for being byte-code (just as you 
criticised some Cobols), yet because most time was spent in database access 
and painting windows the results were insignificantly different.

> Fair enough. One place where I worked got similar sizes. Here I get one
> meg of 'run time' linked in. Apparently I need to research compiler
> directives.

Why are you 'linking in' ?  I use SCO and Linux MF and just use the cobrun 
and dozens of .gnts.
```

```
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

I do the same. One place compiled to Unix executables of reasonable size. It
must have been loading cobrun as a shared object; I can't find that option.
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >Do they ?  I don't think so.  It's all in the database indexing.
> 
> We're both speculating. I can see where indexing *might* be used.


Actually, no, mine wasn't:

""Google delivered the GB-8008 in the morning, and we had it crawling
and indexing on our network that afternoon. It's amazing how simple it
was to install," says Corley. "What's more, the Google Search
Appliance is easy to administer and saves time by streamlining the
process of finding and organizing all our intranet data. In fact, with
Google, we doubled the number of documents our employees can search."
Besides doubling the size of the index, the increased throughput of
the Google crawler and indexer enabled Corley to improve the refresh
rate for the index by a factor of four over the previous search
engine. "Where the old engine indexed 2,500 documents an hour, the
Google Search Appliance indexes roughly 25,000. This allows us to
completely reindex the documents of relevance in about twelve hours,
so we don't have to worry about incremental indexing. Adding
subcollections is also very easy, with no need for a separate crawl
and index for that content.""
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 88)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T01:03:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef2290e.99596975@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bclujp$q7f$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3eefa8f5.271443759@news.optonline.net> <bcogeo$sc2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ef00640.295329873@news.optonline.net> <bcqhtv$nrl$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ef17a83.54906703@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306191150.6bcd3392@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>and index for that content.""

That doesn't say how documents are retrieved. I do the same sort of indexing --
an entry for each word with a pointer to the source. There are three ways to
process a query for, let's say, A B C. One way is to query each of the three
words or fragments (LIKE A%), then ask the database to do an INNER JOIN. The
second way uses subqueries: SELECT source FROM words WHERE word LIKE A% and
source IN (SELECT source FROM words WHERE word LIKE B% and souce IN (SELECT
source FROM words WHERE word LIKE C%)). The third way figures out which of the
three words is least frequently represented in the index, fetch those documents
and write your own code to look for the other two words. 

I've tried all three and found the third to be fastest and also most flexible
(below). The problem with the first two, if not optimized, is a large number of
hits in the index. I have words which occur 500,000 times in 10M documents.
Retrieving 500K index entries is unacceptly slow. 

As to flexibility, I have a requirement that Google doesn't -- the need for
approximate matches on numbers and dates as well as words. For example, 06/03
should match any date in 06/??/2003; the number 7 should match any number
between 7.0 and 7.9999; 2003 should match any date in ??/??/2003. If done with
LIKE %2003, the number of hits and index entries would be enormous. I don't
bother to index on numbers and dates.

I don't know which approach Google uses. Judging from speed, I speculate they're
using the third.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 92)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T21:41:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef345af.13118671@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ef2290e.99596975@news.optonline.net> <bctoi4$56g$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ef27a80.120449903@news.optonline.net> <bcutv3$l7n$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ef27a80.120449903@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>one that indicated how you were 'factually impaired'?

The quotation Richard posted did not contain facts about query processing. It
talked about creating an index database. 

'Database indexing' is a function the database engine performs for you when you
tell it to CREATE INDEX. An index database is created by code you write. In this
case, the code parses words out of a document and creates an entry in the index
database for each word in the document database.

Getting the database engine to do this for you would be inefficient and/or
require a non-standard SQL extension like Oracle's VARRAY. You'd have to parse
the document into an array of varchar words before inserting it into the
document database. The only fact we have is Google doesn't do that; it creates
its own index.

Once you have an index database, a query *could* use it for a 100% SQL document
retrieval. I described why a user-written query is faster.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Robert Wagner's latest error (was: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 93)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T10:17:47+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd01aq$3r2$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <bctoi4$56g$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ef27a80.120449903@news.optonline.net> <bcutv3$l7n$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ef345af.13118671@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> The quotation Richard posted did not contain facts about query processing.
> It talked about creating an index database.

It is the indexes that are the basis of satifying the query.
 
> 'Database indexing' is a function the database engine performs for you
> when you tell it to CREATE INDEX. An index database is created by code you
> write. In this case, the code parses words out of a document and creates
> an entry in the index database for each word in the document database.

You have no information that it creates 'an entry .. for each word'.

An essense of the Google engine is that there is a ranking built into the 
database.  It is not just words in the index.
 
> Getting the database engine to do this for you would be inefficient and/or
> require a non-standard SQL extension like Oracle's VARRAY. 

Given they use MySQL, which is extremely fast at retrieval, they may also 
modify it in any way they feel makes it suitable for their needs.

> You'd have to
> parse the document into an array of varchar words before inserting it into
> the document database. The only fact we have is Google doesn't do that; it
> creates its own index.

In what way is your speculation a 'fact' ?
 
> Once you have an index database, a query *could* use it for a 100% SQL
> document retrieval. I described why a user-written query is faster.

You speculated that it might be faster (not _is_) but that depends on 
things about which you have no information at all, such as MySQL 
performance characteristics for what Google uses it for and modifications 
and optimisations built into Google's versions of this.
```

```
> >>>> I don't know which approach Google uses. 
> 
> Once you have an index database, a query *could* use it for a 100% SQL document
> retrieval. I described why a user-written query is faster.

From a recent item about MySQL:

"""Prior to MySQL 4, full-text searching was fast but not very
powerful, but that has all changed.  From 4 onwards, you can now pass
full-text searches to MySQL using the standard boolean operator set. 
Giving MySQL control of it all, means the query optimizer can adjust
its scanning process to get the best result for whatever situation you
find yourself in."""
```

- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <bc2qso$1noe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee7aec9.33391375@news.optonline.net> <bc8uud$5m7$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3ee8383c.68583851@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306121124.3c9c05d7@posting.google.com> <3eee473e.180878547@news.optonline.net> <bcna08$8uk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bcna08$8uk$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 16-Jun-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Cite?

Microsoft Bookself 98 has the following definition for pseudocode.
------
pseudocode

pseudocode (s�'do-kod`) noun
1. Abbreviated p-code. A machine language for a nonexistent
processor (a pseudomachine). Such code is executed by a
software interpreter. The major advantage of p-code is that it is
portable to all computers for which a p-code interpreter exists.
The p-code approach has been tried several times in the
microcomputer industry, with mixed success. The best known
attempt was the UCSD p-System. See also pseudomachine,
UCSD p-System.
2. Any informal, transparent notation in which a program or
algorithm description is written. Many programmers write their
programs first in a pseudocode that looks much like a mixture
of English and their favorite programming language, such as C
or Pascal, and then translate it line by line into the actual
language being used.

Microsoft� Bookshelf� Computer and Internet Dictionary� 1997
Microsoft Corporation. All rights reserved.
Portions, Microsoft Press� Computer Dictionary, Third Edition.
Copyright � 1997 by Microsoft Press. All rights reserved.
------

Elsewhere I referred to a book by Thomas C. McIntire which
appears to support an equivalence between 'intermediate
code' and the first definition given here.

Mr Plinston provided a quote similar to the second definition
given here. It is that quote to which Mr Wagner referred.

Thus, Mr Wagner's statement is not without support.

Again, my apologies to all. <g>
```

```
Words change meanings, but when the prefix pseudo is so well known, it is nice
to keep its meaning similar to the English meaning.   Pseudo-anything isn't the
real thing.

On the other hand, the general public thinks a database is anything with data in
it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 77)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T22:58:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306112158.3979fd2d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <bc2qso$1noe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee7aec9.33391375@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> Before Realia, all PC COBOL compilers were written in C-like (or possibly
> ALGOL-like) languages. 

Were they indeed ?  ALL of them ?  Does that include Microfocus which
had written its compilers in Cobol since before there were IBM PCs ?

Does it include MicroSoft version 1,2 and 3 which, I am assured, were
in assembler.

Nevada Cobol was assembler, like Microsoft Cobol it predated the
availability of C compilers on PCs.

What about BOS microCobol ? What language is that written in Robert ?

> They were all mediocre. Some would argue, and I might
> even agree, that C and ALGOL are 'better tools' for compiler development. But
> the FACTS belie the argument. In FACT, Realia was the best compiler by a wide
> margin. 

What criteria are you using for 'best' ?  Is this some sort of
absolute 'best', or is it just your subjective and emotional
attachment to it ?

Please explain how it is better than, say, Accu, for distributing
applications.

What part has the language the compiler been written in made this the
'best' ?


> Before Realia, competitors would have said "Our users have agreed with our
> choice." That begs the question. It is a circular argument which uses its
> conclusion as one of its premises. 

Would they have said that ?  Isn't that a conclusion that you have
used as a premise ?

> A byproduct of writing the Realia compiler in COBOL is that we application
> programmers got extensions such as dynamic memory allocation, bit-wise inline
> functions and calls to the operating system API. 

Geez, can't C or Algol or Assembler do those things ? What stops other
run-time libraries having those ?

> If the compiler had been
> written in another language, we wouldn't have gotten them. (Remember, it was
> written prior to '85). 

Surprisingly enough the vast majority of business systems don't give a
rats arse about using such C-isms as malloc().  I've managed to call
whatever OS API that I've needed to since using MP/M II and other 8bit
systems, it just ain't hard to do.  And have had perfectly adequate
bit handling tools for my needs since the very late 70s.

You may have thought Realia was the 'best' because it allowed you to
write C programs in COBOL.

Others may have thought that Accu was the 'best' because it allowed
them to develop applications on one system and distribute them to
completely different systems without even having to recompile.

Others thought that MBP Visual Cobol was 'best' because they could
write GUI apps for Windows 3.1.
```

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>had written its compilers in Cobol since before there were IBM PCs ?

>Does it include MicroSoft version 1,2 and 3 which, I am assured, were

http://216.239.53.100/search?q=cache:UCKRws6h0dQJ:supportline.microfocus.com/documentation/books/oc41books/cg2sou.htm+microsoft+cobol&hl=en&lr=lang_en&ie=UTF-8

>Nevada Cobol was assembler, like Microsoft Cobol it predated the

Nevada Cobol ran on CP/M. The MSDOS version was called Utah Cobol. I'll take
your word that it was written in assembler. 

>What about BOS microCobol ? What language is that written in Robert ?

Surely not Cobol. It was very non-standard. 

>> They were all mediocre. Some would argue, and I might
>> even agree, that C and ALGOL are 'better tools' for compiler development. But
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>attachment to it ?

It produced fast-running code, had a very fast file system, nice extensions, a
good dynamic call. 

>Please explain how it is better than, say, Accu, for distributing
>applications.

>What part has the language the compiler been written in made this the
>'best' ?

>> A byproduct of writing the Realia compiler in COBOL is that we application

I prefer doing everything in Cobol.

>> If the compiler had been

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>bit handling tools for my needs since the very late 70s.

Business programming needs go beyond inventory systems and printed reports. They
need system level utilities as well.

>You may have thought Realia was the 'best' because it allowed you to
>write C programs in COBOL.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>completely different systems without even having to recompile.

Users minded slow running p-code. 

>Others thought that MBP Visual Cobol was 'best' because they could
>write GUI apps for Windows 3.1.

Norcom produced a GUI tool for Realia.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 79)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T14:42:07-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306121342.766c3e5d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <bc2qso$1noe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee7aec9.33391375@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306112158.3979fd2d@posting.google.com> <3ee83a2d.69080152@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >Does it include MicroSoft version 1,2 and 3 which, I am assured, were
> >in assembler.
> 
> I thought Microsoft 1-3 was a rebrand of RM, which was written in C. Look here
> and note 'perform-type "RM"'

You are quite wrong.  It has nothing to do with RM at all.  Any even
casual reading of that page would show that MF caters for MS Cobol in
ways completely different from how it caters for RM.

> >What about BOS microCobol ? What language is that written in Robert ?
> 
> Surely not Cobol. It was very non-standard. 

And yet some of the things you praise Realia for are the
'non-standard' bits.

> >What criteria are you using for 'best' ?  Is this some sort of
> >absolute 'best', or is it just your subjective and emotional
> >attachment to it ?
> 
> It produced fast-running code, 

So does MF Cobol/2 (since 1984). 'Fast running code' is not useful in
most cases for multi-user interactive business applications.

> had a very fast file system, 

So do others when used in EXCLUSIVE mode, not bothering with sharing.

Of course if the system is restricted to no file sharing on a
non-multitasking system (DOS) then the user must stare at the screen
while batch runs, reports, etc are done.  Of course 'fast running'
then becomes an issue to reduce the user's frustration at not being
able to get on with the next thing.

I have always used multi-tasking OSes: MP/M, TurboDOS, CDOS,
DR-Multiuser, Unix. where batch programs and reports are done as a
background task and the user gets on with other things and the
programs share files with locking as required.

No 'sitting staring at the screen', fast, fast, fast at the expense of
everything else is not required.

I can see why you may have your criteria.  However those are just
things that I don't care about because there are better ways of
working, smarter ways (IMHO).

> nice extensions, 

So do other Cobols. It depends on what one is used to as to whether
they are 'nice'.  I've used MF since the late 70s and liked the X/Open
screen and file sharing extensions.

> a good dynamic call. 

Don't care, never needed one.

> Distributing was done by copying files. What are you getting at?

Distributing to machines that are not the same as the developer's
amachine.  With Accu I can develop an application on, say, my Intel
Mandrake 9 laptop and copy it to your Sun box and have it run
correctly.  Realia can't do that.

> >What part has the language the compiler been written in made this the
> >'best' ?
> 
> The two who wrote it really understood Cobol. 

No. That is not an answer.  Writing a Cobol compiler may require
dynamic memory, fast code, certain extensions, but it doesn't touch
the things that _I_ care about: multi-user shared files, embedded SQL,
X/Open screens and GUI, user interaction.

They may well have understood 'Cobol', but MicroFocus RM and Accu
understood _Business_Applications_.

That is why their compilers are 'best' for creating business
applications.

> >Geez, can't C or Algol or Assembler do those things ? What stops other
> >run-time libraries having those ?
> 
> I prefer doing everything in Cobol.

Regardless, it seems, of which tool is the best for a particular job.

> Business programming needs go beyond inventory systems and printed reports.
> They need system level utilities as well.

That is why there are, in fact, several different languages.

> Users minded slow running p-code. 

Not when run on a suitable Operating System.  Not when being p-code
made zero difference anyway.
```

```

On 11-Jun-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> Before Realia, all PC COBOL compilers were written in C-like (or possibly
> ALGOL-like) languages. They were all mediocre. Some would argue, and I might
> even agree, that C and ALGOL are 'better tools' for compiler development. But
> the FACTS belie the argument. In FACT, Realia was the best compiler by a wide
> margin.

So there weren't any good compilers and there weren't any lousy compilers?

You seem to be implying that the reason Realia was the best compiler by a wide
margin (who determined this?), is because of the tool used to write the
language.   It isn't the Indian, it is the arrow?


> A byproduct of writing the Realia compiler in COBOL is that we application
> programmers got extensions such as dynamic memory allocation, bit-wise inline
> functions and calls to the operating system API. If the compiler had been
> written in another language, we wouldn't have gotten them. (Remember, it was
> written prior to '85).

Is that why Realia was a better compiler?

Why wouldn't you have gotten those extensions if Realia had been written in
another language?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 77)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T15:08:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcatl6$1b2r$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <bbnou8$358$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee057d7.648729578@news.optonline.net> <bbqckj$1utg$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee111bd.46350349@news.optonline.net> <bc2qso$1noe$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ee7aec9.33391375@news.optonline.net>`

```
I wrote:

> >As it happens, our company has found ALGOL and its dialects (e.g., NEWP)
and
> >its descendants (e.g., Pascal83) useful languages for compiler
development,
> >and well-suited in terms of efficiency and clarity to that task.  Our
users
> >have agreed with our choice, and have found our compilers efficient on
the
> >whole.

Robert Wagner responded:

> Before Realia, all PC COBOL compilers were written in C-like (or possibly
> ALGOL-like) languages. They were all mediocre. Some would argue, and I
might
> even agree, that C and ALGOL are 'better tools' for compiler development.
But
> the FACTS belie the argument. In FACT, Realia was the best compiler by a
wide
> margin.

I don't find ALGOL much like C, and I *personally* know of no PC COBOL
compilers written in a language that I would characterize as "ALGOL-like".
The parenthetical comment strikes me as irrelevant.

In what way does the execution-time performance of a program have a direct
and demonstrable correlation to the language in which the compiler used to
produce whatever is being used to carry out the programmer's instructions?

If the problem is that all pre-Realia PC COBOL compilers produced
intermediate code of some sort that was subsequently interpreted, it doesn't
seem to me that the language the program that produced that intermediate
code is particularly relevant to the question of the performance of that
code.

Can you explain once again how a RPG program compiled with a compiler that
happens to have been written in RPG will always compile and execute more
efficiently than the same RPG program compiled with a compiler written in
ALGOL (or C or Pascal or PL/1 or ADA or ALTAC or SPS)?

> A byproduct of writing the Realia compiler in COBOL is that we application
> programmers got extensions such as dynamic memory allocation, bit-wise
inline
> functions and calls to the operating system API. If the compiler had been
> written in another language, we wouldn't have gotten them. (Remember, it
was
> written prior to '85).

Generally the extensions we put in our compilers are those that users
request, and those that represent access to new features elsewhere in the
operating environment.   I'm not convinced that extensions like dynamic
memory allocation are features for which our RPG users are clamoring; they
can already call ALGOL libraries for any such feature to which they don't
have direct access anyhow.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
In article <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>why didn't you list Excel as the primo programming language?

A dodge, Mr Wagner.  What Mr Wojcik did or did not choose to list has 
nothing to do with the fact that you changed your assertion (or, as he put 
it, 'add(ed) arbitrary conditions after the fact to support your 
argument.').

[snip]

>>Python is a general-purpose language that has been used to build large
>>applications.  It's interpreted, but it's not a "scripting language", in
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>To me, scripting language means interpreted source. 

Mr Wagner, 'meaning' is the result of interpretation; to you an entire
language, such as Catalan, might be meaningless.  A language like
EasyTrieve can be run both through an interpreted or a compiler; to you is
that a 'scripting language' which runs on mainframes??  GWBasic had an
interpreter that came with the older versions of MS-DOS... was that a
'scripting language' for PCs?

DD
```

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>I've seen a PC batch compiler.

It was called cb (compiled basic). I used it full-time for a year, during which
I wrote a major application in it. My app did voice synthesis and managed
multiple modems. 

When Realia COBOL became available, I rewrote the app in COBOL. It ran three
times faster, a lot smoother and let me do things which had been impossible
under Basic, for instance indexed files. 

>EasyTrieve is an interesting case, it is sort of a run-time p-code compiler.  
>If there are syntax errors, it doesn't start running.

See my response to Doc, who also mentioned EasyTrieve. 

>How about Rexx?

I don't know Rexx.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 73)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T07:56:24+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbqrm8$381$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net> <bbpu0g$m93$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>
> If you find my conditions arbitrary, 

I find your conditions retrospective.  Having lost the argument you want to 
change it to discard your loss win

>>Python is a general-purpose language that has been used to build large
>>applications.  

>To me, scripting language means interpreted source.

Python is not 'interpreted source', it has a JIT compiler that compiles to 
a .pyc file the first time the soutce is run, subsequent runs use only the 
compiled version (based on date/time).  There is also an optimiser which 
produces .pyo files which will be used in proference.

Your knowledge of what Python is, is less than the required level to make 
meaningful comments (as it was with many others).

'scripting languages' are any language, whether compiled or interpreted, 
that is weakly typed.

Whether an implementation of a language is an interpreter or a compiler is, 
in general, independant of the language itself.  The usual Python is JIT 
compiled, Jython is a Python compiler that produces Java byte code, others 
may do something completely different.

There are, in fact, source code runtime interpreters for C, but that 
doesn't make it a scripting language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

```
>doesn't make it a scripting language.

Ok, but ANSI C is weekly typed so THAT makes it a scripting language.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 75)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T23:41:04-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306072241.353a73ee@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net> <bbpu0g$m93$1@panix1.panix.com> <bbqrm8$381$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ee23ddc.123192452@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> COBOL is weakly typed. Is it a scripting language?
> 
> Ok, but ANSI C is weekly typed so THAT makes it a scripting language.

If they are used for 'scripting' then they can be a scripting
language.  It is _not_ whether they are compiled or interpreted that
makes them this.

Cobol or C is a 'script' if it runs as a CGI script under httpd.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 73)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T20:21:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee237d1.121645120@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net> <bbpu0g$m93$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> A language like
>EasyTrieve can be run both through an interpreted or a compiler; to you is
>that a 'scripting language' which runs on mainframes?? 

I've worked in mainframe shops that used interpreted EasyTrieve in production
batch jobs. When I asked why they didn't compile it, the response was "huh?" and
a blank stare. They'd been using it for years and didn't know compilation was an
option. 

So I'd say, as a practical matter, it's a scripting languge. 

>GWBasic had an
>interpreter that came with the older versions of MS-DOS... was that a
>'scripting language' for PCs?

Before GWBasic, the interpreter was cast in silicon and mounted on the
motherboard. Yes, that was a scripting language. It had lousy heap management,
no library, couldn't call another program (except runtime functions and assembly
language), automatic variable declaration.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 74)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T11:08:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbtrbf$g7b$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net> <bbpu0g$m93$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee237d1.121645120@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

> I've worked in mainframe shops that used interpreted EasyTrieve in
> production batch jobs. When I asked why they didn't compile it, the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> So I'd say, as a practical matter, it's a scripting languge.

So, you agree that uou _can_ use scripting languages in production batch 
jobs.

The reason for not compiling is more likely that it makes almost no 
difference at all.
 
>>GWBasic had an
>>interpreter that came with the older versions of MS-DOS... was that a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> motherboard. 

That is _only_ true of actual IBM brand and smoe IBM BIOS licencees - these 
came with PC-DOS, not MS-DOS.

'Older versions of MS-DOS' (note not PC-DOS) were for non-IBM machines, 
such as Sharp, Sirius, BondWell, DEC Rainbow etc.  These did not have BASIC 
in ROM.

The original MS Basic came on paper tape.  It was put in ROM only because 
paper tape readers were not common accessories.  For CP/M systems MS had 
BASIC-80, MBASIC and BASCOM compiler which were on disk.  For non-IBM 8086 
machines there was MBASIC-86.

> Yes, that was a scripting language. 

Only for very poor definitions of 'scripting'.

'Scripting' is most often associated with providing 'scripts' for running 
in some larger application:  configuration scripts for an editor, macro 
scripts for a spreadsheet, program run scripts for an operating system.  In 
the case of many so called scripting languages this is most often for CGI 
in httpd.  In this environment even C programs are called 'scripts'.

Word Basic _is_ a scripting language, ROM Basic and BASIC-80 is _not_.

MS's BASCOM was a compiler that produced .EXEs

> It had lousy heap
> management, no library, couldn't call another program (except runtime
> functions and assembly language), automatic variable declaration.

It was BASIC.  ROM BASIC was designed to run on a machine with 64Kb and no 
disk.  Other versions had different characteristics.

Of course ROM Basic, MS Basic, GWBasic, QBasic are just 3 implementations 
of the language BASIC designed by Kemeny and Kurtz.  Other implentations 
may have quite different characteristics.  CBASIC, for example, was quite 
suitable for building applications and was compiled.
```

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>

```
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:

>Years ago I rewrote one of them into a JIT compiler. It ran at least ten
>times faster. I just don't have numbers to support that.

I have see the type of 'benchmarks' that you do.  For example you compared 
Cobol COMP and COMP-5 using just an ADD 1 which may have resulted in the 
optimiser making it all go away creating results which are not reproducable 
with any realistic code.

Compiled code may well run 10 times faster, but that wouldn't be a 'batch 
run' doing file I/O as the file speed will be unchanged by the compile.
```

_(thread depth: 76)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

```

On  8-Jun-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> >> I've worked in mainframe shops that used interpreted EasyTrieve in
> >> production batch jobs. When I asked why they didn't compile it, the
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> I don't have any data on execution speed.

Since we can compile EasyTrieve (the compiler is paid for), companies must not
feel that the time spent compiling the code at run time isn't significant.  
This isn't quite the same thing as "execution speed", even though it is a
run-time cost.   Systems programmers pay a lot of attention to such ways of
speeding up response, especially when the solution is as easy as telling
applications programmers to compile their EasyTrieve.

I have some EasyTrieve programs which actually have user owned code concatenated
in the middle of the programmer supplied source.  They change their code before
running.
```

```
>>language), automatic variable declaration.
>
>Before GWBasic - about two decades before, that is - there was Kemeny and
>Kurtz's Beginner's All-Purpose Symbolic Instruction Code; what seems to 
>have happened is that a compiled language became an interpreted one.

Thanks in part to Bill Gates and Paul Allen, whose Basic interpreter for the
Altair spawned an empire.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 77)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T02:43:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee3d1ef.6051725@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee237d1.121645120@news.optonline.net> <bbu910$8n5$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee3684d.199605532@news.optonline.net> <bc0dms$ior$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ee3684d.199605532@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>motherboard. Yes, that was a scripting language. It had lousy heap
management,
>>>>no library, couldn't call another program (except runtime functions and
assembly
>>>>language), automatic variable declaration.
>>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>language into a scripting one.

Gates and Allen did not write the _first_  Basic interpreter, but they
popularized it. DEC PDPs and DG Novas had interpreters before Gates and Allen
wrote theirs. They developed it on a PDP8 (if memory serves), so it is
reasonable to assume they got the idea from PDP interpreters.
```

```
Mr Wagner, so you are saying that USERS (caps original) wrote the 
interpreter?  Well, that would not be too wrong, as per your usual... 
since everyone who manipulates a computer towards a given end is, in some 
wise, a 'user'.

But... that 'given a choice' indicates that you are not considering those 
who wrote the interpreters in this manner.  USERS (caps original), it 
seem, are capable of turning one thing into another by their employment 
thereof; that being the case it seems that you disagree with Mr Lincoln, 
in that he asserted 'calling a tail a leg does not make it one'.

(Likewise 'using a tail as a leg' does not make it one, either.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 80)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T23:03:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306092203.4ec8b26e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee3684d.199605532@news.optonline.net> <bc0dms$ior$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee3d1ef.6051725@news.optonline.net> <bc1qmj$p6s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee51ecb.91276843@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> I prefer to say USERS turned Basic into a scripting language. Microsoft
> offered
> BASCOM, a true Basic compiler, but it was largely ignored in favor of the
> interpreter.

Your preference does not necessarily create reality.

Given that there are, for most purposes, no significant difference
between the source language for MBASIC and BASCOM what in the language
was actually 'turned' ?

In any case MBASIC and BASCOM are not generally called 'scripting
languages'.  'MS Word BASIC' _is_ a scripting language (because it is
embedded in a larger application.

Perl and PHP are 'scripting languages' because they are often embeeded
in httpd as CGI scripts.

BASCOM was not 'freely' available, it cost several hundred dollars. 
For most the cost was not worth the results.  Many other professional
programmers bought CBASIC or Turbo BASIC or TRUE Basic or similar and
used it as a compiled languages (or languages).
```
