###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 82)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T13:30:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3ee3684d.199605532@news.optonline.net> <bc0dms$ior$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee3d1ef.6051725@news.optonline.net> <bc1qmj$p6s$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee51ecb.91276843@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306092203.4ec8b26e@posting.google.com> <bc4p6p$guo$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 

> I still have no good handle on what "scripting language" means in a context that
> makes sense for Robert to discard it as being unworthy.   It seems that he is
> describing interpreted languages, but that doesn't make sense.  What's the
> difference (worthy-wise) between interpreted and compiled languages?

Especially when it has little to do with the language itself.  It is
an implementation issue in many cases - as exemplified by BASIC which
is both.

There is/was also Quick-C which was an interpreter for C.

The only reason for RW is that he many claims about how programmers
had 'voted' for several languages and he had thought of C++, Java, and
a handful of others.  He didn't want to admit that his horizon was
severely limited.  It was another excuse for why he 'meant' 'only
those he could think of'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 83)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T21:28:55-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc60kn$chc$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306092203.4ec8b26e@posting.google.com> <bc4p6p$guo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com>`

```
In article <217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com>,
Richard <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote:
>"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>is both.

Ah, so someone *did* see this... I thought I was getting too obscure 
again.  What I was trying to point out was that the same series of 
statements in a language - in one case EasyTrieve, in another case BASIC - 
were treated differently, at one time being compiled, in another case 
being interpreted.

In both cases the language did not change... but for some reason Mr Wagner 
believes that running a source file one day through an interpreter makes 
contain a 'scripting language' while running the same source file through 
a compiler the next day makes it otherwise.

It can be said, then, that every source-file contains *two* sets of 
instructions, one 'scripting language', one 'compiled language'... and 
only by looking at what comes out of the process can one determine what is 
inside.

No, wait... what comes out of both processes will be identical results (or
so one should hope, given the Nature of Data Processing) so it really
*isn't* an instance of Schoredinger's Source.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 84)_

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T21:56:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2jAFa.65044$%42.58475@fed1read06>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306092203.4ec8b26e@posting.google.com> <bc4p6p$guo$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com> <bc60kn$chc$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc,

You and the gang are way over my head with this script discussion. However,
I don't have sence enough to stay out of it from my point of experience.

When I ran a BBS, the setup was often difficult for my slow mind. Yet,
The Modem Vendor provided a method of saving each operator action in the
process, and allowing it to be saved, and

Then they had software that would feed the "script" to the OS as though
it was a single instruction. The script was a capture of a correct entering
of commands. The commands in turn executed the program*S.

So, as an example, Steve Wright's Meta COBOL was (to me) a script as
it had to be processed like any other program to create the desired COBOL.

I use such illustrations to clear my mind only. I have no experienced with
any
other "script" but JCL.

What say you guys and gals?

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 85)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T11:35:09-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc7i7d$2b5$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306101230.55b0662e@posting.google.com> <bc60kn$chc$1@panix1.panix.com> <2jAFa.65044$%42.58475@fed1read06>`

```
In article <2jAFa.65044$%42.58475@fed1read06>,
Warren Simmons <wsimmons5@cox.net> wrote:
>Doc,
>
>You and the gang are way over my head with this script discussion. However,
>I don't have sence enough to stay out of it from my point of experience.

Pfoo, Mr Simmons... that over-headed-ness does not seem to have stopped 
many of the folks here, jump on in!

>
>When I ran a BBS, the setup was often difficult for my slow mind. Yet,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>What say you guys and gals?

The difficulty I have, Mr Simmons, is that during the course of the 
discussion here Mr Wagner seems to have been proven wrong about the number 
of languages to which 'We programmers... have voted Yes' by Mr Wojcik's 
list in:

<http://www.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&selm=3ED9202C.30200%40newsguy.com>

Rather than admit to (yet again) being wrong in one of his assertions Mr 
Wagner attempted to (in a style which he has demonstrated previously and 
still insists on employing) change his assertion by adding new conditions, 
as found in:

<http://www.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&selm=3eda9f13.273692202%40news.optonline.net>

One of the new conditions that Mr Wagner presented was that he was 
referring to 'compiled, not interpreted scripting languages'.

By using the examples of EasyTrieve and BASIC I was attempting to 
demonstrate that since the same source file can be either compiled or 
interpreted in EasyTrieve and BASIC and that given the same data the 
results of the compiled or interpreted code should be the same then Mr 
Wagner's criterion of 'compiled, not interpreted scripting language' was, 
in addition to being posted after he was, once again, proven wrong, 
completely and utterly idiosyncratic and spurious.

Mr Wagner, it seems, disagrees.

DD
```

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306061457.62c0c222@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >And what the hell is "not supported" supposed to mean? 
> 
> It means you cannot call their help desk on the phone. 

I seem to recall that one cannot call MF or Fujitsu's help desk on the
phone either, they require email problem notification.

Does this put Cobol into the 'not supported' categorey ?
 
> >There are
> >Haskell compilers which are still actively supported.  nhc98 was last
> >updated in March.
> 
> It runs on MacOS and POSIX, not Windows.

nhc98:

""Available for all Unix-like platforms (including MacOS-X,
Cygwin/Windows, Linux, Solaris, *BSD, AIX, HP-UX, Ultrix, IRIX,
etc.)""

also - in case you chage your assertion to 'I meant Windows without
Cygwin':

""The Glasgow Haskell compiler is a full implementation of Haskell,
[...] Available for a few Unix platforms (including Linux, Solaris,
*BSD, and MacOS-X) and also for Windows.

""Hugs 98 is conformant with Haskell 98. Available for all Unix
platforms including Linux, DOS, Windows 3.x, and Win 32 (Windows 95,
Win32s, NT) and Macintoshs. It has many libraries including Win32
libraries,""

Just a tiny amount of research might sometimes save you from looking
like the village idiot.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 72)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T14:49:10-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE62826.4070309@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ebe5d08.253684134@news.optonline.net> <3eceb57b.67668169@news.optonline.net> <3ed3bc58.338283155@news.cis.dfn.de> <3ed41e32.422208968@news.optonline.net> <bb1gvo$429$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d50e.44025608@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291243.2cb17b76@posting.google.com> <3ed727b0.46486933@news.optonline.net> <3ED9202C.30200@newsguy.com> <3eda9f13.273692202@news.optonline.net> <3EDF58B5.1040208@newsguy.com> <3ee04676.644279046@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
 > Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:
 >> Robert Wagner wrote:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
 > programming language?

For three reasons:

1. Excel didn't occur to me when I composed my off-the-cuff list, so I
didn't consider it for inclusion.

2. I haven't read that claim (which I'm suspicious of anyway).

3. I don't consider the Excel macro language a general-purpose language,
any more than I consider the sed language a general-purpose language,
even though they both may be Turing-complete.  That's a subjective
evaluation that may indeed be arbitrary, but I imposed it on my own
argument, not on yours, and didn't raise it in an attempt to salvage my
position, as you did.

 >> Python is a general-purpose language that has been used to build
 >> large applications.  It's interpreted, but it's not a "scripting
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
 > To me, scripting language means interpreted source.

Fair enough.  I don't think that's a useful definition, but I see little
benefit in trying to convince you of that.  (I also don't think it has
much etymological justification.  "scripting" languages were so named
because they were used to write "scripts", which named the actions that
other, preexisting programs should perform.  Like the scripts that human
actor perform, see?)

So I'll fall back on the more basic argument: why is a language
implemented as a source interpreter, rather than a compiler, not
elegible for the list of languages in which major applications are written?

 >>>> Haskell	Academic, not supported
 >>>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
 > an experimental telephony thing.

The same list, eh?  And how do you know what list that is?  The lists
I'm looking at include several major applications, at least to my
satisfaction.

No doubt you'll want to impose arbitrary conditions on "major
application" until none of the publicly-described Haskell ones fit.  How
tiresome.  Suffice it to say I find your bogus definitions unconvincing.

 >> And what the hell is "not supported" supposed to mean?
 >
 > It means you cannot call their help desk on the phone.

Ah.  You might have been clearer.  I'll grant you this one: commercial
support for a language is important in many instances.  Had you
originally written, say, "relatively few general-purpose programming
languages which are currently commercially supported", I suspect there
would have been less controversy.  Of course, it might have been wiser
yet to write, "I can only think of a few..." or some such, rather than
making a wild declaration and then having to wriggle when it was easily
shown wrong.

 >> There are Haskell compilers which are still actively supported.
 >> nhc98 was last updated in March.
 >
 > It runs on MacOS and POSIX, not Windows.

And the non sequitur of the year award goes to...  What's your point,
exactly?

Actually, according to the nhc98 mailing list, people have built it
under Cygwin to run on Windows.  The backend generates ISO C, so there's
nothing to prevent using it to compile Haskell programs for Windows.
Not that *that* has anything to do with Haskell's status as a language
used to write applications.
```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:<bb05nv$15ab$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>...

>     -Chuck Stevens

The etymology of _island_ from _ï¿½g_ or _ï¿½eg_ + _land_ can be found at
http://www.bartleby.com/61/81/I0248100.html.

It was in the sixteenth century, long after any specific Norman
influence, that pedants began introducing Latin letters back into
words that had long lost them, spelling _det_ as _debt_, _dout_ as
_doubt_ and _rein_ as _reign_.

The introduction of the _s_ into _island_ was part of this same
silliness.

Jim Allan

T

Jim Allan

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T12:25:24-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbg8b5$nek$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <299f1138.0305141914.286dc784@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com> <bb05nv$15ab$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <299f1138.0306010946.48c03e4c@posting.google.com>`

```

"jallan" <jallan@smrtytrek.com> wrote in message
news:299f1138.0306010946.48c03e4c@posting.google.com...

> The etymology of _island_ from _�g_ or _�eg_ + _land_ can be found at
> http://www.bartleby.com/61/81/I0248100.html.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> silliness.

The Merriam-Webster website at
http://www.merriam-webster.com/cgi-bin/dictionary *seems to* indicate a
pre-thirteenth-century attestation for the spelling "island" as influenced
by the old French "isle" (almost certainly brought into English directly
with the Norman Invasion), though it doesn't cite a specific written
example.  My compact Oxford's at home, out of reach at the moment, but I
find the idea that the spelling of OE "iland" was influenced more directly
by OF "isle" in the immediate post-Norman period much more likely than it
was influenced by back-formation directly from Latin "insula" some four
centuries later; were that the case I'd expect an early "n" in the spelling
someplace.

    -Chuck Stevens
    -Chuck Stevens Frencvh .  I find this more likely than the assertion
that the spelling change was a retroversion from the influence of Latin
"insula";
>
> Jim Allan
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jim Allan
```

- **Date:** 2003-06-03T11:52:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0306031052.6bdb728@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <baddsl$m0a$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ecabb10.124867872@news.optonline.net> <bag4ho$2fa$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3eccb560.254503258@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250650.1f65b40d@posting.google.com> <bb05nv$15ab$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <299f1138.0306010946.48c03e4c@posting.google.com> <bbg8b5$nek$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message news:<bbg8b5$nek$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>...
> "jallan" <jallan@smrtytrek.com> wrote in message
> news:299f1138.0306010946.48c03e4c@posting.google.com...
> 
> > The etymology of _island_ from _ï¿½g_ or _ï¿½eg_ + _land_ can be found at
> > http://www.bartleby.com/61/81/I0248100.html.

 
> The Merriam-Webster website at
> http://www.merriam-webster.com/cgi-bin/dictionary *seems to* indicate a
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> "insula";

Well there are a fair number of Middle English documents on the web. I
don't think you will find the spelling _island_ in any of them.

I've read a large amount of Middle English and I don't ever recall
seeing it there or in early modern English.

One *always* finds _iland_.

In the Norman French period you are talking about the _s_ in French
_isle_ (from Latin _insula_) would have been pronounced as written
(something like "eezleh" in English kludge phonetics) and a connection
between _iland_ and _isle_ would be unlikely to occur to anyone.

I'm not sure just when French dropped pronouncing of _s_ between a
vowel and following consonant, replacing it in writing first by a
superscript _s_, then later by a circumflex accent, hence modern
French _ï¿½le_ (and modern French _hï¿½tel_ for _hostel_, _pï¿½tï¿½_ cognate
with English _paste_).

The same idiotic ignorant pedantry led to Carlile being respelled as
Carlisle, though there was never an _s_ in the second element of
_castra lugwallum_.

Jim Allan
```

In article <299f1138.0306031052.6bdb728@posting.google.com>,
jallan <jallan@smrtytrek.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I'm not sure just when French dropped pronouncing of _s_ between a
>vowel and following consonant, replacing it in writing first by a
>superscript _s_, then later by a circumflex accent, hence modern
>French _�le_ (and modern French _h�tel_ for _hostel_, _p�t�_ cognate
>with English _paste_).

My junior-year French professor, Mr Bruce Venable - may he sleep with the
angels! - ('Three-Dollar Bruce', as we used to call him) taught us,
e'er-so-pithily, that 'The circumflex is the tombstone of an 's' that has
passed on.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 61)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T15:19:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0306011419.53805bb3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <bae29l$lpi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250621.3fea0e32@posting.google.com> <3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net>...
> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:
> 
> >Robert Wagner orginally posted at
> >http://groups.google.com/groups?selm=3eba027b.118744531%40news.optonline.net&oe=utf-8&output=gplain

> >
> >Many have disagreed.
> 
> Are you saying the greater than number of people believing something, the more
> likely it is to be true? That's he fallacy of argumentum ad populum. 

I said: "Many have disagreed".  I am not saying anything beyond that.

I was summarizing the thread.

But if we are talking about ways of perceiving, then what others
perceive is evidence, which it would not be if talking about matters
of fact, such as whether COBOL does or does not allow "<>" as an
operator.

I would take evidence from this small sample as proof that the
majority perception here applies universally. But it does put the
burden of proof more onto you.

> >I argued that most of the ideographic symbols used mathematical
> >notations and programming (and logic and chemistry and linguistics)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> show structure, or at least they show each radical separately. 

Wrong. 

The old notation continues to be used alongside newer notations. (And
the graphic diagrams do not spell out the full name in graphics
either.)

This is not surely not just laziness.

Here's one paper at http://www.courses.fas.harvard.edu/~apphy298/Papers/Bawendi/MurrayetalAnnuRevMatSci,_30.pdf,
just the first I found on the web.

Find a chemistry paper of reasonable length that always spells out
elements and compounds in full, that does not use abbreviations, both
in text and in diagrams.

> >Modern mathematics and logic largely grew from and fed back into
> >improvements in notation.
> 
> Following that logic, we should we use one character variable names, because
> that's what they use in math.

No.

Nowhere have I or anyone said that all notations must be identical.

Nor is their any logic dictating that only one notation system should
be used.

Not all mathemitcal and logical notations are identical. People keep
fiddling with them. New conventions take the place of old conventions,
either in particular diciplines or generally.

Nor are all computer languages identical, nor do they all use symbols
identically. The symbol "!" can mean either NOT or OR. The symbol "^"
may mean "to the power of" or "exclusive or", "=" can mean either
assignment or testing for equivalance or both.

It doesn't bother most people more than other differences between
langauges.

> >If I can express myself more clearly and make do with less characters
> >by using Arabic digits and arithmetical operators instead of spelling
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> they are good for other purposes. 

What hasty generalization?

Is this not what you are claiming? Did you not claim that you accepted
fully symbols for arithmetic operators?

What is incorrect about my generalization of your position at that
time and the difficulties you were having in defending it?

> >First, I pointed out that "multiplication" (if you must call the
> >normal use of numbers to indicate how many "multiplication") is
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> That forces you to use single-character variable names. 

Yes. You win one way and lose another. For most mathemtical use the
one symbol method is seen as a win, allowing more information to be
notated more concisely and clearly.
 
> That's a good reason to avoid <=.  <= means NOT >. NOT <= would be NOT NOT >.
> Double negatives are not permitted in COBOL, which is why NOT <=  is not
> permitted.

Yes, <= can be translated mentally as "not greater than" and so was
not necessary in COBOL, though it was introduced recently along with
its extended equivalent "GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO".

So avoid the new term if you wish.

But is harder to read the extended wording "NOT GREATER THAN" as "less
than or equal" without the words getting in one's way, somewhat like
trying watching a film with a soundtrack in a language you understand,
but also with subtitles, and trying to concentrate on both at the same
time.

> >> My objection is to the context in which symbols are used. In 'if invoice-date
> >> less than date-today', we see all words. In 'if invoice-date < date-today',
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> bug. You're about to charge sales tax on the refund rather than the sale.

Correct as to the error. But I think I made it because I was
concentrating on producing a statement which would make my point about
verbosity and was not concentrating on its meaning which is irrelevant
for the example.

I don't see that typing the words "MINUS" and "TIMES" if I were
allowed to do so in COBOL would have prevented me from making that
error.

Writing it in one-line statments would have done so.

> I wouldn't object to > if we had symbols for AND, OR and NOT. Then we would have
> a complete notational system for logic. Suppose Cobol provided a symbol for
> subtraction (comparison) while making you write words for addition (or) and
> multiplication (and). Would you support using such an incomplete notation?

You are in fact supporting an incomplete notation, if you insist that
all operators or no operators be indicated by symbols. The arithmetic
operators are indicated by symbols.

> I don't find the symbol > any more nor less clear than 'greater'. They mean the
> same thing. 

I find the symbols far clearer when attempting to parse the structure
of a statement, just as I do for the symbols for arithmetic operators.

 As Jeff York said:

> So what?  They're a damned-sight easier to spot! :-)

> They did it to avoid the 'trouble' of defining variables. 

Yes, and in Fortran defining variables is now necessary. And it is
preferred in Visual Basic (and probably other Basics) where one may
turn off such checking only to avoid having to change old programs
which work as they stand.
 
> Modern Fortran sounds like an oxymoron. 
 
Sigh! So what does modern COBOL sound like? Gratuitous and not to any
point.

> >Does the new COMPUTE command in COBOL only save typing? Or does it
> >also make things clearer when several calculations can be put on one
> >line instead of each one on a separate line?
> 
> I agree. COMPUTE is not new.

My meaning was that the COMPUTE instruction was not part of original
COBOL, and was not *needed* in COBOL. Everything it does can be done
in other more verbose ways.
 
> >Verbosity is not always clarity, may often be the opposite of clarity.
> >Look at legal jargon. Look at COBOL, blamed for its needless
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> make one language look better. 

No.

You can make the line count the same, if you start with a COBOL
program and translate roughly line for line and don't use features
allowed in c which are not found in COBOL.

Going the other way is more difficult. Here are some *normal* c
statements:

     if (a == ( b = c++)) {

     total *= ( a >= b ? c : d ) / 5

They would have to be expanded into multiple lines in COBOL.

Even when COBOL can be translated into c (or BASIC) line for line,
there will be more lines in the destination code that are shorter than
lines that are longer. "MOVE A TO B" is unncessarily verbose for "B =
A". The dBASE languages originally imitated this with "store A to B",
but at least by version III dBASE allowed "B = A" as an alternative.

Almost no-one now uses "store" other than in an expression like "store
0 to m.lnI, m.lnJ, m.lnK" where it can replace multiple assignment
statements.

> Nor does C any string handling. How unreasonable is that in a general purpose
> language? C doesn't have SEARCH, you're forced to roll your own. It punishes you
> for writing structures by requiring full-path qualification, even though the
> variable name is unique. How's that for discouraging good programming practice?
> 

Again, no. 

C has numerous string handling library functions. The standard
libraries were almost always implemented along c (though a few early
implementations did of non-standard libaries and functions). The
standard libraries are *essential* parts of ANSI c.

(C also has no input/output without these libraries, cannot even
display or print a character or write it to a file without them. So
what? It is part of c that the language itself is very small and
distinct from the functions which are something outside that core
language, and can be redefined at whim.)

For searching an array, which is mostly about the same as a COBOL
table, use the standard bsearch function. See
http://www.cise.ufl.edu/class/cop4600/docs/C_Ref_Card.pdf for standard
c functions.

I don't see how "extra typing" to indicate that something is part of a
structure is necessarily bad. It is no more redundant than the
standard practise of many COBOL programmers to use IN-, WS- and OUT-
as prefixes for COBOL variables.

You can always set a pointer or preprocessor alias to a particular
element in a struct or union if you need to use it a lot.

> >COBOL actually has a reasonably complete set of arithmetic operators
> >represented by symbols. It has a complete set of equality/inequality
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

> After 40 years, we're accustomed to AND, OR and NOT. <shrug>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >symbols clearer. The more logical approach is to take what I can get.
 
> >If COBOL did introduce such symbols as & | ~, would the
> >equal/inequality operators suddenly become acceptable to you, or would
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Most irrational.

Because you can't use symbols for all operators, you will use them for
as few as you can.

> I didn't argue for a context-free language. On the contrary, I've argued against
> it here. Every C programmer has made this mistake at least once 'if (a = b) ...'
> The compiler happily does the assignment, then tests for truth. Or 'if (a-string
> == b-string) ...', the compiler compares two pointers. What poor design.
> 

Not poor design.

It is more powerful behvavior that one has to get used to in c,
perhaps by mentally saying "is set to" for "=" and "evaluates to" for
"==".

Of course, for Fortan and Pascal and some other languages it is ":="
for assignment equals and "=" for relational equals.

In English works the word "equals" does just as well for assignment
and testing for equality, and so spelling it out for one of the uses
is not much help. The beginning programmer still has to get used to a
new distinction in meaning.

> >I think it probably took me about ten minutes when first encountering
> >"<" and ">" along with "<=" and ">=" in BASIC to become totally
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll grant they are widely known, and withdraw my argument on that ground.

> >A = B OR NOT >= C OR D
> 
> Syntax error. You can't put NOT in front of >=. 

Not a syntax error.

The statement is expanded by the compiler somewhat as A = B OR NOT (A
>= C) OR A >= D.

See http://www.helsinki.fi/atk/unix/dec_manuals/cobv27ua/cobrm_020.htm#expanded_equivs
and http://docs.hp.com/cgi-bin/doc3k/BB243390047.15064/45.

> >All the symbol notations get rid of those stupid extra OR's that
> >appear in the full word notation, the OR's that one might say when
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

A GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO B AND A EQUAL TO C

That seems somewhat confusing.

A >= B and A = C

There is good reason to get rid of "GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO", not to
get rid of ">=" which you can always read as "EXCEEDS" or "AT LEAST"
if you must verbalize it, or as "greater-equals".

Your belief that "NOT <" is innately superior to ">=" is idiosyncratic
and logically unsupportable as indicated elsewhere in this thread.

> What's wrong with 'an offer not less than five thousand dollars'? On the trading
> floors they say 'five thousand dollars or better', where or better is often
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> select max(price) in (select price from open-buy where price >= 24)

Nothing is wrong with any of those. Why would I think there was?

> >If COBOL were really English-like, we might have had something like:
> >
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> You got 'em backwards. The negative of LESS THAN is AT LEAST. 

Yes! I should stick to symbols. :-)

> >As far as I know, no other language has imitated COBOL in introducing
> >verbose phrases for equality/inequality operators. Why not?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I would not say that.

I think they were adopted because the symbols were available and those
who introduced them felt they made things clearer. (In original COBOL
the symbol were barely available, not at all available in most
business environemnts, but were introduced anyway.)

That this feeling was correct is supported when those who made later
languages did not drop the symbols introduced by earlier languages and
certainly did not introduce the same or different long-winded word
substitutes that COBOL provided.

Your "imagine themselves to be mathemmaticians" is an unfounded ad
hominem accusation.

Some programmers are mathematicians.

Some are not.

What has this to do with preferring symbols for operators?

Give some indication that those who included symbols in programming
languages (including "<", "=", ">") in COBOL, did so not because they
found statements containing symbols easier for themselves to parse,
but because "they imagined themselves to be mathematicians".

I am not a mathematician, though I know some math. I find such symbols
easier to parse, and not just in programming.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 62)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T11:06:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3edb253a.308040929@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <bae29l$lpi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250621.3fea0e32@posting.google.com> <3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0306011419.53805bb3@posting.google.com>`

```
jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message
news:<3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net>...
>> jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan) wrote:

>> >Many have disagreed.
>> 
>> Are you saying the greater than number of people believing something, the
more
>> likely it is to be true? That's he fallacy of argumentum ad populum. 
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>burden of proof more onto you.

I present facts from my experience and logic tying them together. I wish others
would do the same rather than telling us how they feel. Feelings are not
relevent to the issue and venting them here is a self-indulgence. 

>> >I argued that most of the ideographic symbols used mathematical
>> >notations and programming (and logic and chemistry and linguistics)
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>> I think those ideographs are a carry-forward from the days when statements
were
>> handwritten. 
>> 
>> Chemists no longer use the old count-the-atoms notation such as C6 H12 O6.
>> Glucose, fructose and galactose all have that same formula. They use graphics
to
>> show structure, or at least they show each radical separately. 
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>either.)

I didn't say they spelled out names of elements. What gave you that idea?

>This is not surely not just laziness.
>
>Here's one paper at
http://www.courses.fas.harvard.edu/~apphy298/Papers/Bawendi/MurrayetalAnnuRevMatSci,_30.pdf,
>just the first I found on the web.

It is more about engineering than chemistry. Yes, it uses old notation. 

>Not all mathemitcal and logical notations are identical. People keep
>fiddling with them. New conventions take the place of old conventions,
>either in particular diciplines or generally.

People keep fiddling with programming languages too. Not just their grammar but
also their structural model. The goal is making programmers more efficient or
obsolete. 

>> That's a good reason to avoid <=.  <= means NOT >. NOT <= would be NOT NOT >.
>> Double negatives are not permitted in COBOL, which is why NOT <=  is not
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>its extended equivalent "GREATER THAN OR EQUAL TO".

NOT GREATER had been there for decades; compilers understood it. When <= is
introduced, how would a compiler writer handle it?

a.  Write all new compiler code.
b.  Tokenize <= the same as 'not >', a one-line change.

FWIW, Micro Focus permits 'not <=', but does not accept not not. 

>> I wouldn't object to > if we had symbols for AND, OR and NOT. Then we would
have
>> a complete notational system for logic. Suppose Cobol provided a symbol for
>> subtraction (comparison) while making you write words for addition (or) and
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>operators are indicated by symbols.

All or none within each 'branch of computer math'. Arithmetic has a complete
symbology; logic is 50-50.

>> I don't find the symbol > any more nor less clear than 'greater'. They mean
the
>> same thing. 
>
>I find the symbols far clearer when attempting to parse the structure
>of a statement, just as I do for the symbols for arithmetic operators.

You convinced  me, which is why I requested symbols for and/or/not.

>My meaning was that the COMPUTE instruction was not part of original
>COBOL, and was not *needed* in COBOL. Everything it does can be done
>in other more verbose ways.

Exponentiation had no wordy counterpart. COMPUTE appeared early in the history
of COBOL, certainly by 68, probably before.

>> I dispute the assertion that COBOL is verbose. Write the same program in
COBOL
>> and C. You'll find the line count about the same, unless you contrive a case
to
>> make one language look better. 
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>They would have to be expanded into multiple lines in COBOL.

Those are contrived. In the large volume of commercial C I've maintained, I've
never seen a ternary operator, seldom an assignment nested in an if statement. 

>Even when COBOL can be translated into c (or BASIC) line for line,
>there will be more lines in the destination code that are shorter than
>lines that are longer. "MOVE A TO B" is unncessarily verbose for "B =
>A".

More typically "strcpy(b, a);" Punctuation are lexemes requiring almost as much
mental effort as words. The score: COBOL-4, C-7.

If C's string handling were adequate, there wouldn't have been dozens of
scripting languages invented to overcome the deficiency. 

>> Nor does C have any string handling. How unreasonable is that in a general
purpose
>> language? C doesn't have SEARCH, you're forced to roll your own. It punishes
you
>> for writing structures by requiring full-path qualification, even though the
>> variable name is unique. How's that for discouraging good programming
practice?

>(C also has no input/output without these libraries, cannot even
>display or print a character or write it to a file without them. So
>what? It is part of c that the language itself is very small and
>distinct from the functions which are something outside that core
>language, and can be redefined at whim.)

I concur there. File systems don't belong in the language except at a high level
of abstraction -- START, READ NEXT, etc. COBOL has evolved to treating the file
system as external. 

>I don't see how "extra typing" to indicate that something is part of a
>structure is necessarily bad. It is no more redundant than the
>standard practise of many COBOL programmers to use IN-, WS- and OUT-
>as prefixes for COBOL variables.

First you say COBOL is too verbose, now you don't mind "extra typing". You can't
have it both ways. 

In practice, C programmers have been conditioned to avoid structures more than
one layer deep, whereas COBOL programmers routinely define them multiple layers
deep. I think that's a serious structural issue.

>You can always set a pointer or preprocessor alias to a particular
>element in a struct or union if you need to use it a lot.

Workarounds. 

>> >If COBOL did introduce such symbols as & | ~, would the
>> >equal/inequality operators suddenly become acceptable to you, or would
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>as few as you can.

I gave my reason -- regularity -- and attempted to remedy the problem. How is
that irrational?

>> >A = B OR NOT >= C OR D
>> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>= C) OR A >= D.

Maybe. The meaning was vague in the 85 standard. As a result, compilers
interpreted it in various ways. The 2002 standard says it is not valid.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3EDB3A4F.9020404@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <bae29l$lpi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250621.3fea0e32@posting.google.com> <3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0306011419.53805bb3@posting.google.com> <3edb253a.308040929@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
>>My meaning was that the COMPUTE instruction was not part of original
>>COBOL, and was not *needed* in COBOL. Everything it does can be done
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of COBOL, certainly by 68, probably before.

I thought this too, but there -is- a "more verbose" way of doing it... 
For example, if you had something today that says "Compute X = Y ** Z", 
you could, in COBOL 68 syntax...

001-EXPONENTIATION.
     MOVE Y TO X.
     SUBTRACT 1 FROM Z GIVING A.
     PERFORM 002-COMPUTE-IT A TIMES.

002-COMPUTE-IT.
     MULTIPLY X BY Y.

-Much- more verbose....  :)
```

> I've never seen a ternary operator,

so)", or "fewer than a dozen", where you will 'correct' your assertion

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 63)_

- **From:** jallan@smrtytrek.com (jallan)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T18:16:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<299f1138.0306081716.10d129e2@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<45147cf3.0303250704.6a7dd28@posting.google.com> <3ec667d1.72442892@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305191427.12bf6003@posting.google.com> <3ec9d0dc.64902913@news.optonline.net> <bae29l$lpi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ecb0e2a.146144909@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0305250621.3fea0e32@posting.google.com> <3ed15160.238675055@news.optonline.net> <299f1138.0306011419.53805bb3@posting.google.com> <3edb253a.308040929@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3edb253a.308040929@news.optonline.net>...
> 
> It is more about engineering than chemistry. Yes, it uses old notation. 
> 

And so do many pure chemistry papers.

> >Yes, <= can be translated mentally as "not greater than" and so was
> >not necessary in COBOL, though it was introduced recently along with
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

This is irrelevant. Compilers these days often optimize the code
written in any case. Unless you know the details of how a particular
compiler works you have no way of knowing and probably should not care
what underlying machine instructions appear for <= and >= or other
operators.

What is the more efficient way to code on one compiler and one
operating system may be less so on another compiler and/or another
operating system. At one time branching was generally more efficient
than hardware table lookup. Today it is often the reverse, depending
on applicaton.

> All or none within each 'branch of computer math'. Arithmetic has a complete
> symbology; logic is 50-50.

Far too purist!

No notation is perfect.

> 
> Exponentiation had no wordy counterpart. COMPUTE appeared early in the history
> of COBOL, certainly by 68, probably before.

I am aware of that COMPUTE was an early command. But it was obviously
introduced to provide more concise but *unneeded( power for
arithmetic.

As to exponentiation, LX-i has answered that. I already provided the
looping method in a previous post in any case.

But, admittedly, part of COBOLs reputation for verbosity comes from
its early forms which necessitated far more coding than is now needed.

The intended point was that changes are often made to computer
languages not because they are *needed*, but because they are believed
to be desireable.
In this case they provided a less long-winded and easier-to-read
method of performing computations.

A side-effect is that it is now easier to mess up the order than it
once was, if you forget about priority of operations or don't put in
necessary parentheses.

"With great power comes great responsibility." -- Stan Lee

> >
> >     if (a == ( b = c++)) {
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> never seen a ternary operator, seldom an assignment nested in an if statement. 

Well, then you've been maintainng baby c written by people who haven't
a clue how to make c sing.

These are normal enough for application code. I could have presented
far more complex real examples from working applications and operating
system code.

One of the nice things about programming in c (and similar languages)
is you can get so much logic onto a single line where you can see it
all at once.

The ternary structure appears in xBase languages also as the
immediation if function: iif( , ,). The same line could be coded in
xBase as:

    lnTotal = m.lnTotal * iif(m.lnA > m.lnB, m.lnC, m.lnD) / 5

Again, this is very normal code.

> >Even when COBOL can be translated into c (or BASIC) line for line,
> >there will be more lines in the destination code that are shorter than
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> mental effort as words. The score: COBOL-4, C-7.

Agreed on the puncutation, or close enough as no matter. I was
thinking of numeric variables in my example.

Actually your score here is COBOL-9, C-11, using "MOVE``TO`" and
"strcpy(,`);" reasonably putting a syntactically unnecessary space in
the c to aid readiblity.

But strcpy can  also return the string as copied, which is sometimes
wanted. That would be another line in COBOL.

But compare COBOL verb "PERFORM" with c command "for" for looping or
with no nocommand at all in c for for calling a procedure/function
other than its name and parentheses.

> If C's string handling were adequate, there wouldn't have been dozens of
> scripting languages invented to overcome the deficiency. 

What do you think these scripting languages are written in? COBOL?

Perl was originally intended to replace the Unix specialized "general
expression" string-handling utilities awk and sed which is why it and
its successors have so much extra string-handling functionality built
into them.

But c out of the box has as much string-handling ability as any other
general normal purpose language. Mostly they have comparable functions
with different names. COBOL is the language lacking what is normal in
c and other lanaguage.

Here's a site with a semi-general routine to bring COBOL up to the
norm of string handling:
http://www.csis.ul.ie/COBOL/examples/Strings/RefMod.htm.

Of course your strings have to be exactly the right length to use this
utility, or you need make extra versions for every different lengths
of strings you wish to use with it.

*Sigh*!

> >I don't see how "extra typing" to indicate that something is part of a
> >structure is necessarily bad. It is no more redundant than the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> have it both ways. 

Sure I can.

I don't mind doing extra typing when it helps *me*. I do mind doing it
to no purpose.

I like short, concise and clear.

But when there are good reasons to use conventions like IN-, WS-, and
OUT- in COBOL and for doing similar things in other languages, then I
don't mind.

Yet why PERFORM instead of DO, for example? Or why not just give the
procedure name by itself as in c, xBase, etc.
 
> In practice, C programmers have been conditioned to avoid structures more than
> one layer deep, whereas COBOL programmers routinely define them multiple layers
> deep. I think that's a serious structural issue.

If you are talking about REDEFINES, such a thing hardly exists in most
other languages, not just c. Of course it is not used.

String functions tend to be used instead to pull out parts of a string
or to concatenate two or more strings into a single string.

But STRING and UNSTRING in COBOL are notoriously inefficient for some
reason, though wonderful to work with for many purposes. Otherwise
COBOL is lacking in built in string manipulation facililities.

> >You can always set a pointer or preprocessor alias to a particular
> >element in a struct or union if you need to use it a lot.
> 
> Workarounds. 

Of course. Normally it isn't done.

Normally you've assigned a short pointer name to the structure. Code
is clearer when you say something like "in->fullname" instead of just
"fullname". If it's part of a structure, then why not indicate it is
part of a structure?

This is called self-documentation.

Self-documentation is a claim often made for COBOL, and a bogus one as
any COBOL programmer knows. :-(

But it doesn't hurt for a language to force some clarity on the
programmer.

Personally I liked flavors of BASIC where suffixes like $, #, %, &
indicated the type of variable and were *necessary*.

When sub-elements are part of the a great element, many COBOL
progammers indicate this when there is any chance of confusion. It
really is not much of an issue.

> >The statement is expanded by the compiler somewhat as A = B OR NOT (A
> >>= C) OR A >= D.
> 
> Maybe. The meaning was vague in the 85 standard. As a result, compilers
> interpreted it in various ways. The 2002 standard says it is not valid.

A reasonable change or clarification. The use of NOT in these
circumstances was confusing.


Jim Allan
```

## Thread: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant??? — 84 this month / 195 total

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T04:16:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32a50$04748040$4291f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934465.1054186328@dbforums.com>`

```
Well Howard I bought your CDs and I figured I might as well be bogged down
closer to the bleeding edge so tonight I was looking through the booklet
that came with the CDs.

From the Introduction(with some minor editing by me):

"NetCOBOL for .NET comes in three packages: Professional($1800),
Developer($3300), and Universal($4200).

The Professional package contains the .NET supporting components and only
supports the development of applications that will execute in the .NET
environment. (From the back of the book: functions that are currently _NOT_
supported such as SORT/MERGE or ASSIGN TO PRINTER)

The Developer package adds Fujitsu COBOL V6 Standard Edition product and
multi-threaded run-time, giving the ability to develop native Windows
applications as well as .NET-targeted applications and integrate the two
together.

The Universal package is a combination of the NetCOBOL for .NET
Professional product and the Fijitsu COBOL V6 Enterprise Edition, giving
you our top-of-the-line Windows application development environment along
with the leading-edge NetCOBOL for .NET  product."

-------<RANT ON>---------
Well I guess COBOL in now on a level playing field, except for price. Maybe
when I see all of the neat things that I can do with the Professional
version I may feel differently, but to pay $1800 and NOT be able to develop
any native Windows apps and NOT have SORT/MERGE and to NOT be able to print
to a printer.........well the only adjectives that come to my mind might
well get me sued for libel and anyway they don't seem nearly strong enough!
This kind of pricing really puts people off.

I still think if Apple had priced their computer competively with the PC it
would be a much different world computing wise. Uncompetitive pricing
bespeaks of hubris and arrogance, the old we can charge much more because
our product is Sooooooooooooo much better. Yuck! Yeah I know it is "highly
integrated" as though other products aren't. Yeah I know it is only those
pesky individuals that complain, companies actuall don't mind being ripped
off.
-------<RANT OFF>---------

None of the above is directed at you personally. I really could not decide
where in this thread to place my comment and decided to put it here because
you produced the CDs. Further down in this thread Chris Richardson mentions
J2EE/JAVA. For a programmer intending to work on the mainframe into the
future this "might" be a better choice of what to learn. It certainly seems
to be the way IBM is pushing with COBOL interoperabilty with JAVA and use
of the Java Native Interface (JNI).

hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com> wrote in article
<2934465.1054186328@dbforums.com>...
> 
> Interesting discussion folks.... I thought I would throw my 2 cents in.
…[76 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Well, Charles - as long as you are commenting on my posts, I suppose I
should point you to a couple of mine on Fujitsu's COBOL for .NET product.
See (for example)

http://www.google.com/groups?q=fujitsu+sort+ansi+group:comp.lang.cobol+autho
r:klein&hl=en&lr=&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8&selm=ab4537%248pe%241%40slb0.atl.mindspr
ing.net&rnum=5

http://www.google.com/groups?q=fujitsu+sort+ansi+group:comp.lang.cobol+autho

> > >    many (if any) computer science departments teaching COBOL. Even
many
> > >    business schools are dropping it from their curriculum. This lack
of
> > >    fresh COBOL blood coming into IT is far more detrimental to COBOL
> > >    than typically noted.
> > >
> > > 2. What kept COBOL popular previously was its ease of use,
portibility,
> > >    and ability to handle business problems. COBOL began to lose its
> > >    ability to handle business problems when it failed to embrace
newer
> > >    technologies such  as GUI and later, the Internet, in a timely
> > >    fashion. The COBOL vendors failed to deliver proper tools in a
timely
> > >    fashion (from day one) that allowed enterprises to develop best of
> > >    breed applications on these new platforms - END OF SUBJECT.
> > >
> > > 3. The fact that COBOL compilers were selling for $3000+ against $99
> > >    Visual Basic and C++ compilers also had something to do with this
(a
> > >    fact I seldom see mentioned...).
> > >
> > > 4. Object COBOL was a bust. It is interesting to consider the
argument
> > >    for Object COBOL over 10 years ago. C++ was a "natural" extension
of
> > >    OO to the "C" language and most "C" programmers became C++
> > >    programmers. So it seemed to be a no-brainer that most COBOL
> > >    programmers would become OO COBOL programmers and the COBOL market
> > >    would continue down a successful path of modernization. Problem
was -
> > >    there was no compeling reason to use OO COBOL. This fact was made
> > >    worse by the lack of OO libraries (such as GUI) with early
deliveries
> > >    of OO COBOL. And another fact seldom noted is the fact that OO
COBOL
> > >    was almost a completely different language - it seemed easier to
> > >    learn by non-COBOL programmers. Additionally, it was (and  still
is)
> > >    far easier to convert typical "C" programs to "C++" programs than
to
> > >    convert legacy procedural COBOL programs into OO COBOL (and
consider
> > >    the additional fact that COBOL programs are typically muchg larger
on
> > >    average than "C" programs so the effort to convert them to OO was
> > >    often greater as well.
> > >
> > > 5. In the PC environment, COBOL was never on a level playing field
with
> > >    the other PC languages. You often had to wait for a COBOL vendor
to
> > >    provide glue or wrapper code to give you a change at accessing
some
> > >    new technology such as COM that was available from day 1 with
other
> > >    major PC languages. You had to wait for the COBOL vendor to
provide
> > >    some unnatural highly abstracted GUI support, which was often
kludgy
> > >    at best and created applications that looked like no other in
their
> > >    native environments. You had to wait for the COBOL vendor to
create
> > >    database support in the form of an EXEC SQL precompiler and
runtime,
> > >    etc. Using COBOL simply became more and more of a chore as time
> > >    progressed.
> > >
> > > 6. The COBOL committees themselves contributed to these problems by
> > >    becoming mired down in  theoretical arguments over archane issues
in
> > >    OO and such and still refused to deal with pressing needs such as
> > >    GUI. Now that they have FINALLY put out an OO spec (I believe some
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > happened to COBOL. If you look at some of the guiding principles
behind

the

in

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T16:43:47+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed984d3_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel>`

```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel...
> Well lets get real. Even if your book and Howard Hinmans's CDs are the
> absolute best educational materials ever produced, no employer will hire
> anyone just because they have read a book (and please note that I am _NOT_
> claiming that anyone said or implied this). They want experience and to
get
> experience with NetCOBOL for .NET Professional requires an up front cost
of
> $1800. Maybe not too much for a company but definitely an obstacle for an
> individual just trying to increase his/her marketability. Basically it is
a
> bet on the future and no one knows that with anything approaching to
> certainty. Is the future going to be as you describe?, or is it in
> components as Pete Dashwood proposes?, or is it both?, or will it be
> something else?.  NetCOBOL for Windows Professional is $1500 so  the
> component approach also has significant costs.  Gee maybe I should buy
both
> and double up my bet!
>
> I personally have enjoyed this thread and I think it is great that we have
> these educational materials  available. The expense of the material is
> small compared to the time to master the material and the cost of the
tools
> required to gain marketable experience. Even if you make this bet there is
> no guarantee that you will be employable in the future. All you you have
to
> do is look at all the people trained in the lastest and greatest who are
> unemployed due to the market down turn. Each individual must make his/her
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I thought that was a very well-balanced post, Charles and you highlighted a
couple of entirely relevant points:

1. Nobody is buying theory. (That is not to say that that both Chris's book
and Howard's CDs are not of value. We all need to start somewhere, but we
should not believe that doing the course will in and of itself, guarantee a
job.)

2. The Marketplace IS in a state of flux at the moment with several
different approaches vying for supremacy. I believe components will win, but
it IS speculation. You are absolutely right there is a Market downturn at
the moment and it could be more profitable to look at the Market, rather
than what technical options are available. .NET is by no means an assured
"Winner of the Future"...yet.

In passing I would mention that it has been possible to easily build
components in COBOL since Fujitsu V 4.2. (Maybe you DON'T need to pay $1500
for admission to the playground; have a look on e-bay for versions 4.2 or 5
or, particularly, 6...).

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T17:58:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306011658.369d2e31@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel> <01c32862$253bfbe0$a093f243@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote 

> Also Microsoft seems to have a habit of continually changing their vision
> of the future and to my suspicious mind it seems more related to the influx
> of more profits than to actual real value.

In the first edition of Bill Gates 'The Way Ahead' there was no
mention of the Internet at all even though (or perhaps because) at the
time IBM's OS/2 came with internet connection and Unix sites used it.

The book was quickly updated and reprinted and MS rushed to the front
of the crowd waving a flag saying 'follow me'.

Of course MS's initial attempt was to subvert the internet and replace
it with MSN where only Windows machines would work.  This was MS's
normal business practice.  For various reasons (mainly Netscape) MS
failed to kill of the internet and replace it with their 'better'
vision and so they had to allow Windows users access to the real
internet, because that is what they were doing in spite of MS.

Subsequently MS has made attempts to control the internet through
other means, such as trading Windows advertising real estate on your
desktop (for ISP connections to be in issued Winows system) for those
ISPs to be _required_ to use non-standard HTML features so that IE is
required to view the pages. (see recent arguments with Opera and the
Swedish Chef edition for how this continues).

Later, MS introduce Passport as a means of MS becoming everyones
doorkeeper. (see Gartner group report on avoiding passport).

.NET is another case where MS is rushing to the front of the crowd and
trying to seem as if they started out as leaders.  Part of .NET is be
MS's version of Java and the enterprise tools built around it.

Another part of .NET will be DRM.  A 'vision' where all music, movies,
and other media are distributed through channels that are secure and
_paid_for_.  Where users don't buy a CD, they licence the ability to
listen or view on a per use or per player basis.

No more taking your CDs from your lounge to play in your car or at
your friend's house, unless you have bought a licence to do that of
course.

On the one hand it would cater for making movies and music available
'on demand' as a secure channel would (try to) prevent piracy and
would allow payment without Music shops and Video rentals or Cinemas. 
This means that all can be complete couch potatoes.  On the other hand
the existing distribution channels would go broke, or be restricted to
niche markets when MS replaces them with its own channels.

Will it work ?

Currently many see .NET as a stick to beat Java with.  Of course some
see Java as Sun's stick to beat Windows with.

Others may see DRM as a stick to beat _everyone_ with.  There are
attempts to make DRM compulsory in the US, this may make the rest of
the world go elsewhere, as they seem to be doing increasingly now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T17:55:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0306011655.4a4ff6a3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message news:<01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel>...
> Yes  but is the future .NET for COBOL or .NET for/with some other language.
> VB and C# spring to my mind.
> 

Good question. While no one knows the future, here are my thoughts:

I believe that one of the reasons Microsoft designed the .NET platform
openly to support multiple languages (their own languages, plus those
of other vendors) is that they are staying true to the "we will build
a sand box, you can bring your own toys" approach. As long as you play
(and pay) in the sandbox, I don't think they really care what toys you
bring. They just don't want you to go play in the J2EE/Java sandbox.

Yes, on the mainframe side of the world, you have people wondering if
COBOL will survive on the other side of the fence. Note, that you also
have people wondering which language VB.NET or C# (or J#) will
survive. I believe that Microsoft leveraged their own bet - to them,
it really does not matter. Either way, VS.NET and the .NET Framework,
Windows, etc. survives.

This is where we (the developers) come in. Depending on use and
adoption (and vendor support!) all, either, or neither of the
languages could remain as part of the .NET picture.

Take Javascript, VBScript, and JScript as examples. Every web
developer that I know uses Javascript (a non-Microsoft product). Does
Microsoft care? My guess...not really. Sure, Microsoft offers their
own Client Scripting languages (VBScript and JScript ) that are
Internet Explorer specific. Still, Microsoft "loyal" developers still
migrate over to Javascript - all the while building Web applications
using Microsoft's ASP.NET toolset.

Developers made the choice...not Microsoft! All the deep pocketing
Microsoft Hype did not make the difference (in this case). VBScript
and JScript are still alive...but Javascript is King.

<off-the-wall analogy section>

To me, it's like going into the store to buy a bag of chips. I do not
think that the store merchant really cares what kind of chips I buy -
or whether I even buy any chips at all - as long as I buy "something".
let's say that I do buy a bag a chips...Likewise, I don't think that
"Lays" would really care whether I buy the "fried" or "baked"
type...as long as I buy something that carries the "Lays" seal.

As a consumer, If their is nothing in the store that I want, I will
not buy anything. It's up to the powers that be to make it worthwhile
to go into the store and purchase something. Sure, a few foolish
purchases will be made...but over the long haul, quality and customer
satisfaction will win.

</off-the-wall analogy section>

So, the .NET technology suite is just one huge sandbox. Time will only
tell which toys people will ask to bring in, attempt to bring in, then
insist on bringing in to play with. So, which language(s) will win the
hearts of the developers? I really hope that COBOL remains on the
list. It really is a good bridge between the two worlds. Hopefully,
the vendors (i.e. Fujitsu) will stay committed for the ride.

At the same time, I suppose you could say that I leveraged my bets as
well (by including both COBOL and VB.NET in my book (and as part of my
own career development). I realize that some mainframe developers want
nothing more than to code exclusively in COBOL - no problem. I wanted
to provide a mainframe-friendly book (to learn .NET) for that crowd.
At the same time, there are those mainframe developers that do not
want to code in the COBOL language any more (shame, shame, shame) - in
favor of some other language - maybe VB.NET. Well, my
mainframe-friendly book will equally speak to that crowd as well -
while they learn .NET.

(Note, I did include a C# and J# section in my book as well.)

Me, I'm part of a third crowd. As I've mentioned a few times, I
advocate being multi-lingual - flexible - still, while learning .NET.
For me, this seems like a safe position. I enjoy developing in multple
languages - it's fun. It's not trying to straddle both sides of the
fence...I see it as standing securely smack-dab in the middle of the
.NET fence.

Which language will survive as part of the .NET picture? Not sure. I'm
not convinced that Microsoft really cares. So, I'm leaning in the
direction of not making that my biggest priority. As you can see, I'm
more concerned about which "developer group/community" - while made up
of multiple "crowds" - will survive.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T04:08:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c328bc$8946ff40$c891f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel> <b11feff.0306011655.4a4ff6a3@posting.google.com>`

```
Well I agree that Microsoft may not care, but a programmer looking to
develop marketable skills does care, as it determines what he/she needs to
learn.

I entered into using PCs kind of late. At one point it looked like knowing
Unix and C++ would be a plus at my workplace. I already knew some UNIX from
getting my masters degree. So I signed up for a C++ course and bought
Borland's C++ compiler. I think it was version 5. Next I took a Windows
programming course and bought Visual C++ from Microsoft. Unfortunately the
course was very poor. At this point  my agency decided they did not have
the money to build and support the multi-tiered architecture they had
envisioned. Mutiple hundreds of millions down the drain with very little to
show for it, but that is another story. Meanwhile I could not help but
notice that the C++ compilers I had used became obsolete environments
mighty fast, replaced by newer versions or different application building
products/frameworks. 

Undaunted I bought Mike Murach's book on VB 5.0 and purchased MS VB 5.0. I
learned quite a lot even though my version of MS Access was already
obsolete and not the version used in that book. My timing was poor as
within two to three months VB 6.0 was on the market. To this point I had
always purchased the professional editions thinking I could learn more
features.

What is the point of all this long, tragic, and very sad story? Well I
realized that I simply could not afford to keep up with the latest and the
greatest. Instead of keeping myself employable I was just helping to make
Bill Gates and Microsoft rich. The lastest and greatest often were not so
much greater than what came before.

So I got smart and read Mike Murach's book on JAVA and the price was right.
But to me it seemed like just another way to do GUI and afterwards it
seemed that most JAVA coding was being done on the server side (so maybe I
was _NOT_ so smart after all).

Along the way I learned some HTML which was easy for me because I had a lot
of experience with typesetting languages. One book had a C program which
listened to port 80 and showed what the browser was passing and this
evolved into how to do some basic CGI programming. I read Wilson Price's
books on Elements of COBOL Web Programming and Elements of OO COBOL
Programming. Now as I mentioned previously I am learning more about
components.

So what is my point? After all of this I doubt that I am any more
employable than if I had not done any of these activities. Part of this may
be due to poor timing and poor choices on my part, but I will cut myself
some slack on that, as it simply shows poor aptitude in predicting the
future. The main shortcoming is that I never used any of this stuff at work
so I don't have real experience to offer. In the past I taught myself IBM
mainframe assembler and CICS and parlayed limited on the job experience
into real, extensive, and substantial experience. In todays environment
management wants and can get years of experience in exactly the computer
environments and languages that they need. The problem for someone trying
to maintain marketablity is that there are so many platforms, languages,
and products that it is an almost impossible job to decide what is best to
learn.

So if I learn .NET and COBOL and VB.NET I doubt that someone who wants C#
or whatever will even consider someone like me. I certainly am extremely
leery of betting multiple thousands of dollars on the prospect when I have
already bet multiple hundreds of dollars in the past to little effect. I
simply do not believe that the choice is as clear cut and as certain as you
make it out to be. Of course _NO_ choice is also a choice as you pointed
out, and learning something is better than nothing. Certainly I have
receieved the enjoyment of learning and all my effort was not in vain, but
it is a very difficult marketplace out there right now. Hell even if I were
to pick exactly right I might have to move to India to get a job for a
small fraction of what I make now. In my opinion the future is way less
certain than you make it out to be.

Chris Richardson <richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com> wrote in
article <b11feff.0306011655.4a4ff6a3@posting.google.com>...
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:<01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel>...
> > Yes  but is the future .NET for COBOL or .NET for/with some other
language.
> > VB and C# spring to my mind.
> > 
…[82 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T09:31:41-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0306020831.656577c9@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel> <b11feff.0306011655.4a4ff6a3@posting.google.com> <01c328bc$8946ff40$c891f243@chottel>`

```
I do recall a few years ago, just as I was gearing up for the .NET
dive in acknowledging to a friend that I was going to dive in ï¿½ in
spite of the risk. At that time, I used Microsoft's statement about
how they where "betting the farm" as my justification for "betting my
own farm". Yes, my farm (a few hundred dollars) pales in comparison to
theirs (more money than I care to imagine). But, it's relative <g>

I've resolved to jump aboard the technology train as it speeds ahead.
I realize that it's going to change colors from time to time and will
visit different towns ï¿½ one after another. The only thing that is near
certainï¿½the train will not stop. That's one of the exiting things
about technology and being so intimately tied to it's changes.

As your own experiences prove, this is simply part of the package. I
would cut you tons of slack. I don't think it's your choices or
timingï¿½it's just the nature of technology. Take HTML for example, you
mentioned that you had learned a lot of it. Well, now, there's a
strong movement now to transition to XHTML (well formed HTML, like
XML). Does that mean that it was wrong to learn HTML? No, of course
not.

I've shared a few of these experiences as well (taking a VB5 class,
only to find out that it was obsolete before the class ended <g>).

At any rate, your points are well taken. I suppose my optimism for the
future mistaking comes across as if I'm certain about the future. I
think the only real safe prediction is that things will look very
different than they look right now.

These things just evolve. And that's just the way that it is (in my
opinionï¿½of course).

- regards.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDB926A.81B9B09E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel> <b11feff.0306011655.4a4ff6a3@posting.google.com> <01c328bc$8946ff40$c891f243@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> Well I agree that Microsoft may not care, but a programmer looking to
> develop marketable skills does care, as it determines what he/she needs to
> learn.
>

A good one Charles, and Chris came back at you on a positive note.

Before typing this I checked the Saturday Careers Section of my local paper.
Not ONE advertisement for a programming job for *ANY* language. Just two ads on
the back page for learning from SAIT(Southern Alberta Institute of Technology)
and OOST(OO Software Technology) offered by the Universities of Calgary and
Regina. (Like the oil/gas patch in Texas, Alberta, and specifically what has
been dubbed 'the Calgary/Edmonton Corridor', is extremely high tech. About a
year back there was competition between Vancouver and Calgary for the title
'Silicon Valley North' - Calgary wins hands down). No we aren't in the doldrums
up here; at the moment the indicators are our economy is doing better than
yours, with an increase in the Canadian dollar value.

The computing market , (hardware and software) is just drying up. What puzzles
me though is that both Smalltalk and Java NGs have lots of active input - so
what the hell are they programming ? I previously mentioned my neighbour
working from home programming for his corporation in Australia in C++ - but he
is into a very SPECIFIC niche market.

At the high end - SAP, Siebel etc., have attacked the traditional number
crunching market. How about sitting down and writing an accounting application
for a PC. Forget it - why even attempt to compete against products like
Intuit's QuickBooks. A savvy user can apply their package at a fraction of the
price of a product you can produce - and that's just putting the meagre crumbs
on your plate.

Somewhere in between the two, SAP which is just too damned expensive for them,
and Quickbooks which although excellent just doesn't contain the additional
whistles and bells needed, there is a middle market - but that is a difficult
one to find.

Am I glad I'm not just starting my career in this racket ! To succeed you have
got to find that speciality niche, regardless of which language/tools you are
using. (I couldn't help but smile - I don't really think writing a nifty
routine in .NET to compress/zip your files really counts - unless of course you
are hoping to knock Winzip off the shelf <G>).

Jimmy
```

- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel> <b11feff.0306011655.4a4ff6a3@posting.google.com> <01c328bc$8946ff40$c891f243@chottel> <3EDB926A.81B9B09E@shaw.ca> <b11feff.0306021559.740c5529@posting.google.com>`

Chris Richardson wrote:

> > Am I glad I'm not just starting my career in this racket ! To succeed you have
> > got to find that speciality niche, regardless of which language/tools you are
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> pages via a web services connection <g>

You know perfectly well the last idea wont work, because it will only include US
yellow pages, (something to do with LOCALE ???). Even if they produced a coloured
map, with the exception of Alaska, the bits above the 49th would be coloured WHITE
<G>

You think I'm kidding. I kid you not. Back when I was using MS COBOL their *very own*
version, somewhere around '85-'90, I could write their COBOL support a note, fold the
paper into a little aeroplane, go out in the garden and chuck it in a general south
westerly direction, at the university there in Bellingham, Wa. This is of course
before the Web.

In due course, they would reply, the envelope being postmarked "Bruxelles, Belgique".
Yep they let that little kid in the fountain pee all over it before forwarding it on
to Canada. There's some folks up here speak French so I suppose they thought we were
a part of the EU.<G>

Jimmy
```
