- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0306022156.71cf448b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel> <b11feff.0306011655.4a4ff6a3@posting.google.com> <01c328bc$8946ff40$c891f243@chottel> <3EDB926A.81B9B09E@shaw.ca> <b11feff.0306021559.740c5529@posting.google.com> <3EDBF0BB.4549EC2D@shaw.ca>`

```
"James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3EDBF0BB.4549EC2D@shaw.ca>...
> 
> You think I'm kidding. I kid you not. Back when I was using MS COBOL their *very own*
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> a part of the EU.<G>

I read the above response...and just as I was thinking that there were
several LOCALE type references ... ones that I have not the pleasure
of really understanding and appreciating...I noticed one word that I
do understand dearly.

"chuck"

This is the first time in a while - since I left my home state of
Texas and headed to sunny California that I've read/heard someone use
the word "chuck" while not referring to steak or the Rugrats cartoon.
In Texas, we used that term to mean "throw". It looks like you are
using it in the same way. How refreshing! (and I'm not being sarcastic
or disrespectful).

By the way...The notion of LOCALE...reminds me of an article read
recently that discussed the concerns of using a date reference and the
various other LOCALE concerns. For example (as I recall), a reference
of 9/11 to some may mean Sept 11th and to others it is Nov 9th or
better put as 11-9 if it is to really be meaningful...or something
like that.

Oh well...chuck that one to the fire <g>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT : American and English usage

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T17:21:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDCD59B.342EF681@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32861$74adecc0$a093f243@chottel> <b11feff.0306011655.4a4ff6a3@posting.google.com> <01c328bc$8946ff40$c891f243@chottel> <3EDB926A.81B9B09E@shaw.ca> <b11feff.0306021559.740c5529@posting.google.com> <3EDBF0BB.4549EC2D@shaw.ca> <b11feff.0306022156.71cf448b@posting.google.com>`

```


Chris Richardson wrote:

> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in message news:<3EDBF0BB.4549EC2D@shaw.ca>...
> >
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Oh well...chuck that one to the fire <g>

I think the use of words and there origins is fascinating. I envy both Chuck and BIll  who
said they did linguistics at university.

"Chuck" I use exactly as you did in Texas. But that's 'English' usage; I don't know if it
is a Canadianism. One of the Aussies or Kiwis would have to confirm, but I wouldn't mind
betting they say, "Chuck another steak on the barbie".

Your reference to dates - "9/11" is an Americanism which is scorched into the mind just
like December 7, 1941. Had the WTC disaster occurred in UK (God forbid !), I'm not sure how
it would have come out - "11/9" perhaps but not necessarily so. I think it would be 'so
un-English' to do it that way.

For an English mind parsing N.American dates it is a bit of a problem :-

04/05/2003 or 05/04/2003 - in isolation I don't know whether you are talking about May or
April. Given 04/13/2003 - then I've got it figured. It's even more difficult in Canada
because we use both dd/mm and mm/dd. For at least the last thirty years, whether in
programming or private usage I go with the ISO format : ccyy/mm/dd.

The English 'style' used to be, when I was shorthand/typing, to specify days with a suffix
:-

1st, 2nd, 3rd, 4th through 20th, 21st..... and so on....

So :        31st May, 1999

The other one I get caught on is N. American usage of 'hundreds' instead of 'thousands' -
although on BBC World News the other night I heard "fifteen hundred".

The one which really blows my mind, when referring to stocks/shares, "The market dropped by
half of one percent". Jeepers ! Why can't you folks say, "The market dropped by Oh point
five percent".

Military is a good focus point - "Friendly fire". Not too much "friendly" about being fired
upon ! I picked up from Gulf War II that the British Army uses "Blue on blue". Harkens back
to when the good guys were 'blue' and the Soviets were 'red'.

Dump is an interesting one. I think its general usage in N. America is 'to defecate' or 'do
a dump'. Entirely different meaning in UK. "Take the rubbish to the dump (landfill)", or
"Dump the leaves from the wheelbarrow over there".

Chuck (that's "Charles" not "throw" <G>), discussed the origins of the word "island" with
somebody, and "isle" came into the text. I can immediately think of the Isle of Man, or
looking southwards from my hometown Portsmouth, the Isle of Wight some 5-8 miles south..

Even Bill, who is somewhat 'trans-Atlantic', having worked in UK, recently queried Pete and
myself on English usage <G>.

Jimmy
```

"William M. Klein" wrote:

> I had to read thru the other replies to this thread before posting to see if
> we had any Bette Davis fans. For "authentic" American usage, you may
> download and LISTEN TO:
>
>    http://www.meredy.com/bettedavis/bd-dump.wav

Could be a problem with Netscape. All I got was the "player" in the centre of
the screen and the one and only phrase "What a dump !".  Couldn't even recognize
it was Bette D. Do people get paid for this ?

Take a look Pete you could make zillions out of selling simple one-phrase sound
Components ! No Chris - I'm not about to suggest he should use .NET to do it
easier. Mind you he might - $1 times a zillion ain't no chikenfeed <G>

Bill - seeing as you are listening to those book tapes. Ever thought back to the
stars of the '30s/'40s in the States. Accents have changed dramatically since
then. They were from the THEATAH - much more dramatic and precise. That's why so
many of them were able to transition to English parts in movies - their accents
were credible. Bette for instance as QE1 in "Elizabeth and Essex", along with
Errol Flynn (an Aussie, I believe).

To-day by comparison, some don't even try. Kevin Costner as Robin Hood - you
gotta be joking.

But back to the late '30s one of my 'funny' favourites an English 'Technicolor'
- Sabu, Raymond Massey (Canadian) and others in "The Drum". The jolly old Empire
in India. As you can imagine Sabu didn't have too much problem sounding like an
Indian (East). Raymond Massey turned in a good performance as the villain, with
his menacing tones he could put on. By contrast all his supporting villains were
from the English Theatah. They were dressed as Indians, turbans, beards the
works and used shoe polish to tan up to the local colour. But their dialogue
pronunciation, oh my gawd :-

"I say sahib old boy. Kind of hot. What ? What ?".

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : American and English usage

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>

```

On  4-Jun-2003, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> Well for sure it wasn't "The Viking(s)" - Tony, Kirk Douglas and TC's
> future./ex-wife Janet Leigh. Although it's a fair bet TC uttered some immortal
> phrases even in that one. I wonder where the Bronx village is in Scandinavia ?

I still think it is funny when people accept some modern accents in a historical
drama but not others.   In Sparticus, the Roman elite all had upper class
British accents (an accent that is at most 200 years old), while the slaves all
had American accents.

People didn't like it that Kevin Costner spoke with an American accent in Robin
Hood.  But experts guess that the people who speak closest to the way Robin Hood
spoke are in America, not Britain.

Some people even complained about Sean Connery speaking with a Scottish accent
in /Hunt for Red October/ .   But that couldn't be more appropriate since he
wasn't playing a Russian - he was playing a Lithuanian(?) who spoke Russian - in
English, so having an acceptable British Empire non-English accent (particularly
of one associated with engineers) worked perfectly.

Of course they could have Jesus speak with a New York City Yiddish accent - to
indicate that he was a Jew.   No sillier than having him speak with an upper
class educated accent.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : American and English usage

_(thread depth: 26)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T11:59:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bbr2of$7o2$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <bbqtc6$4ur$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
> >There are medieval (or maybe Renaissance?  I don't know art) paintings
with such
> >things as Mary, Joseph, and Jesus heading to Egypt with a Star of David
sewn to
> >their clothing.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I believe you may both be right.

The Magen David did not become the universally recognised symbol of  Judaism
until the 19th century, but its use by Jews goes back much further than
that.

Although I am not Jewish myself, I have a number of friends who are, and
most of them (especially the females) have  at least one of these in their
possession and/or wear one most of the time. So whatever it may symbolise to
others, Jewish people are pretty passionate about it...

This is the best reference I could find on Google:
http://www.menorah.org/starofdavid.html

Pete.
```

Peter E.C. Dashwood <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bbr2of$7o2$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>I believe you may both be right.

Less taste *and* fills great?

>
>The Magen David did not become the universally recognised symbol of  Judaism
>until the 19th century, but its use by Jews goes back much further than
>that.

Hence the moniker of the 'Seal of Solomon' (which can be found, as I 
recall, in Burton's translation of '1,001 Arabian Nights' as the seal of a 
bottle which contains yet another djreadful djinn).

Speaking of 'Magen David'... one of the various dialects pronounces this 
as 'Moh-gehn Day-vihd', which was (and still is) the name of a sacramental 
wine... but when the alcohol content was changed from 12% to 20% and the 
name became less religiously associated it sold briskly as 'MD (or 
'MadDog') 20/20'.

>
>Although I am not Jewish myself, I have a number of friends who are, and
>most of them (especially the females) have  at least one of these in their
>possession and/or wear one most of the time. So whatever it may symbolise to
>others, Jewish people are pretty passionate about it...

Gah... in your experience, related above, *some* are 'pretty passionate' 
about it; I'd imagine (based on the Ancient Wisdom of 'three Jews = five 
opinions = seven political parties') that some are not.  Perhaps your 
acquaintances merely live in fear of vampires?

>
>This is the best reference I could find on Google:
>http://www.menorah.org/starofdavid.html

From the abovementioned site:

--begin quoted text:

The evolution of the six-pointed Jewish star, the "Magen David," literally
the "Shield of David." also known as the hexagram, or more rarely,
*Solomon's Seal, is long and complex. Although it is now the most common
and universally recognized sign of Judaism and Jewish identity, both
within and outside of the Jewish community, it has only achieved this
status in the last two hundred years. Before that it was chiefly
associated with magic or with the insignia of individual families or
communities. 

--end quoted text

... which is kinda close to my earlier recollections about 'the Seal of 
Solomon did not become a popular symbol of Materia Hebraica until the 
mid-19th century' and the bottle-seal of Great Power... and a bit farther 
down, same page

--begin quoted text:

In the Middle Ages, hexagrams appear frequently on churches, but rarely in 
synagogues or on Jewish ritual objects. It was the *menorah that served as 
the primary Jewish symbol from antiquity until the post-Renaissance 
period, not the "Jewish star."

--end quoted text

... and that seems kinda close to what I said with '... and that prior to 
that the most-recognised symbol was the multi-branched candelabrum (as 
seen on Trajan's Arch)'.

My memory is, admittedly, porous.

DD
```

Howard Brazee wrote:

> On  3-Jun-2003, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> "dust man".   Dust in the US is stuff that collects on window sills.

I know a lady in Calgary who's not too keen on dusting, so she has dust bunnies
<G>. Dust in UK is also the stuff on window sills. Dust man - that's because the
garbage, including coal cinders, was put into metal dustbins. (Lonnie Donnegan,
now dead, "My old man's a dustman...."). We have plastic garbage bags up here,
which you probably have as well.

Jimmy
```

[snip]

>We have plastic garbage bags up here,
>which you probably have as well.

A friend's mother - may she sleep with the angels! -, a woman raised on
the Lower East Side of Manhattan more than a few decades back, used to say 
'I remember that city in the summer... they oughtta put up a memorial 
statue to the man who invented the plastic garbage bag.'

DD

On  3-Jun-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >We have plastic garbage bags up here,
> >which you probably have as well.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> statue to the man who invented the plastic garbage bag.'

I was reading a novel where with New York City and time travel  (Lady Liberty's
hand was in Central Park in the past).

I remember that the woman from the past wondered why we press our pants with the
crease in the front instead of in the side where it belongs, and why we close up
our trash cans so that the trash can't breathe.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : American and English usage

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T16:01:04-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbiuq0$6vc$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3EDBF0BB.4549EC2D@shaw.ca> <3EDCD59B.342EF681@shaw.ca>`

```
In article <3EDCD59B.342EF681@shaw.ca>, James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

[snip]

>"Chuck" I use exactly as you did in Texas. But that's 'English' usage; I don't know if it
>is a Canadianism. One of the Aussies or Kiwis would have to confirm, but I wouldn't mind
>betting they say, "Chuck another steak on the barbie".

It seems that a common colloquial use for 'chuck' is a variety of 
Englishes is 'to toss/throw casually'.

>
>Your reference to dates - "9/11" is an Americanism which is scorched into the mind just
>like December 7, 1941. Had the WTC disaster occurred in UK (God forbid !), I'm not sure how
>it would have come out - "11/9" perhaps but not necessarily so. I think it would be 'so
>un-English' to do it that way.

A few decades back I took the ferry from Calais to Dover and ran into a 
rather... snide Customs official who looked at my paperwork and said 
'What's this for a birthdate?... oh yes, you're American, some day you'll 
learn to write dates correctly.'

My response was 'Shortly after you learn to drive on the correct side of 
the road, perhaps.'

[snip]

>The English 'style' used to be, when I was shorthand/typing, to specify days with a suffix
>:-
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>So :        31st May, 1999

That's the style I use (but with cardinals instead of ordinals, eg. 31 May 
1999) after having been taught it by the Air Force... as long as one is 
familiar with the English names of the months it is utterly unambiguous... 
doesn't sort very well, though.

>
>The other one I get caught on is N. American usage of 'hundreds' instead of 'thousands' -
>although on BBC World News the other night I heard "fifteen hundred".

Isn't that the year between 1499 and 1501?

>
>The one which really blows my mind, when referring to stocks/shares, "The market dropped by
>half of one percent". Jeepers ! Why can't you folks say, "The market dropped by Oh point
>five percent".

News-speak is not always indicative of how 'you folks' speak; folks I hang 
around with say things like 'likely to rain' and not 'probability of 
precipitation'.

>
>Military is a good focus point - "Friendly fire". Not too much "friendly" about being fired
>upon ! I picked up from Gulf War II that the British Army uses "Blue on blue". Harkens back
>to when the good guys were 'blue' and the Soviets were 'red'.

'Terminate with extreme prejudice', anyone?

>
>Dump is an interesting one. I think its general usage in N. America is 'to defecate' or 'do
>a dump'. Entirely different meaning in UK. "Take the rubbish to the dump (landfill)", or
>"Dump the leaves from the wheelbarrow over there".

A variety of meanings to that one, as well; 'defecate' is one but there
are uses for more polite society as well.  'Rubbish tip' (as in 'the city
dump'), to drop unceremoniously ('I dumped off a load of manure at the
city dump'), to cease a particular form of relationship ('My significant
other dumped me'), a generally disorderly or unpleasant place ('What a
dump!' - Bette Davis in 'Beyond the Forest', 1949), etc.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : American and English usage

_(thread depth: 17)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T20:35:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbj0qb$oln$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3EDBF0BB.4549EC2D@shaw.ca> <3EDCD59B.342EF681@shaw.ca> <bbiuq0$6vc$1@panix5.panix.com>`

```

On  3-Jun-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

> >So :        31st May, 1999
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't sort very well, though.

That's where I learned this.   Any format that depends on knowing where the user
is from is IMHO "wrong".


> >The one which really blows my mind, when referring to stocks/shares, "The
> >market dropped by
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> precipitation'.

Either seem silly - why use "per cent" if parts per 100 aren't significant?  
When I was a child I was taught to calculate percentage by dividing the number
and multiplying by 100.   It took me a while to catch on why we multiplied by
100, as I honestly didn't see any difference between .05 and 5%, so I didn't see
that we needed a "multiply by 100" step to translate from one to the other.


> A variety of meanings to that one, as well; 'defecate' is one but there
> are uses for more polite society as well.  'Rubbish tip' (as in 'the city
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> dump!' - Bette Davis in 'Beyond the Forest', 1949), etc.

What's odd is that it seems to have become acceptable to say "poop" when we are
talking about what we have stepped in (or what my grandchildren do in their
diapers).   But "shit" is only acceptable when it is used to describe what
someone says or does.   (Am I bull-pooping you?)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : American and English usage

_(thread depth: 18)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T21:01:40-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbjgdk$c27$1@panix5.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3EDCD59B.342EF681@shaw.ca> <bbiuq0$6vc$1@panix5.panix.com> <bbj0qb$oln$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bbj0qb$oln$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote:
>
>On  3-Jun-2003, docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>the user is from is IMHO "wrong".

But... what about that Personal Touch?

>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Either seem silly - why use "per cent" if parts per 100 aren't significant?  

That might be why they say things like 'likely to rain', aye.

[snip]

>
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>someone says or does.   (Am I bull-pooping you?)

Language use changes in different places and times... but I still recall
the amused grin I could not suppress when I was taking the elevator back
from lunch at One Travellers' Plaza in Hartford, Connecticut... Old Yankee
Insurance Money built that place and it still reeked of it, even after all
those years... anyhow, a fellow in his mid-to-late 20's got on at one
floor and a woman he knew of similar age got on at another... as we moved
upwards they discussed the various aspects of their childrens'
developments and one suddenly remembered that a promise had been made
earlier... and intoned 'Oh, that's *right*, I *forgot*... have *I* got a
potty-book for *you*!'

And then... have I ever mentioned the time that I returned a niece to
Respected Sister-in-Law after babysitting for an afternoon and I was given
the gift of The Look when the youngster sang out 'Mommie, I gotta cop a
squat!'?

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : American and English usage

_(thread depth: 19)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T03:25:23+00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<3EDD602C.703A4E69@shaw.ca>`

docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>
> And then... have I ever mentioned the time that I returned a niece to
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, that's a new one on me. But it does have a certain directness about it. <G>

My very favourite TV bobby, Detective Chief Inspector Morse. (Sadly actor John Thaw
is with the angels now). Apart from solving murder mysteries the great passions of
life for Morse were choral/classical music and a good glass of beer, or two, or
three.....

He's with his sergeant sidekick in a local watering hole, supping the last of his
ale.
Sergeant says, "I have to go spend a penny". He dithers, not quite sure where to go.
Sarcastically Morse says, "I suppose the next thing you'll be telling me is you have
to do a number 2 !".

Jimmy
```

>
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Well, that's a new one on me. But it does have a certain directness about it. <G>

What I liked was that the situation was reduced to an immediate, almost 
visceral, description and yet contained no words which could be labelled 
as 'inappropriate'... granted that using 'cop' in the sense of 'take' (AHD 
2b) is often seen as slang but hey... let the Bachelor Uncle babysit and 
the little darling might learn such things.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT : American and English usage

_(thread depth: 23)_

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T11:18:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<21c1ev86ro22893apurg294fb0t0ubnogg@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bbkv01$qp9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bbl0um$7co$1@panix1.panix.com> <lakudvklt8bo3aubof441vr75445m2m4qr@4ax.com> <bbnvgq$ak6$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
On 5 Jun 2003 13:43:54 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:

>In article <lakudvklt8bo3aubof441vr75445m2m4qr@4ax.com>,
>SkippyPB  <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
>DD

Aaah, but I trained at Ft. Campbell and Ft. Polk!!  Actually,
according to "Speaking Freely: A Guided Tour of American English from
Plymouth Rock to Silicon Valley" by Stuart Berg Flexner and Anne H.
Soukhanov (Oxford University Press, New York, 1997). the term "grunt"
was originally used by U.S. Marines to differentiate infantrymen and
aviator.  However, the word was picked up by soldiers in Viet Nam and
adopted by all infantrymen.  I heard the term in basic training and in
my tour of Viet Nam.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T12:05:08+00:00

- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bbkv01$qp9$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bbl0um$7co$1@panix1.panix.com> <lakudvklt8bo3aubof441vr75445m2m4qr@4ax.com> <bbnvgq$ak6$1@panix1.panix.com> <21c1ev86ro22893apurg294fb0t0ubnogg@4ax.com>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.NOSPAM.rr.com> wrote:

>On 5 Jun 2003 13:43:54 -0400, docdwarf@panix.com enlightened us:

>Aaah, but I trained at Ft. Campbell and Ft. Polk!!  Actually,
>according to "Speaking Freely: A Guided Tour of American English from
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>my tour of Viet Nam.

I heard 'grunt' in early-60s USMC (prior to VietNam) to distinguish between
infantry and technical specialists, including but not limited to aviation.
Within infantry, 'ground pounder'  distinguished regular infantary from special
ops.
```

On  3-Jun-2003, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote:

> > You mean as in "In fourteen hundred and ninety-two, Columbus sailed the
> > Ocean
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> hundred shares".

It is interesting - I am much more likely to say 19 hundred as a date than I am
for a count of inventory units.   But dates are numbers, I wonder why I make
this distinction.   You're saying the media don't make this distinction.   I
guess in this case they're more logical than I am, as this isn't a real
distinction.

Why don't we hear this is June of 20 hundred and three?
```

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T17:13:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32861$047326a0$a093f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com>`

```
Good response. I have 4 years until I can retire but I would like to work
10 more years. I like learning new things but I am sick and tired of
reading, reading, reading and never actually getting to use this stuff. I
personally find actually using it to be a strong motivator for both
learning and remembering. When I do not use it I find I seem to lose it.

I am currently reading a book on components in Visual Basic, but I keep
getting bogged down. I would be reading about COBOL components and OO COBOL
but I am not because of the cost of a compiler. VB, C, C++ are all useful
in their own way but I prefer COBOL to any of them.

Your are right about it being a bet either way.  I am sure I will probably
buy your book eventually and Howard's CDs also. I already have a Mike
Murach book on VB ,NET just waiting for me. Someday I might even shell out
for a compiler if the demands of daily life ever allow me to accumulate
enough savings.

Chris Richardson <richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com> wrote in
article <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com>...
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:<01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel>...
> > Well lets get real. Even if your book and Howard Hinmans's CDs are the
> > absolute best educational materials ever produced, no employer will
hire
> > anyone just because they have read a book (and please note that I am
_NOT_
> > claiming that anyone said or implied this). They want experience
> 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > experience with NetCOBOL for .NET Professional requires an up front
cost of
> > $1800. 
> 
> By the way, both Fujitsu (NetCOBOL for .NET) and Microsoft (VS.NET and
> .NET) offer free 60 day trials for their products. Visit their
> websites
(http://msdn.microsoft.com/vstudio/productinfo/trial/default.aspx
> and http://software.fsw.fujitsu.com/DownloadPlus/Download.html)
> 
> > Maybe not too much for a company but definitely an obstacle for an
> > individual just trying to increase his/her marketability. Basically it
is a
> > bet on the future and no one knows that with anything approaching to
> > certainty. Is the future going to be as you describe?, or is it in
> > components as Pete Dashwood proposes?, or is it both?, or will it be
> > something else?.  NetCOBOL for Windows Professional is $1500 so  the
> > component approach also has significant costs.  Gee maybe I should buy
both
> > and double up my bet!
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I personally have enjoyed this thread and I think it is great that we
have 
> > these educational materials  available. The expense of the material is
> > small compared to the time to master the material and the cost of the
tools
> > required to gain marketable experience. Even if you make this bet there
is
> > no guarantee that you will be employable in the future. All you you
have to
> > do is look at all the people trained in the lastest and greatest who
are
> > unemployed due to the market down turn. Each individual must make
his/her
> > own decision about this, and as much as I appreciate your optimism, I
am
> > concerned that less experienced may be unduly influenced by it and not
> > consider all of the costs and risks.
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
>

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T22:48:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0306012148.6ec56afd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <01c32861$047326a0$a093f243@chottel>`

```
"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message news:<01c32861$047326a0$a093f243@chottel>...
> Good response. I have 4 years until I can retire but I would like to work
> 10 more years. I like learning new things but I am sick and tired of
> reading, reading, reading and never actually getting to use this stuff. I
> personally find actually using it to be a strong motivator for both
> learning and remembering. When I do not use it I find I seem to lose it.

I agree. 

That, by the way, has on occassion been my excuse for programming
after hours.

One of the cool things about "PC" based programming (including the
whole .NET technology suite) is that it is much easier to apply the
tools to your personal life. While at home, any time that I'm using
the computer and come across an "opportunity" for developement, I try
to recognize it as a chance for more hands-on practice. Not that I
don't get enough practice at work... (however, sense I've entered the
world of mgmt...well, you know the story I'm sure)

For example, let's say that you are using your personal computer (at
home) and decide to backup your "MyDocuments" folder. After doing this
a few times, you come across the notion that you could write a .NET
program (language of your choice) that will present a Windows GUI Form
that allows you to select a folder location (or go with a default),
ZIP/Compress the contents, and copy to a particular location. Later,
let's say that you have refined this down to a one-click operation.
Then, you decide to automate the whole process. Still with .NET, you
create a Windows Service that runs quietly in the background - waking
up according to a schedule, ZIP/Compressing your folder and copying
your data to a preferred location. Heck, maybe you'll even add in the
SMTP type code (shown in Howard's earlier posting) to send you an
email to notify you each time...just to let you know that the
operation completed ok.

Of course, you could go out and purchase a package off the shelf (most
likely) that does these things. However, with .NET, you could easily
build your own. I agree that having a purpose, makes learning and
remembering easier. I try to remind myself that when I'm at work, my
job is often to make life easier for the business, for the user's, and
to increase the productivity of my programming staff. While at home, I
try to reward myself by remembering to make life easier for myself
(letting my computer work for me <g>) if possible.
 
Not a new concept...but with "PC" based programming, it's much easier
to bring these benefits into your own home.

Now, if I could just write a .NET program to "automate" taking out the
garbage <g>

> 
> I am currently reading a book on components in Visual Basic, but I keep
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Many of the existing VB programmers (that are using VB version 5 or 6)
are gearing up to start learning VB.NET. They ARE in for a learning
curve. You might use caution if you are in the process of learning VB.
I mean...If you are "starting" to learn VB and not already into
VB.NET...I would think seriously about switching to VB.NET first. This
will save you the trouble of going through the learning curve twice.
Once, learning VB. Then, unlearning some of the VB5/VB6 stuff to start
learning VB.NET.

- cheers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is an OO Program? (was: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T00:35:43+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed9f36f_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2939086.1054279233@dbforums.com> <bb75a4$2ei$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed73866_6@news.athenanews.com> <xnTBa.691$QF2.135564@news2.telusplanet.net> <3ed809d5_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <tZ2Ca.9355$y77.855193@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
OK Don,

as you have shed the cloak of invisibility <G>. I'll respond...

Comments below...

"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:tZ2Ca.9355$y77.855193@news20.bellglobal.com...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
> news:3ed809d5_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Maybe I haven't communicated as well as I hoped.

I'm not suggesting that the "art" of programming will become obsolete. ( I
don't believe ANY "art" form should or can become obsolete. But that
addresses a different issue...). I am saying that in-house computer
programming as we have done for the past 40 years will be rendered
redundant. (I believe it is already. This is not based on some
hallucinogenic vision, Don, it is based on what I have seen and been privy
to in the real world, in real corporations, recently.)

Here is a brief statement of my position.

1. Users and software are getting much "smarter". (The Users, not in an IQ
sense but in their degree of "computer literacy"; I think I mentioned
elsewhere, that in the last project I managed (up until April this year)
there were more computer and IT graduates in the USER area than there were
in the IT department!

These people are becoming more and more capable of doing it for themselves,
and the improvements in software and interfaces are not only facilitating
this, it is actually encouraging them to do so... (It just so happens that
component technology is playing a large part in this, and , to a large
extent, has made and is making, it possible, but that just happens to be an
area of particular interest to me.)

2. As Users become more empowered and better informed about "things
computer" they expect and demand more. They are no longer prepared to be
"fobbed off" by overloaded IT departments. If their request for a system is
deferred or costed beyond reasonability, they will certainly "have a go" at
doing it themselves, and SOME of what they are doing is pretty good stuff
<G>. (OK, some of it is rubbish, too...but we can only expect them to get
better as they make more attempts...)

Programmers don't need to be "computer psychologists". Machines are not
smart enough (yet <G>) to need that. What Programmers DO need is to realise
that the the way w have "always" done things, is becoming less and less
viable.
The Market has changed (and is still changing), the computers (hardware and
software) have advanced in quantum leaps, and the User base is no longer
ignorant about the technology.

There is becoming more demand for direct interface between Users and
computers. Software and Methodologies that can utilise this, are winning
against "computer programming" and the Waterfall.

Systems are being being "evolved" by repeated iterations towards stated
goals, rather than being "designed" then "written" then "implemented", then
repaired...

You were pretty scathing in your post about "packages"... But you are
forgetting that yours is a niche market. It may well be true that you have
the best solution in that market (at the moment) but...right now a bunch of
spotty nerds in a garage somewhere could be plugging components together to
build an equivalent solution. Maybe they are working with a particular
trucking company, offering them something virtually free in exchange for
their application knowledge...using software tools that enable them to build
and dismantle various solutions in a day or so instead of the weeks you
might expect it to take...

Even if they're not, my point is that for the majority of mainstream
businesses, awareness is starting to grow that EVERBODY needs stuff like
Accounts Receivable, Accounts Payable, Sales Analysis, Payroll and HR, Asset
Register, Warehouse and Production Control, and these things are all
available as packages without it costing an arm and a leg to develop and
maintain them in-house.  Furthermore, component technology is making it even
easier to implement the package then "tailor" it , without having to change
the basic configuration (which many have learned to their cost is disastrous
because the package is then "non-standard").

You also claimed that you are making more money as a consultant because
people are failing to implement $1,000,000 solutions. Would it be fair to
say that this "consultancy" is based around the application your business is
based on, which, as I said before, is a niche market?

I'm not doubting what you say, Don, it is just important (for the sake of
this debate) that it doesn't get spun into something it isn't. You are not
"consulting" on IT and Business in general, right? You ARE consulting in the
sense that you can provide a specific solution  (and advice and experience)
to a specific Business sector.

> Even with humans, we need people to teach. What you are really saying is
> that the computers are becoming more intelligent than the average
employee.
>

No I'm not. You won't find anything remotely approaching that statement in
anything I've written here or elsewhere.
(If you're going to put words in my mouth, at least make them sound like
words I MIGHT have said <G>).

What I'm saying is that BOTH computers and Users are getting "smarter"
(closest word I can find without a lot more effort) and this combination is
squeezing the need for an intermediate layer (programmer), as we have
traditionally been used to. (Even that is not EXACTLY what I want to convey,
but it is as close as I can manage without writing a paper on it.<G>)

Your statement that we need people to teach Humans is definitely debatable.

I watch kids using software to educate themselves and I marvel at it. I know
a particular two and a half year old who switches on the computer, starts up
his own particular programs and interacts with them to increase his literacy
and numeracy skills, while he thinks he's playing a game...  Keyboards and
mice hold no fears for this kid (nor for most of his friends). If you think
it isn't fair to debate this using kids' software, I can offer myself (far
from being a kid...) as an example.

I learned Java, HTML, JavaScript, ASP, ActiveX/OLE/COM/DCOM/COM+ and OO
technology without the assistance of a Human Teacher. I don't claim in depth
expertise in all of them (I do in some...<G>), but I certainly know enough
to do the things I want and need to with them, and enough that other people
seem quite happy to ask for and take my advice about them. It wasn't easy,
it took time and effort, but a Human tutor was certainly not indispensable.

(Having said that, I wouldn't discourage others from using Human Tutors,
neither would I necessarily NEVER use one myself. Everyone is different, and
I was trained as a teacher so I find it relatively easy to educate myself...
This is a debate on your statement "Even with Humans we need people to
teach..." I am trying to make the point that "it ain't necessarily so..."
not that "It is never so...".

Thanks for responding as yourself.

I have tried to summarize my position, communicate it better, and correct
some misconceptions you seem to have about what I'm saying. Let me know
whether I succeeded or not <G>...

Pete.
```

As pet peeves go, this has to be my number one.  I don't mind the 
no@no.com (or anon@anon.com, or no@anon.com, etc), in fact I use a group 
only email address myself to reduce spam.  These days one has to hide 
ones email address or have a very tasty spam filter at work.

What pisses me off is the "anon" name that you use.  Every poster here 
except some unknown number of "anon" posters provides a reasonably 
unique name that allows other readers to know who they are typing with.

You claim to be a consultant, John, but how do I know you are not the 
punk-hacker "anon", or even stuffy-professor "anon", instead of 
super-consultant anon?  You could well be any of the many and varied 
anons that post to UseNET and CLC every day.

We all read hundreds (perhaps thousands since the Alphabets thread 
started) of messages each day.  How are we to know with whom we are 
typing if we have no name.  And how are we to associate the history and 
personality of said poster if we have no name.

Please, John, just post a name, any name, to work with your voice to let 
the readers of your messages have some idea.  By all means, keep your 
email cloaked.  But take off the hood if you wish for discussion with 
any respect.

Kind regards,

Joe Zitzelberger

- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2939086.1054279233@dbforums.com> <3ED7F1FD.2020801@Knology.net> <b11feff.0305312100.48f1d142@posting.google.com> <3ED9933D.8060109@Knology.net>`

LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message news:<3ED9933D.8060109@Knology.net>...

(1) You could go with the free 60 day trial that both Fujitsu and

<or>
(2) You could download the ASP.NET WEB Matrix IDE. I have to admit,

(2.b) You can develope ASP.NET applications using VB.NET or C#. I'm

(2.c) It does NOT have some of great bells and whistles that the full

##### ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** address@unkown.com (JF)

```
On Fri, 30 May 2003 19:20:40 +0000, hhinman <member30421@dbforums.com>
wrote:

>
>The most frustating experiences in my career are going into shops who
>have taken very tight and well-working COBOL applications and tried to
>rewrite them into JAVA or VB and have achieved very slow and/or volatile
>applications and have decided to throw them out and go back to COBOL. 

What frustrates me, is when they attempt to migrate JCL and the batch
model.  They don't know how to do it.  Even so-called legacy migrators
I know thought they could write a JCL to JAVA converter, easily, and
they expected it to work!   There is some very strange and expensive
thinking going on these days.  Perhaps they need to go back to some
old fashion batch model thinking too - for the stuff outside of the
COBOL code.

JF
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** "paul" <paulpigott@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T23:50:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vpRCa.27994$rO.2582037@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
That's always been one of my concerns with the whole client/server
methodology.  It's great for interactive processes (a la CICS) but the batch
processes, the long running tasks that are the perogative of the third
shift, it's hard for me to see the appropriateness of that.

Perhaps experience will provide insight.  I've just moved from a COBOL team
to a WEB team and it's proving to be most exciting and informative.  We'll
see.

Paul

network drives. 

Do NOT block SQL or ODBC. Single-user machines need them.

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T13:44:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1054474839@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

<snip>
>. If you've ever had
> sex with a dog or even fantasized about doing it, we'd really like to hear

Well, Gee, Christine, I spend most of my time trying NOT to have sex with a
dog...

Joking aside, the thought occurs to me that if this was posted by somebody
who has a grudge against the person whose details are given, it is one case
where I would reconsider the right of Free Speech.

It would be EXTREMELY hard for anyone to counter this type of vicious
attack. Hard to trace who did it, hard to get legal redress....

The fact is that the Internet is such a powerful means of communication that
misuse of it could be catastrophic.

I hasten to add that I have no problem with the post, if it really was the
person concerned ... If someone is sick and stupid enough to do this, OK, it
harms nobody except themselves.

(The collar number is a nice touch <G> She had sex with a dog and never even
knew his name ???!!!)

"I'll never forget Fido <sigh>..."

Pete.

## Thread: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant??? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: What is the future of COBOL? Answer: Irrelevant???

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T02:21:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3edaa1d0_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<bam318$clv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <2934582.1054186897@dbforums.com> <b11feff.0305291104.5a1d8f99@posting.google.com> <bb5nkh$opm$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <b11feff.0305292017.681f109c@posting.google.com> <01c327c7$c9262000$d1c2f943@chottel> <b11feff.0305312230.1478d792@posting.google.com> <3ed9e117_3@news.athenanews.com> <t_lCa.416$ig1.437185@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
Agree completely Michael.

I mentioned somewhere else that we have yet to see whether .NET will sweep
the market.

There is a lot of resistance to MS (Not from me...I see them like the
Borg...<G>)

Certainly I know of  (2) Organisations who are "considering" the move to
.NET, but haven't moved yet and may be waiting to see what the Market
thinks...

I reckon that .NET won't be evaluated totally on its merits (or lack of
them). The MS thing is becoming more like IBM in the 70s (people knew they
were being screwed by a monopoly, but there was nothing they could do about
it. They either resented it and hoped it would change, or embraced it and
hoped it never would. Either way, if you wanted credible computing you HAD
to go to IBM. And if you wanted to be taken seriously as a "consultant" (I
don't just mean as a "contract programmer", I mean as someone who actually
gave advice about things computer) you needed IBM on your CV.  It might have
gone on indefinitely if IBM hadn't invented the PC and nearly lost their
Company as a consequence....)

I heard it expressed once as "containing the seeds of their own
destruction". I'm wondering if MS can avoid the same fate.

I guess 40 Billion would help you be miserable in comfort...<G>

Pete.

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:t_lCa.416$ig1.437185@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com...

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Yeah, yeah, I know: .NET is "open."  But MS said that about Windows -
until
> the DOJ pointed out that undocumented APIs available only to MS
applications
> products rendered that statement untrue. MS said that about the Internet -
> until the DOJ pointed out that using its monopoly in operating systems to
> corner the browser market was a non-no.  And just reading the papers the
> week, I have to believe that when MS pays a settlement of $750 million
> dollars out of petty cash (ater paying up they are down to about $40 B
cash
> on hand), there are more than enough folks leery of being put in thrall to
> those kinds of resources.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

## Thread: Symbols vs Words — 4 this month / 71 total

_Thread started [2003-05](2003-05.md#t-aeff71e2be)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 21)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T20:20:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ed9fb89.231820582@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed3f77b.412296085@news.optonline.net> <bb18dt$vuj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d9c4.45232037@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291208.6357ad23@posting.google.com> <3ed72976.46941081@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305301155.1d800fa@posting.google.com> <3ed8a1ca.143296106@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311314.64048e15@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>core and a listing from the compiler would show the trigraphs.

Manx (Aztec) is out of business. Mix describes the compiler as "ANSI
compatible", not compliant. 

>It is unwise of you to make generalisations about what things outside
>your experience may do.

You criticize me for failing to read manuals, so I cite the  ANSI language
standard for C. Not good enough. I should have checked a shareware compiler from
a company that's out of business. 

>> Just because they lack a key for { doesn't mean it has been deleted from the
>> ASCII code set. 
>
>Geez, Rob, not all computers use ASCII. Have you heard of EBCDIC ?

EBCDIC has the three characters. Carat is at 5F.

>> The keyboard doesn't define the code set, nor does it delete
>> font symbols used for screen and printer.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>is the currency symbol pound sterling (L-).

The symbol is called 'pound sign' in America, thus replacing it with the pound
sterling symbol is logical. You would have mentioned carat, pipe or bang if
there was any evidence they had been replaced. 

>I have a table here showing the 'ASCII National Replacement
>Characters' for 18 languages.  In the case of the UK only hex23 is
>affected.

The 7-bit National Replacement Characters standard (ISO 646) has been obsolete
since ca. 1985. It was replaced by 8-bit ISO 8859, which leaves the low 128
codes as per US-ASCII (ANSI X3.4) and puts foreign characters in the high end. 

But then you knew that, didn't you?

>For particular languages up to 11 codes in the range hex20 to hex7E
>are affected.  For example what you see as '{' in Czech is 'a-acute'. 
>in Danish it is  'ae', in  Spanish it is 'circle with line'.

You should donate your charts to a museum. They are of historic interest only.

>These are the codes that C uses trigraphs for and which are not in the
>Cobol character set (for an exact reason).

That was a good reason twenty years ago; it is not a good reason today, nor has
it been since at least 1990. 

http://www.tldp.org/HOWTO/Text-Terminal-HOWTO-8.html
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Symbols vs Words

_(thread depth: 22)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T08:57:40+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbdpcd$e4o$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3ed8a1ca.143296106@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311314.64048e15@posting.google.com> <3ed9fb89.231820582@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:

>>example hex23 on an American IBM PC is 'hash'.  On a UK machine this
>>is the currency symbol pound sterling (L-).
> 
> The symbol is called 'pound sign' in America, thus replacing it with the
> pound sterling symbol is logical. 

The octothorpe symbol is called many things in different countries.  Its 
use as 'pound' derives from hastily chalked 'lb' for the measure of weight. 
Hardly logical for using it for a currency symbol.  The actual reason is 
that hash is not used in the UK for 'number' as it is in the US, in fact it 
is hardly used at all.

>>These are the codes that C uses trigraphs for and which are not in the
>>Cobol character set (for an exact reason).
> 
> That was a good reason twenty years ago; it is not a good reason today,
> nor has it been since at least 1990.

The point being that to introduce your symbols you also have to increase 
the Cobol character set to include them.

And I did get you to do the research that you should have done _before_ 
making your proposal.

Now you should be able to make a case for increasing the character set 
quoting how things have changed since the last standard in 1985 and then 
propose how these may be used.
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbdpnr$32f$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bajasd$a7k$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <bav0mv$13j$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed31dd7.356571619@news.optonline.net> <bavbub$5qp$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed3f77b.412296085@news.optonline.net> <bb18dt$vuj$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3ed5d9c4.45232037@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305291208.6357ad23@posting.google.com> <3ed72976.46941081@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305301155.1d800fa@posting.google.com> <3ed8a1ca.143296106@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0305311314.64048e15@posting.google.com> <3ed9fb89.231820582@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ed9fb89.231820582@news.optonline.net...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
 <snip>
>
> The 7-bit National Replacement Characters standard (ISO 646) has been
obsolete
> since ca. 1985. It was replaced by 8-bit ISO 8859, which leaves the low
128
> codes as per US-ASCII (ANSI X3.4) and puts foreign characters in the high
end.
>
> But then you knew that, didn't you?

Hopefully Richard did NOT know that - because it is simply WRONG (as so many
of your comments about ANSI and ISO Standards are).  ISO 646 was last
REVISED in 1991.

For the fact that it is STILL a valid ISO Standard, see:


http://www.iso.org/iso/en/CatalogueDetailPage.CatalogueDetail?CSNUMBER=4777&
ICS1=35&ICS2=40&ICS3=
```

<a id="t-c942a3a021"></a>
## Thread: Enhancements for future COBOL Standard — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### Enhancements for future COBOL Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T16:18:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbdqlp$qur$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
*IGNORING FOR THE MOMENT* when/if any of us will ever see a "full
implementation" of the recently approved COBOL 2002/2003 Standard, I did
want to point this group to two J4 papers (one of which I think I have
mentioned previously).

at:
  http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/s16.doc

Is the full list of (currently under consideration - with "rankings")

  "Candidate features for a future revision"

At:
  http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/03-0144.doc

is a paper from a SINGLE individual commenting on enhancements under
consideration.

    ***

As I have no way of knowing whether there will or will not be a "relatively
quick" (e.g  less than 10  years) revision of the recently approved
Standard, I can't suggest strongly enough that those of you who DO believe
in the COBOL Standardization process provide your PERSONAL input on those
features currently under consideration NOW - while the list is still being
developed.  If you create a paper "comparable" to that listed above (from
Artur Reimann) and send it to the J4 Chair, it will be looked at by the
Standards committee to give them "outside" input to validate or change their
priorities.

P.S.  Just as Artur did, please DO indicate that your personal opinions are
just that - your opinions and not those of any "group" - unless such a group
authorizes you to do so.
```

#### ↳ Re: Enhancements for future COBOL Standard

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T00:04:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDA90F5.DD02DE86@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<bbdqlp$qur$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```


"William M. Klein" wrote:

> *IGNORING FOR THE MOMENT* when/if any of us will ever see a "full
> implementation" of the recently approved COBOL 2002/2003 Standard, I did
> want to point this group to two J4 papers (one of which I think I have
> mentioned previously).
>

Two things :

(1) Good points made by Artur, "If it ain't broke don't fix it" and only "add"
as a new feature if it offers *significant* value to COBOL. I noted his comment
on XML - still have some reading to do, but I think I'm with him when I've done
checking.

(2) Rather than trip over their feet down the road, J4 participants have given
themselves a score card to rate proposals from 0 - 60. Doesn't matter who puts
up an idea, flaky or not. They've arrived at a rating system which allows them
to target, by their general consensus,  what is important.

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Enhancements for future COBOL Standard

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@cox.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T17:19:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LMwCa.57879$%42.46223@fed1read06>`
- **References:** `<bbdqlp$qur$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
It is refreshing to see an attempt to change the direction of the
discussion.

As a reader here with a very large emotional attachment to this subject many
things
that come to mind have not been shared. Why?

You are it seems to me at the wrong level of management to grasp the kinds
of things
that could be done to aid the growth of a commond language for systems.

I am too.

When I review what I lived through, it comes down to the fact that
programming was
the cause of lots of expense and much of it was connected to getting it
done. Kiss seemed
to be the password of the people who began CODASYL. The free style dog fight
for
business mostly made things worse.  Plugboards seemed better. Yet, few
interfaces existed
for tab operations except from keypunch or paper tape.

If I were to look at today's "market" for improvement in systems, I'd list a
whole lot of
efforts going on in parallel, and on top of the existing tools. Granted
tools means more than
a compiler, and new I/O devices call for additional methods. But do we need
so many things or
do we need a new study using a new objective?

What are the benifits of one method? Why do we need more than one way to
code, one for
batch, one for screen, one for etc. Given the same assignment the Short
Range Task Force had
NOW, I don't think the job would produce todays multiple methods for
supporting application
development.

I believe that the response to the original question of "where do we see..."
should have been
proposals to reduce redundancy. How I wish any text composition would
include as you go
spell checking.  My spelling has improved, but mainly because WP usually
provide that.

COBOL does not provide everything "people" see the need for. It should not.
But, if it has to do
with I/O for example, maybe it should. Maybe new devices should have a way
to be introduced into
the program without calling first something in lang-a then something in l-b,
etc.

It is crazy for two methods to exist because a new one would require a
complete recompile?

At this stage you are seeing what I'll call a WGS garbage dump. The needs
are more universal than
a clever way to process strings, etc. Cobol PROGRAMMING has become a case of
mixing methods.
A path to reducing that to as near one as possible is worthwhile.

Warren Simmons
wsimmons5@cox.net
```

---

<a id="t-70a4105ecf"></a>
## Thread: OT - What's "unanamous" - in replies to RW — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT - What's "unanamous" - in replies to RW

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-01T16:57:36-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbdssh$jn3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
To start a new thread (in reply to a comment by Mr. Wagner in another
thread).  The following is my PERSONAL perception of what is (almost)
unanimous in replies to his posts:

1) When he argues that one STYLE is "superior" to another, most comments
will be negative (even from those who PERSONALLY prefer the same style as he
does). Failure to distinguish between "style" and non-subjective issues is a
reoccurring problem

2) When he states "as fact" something that is actually his opinion, replies
will cite counter-examples and ignore his "content".  (In other words his
implied "In my opinion" causes negative responses.)

3) When he "generalizes" from his experience to claim that what he has
experienced is "generally true" those with other experiences will argue with
him. (Particularly when he "jumps" from IBM mainframes to ALL mainframes and
when he fails to distinguish between experiences 20-30 years ago with those
of today.)

4) Actual "errors in fact" (e.g. what the Standard says, what Micro Focus
does in <implied> all environments, what is true in linguistics - as applied
to either natural or artificial languages, etc) will often get corrections
ALONG WITH comments on his "usual accuracy".

    ***

As my perception (and I have *not* quantified this to verify this) is that
the MAJORITY of his posts have one or more of the above, I probably agree
with HIS perception that whatever he posts many/most of this group will
"disagree" with.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT - What's "unanamous" - in replies to RW

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T13:29:37+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3edaa8d2_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bbdssh$jn3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bbdssh$jn3$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
> To start a new thread (in reply to a comment by Mr. Wagner in another
> thread).  The following is my PERSONAL perception of what is (almost)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> will be negative (even from those who PERSONALLY prefer the same style as
he
> does). Failure to distinguish between "style" and non-subjective issues is
a
> reoccurring problem
>
> 2) When he states "as fact" something that is actually his opinion,
replies
> will cite counter-examples and ignore his "content".  (In other words his
> implied "In my opinion" causes negative responses.)
>
> 3) When he "generalizes" from his experience to claim that what he has
> experienced is "generally true" those with other experiences will argue
with
> him. (Particularly when he "jumps" from IBM mainframes to ALL mainframes
and
> when he fails to distinguish between experiences 20-30 years ago with
those
> of today.)
>
> 4) Actual "errors in fact" (e.g. what the Standard says, what Micro Focus
> does in <implied> all environments, what is true in linguistics - as
applied
> to either natural or artificial languages, etc) will often get corrections
> ALONG WITH comments on his "usual accuracy".
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bill,

there is a lot of nonsense posted here (I post a lot of it myself...<G>) but
it really doesn't destroy anybody's life or cause cancer...

I neither agree nor disagree with your evaluation above. ("I have no opinion
about that" - Paul Simon)

What I do think is that you may be taking Robert too seriously. His opinions
are not going to bring about the downfall of the Free World or hasten the
demise of COBOL.

Sometimes I gasp in amazement at the sheer breadth of his arrogance and/or
stupidity, but I am more amused by it than upset. He is NOT a wicked person.
He just has a different personality. It can only offend you if you let it.
Sometimes I find the interactions between him and the Doc or Richard
entertaining (they are both quite merciless on him, but he deserves it),
sometimes it is just tedious.

And, if you were being fair, you would have to admit that the cut and thrust
of posting here is slowly having an effect. Robert's posts are (for the most
part <G>) better now than they were.

It is unlikely that you are ever going to LIKE what he says (or, more
correctly, the WAY in which he says it), and most of the readership here
would be in that boat also, so I guess your conclusion above is probably
true, but it would be a pretty feeble forum if everyone just agreed about
everything...<G>

I enjoy ALL the posts here (including Robert's) and I would not disagree
with something, just because it was said by a disagreeable person.

Pete.
```

<a id="t-bda2a757cb"></a>
## Thread: MF NE3.1 RTS 9/199 Network Error — 1 this month / 2 total

_Thread started [2003-05](2003-05.md#t-bda2a757cb)._

#### ↳ Re: MF NE3.1 RTS 9/199 Network Error

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@microfocus.uk.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T10:33:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbf5dg$t44$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<7cb2e24f.0305301439.47f3eec8@posting.google.com>`

```
Pepe,

As you found, the file status error is returned as a direct result of a
system level error being returned from the file APIs indicating that some
sort of network error occurred. That being the case, check whether there are
any updates available for the network drivers you are using as that can have
a major bearing on this sort of problem.

Note that if you had applied the Net Express service pack you would have got
a 9/209 error which would given you a better idea of what the problem was
without having to call GetLastError directly.

"Pepe" <pepe@gmx.com> wrote in message
news:7cb2e24f.0305301439.47f3eec8@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
>
> COBOL reports an 9/199 error. Calling GetLastError directly after the
> file access only leads to "an unexpected network error occured".
```

## Thread: Help Needed With Sort With Input-Output Procedure — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help Needed With Sort With Input-Output Procedure

- **From:** mikey@mailbox.co.za (Michael)
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T07:21:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5cddb7f2.0306020621.1c683bbc@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi, I'm a Cobol student and am having problems with a simple sort with
input-output procedure.  My code is below but it's a bit of a mess.  I
was wondering if anyone could guide me in the right direction.

I've included the program specifications here with my code underneath.

If you could help, I'd really appreciate it.

Thanks,
Michael
-----------------
Program description:

Records are to be sorted by student number within student name within
grade point average within college-year status.  Each record to be
printed in its sorted sequence together with the total number of
students in each college year to be printed at the end of the report.

Input:

Student Number		9(7)
Student  Last Name 	X(14)
Student First Name	X(10)
Grade Points		999
Units Completed		999


Program Operations:

1 Determine the College-Year status for each student in accordance
with the value of the units-completed field.

(a)	0 ï¿½ 29	= Freshman
(b)	30 ï¿½ 59	= Sophomore
(c)	60 ï¿½ 89	= Junior
(d)	over 89	= Senior

2 Calculate each student's grade point average by dividing the grade
points field by the units-completed field.  Define the quotient with
two decimal places.

2 Sort the records in the required order

3 Print the output and at the end ï¿½ on separate lines print

FRESHMEN	ZZ,ZZ9
SOPHOMORES	ZZ,ZZ9
JUNIORS		ZZ,ZZ9
SENIORS		ZZ,ZZ9


My code and the input file are below: - 

000010 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000020 PROGRAM-ID.  OP2PTSRT.
000030 AUTHOR.      A N OTHER.
000040**********************************************************
000050*RECORDS ARE TO BE SORTED BY STUDENT-NUMBER WITHIN       *
000060*STUDENT-NAME, WITHIN GRADE-POINT-AVERAGE, WITHIN COLLEGE*
000070*YEAR STATUS.  PRINT EACH RECORD IN ITS SORTED SEQUENCE  *
000080*TOGETHER WITH THE TOTAL NUMBER OF STUDENTS PER COLLEGE  *
000090**********************************************************
000100
000110 ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
000120 INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
000130 FILE-CONTROL.
000140     SELECT STUDENT-FILE ASSIGN TO DISK 'OP2PTSTD.DAT'
000150         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
000160     SELECT PRINT-FILE   ASSIGN TO PRINTER 'CON'.
000170     
000180 DATA DIVISION.
000190 FILE SECTION.
000200 FD  STUDENT-FILE.
000210 01  STUDENT-RECORD.
000220     05  STUD-DATA.
000230         10  STUD-NUMBER         PIC 9(7).
000240         10  STUD-LAST-NAME      PIC X(14).
000250         10  STUD-FIRST-NAME     PIC X(10).
000260         10  GRADE-POINTS        PIC 999.
000270         10  UNITS-COMPLETED     PIC 999.
000280         
000290 FD  PRINT-FILE.
000300 01  PRINT-RECORD                PIC X(80).

       SD SORT-FILE
       01 SORT-RECORD.
	  05 SORT-DATA.
	     10  SRT-COLLEGE-YEAR	PIC X(10).
	     10  SRT-STUD-NUMBER	PIC 9(7).
	     10  SRT-LAST-NAME		PIC X(10).
	     10  SRT-FIRST-NAME		PIC X(10).
	     10  SRT-GRADE-POINTS 	PIC 999.
	     10  SRT-UNITS-COMPLETED	PIC 999.
	     10  SRT-GPA		PIC 9V99.

000310 
000320 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000330 
000340* HEADING AREAS AND MAIN PRINT DETAIL LINE
000350****************************************** 
000360 01  HEADING-1.
000370     05                          PIC X(60)  VALUE
000380         'STUDENT LIST BY GPA BY COLLEGE YEAR'.
000390         
000400 01  HEADING-2.
000410     05                          PIC X(30)  VALUE
000420         ' COLLEGE       STUDENT'.
000430     05                          PIC X(29)  VALUE
000440         '------STUDENT NAME-------'.
000450     05                          PIC X(15)  VALUE
000460         'GRADE   UNITS'.
000470         
000480 01  HEADING-3.
000490     05                          PIC X(30)  VALUE
000500         '   YEAR        NUMBER'.
000510     05                          PIC X(29)  VALUE
000520         '  LAST          FIRST'.
000530     05                          PIC X(20)  VALUE
000540         'POINTS  COMP.    GPA'.
000550         
000560 01  PRINT-LINE.
000570     05  P-COLLEGE-YEAR          PIC X(10).
000580     05  P-STUD-NUMBER           PIC B(6)9(7).
000590     05                          PIC X(7)    VALUE SPACES.
000600     05  P-LAST-NAME             PIC X(14).
000610     05  P-FIRST-NAME            PIC BX(10).
000620     05  P-GRADE-POINTS          PIC B(4)ZZ9.
000630     05  P-UNITS-COMP            PIC B(4)ZZ9.
000640     05  P-GPA                   PIC 9.99.

      01   TOTAL-FRESH		       PIC 999.
      01   TOTAL-SOPH		       PIC 999.
      01   TOTAL-JUN		       PIC 999.
      01   TOTAL-SEN		       PIC 999.	

000650     
000660 PROCEDURE DIVISION.                      
000670 BEGIN-PARA.
000680                    
000690     SORT SORT-FILE
		ASCENDING KEY SRT-COLLEGE-YR  
			      SRT-GRADE-POINTS		
			      SRT-STUDENT-LAST-NAME
		INPUT PROCEDURE CALC-AND-EXTRACT
		OUTPUT PROCEDURE PRINT-STUDENTs
	   STOP RUN.

	   CALC-AND-EXTRACT.
		OPEN INPUT STUDENT-FILE
		PERFORM READ-STUDENT
		PERFORM PROCESS-STUDENTS UNTIL END-STUDENT
		CLOSE STUDENT-FILE
	
	   PROCESS-STUDENTS.
		IF UNITS-COMPLETED < 30
		   MOVE 'FRESHMAN' TO SRT-COLLEGE-YR
		ELSE
		   IF UNITS-COMPLETED > 30 AND UNITS-COMPLETED < 60
		      MOVE 'SOPHOMORE' TO SRT-COLLEGE-YR
		   ELSE
		      IF UNITS-COMPLETED > 59 AND UNITS-COMPLETED < 90
		         MOVE 'JUNIOR' TO SRT-COLLEGE-YR
		      ELSE
			MOVE 'SENIOR' TO SRT-COLLEGE-YR.

		DIVIDE GRADE-POINTS BY UNITS-COMPLETED GIVING SORT-GPA.
		
		MOVE STUD-NUMBER T0 SRT-STUD-NUMBER
		MOVE STUD-LAST-NAME TO SRT-STUD-LAST-NAME
		MOVE STUD-FIRST-NAME TO SRT-STUD-FIRST-NAME
		MOVE GRADE-POINTS TO SRT-GRADE-POINTS
		MOVE UNITS-COMPLETED TO SRT-UNITS-COMPLETED.
		
		RELEASE SORT-RECORD
	
		PERFORM READ-STUDENT.

	PRINT-STUDENTS
		PERFORM READ-FROM-SORT
		PERFORM PROCESS-SORT-RECS UNTIL EO-SORT
		PERFORM PRINT-SUMMARY.

	PROCESS-SORT-RECS.
		MOVE SORT-COLLEGE-YEAR TO P-COLLEGE-YEAR	
		MOVE SORT-STUD-NUMBER TO P-STUD-NUMBER
		MOVE SORT-LAST-NAME TO P-STUD-LAST-NAME	
		MOVE SORT-FIRST-NAME TO P-FIRST-NAME
		MOVE SORT-GRADE-POINTS TO P-GRADE-POINTS
		MOVE SORT-UNITS-COMPLETED TO UNITS-COMP
		MOVE SORT-GPA To P_GPA
		
		IF SORT-COLLEGE-YEAR = 'FRESHMAN'
		   ADD SORT-GPA TO TOTAL-FRESH
		ELSE 
		    IF SORT-COLLEGE-YEAR = 'SOPHOMORE'
			ADD SORT-GPA TO TOTAL-SOPH
		    ELSE
			IF SORT-COLLEGE-YEAR = 'SENIOR'	
			 ADD SORT-GPA TO TOTAL-SEN
			ELSE
			   ADD SORT-GPA TO TOTAL-JUN.
		
		WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM PRINT-RECORD.
		PERFORM READ-FROM-SORT
	  
	 PRINT-SUMMARY.
		FRESHMAN		
                      
-----------------------------------

Input File:



1234567TICKS         POLLY     300029                 
1122334CUSTARDE      COWARDE DE350089
0089999MBEKI         SMILEY    450035                       
0067766MACDONALD     CHAS      700090
5325345SEPTEMBER     APRIL     800095
6436215KHUMALO       SIBONGILE 600025
1237659O'MALLEY      PADDY     720050
0088996JONES         EVAN      534075
7610091VAN DER MERWE JOHANNES  230060
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Needed With Sort With Input-Output Procedure

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T18:12:08+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rspmdvgv1b0u22cm4d1sb9rlmlb5uh9us1@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<5cddb7f2.0306020621.1c683bbc@posting.google.com>`

```
On 2 Jun 2003 07:21:42 -0700 mikey@mailbox.co.za (Michael) wrote:

:>Hi, I'm a Cobol student and am having problems with a simple sort with
:>input-output procedure.  My code is below but it's a bit of a mess.  I
:>was wondering if anyone could guide me in the right direction.

:>I've included the program specifications here with my code underneath.

You seem to be in general in the right path. Put in the missing pieces and see
what happens.

:>	     10  SRT-COLLEGE-YEAR	PIC X(10).

You might find it more efficient to use a code such as {1,2,3,4} rather than
the text strings.

:>      01   TOTAL-FRESH		       PIC 999.
:>      01   TOTAL-SOPH		       PIC 999.
:>      01   TOTAL-JUN		       PIC 999.
:>      01   TOTAL-SEN		       PIC 999.	

I would suggest making accumulators COMP.

:>	   PROCESS-STUDENTS.
:>		IF UNITS-COMPLETED < 30
:>		   MOVE 'FRESHMAN' TO SRT-COLLEGE-YR
:>		ELSE
:>		   IF UNITS-COMPLETED > 30 AND UNITS-COMPLETED < 60
:>		      MOVE 'SOPHOMORE' TO SRT-COLLEGE-YR
:>		   ELSE
:>		      IF UNITS-COMPLETED > 59 AND UNITS-COMPLETED < 90
:>		         MOVE 'JUNIOR' TO SRT-COLLEGE-YR
:>		      ELSE
:>			MOVE 'SENIOR' TO SRT-COLLEGE-YR.

I would suggest EVALUATE and removing the superfluous conditions.

As a side point, if the student has exactly 30 units completed, what is he?
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Needed With Sort With Input-Output Procedure

- **From:** "Vince Coen" <Vince_Coen@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T21:47:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1054590261@f609.n257.z2.fidonet.ftn>`
- **References:** `<5cddb7f2.0306020621.1c683bbc@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello Michael!

02 Jun 03 14:21, Michael wrote to All:

 M> Hi, I'm a Cobol student and am having problems with a simple sort with
 M> input-output procedure.  My code is below but it's a bit of a mess.  I
 M> was wondering if anyone could guide me in the right direction.

You seem to have left out full stops in various places that can well confuse 
a compiler. Such as last para before a paragraph name and at the end of 
paragraph names. Also you need a SELECT for the sort file. I did not look at 
your logic within the procedures.


Vince
```

<a id="t-a280a9f631"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL Server Express OUTPUT to EBCDIC — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL Server Express OUTPUT to EBCDIC

- **From:** klaatu62@hotmail.com (TV_Junkie)
- **Date:** 2003-06-02T08:50:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5679495.0306020750.9dd16d1@posting.google.com>`

```
Folks,  

I am in the process of migrating from WANG to HP-UNIX using MFCobol. 
The process is going very well to date, but I have hit a small
problem.

On the VS there was a 2024 byte Line Sequential file that we output to
tape and sent to clients.  This file had PACKED fields in it because
the longest sequential record supported on the Wang VS was 2024. 
However we had a Wang utility program to move the file to tape and do
ASCII-EBCDIC translation and we could identify packed and binary data
areas to the program so that the translation process took this
information into account and translated accordingly.

Now however our output to 3490 program permits us only 2 options -
Translate to EBCDIC or DON'T TRANSLATE.

Now I know the easy fix is unpack the file, but this affects MANY
customers that would then have to make changes themselves in both
their programs and their JCL to offload the tape.  Therefore this
seems not to be an option.

I know that we can use the FD CODE-SET clause in MF to cause a
linesequential file to be output in a different character set, but I'm
having trouble getting the pre-requisites straight.  There are issues
with the ALPHABET clause that have to be taken into account and the
documentation is well... we've all been there haven't we! :-)

If anyone can offer a little assistance in getting me going with this
minor problem it would be very much appreciated.

R Boback
Quincy Mass.
klaatu62@hotmail.com
```
