<a id="t-73d51cce1f"></a>
## Thread: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly — 45 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T03:56:28+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel>`

```
I am simply amazed at the amount of wasted energy spent in seemingly
endless debates with RW over IMHO trivial drivel. I know it is a diverse
world but I am stunned by the appetite displayed here for argument and
debate for no other purpose than for argument and debate. Can't we find any
loftier topics to discuss?

Even if none of us ever used COBOL .NET  wouldn't we be better off reading
about it, say a chapter a week, and dicussing it here.   C++ has a standard
template library (STL). Should that be added to OOCOBOL? How could we
improve on it so it is more readable? There are many interesting books out
there whose ideas could be expressed in COBOL.  It seems to me that there
are endless amounts of more interesting things that we could be using this
newsgroup for.

Well I feel like a voice crying out in the wilderness and  I suppose there
are those whose tastes prefer the current threads to what I am proposing.
The whole concept of "interests" is subjective I suppose. Newsgroups for
many other languages seem to me to be more vibrant and full of enthusiasm
and I would like to see a little more of that here.
```

#### ↳ Re: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T06:17:55+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> I am simply amazed at the amount of wasted energy spent in seemingly
> endless debates with RW over IMHO trivial drivel. I know it is a diverse
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sure sympathize with you Charlie over the GREAT DEBATE, but hope you don't mind
a little humour thrown in, now and again. You are right about the other groups
they *are* far more productive. I occasionally look at Smalltalk and Java and
they are all gung ho on getting things fixed.

If you reflect, plain 'ole COBOL - there isn't too much to get excited about -
it is so well established. Then of course somebody starts a thread on EXITS, GO
TOs etc., and everybody goes at it hammer and tongs, and when finished nobody
is convinced or is going to change their style.

Think of Smalltalk, the complete syntax can be printed on one page ! Therefore
there are lots of requests on "How to...." which boils down to a mix of the
prime words/notatoins (RESERVED) mixed with the appropriate method/class names.
With more formalization, the same approach seems to occur in Java, a mixture of
methods/classes in a particular situation.

Now if this Group was geared to PCs, and take the M/F Answer Exchange as an
example, there would be a tremendous amount of 'How to .......", using GUI
tools, OLE, Web, some SQL etc.

Now if you want to kick-start that STL idea, go for it, and let's see what gets
contributed. Not being familiar with C++ I don't know what the template library
is.
Specifically what are these templates composed of - are they support classes or
are they geared to the area close to Pete's heart - Components ?

The STL *might* be what J4 is currently discussing - a set of COBOL Standard
classes to amalgamate what exists currently as extensions in Fujitsu, Hitachi
and Micro Focus versions of OO.

As regarding readability for C++ - there are of course the 'heretic' books, "C
for COBOL programmers" and "Java for COBOL programmers". Unfortunately nothing
written the other direction. Strangely enough it's only about a week ago that I
raised this one in M/F Answer Exchange, (i.e. I can look in the MS SDK for
examples - always, always written in C or C++ !).

I'm sure the feature must be available in other compilers, but M/F  (Chris
Glazier, I think), pointed out that M/F has routines to convert the C data
types, and I've squirreled away a copy of his message. Even so, don't forget
C++ is OO, so the converted examples would only really make sense as OO COBOL.

Anyway, you've let off steam - now quiz the rest of us.

Jimmy
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T17:32:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca>`

```
Well I don't know much detail on STL.  From the C/C++ Users Journal I know
it adds very powerful capabilities to C++ but sometimes at the expense of
readability. Yeah I know it had to believe you can make C++ ever harder to
read.

STL is a set of template classes that implement many  algorithms and data
structures. It lets you write functions that work with any data type. It
implements iterators, algorithms, deques, vectors, lists, maps, and
multimaps, sets, and multisets; searching and sorting algorithms, function
objects etc. yet another book/topic I have not yet got around to reading
about, but I can excerpt from the table of contents :-).

It is related to the concept of reuseable software components. It
originated in 1979 with Alexander Stepanov and later with David Musser. A
lot of the work was done at Hewlett-Packard.

Someday I hope to take topics like this and other interesting books and
translate the ideas into COBOL and perhaps make the programs available on a
web site. All it takes is time. It might not be kosher to write my own book
on the same topic only using COBOL, although I have seen data structure
books that seemed to follow Knuth's books to a great extent. Taking the
same topics and making them attractive to programmers in another language
would probably not be objectionable as it can only increase the possible
readership range for those books. This is something I could do after
retirement and would be a possible justification for buying a PC based
COBOL compiler. It would be fun to program what I want and am interested in
for a change, instead of what others want. 

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in article
<3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca>...

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

- **From:** "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T12:50:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbin4t02fpj@enews1.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel>`

```

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel...
> Well I don't know much detail on STL.  From the C/C++ Users
Journal I know
> it adds very powerful capabilities to C++ but sometimes at
the expense of
> readability. Yeah I know it had to believe you can make C++
ever harder to
> read.
>
> STL is a set of template classes that implement many
algorithms and data
> structures. It lets you write functions that work with any
data type. It
> implements iterators, algorithms, deques, vectors, lists,
maps, and
> multimaps, sets, and multisets; searching and sorting
algorithms, function
> objects etc. yet another book/topic I have not yet got around
to reading
> about, but I can excerpt from the table of contents :-).

The STL is the only way to do some things, but it takes a hit
(as does all templating) from it being a pre-compile operation.
Handy as it is, compilers can only report on error produced
after the template is applied, thus obfuscating any defects in
the template itself.

The theoretical improvement on this idea is the "generic
class."  Where templates use text replacement, generic classes
will use inheritance or polymorphism to achieve the same
result -- all while maintaining a higher degree of type
enforcement.  I can't say that I've ever used a development
environment that has a sufficiently powerful generic class
mechanism to make STL obsolete, though.

There is an ongoing debate about introducing generic classes
into dotnet.  If this happens, it's entirely likely that there
will be a COBOL that supports them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T21:37:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> STL is a set of template classes that implement many  algorithms and data
> structures. It lets you write functions that work with any data type. It
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> about, but I can excerpt from the table of contents :-).

OK Charles, let's pick up from there, but it's not quite specific enough for me
at the moment.

Take as a starting point Smalltalk, (If you want to be particularly correct, go
back to Simula). The WHOLE language is based on objects - what is it in C :-

        printf (string) or something......

Same applies with all the other OO languages, so they have to provide
classes/methods to handle any eventuality. There is nothing equivalent to our
pic zzz,zzz.99. You get there by finagling. using the various string support
classes.

Ignoring GUIs/Web etc... for the moment, we already have a RICH complement in
standard COBOL to do both numeric and text handling, (STRING, UNSTRING,  MOVE,
Reference Modification. etc.).

Donald and Pete will chip in if they disagree - but quite a slice of OO COBOL
can be done using conventional pic 9 or pic x - so for the most part, we don't
require OO string handling techniques. Even in preparing lists
(collecitons/dictionaries) the elements in the collections can be expressed as
intrinsics (pic 9 or pic x).

Not one tackled as yet in the proposed OO COBOL Standard, but you *do* require
to get intrinsics into objects for Windows etc. displaying.  F/J and Hitachi -
I'm not aware of what they use, but rather like Smalltalk, Net Express does
provide a set of GUI classes, which spring off from the main branch at a class
called Dependent; this acts as the immediate "super" class for anything to do
with GUIs.

(Recently a suggestion was made by someone, about incorporating GUIs, the J4
folks gave it a rating of  4 out of 60 - so it's as dead as a dodo. OK Dashwood
- no laughter - It *wasn't* ME <G>).

So if you are looking for C functions that *might* be useful with COBOL,
exclude any where you see an obvious 'cousin' already present in our pic 9 or
pic x handling.

To get a bit of background on class structures and how they compare to what you
are looking at with those STLs in the C language, two possibilities.

- Apparently there is a University Edition of Net Express V 4.0, just released.
I don't have V 4.0 - (money !) - so I'm not sure what has been added. Trouble
is with the PC compilers, so much has been added over the years as extensions,
that when we come to look at something "new" based on COBOL 2002 - we don't
recognize it as new because we've been using it for years ! Check with Micro
Focus - you *may* qualify as an interested party to get a copy of the UE. The
old UE version sold for about $100 - don't know what their price would be for V
4. The UE is a learning tool, comprehensive but with a 'lock' so it can't be
used to make a buck on the side.

- The other alternative is to do some class hierarchy checking, if you can
borrow some decent books from an academic tech library. I was going to give you
a reference to some diagrams on class hierarchies at

        http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/index.asp.

but unfortunately when I check the J4 site, doesn't look like 2001 documents
are still on-line.

Back to structuring of class libraries, there is a huge emphasis on
REUSABILITY, sub-classes making constant reference to super support classes. I
*could* change the program to reflect intrinsics, but I'm not going to <G>. The
following extract, based on something I recently posted for Thane is creating
elements as objects in a collection so they can be displayed in a Listbox.

*>---------------------------------------------------------
Method-id. "makeListRecord".
*>----------------------------------------------------------
Local-storage section.
01 ls-length                     pic x(4) comp-5.
Linkage section.
01 lnk-string                    object reference.

Procedure division returning lnk-string.

   if TypeTabbed
       move x'09' to Tab1 of ws-MatListRecord
                     Tab2 of ws-MatListRecord
                     Tab3 of ws-MatListRecord
   End-if

   move length of Name-record to ls-length

   Evaluate true
     when ByName
       move CORR ws-MatListRecord to Name-record
       invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue" using
          ls-length, Name-record returning lnk-string
     when ByKey
       move CORR ws-MatListRecord to Key-record
       invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue" using
          ls-length, Key-record returning lnk-string
     when other
       move CORR ws-MatListRecord to Other-record
       invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue" using
          ls-length, Other-record returning lnk-string
   End-evaluate

   if    Mat-ID of ws-MatListRecord > ws-LastNumber
         move Mat-Id of ws-MatListRecord to ws-LastNumber
   end-if

End Method "makeListRecord".
*>----------------------------------------------------------

No big deal about the above method, just traditional COBOL syntax wrapped
inside a method. It only gets different when we get to the lines :-

       invoke CharacterArray "withLengthValue" using
          ls-length, { Name/Key/Other-record } returning lnk-string

To see what's happening I've set the Animator to pause on "invoke
CharacterArray", so I can see which support classes are called. Not something I
have found a need for but there is a TRACE list that gives me an audit trail of
cllasses/methods invoked. As I've only used TRACE very rarely, I've stepped
through the following manually :-

Class            method
-----            ------------
Charray            withLengthValue
Charray             new
Behavior            new
Collectn            initialize
Base                 malloc
Charray            setValue

The above sequence occurs for each element being added to the collection. It
can get much more complicated than that, iterating through some of the support
classes repetitively, until you go dizzy ! Given the power of modern computers
the above of course occurs in a flash.

As you can see from the table above it does emphasize the concept of
ENCAPSULATION. As Pete has stressed you have 'got to take components on faith',
i.e., you have to accept that the third parties that wrote the programs have
done their homework and thoroughly tested them. This is largely achieved by
keeping individual methods minimal in source size so that the function they
perform is simple and easily proven.

You don't need to see the internal combustion engine - and in the above example
all we are interested in  :-

(a) doing the initial invoke with the correct parameters
(b) lots of 'magic' going on between whiles
(c) we get back the return object from our invoke

J4 are working on Standard classes at the moment. I'm not "in the know" so I
don't know any more than you. If you want to check out, go to the URL and
searach out Thane's name along with the topic 'Standard Classes". At this stage
it would appear they are concentrating on the lowest level in the pyramid,
collections. Having straightened that away, then they will have to look at
"super" classes above, bringing together common routines, building the pyramid
with a series of layers where commonality occurs.

Back to your quote above :-

"STL is a set of template classes that implement many  algorithms == ??? and
data
structures.

It lets you write functions that work with any data type == will have to take
account of data types and COBOL 2002 already does..

It implements iterators == N/E certainly allows callbacks with a "do" method

algorithms== ??  deques == ?? vectors== ??

lists, maps, and multimaps, sets, and multisets == depends upon the 'prime'
classes that J4 determines. Lists = collections and your reference to maps,; is
that the same as Maps in Java ? These are called Dictionaries in Smalltalk/Net
Express, and tentatively referred to by J4 as 'Keyed Collections"

searching = Keyed Collections*should* allow for this and another approach is
iterating a callback with a "do" method

sorting algorithms == a default sort, ascending, and currently N/E allows you
to invoke your own SortMethod, the simples being "reverse" gives you descending

function objects = ????

Each language tends to have its own terminology, hence my question marks
against some of your items.Now if you want to zero in and get more specific,
such as "C++ has this - do we have it in OO COBOL etc. .....?"

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T22:58:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca>`

```
It is hard for me to be more specfic about the STL without doing more
in-depth reading. This will teach me to propose a discussion that I am not
yet ready for.

Suppose you wanted to write a sort routine. In PASCAL the typing is very
strong and includes the array size as part of the type. You could sort an
array of 100 integers but to sort a different size array or an array
containing a different type you would have to code another version of the
sort. C/C++ relaxes this somewhat as the array size is not part of the
type. Still your sort would expect a pointer to an array of one particular
type. STL would let you specify a generic type at compile time and at run
time your sort could work on int, char, short, double, or user defined
types etc.. This is one of the basic ideas behind the STL.

iterators - a generalization of an object pointer. You can declare new
classes and override many of the operators with new meanings. You use an
iterator to access the elements of an ordered sequence, Access means either
storing or obtaining. There are output iterators, input iterators, forward
iterators, bidirectional iterators and random access iterators.

memory allocators - deal with unusual ways to allocate storage for elements
of a container

algorithms - numerous template functions. Most closely resemble traditional
function library. Not supplied as linkable library but typically defined as
STL headers that you can specialize in numerous ways to make generic
program components.

numeric - template functions that perform simple arithmetic operations on
the elements of a sequence. Not constrained to operations on arithmetic
types. Given suitable overloads you can perform very general operations in
the guise of simple arithmetic.

functional - greatly enhance the power of algorithms. Each one encapsulates
a critical predicate or other calculation, keeping it separate from the
template function that implements the algorithm. You can write your own
function objects.

containers -  a container is a class that manages a sequence. Member
functions let you insert new elements, delete exisitinf elements, and
locate elements. The functions return iterators to designate elements so
you can apply various algorithms.
   vector - an array of N or more contiguous elements
    list  - a bidirectional linked list of nodes, each contains an element
   deque  - an array of N or more contiguous pointers to separately
allocated             elements
    set   - a red/black tree of nodes, each contining an element, ordered
by             some predicate applied to pairs of elements, with no two
elements             having equivalent ordering
    multset - a set that also permits pairs of elements to have equivalent 
             ordering
    map   - a set of key/value pairs, ordered by some predicate applied to 
           pairs of keys
    multimap - a map that permits pairs of keys having equivalent ordering
    stack  - LIFO queue
    queue  - FIFO queue
    priority_queue - queue ordered by a predicate on pairs of stored values
                     so higest priority element is deliverd first

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in article
<3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca>...

> It is hard for me to be more specfic about the STL without doing more

OK - I'll attempt to give some of it a go in due course. (I'm still learning it
too). Meanwhile perhaps one of the other OO geeks can jump in with observations
?

I think you said you have Will Price's book. Look up collections/dictoinaries.
It will partially cover some of your queries; I don't *think* Will covers it,
but by cloning existing classes to do something slightly different.........?.
Other than my reference to CharacterArray which is strings to objects or vice
versa, from memory, Will's book does use as an example the class Array.

Jimmy

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T03:22:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32b11$b5b6cb80$7893f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel> <3EDE80F1.7EE28720@shaw.ca>`

```
Yes I think he mentions them. My COBOL compiler at work now supports IBM's
New OO and one of the things I want to do is do Will Price's stuff if I
ever have the time.  There is so much cool stuff to learn that it is
overwhelming at times and hard to prioritize what to learn next.

James J. Gavan <jjgavan@shaw.ca> wrote in article
<3EDE80F1.7EE28720@shaw.ca>...
> 
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T03:58:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDEB6AA.5EBF83E9@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel> <3EDE80F1.7EE28720@shaw.ca> <01c32b11$b5b6cb80$7893f243@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> Yes I think he mentions them. My COBOL compiler at work now supports IBM's
> New OO and one of the things I want to do is do Will Price's stuff if I
> ever have the time.  There is so much cool stuff to learn that it is
> overwhelming at times and hard to prioritize what to learn next.
>

Will's stuff would be a good starting point for you. Just make changes for
'Repository' where necessary and they should work. Off the top of my head can't
remember exact syntax, but when using IBM Enterprise there's something like :-

    call Java do this returning object reference

It seems from the documentation that to store an object in IBM COBOL you have
to trigger off to Java to keep a tally on the object references. Probably
because it is Java which takes care of destroying the objects, (auto garbage
collection).

Other than that, Will's examples should work - he uses no 'funny' comps and his
accepts/displays are simple ANSI.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Farewell and OO Methods

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T04:37:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE00BF7.AE1FB8E@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel> <3EDE80F1.7EE28720@shaw.ca> <01c32b11$b5b6cb80$7893f243@chottel> <3EDEB6AA.5EBF83E9@shaw.ca> <01c32bba$83938360$ba8bf243@chottel>`

```


Charles Hottel wrote:

> Thanks.

Charles,

Last post on this or any other topic. A 'finalizer' so I don't leave you in the
lurch.

Got to thinking about you reading up on C - to my mind wrong language. Not
because there is anything wrong with C, but just that IBM Enterprise OO is
GEARED to OO via Java.

Take Will's early examples, (subject to ensuring they contain Repository syntax
- see his references to using the ISO 2000 directive - specific to Net Express
- so you can ignore the directive, but not the 'correct' source layout). You
should be home and dry until such time as he invokes support classes - for
collections/dictionaries/arrays. When you get to that point, then you need to
read up on Java lists. To get what Will is doing in N/E, from Java search out
the comparable classes/methods. (Java has different names to N/E - an N/E
dictionary is I believe a Java MAP).

So from IBM OO you will be invoking Java classes to create lists and return an
object reference to the List, then OO COBOL will have to invoke the the Java
object to access elements in the List.. It's a fairly big topic, but look at
what Will describes and I'm sure you will find Java matches. Look at how N/E
collections are indexed/keyed - then look for similar features in the Java
lists. Should answer those questions you were asking about C.
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Now to bailing out. I got into the Web purely as a route to the Micro Focus
Compuserve Forum - as you'll appreciate specific to M/F products. That folded.
Don't even remember how, but tripped over CLC - Ahhh the bigger, bigger wide
world of COBOL -  so I thought.

Culture shock - we *are* talking COBOL but oh *so* different when you talk
mainframe, Unix, or PCs. Then those 'funny' initials "J4" kept popping up. What
the hell is that ?  Of course I eventually found out, with some background
detail.

Contributors here, a hell of lot of nice people, knowledgeable and extremely
helpful to others in their particular areas. I must specifically single out
Bill Klein - the guy is a real brick.

The humorists - I'd have to really single out both Jerry and Michael M. They
both do a good job of providing help, but have a beautiful twist on humour.
Then another favourite of mine is our buddy Donald Tees from Toronto. I'm not
gong to go into the whole story but he was a 'town planner'; he christened a
few acres as 'West Butt Fuck Montana'. When queried, he replied "Well, I could
have written Assville, Ontario. But who has ever heard of it ?" <G>.

Robert - ahh well, he's bored me to death, but I do have a little compassion
for him - he appears to have had a fairly interesting career, and apart from
his gaffes, he ain't no dummy. But if you get away from 'attack mode' about
Robert, he's not the only one. Do some reflection on stuff that has been posted
- don't be so puppy-like and wide-eyed with awe. There are others that perceive
they have found Nirvana in their approach. You would have more success moving a
huge dung hill with a teaspoon, than any hope of getting them to be flexible in
their thoughts or approach. Given their inflexibility leads me to suspect their
judgement - consequently I consider them no longer trustworthy as a resource.

Where do I fit ? Yes, I've done things and am pleased with the results. But
every time I say to myself, "I wonder, I wonder, is there a more efficient
approach....?  And although I'm getting into old age, and may be stunned by a
suggestion, I have the humility to accept that there are alternatives.

Naturally we all mentally build a picture of the other members here in CLC. We
could be way off in our guessing. The only one I can be sure about is Bill,
because I met him in 2000 - "What you see is what you get".

Then there are others. Hmmm. Am I being unfair in my personal judgement ? Well,
that question was re-inforced by e-mail received referring to two gentlemen.
"Mr. X thinks he has 'arrived'". God, when I read that one, how cutting. Then
thought about it - the author was damn well right.

"Mr. Y is a pompous asshole" or "He's not fooling anybody by wrapping his
insults in a closing '<G>'". Or again the same gent, from another source, "He
treats everybody else in the News Group as though they were morons".

I don't find myself in disagreement with any of the originators of the above
messages.

Having gone through thousands of messages in CLC, what did I get  ?. (1) The
sorry story of the COBOL Standards, (2) from Thane use MOVE CORRESPONDING , and
(3) from Pete and others - TABLE USING SEARCH INDEX.  Nearly forgot, an early
one from Michael on building an array for alphabetical printing. Some useful
nuggets, but hardly compensation for reading all those messages.

All in all, I can't help but think I might just have been better off, up here
in the Great White North, in splendid isolation, figuring my way through Net
Express, plus of course going to M/F Answer Exchange for specific product
queries.

Perhaps I should have kept the above to a one-liner, like Volker's exit - which
was done in a very gentlemanly style. I was so tempted to say much more - but
leave it as above.

Bye, enjoy yourselves.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T12:22:26-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDF6E42.6010506@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel>`

```
Charles Hottel wrote:
 > It is hard for me to be more specfic about the STL without doing more
 >  in-depth reading. This will teach me to propose a discussion that I
 > am not yet ready for.

Seems to me you're doing pretty well so far.

Another way to think about templates is as fancy macros for creating
"helper" classes that implement common operations for your
application-specific data classes.

You have an app that deals with, say, cars, so you create a "car" class
that has all the information you need to know about a car, and methods
for doing the things you need to do with your car data.  So far so good.
Now you need to keep a bunch of cars in the application - a container.
You'll want to do certain things with this collection.  Maybe searching
is important.  Maybe sequential access is, but only in one direction -
your app never needs to back up.  Maybe it's important that insertion
and deletion are fast, or maybe they aren't used very often and it isn't.

Obviously different data types have different performance and feature
trade-offs, which are generally well-established; and good algorithms
for implementing those features are generally well-known; and if you're
lucky, someone's created a good implementation of those algorithms for
your platform.

The idea with templates is that you can say, give me a container for my
"car" class which has features X, Y, and Z.  The author of the template
doesn't know anything about your car class - it's generic.  It just
contains the logic to encapsulate your data class and perform various
useful common operations on it.

The compiler takes the template and plugs your class into it,
customizing it for your data, in effect.

Charles' list of what's in the STL provides various examples.  The STL
also requires that various types of containers have various performance
characteristics - a given container might be required to offer O(1)
insertion-time behavior, for example.  That gives application
programmers some guarantee that the behavior of their template-using
code will have predictable performance; if you choose to use a list,
it'll act pretty much the same on any implementation.

I haven't used OO COBOL, but certainly generic programming features are
useful in OO languages in general (and often come in handy in non-OO
languages as well, though they can be trickier there).  And the STL's
collection is a good place to start a conversation about what kind of
generics would be good for COBOL.

[An aside: Charles, I hate to complain, but when you post a short
response to a long article could you trim the quoted material?  You had
a one-line response to a long post from James Gavan a couple of posts
back in this thread which I had to skim in its entirety to see if you'd
put any comments in line.]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T18:27:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbqmdp$26p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel> <3EDF6E42.6010506@newsguy.com>`

```

On  5-Jun-2003, Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

> [An aside: Charles, I hate to complain, but when you post a short
> response to a long article could you trim the quoted material?  You had
> a one-line response to a long post from James Gavan a couple of posts
> back in this thread which I had to skim in its entirety to see if you'd
> put any comments in line.]

On those rare occasions where a long quote makes sense - top posting makes sense
as well.

Actually what makes sense if you want your post to be read - is to consider
other readers and how they are reading these messages and threads.  Do what is
likely to communicate the best.

And if communicating isn't your goal, there are alternatives to that as well.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T11:40:55+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee1239f$1_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel> <3EDF6E42.6010506@newsguy.com> <bbqmdp$26p$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
I guess I must be stupid, but I don't really understand what the objection
is to top posting.

If I have it right, this is a top posted message, right?

The response appears then, the details of the previous correspondence are
below.

I could place a summary of my position at the top, and then intersperse
specific arguments on specific points in the text below.

How is that a problem?

I do agree with Michael about snippage. We get far too much reiterated
verbiage in posts because people sometimes don't snip.

Pete.

Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> I guess I must be stupid, but I don't really understand what the objection

You have one or two lines of text, and 500 of history below.  Since some 
people say things like "Hey, great post!" before the context, then 
provide further new information within the quoted material, you scroll 
down more, and more, and more.... with no new info found.

Of course, Microsoft Outlook defaults to a "top-posted" message by 
default...

> I do agree with Michael about snippage. We get far too much reiterated
> verbiage in posts because people sometimes don't snip.

which is why I deleted the rest of this post...  :)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T20:35:30-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE67952.50905@newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel> <3EDF6E42.6010506@newsguy.com> <bbqmdp$26p$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ee1239f$1_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
 > I guess I must be stupid, but I don't really understand what the
 > objection is to top posting.

LX-i has already mentioned one reason - the short top-posted response
that makes you have to scroll through a bunch of included text just to
discover there's nothing else new.

Other reasons:

- It keeps material in chronological order.  Older text comes before
newer text.

- Interlaced replies, where a relevant passage is quoted and then the
new post's response appears, provide more specific context for each
piece of the response.  The response can be less verbose and less
material needs to be quoted.  Since trimming quoted material and
otherwise keeping posts shorter when possible is generally considered a
Good Thing on Usenet (to conserve storage and network bandwidth),
interlaced replies are traditionally the preferred form.

- Many news reading agents, dating back to rn, have a feature to skip
past quoted material in a post, by skipping lines until they come to one
that begins with a different character.  If posts use the traditional
">" at the beginning of each quoted line, a reader using one of those
agents can quickly find the new material - if it's not top-posted.  This
feature has reinforced the bottom-posting convention.

(By "traditional" or "traditionally" I'm referring to conventions that
date back as long as I've used Usenet - since 1990, roughly - and
probably date back at least as far as the Great Renaming, which created
Usenet as we know it today.  These traditions are generally acknowledged
by long-time Usenet users and documented in various Usenet style guides.)

The closest thing to a standard for Usenet messages is Henry Spencer's
"Son of 1036", a draft for a proposed RFC (IETF Request For Comments
document) to update RFC 1036, which defined the original Usenet message
format.  While Son of 1036 has not yet made it into the RFC standards
track, and may never do so, it's widely considered a good draft
treatment of current best practices and majority opinion among old
Usenet hands.  Son of 1036 does not specifically recommend against
top-posting, but its recommendations for quoting practice (4.3.2. "Body
Conventions") imply that reply text follow quoted text.

Son of 1036 is available at
<http://www.chemie.fu-berlin.de/outerspace/netnews/son-of-1036.html>,
if anyone's interested.

Of course, bottom-posting and interlaced replies are stylistic
conventions, no more.  I am in general in favor of letting authors
choose their own style; I dislike style guidelines for journals, for
example, since style is clearly part of expression.  In the case of
Usenet, though, those conventions are useful - particularly when people
are trying to read messages in high-volume groups like comp.lang.cobol.
That's why I try to ask people politely to trim quoted material and in
some cases not to top-post.  There are some Usenet readers who are
rather more militant, as you've probably noticed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horses and teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T14:03:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc7cqq$8nr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel> <3EDF6E42.6010506@newsguy.com> <bbqmdp$26p$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ee1239f$1_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3EE67952.50905@newsguy.com>`

```

On 10-Jun-2003, Michael Wojcik <mwojcik@newsguy.com> wrote:

> Of course, bottom-posting and interlaced replies are stylistic
> conventions, no more.  I am in general in favor of letting authors
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> rather more militant, as you've probably noticed.

Agreed.  The #1 consideration in communication is to consider the
reader/listener.   All of your listed reasons for avoiding top posting are valid
because they make it easier for the reader.   But as you indicate, these
shouldn't be set in stone.   For instance, on those rare occasions where it is
appropriate to include a long quote with a short reply, top posting is
appropriate.   The real considerate poster then mentions that he has no further
comments below, so that if we are familiar with the quote, we don't need to page
down.

An irritation of mine is threads where I can't see what is added without paging
down.   I don't know whether it will be some bantering, some insulting, or
something of substance until I page down.   I tend to skip most of these.

Repeat:   If you want to communicate, take the time to consider your audience
and how people read your messages.  (If you don't care to consider them, and you
don't care to edit your reply, then you can save a lot of work by not posting,
with much the same result).
```

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
 > [re templates]
 > Are there any papers on it that you would recommend?

Hmm.  I originally learned about C++ templates mostly through a series
of articles P J Plauger wrote for the _C/C++ User's Journal_ on the
Standard Template Library.  Most of the rest I learned through
documentation and trying them out, with frequent consultation of some of
the popular C++ references: Stroustrop's _The C++ Programming Language_
and Meyers' _Effective C++_ (in current editions) are the ones I use most.

The Plauger articles I'm thinking of were written in the mid-1990s, and
don't seem to be available online, though <http://www.cuj.com/> does
have a lot of articles dealing with the C++ template system and STL
available.  Of course, they're written for C++ programmers, but many
seem to be quite accessible even for someone new to C++ (at least as far
as the text goes - deciphering the code samples without prior knowledge
of C++ syntax would be quite a trick).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OO Methods - COBOL & Other languages - WAS: Flogging dead horsesand teaching pigs to fly

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T00:59:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32c90$103000c0$56c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<01c32984$12c491c0$5195f243@chottel> <3EDC3A14.F1CB1033@shaw.ca> <01c329f6$193fa180$64ccf943@chottel> <3EDD1173.376E668C@shaw.ca> <01c32aec$c33cdc60$7993f243@chottel> <3EDF6E42.6010506@newsguy.com>`

```
Thanks for that insightful response Michael.

Apparantly the STL has a usefulness that was not exactly intented. People
have found ways to use it that were not originally envisoned. Some recent
articles by Pete Becker in the C/C++ Journal have talked about "template
metaprogramming". These techniques allow the STL to be used as another
programming language within C++, but one that gets interpreted at compile
time. I am not certain that I really undestand this, but my idea of it is
that it is "sort of like passing templates (template parameters?) to your
templates.  You have actual template programs that produce more
complicted/powerful templates. These ideas seem to me to related to
recursion but I am not certain. Of course at the end of the compile you
have C++ code." Note that last quoted part is me quoting myself/my hunch
about what template metaprogramming means, not quoting anything Pete Becker
wrote.

For Pete Dashwood: Pete see the C/C++ Users Journal for Pete Becker's
column starting around Septemeber or October 2002. He lists a lot of
references at the end of his columns. They may be of limited usefulness
depending on your knowledge of C++. They say a little knowledge is a
dangerous thing and when it come to C++ I am very dangerous!
```

> Charles Hottel wrote:

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

------ 8<   8<   8<  ----- Snip.......

Jimmy - I agree COBOL doesn't need the same low level class libraries
as other OO languages.  However, that's not what Charles is talking
about here.  STL is the "Standard Template Library" - it's included
SOURCE CODE for common tasks that can be "morphed" into other things
using directives and such.   That's what he's looking for in COBOL and
I don't think it has anything REALLY to do with OO except that C++ an
OO language has it.
```

<a id="t-fb2074d6fc"></a>
## Thread: While the Alphabet Thread Rages... — 4 msgs

### While the Alphabet Thread Rages...

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T02:03:16-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-7FC69F.02031603062003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

I was scanning the alphabet thread and its various child, grand-child 
and great-grand-child threads and I started to wonder:

If negative logic is bad, why does Cobol require its use in many perform 
exit conditions?

In some cases it is hard, or unnatural to state the exit condition 
purely.  One is forced to prefix it with a NOT.

Why is there no perform/while?  I think it would be awfully nice to 
write something like:

   PERFORM Lurk-In-Comp-Compilers
     WHILE Alphabets-thread-is-alive

   
instead of:

   PERFORM Surf-Aimlessly
     UNTIL NOT Alphabets-thread-is-alive


Just thinking out loud...
```

#### ↳ Re: While the Alphabet Thread Rages...

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T14:38:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbibse$caf$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<joe_zitzelberger-7FC69F.02031603062003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

On  3-Jun-2003, Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

> If negative logic is bad, why does Cobol require its use in many perform
> exit conditions?
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>      UNTIL NOT Alphabets-thread-is-alive

Without arguing your basic "If negative logic is bad", I don't code quite the
same way.

PERFORM process-file
    Until file-eof

PERFORM walk-set
    Until ANY-ERROR-STATUS

Those don't seem to be negative logic to me.
```

#### ↳ Re: While the Alphabet Thread Rages...

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T20:35:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDD4CFC.6060609@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<joe_zitzelberger-7FC69F.02031603062003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger wrote:
> In some cases it is hard, or unnatural to state the exit condition 
> purely.  One is forced to prefix it with a NOT.
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>      UNTIL NOT Alphabets-thread-is-alive

Or

PERFORM Drum-Fingers
   UNTIL Alphabet-thread-is-dead

That's the thing I like about 88-levels - you can use words to define 
"not" conditions.  Then, even if your name has the word "not" in it, you 
don't have to use NOT logic to use it.  I routinely use

Perform Until WS-Time-to-Go

...for looping in interactive programs.  (Of course, I did actually code 
a "Go to" loop recently, but it was clear what it did...

001-Endless-Loop.
     Do stuff...
     Go to 001-Endless-Loop.
002-Stop-Run.
     Stop Run.

It is a background run that is designed to run whenever the mainframe is 
up, and all it does is send a transaction every 5 minutes (or whatever 
interval is set in the file).  Then, it goes to sleep for 15 seconds, 
wakes up, checks to see if it's time to fire again or if the interval 
has changed, then repeats.)

Having a COBOL programmer do VB can be good (their code is generally 
easier to read, as they're used to indenting and such), but it can also 
render some humorous constructs.  The Do loop in VB allows "While" or 
"Until" as the condition definition.  However, the VB programmers that 
trained me called it a "Do While" loop instead of just a "Do" loop.  So, 
you end up with "Do While Not" loops, which are actually "Do Until" loops.

I wouldn't be opposed to adding the WHILE clause as an acceptable 
modifier for the PERFORM verb though - so there's one vote "for" it.  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: While the Alphabet Thread Rages...

## Thread: RM-COBOL Index Structure — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### RM-COBOL Index Structure

- **From:** "Davide Bortolini" <davide@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T12:25:11+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pan.2003.06.03.10.25.11.485504@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it>`

```
Hi,
can anyone help me?
I would extract the entire database from a RM-COBOL Index Database but i
must know the RMFK format to extract data.
I've searched with google but I haven't fount too much infos.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: RM-COBOL Index Structure

- **From:** "Johan den Boer" <me@knoware.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T17:05:35+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vdpe9s1ft0usea@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<pan.2003.06.03.10.25.11.485504@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it>`

```
Hi,

In RM cobol you have a tool to display info about your index files. The tool
is

RMMAPINX  :  this produces a report describing the structure of an index
file created by RM/COBOL-85.
The tool display info about the keys, the position, length etc

It can be found on the utility disk of RM cobol.

Hope this helps

regards

Johan den Boer

email : johan@jdb.demon.nl

"Davide Bortolini" <davide@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it> schreef in bericht
news:pan.2003.06.03.10.25.11.485504@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it...
> Hi,
> can anyone help me?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: RM-COBOL Index Structure

- **From:** "Danny Maijer" <info@liveartists.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T17:34:32+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbif5k$evi$1@reader08.wxs.nl>`
- **References:** `<pan.2003.06.03.10.25.11.485504@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it>`

```
Hi Davide,

We have a long time experience in migration of data from RM/Cobol
applications to database environments. There is not a standard tool that
will provide you with a solution for your project. However, if you can get
hold of the file descriptors of the RM/Cobol applications we can provide you
with a toolset that generates conversion programs for your data files.

We can also deliver complete data migration. In other words, you deliver the
data files (.IDX files) and we return flat ASCII files to you for import in
a database.

If you are interested in our products and services, please feel free to
contact us at :

Maijer ICT Services
Attn. D. Maijer
Alkemaderschans 13
3432 CH  Nieuwegein
The Netherlands

Tel. +31 306579755
GSM +31 621242789
E-mail d.maijer@maijerict.net

- **From:** Scott Williamson <syw@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T16:39:00+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hvl6lBAUEM3+Ew$8@cdvdc.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<pan.2003.06.03.10.25.11.485504@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it>`

```
In article <pan.2003.06.03.10.25.11.485504@comune.mogliano-
veneto.tv.it>, Davide Bortolini <davide@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it>
writes
>Hi,
>can anyone help me?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Some years ago, I did some work on analysing the RM index file structure
and compression algorithms.  I was able to recover the original data
from any file (but NOT the internal record structure).
I wrote a brief paper on my findings and posted it to Wotsit's File
Formats (www.wotsit.org), and it should still be there.  
I've also written a number of programs to assist with recovering record
structures, and convert data to readable format.
```

#### ↳ Re: RM-COBOL Index Structure

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T17:48:22+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tpjpdvk8n0jekih3qdeaciagj64hsdtgno@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<pan.2003.06.03.10.25.11.485504@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it>`

```
On Tue, 03 Jun 2003 12:25:11 +0200, "Davide Bortolini"
<davide@comune.mogliano-veneto.tv.it> wrote:

>Hi,
>can anyone help me?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks

Davide,

The internal RM file structure is not available to the public, but
this does not mean that it is not possible to do what you require.

If you have access to the FD (File Description) of the files then my
company can easly do the required programs to extract the data and
create normal text files with whatever field separator you need for
importing to other programs (e.g ".csv").

If you do not have access to the FD's it is still possible but will
imply a lot of trial and error to try and figure out the record
layout. This last one requires direct access to each file.

If you require more information please contact us through either of
the following emails.

info at syssoft-int.com
frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com


Best regards


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

---

## Thread: create procedure — 41 msgs

### create procedure

- **From:** "Mik" <spudorato@gmx.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T15:57:03+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de>`

```
help me!!!!
why the esql compiler return me a syntax error on INTO ret_composto?????

CREATE PROCEDURE readb (curr_composto char(15), curr_nseq char(4))
               RETURNING
   char(15),
    char(4),
    char(15),
    float,
    char(2),
    float,
    char(10),
    float,
    date,
    date,
    date,
    char(1),
    char(15),
    float,
    char(3),
    float;


DEFINE ret_composto char(15);
DEFINE ret_seq char(4);
DEFINE ret_componente char(15);
DEFINE ret_qtaut float;
    DEFINE ret_umis char(2);
    DEFINE ret_scarti float;
    DEFINE ret_config char(10);
    DEFINE ret_coeff float;
    DEFINE ret_datainval date;
    DEFINE ret_datafinval date;
    DEFINE ret_dataagg date;
    DEFINE ret_stampa char(1);
    DEFINE ret_compderivato char(15);
    DEFINE ret_qtautder float;
    DEFINE ret_unmisder char(3);
    DEFINE ret_coeffder float;



    SELECT
    composto ,
    seq char,
    componente ,
    qtaut ,
    umis ,
    scarti ,
    config ,
    coeff ,
    datainval ,
    datafinval ,
    dataagg ,
    stampa ,
    compderivato ,
    qtautder ,
    unmisder ,
    coeffder
    INTO ret_composto ,
         ret_seq ,
         ret_componente ,
    ret_qtaut ,
    ret_umis ,
    ret_scarti ,
    ret_config ,
    ret_coeff ,
    ret_datainval ,
    ret_datafinval ,
    ret_dataagg ,
    ret_stampa ,
    ret_compderivato ,
    ret_qtautder ,
    ret_unmisder ,
    ret_coeffder
    from dbase where composto= curr_composto and nseq >= curr_nseq
    order by nseq;


                   RETURN
   ret_composto ,
    ret_seq ,
    ret_componente ,
    ret_qtaut ,
    ret_umis ,
    ret_scarti ,
    ret_config ,
    ret_coeff ,
    ret_datainval ,
    ret_datafinval ,
    ret_dataagg ,
    ret_stampa ,
    ret_compderivato ,
    ret_qtautder ,
    ret_unmisder ,
    ret_coeffder ;
    END PROCEDURE;

Tnk
MIk
```

and SELECT, but not SELECT ... INTO.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T21:27:47+03:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com>`

```
On Tue, 3 Jun 2003 12:33:38 -0500 "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com> wrote:

:>"Mik" <spudorato@gmx.net> wrote in message
:>news:bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de...

:>> help me!!!!
:>> why the esql compiler return me a syntax error on INTO
:>ret_composto?????

:>>     SELECT
:>...
:>>     INTO ret_composto ,

:>Perhaps it's a difference in dialect, but this is not
:>recognizable as SQL to me.  I'm familiar with INSERT ... INTO
:>and SELECT, but not SELECT ... INTO.

SELECT INTO is available when the cursor would have at most one row. 

It skips the steps of opening the cursor, doing the fetch and closing the
cursor.

It will fail if more than one row matches.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T21:59:43+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eddc368_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com>`

```

"Binyamin Dissen" <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com...
> On Tue, 3 Jun 2003 12:33:38 -0500 "Grinder" <grinder@no.spam.maam.com>
wrote:
>
> :>"Mik" <spudorato@gmx.net> wrote in message
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I believe there is some confusion here between singleton select (which can
only return one row) and normal select, which returns a dynaset. (multiple
rows).

SELECT INTO can be used where NO cursor is available. It doesn't skip the
steps of opening the cursor, doing the fetch, and closing the cursor,
because it is NOT a cursor operation. It is a singleton select.

FETCH INTO...  is used to load Host variables when a cursor is in operation.

Pete.
```

>I believe there is some confusion here between singleton select (which can
>only return one row) and normal select, which returns a dynaset. (multiple
>rows).

Little known fact: select can return more than one row, provided there are at
least as many host variables as the number of results.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T23:26:30-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote


> Little known fact: select can return more than one row, provided there are at
> least as many host variables as the number of results.

Well known fact: Robert Wagner is often wrong.

Not having tried all possible combinations _I_ can only talk about the
ones that I have used, as against Robert who talks for them all (hving
put no limits on his claim).

PostreSQL:

   SELECT list FROM table WHERE something INTO variables;

""We must take care that our SELECT returns only one row, because
additional rows will be silently discarded, only the first row is
assigned to variables listed.""

Fujitsu COBOL SQL:

""Use the SELECT statement if retrieving the data for only one row.""

Of course, RW, you could always try your 'but I meant xyz DBS only' or
even 'ignore the documentation - version x does something different
from what it says'.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T13:51:15-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >""Use the SELECT statement if retrieving the data for only one row.""
> >
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> processors who will process the request.

There is an ANSI standard for SQL. This is what defines what SQL is or
is not. Fujitsu speaks for what it does and for what the standard
requires.

That is, if you put a SELECT .. INTO .. in Fujitsu and more than one
row satisfies the conditions you will get an SQL error.

You started here some months ago making claims about what 'COBOL'
would do (eg pointers) when they were extensions, non-standard, and
were not part of Cobol.

Your claim for 'SELECT' may be for some specific extensions in some
versions of some databases available in to some preprocessors.  But it
ain't [ANSI] SQL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T14:53:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>requires.

Reading multiple rows does not violate the SQL standard. Technically, it is
using an 'implicit cursor' .. as does EVERY select .. into, even the ones
returning a single row. 

Some database implementors such as Postgre (see reference below) and Oracle 8i
have created Set Returning Functins (SRF) which are designed to work with
arrays. They return or update all rows under an implicit or explicit cursor.

What's happening here is Cobol is getting that functionality 'by accident'.
Think about it. The SQL processor doesn't know nor care what language is
calling. It doesn't have one set of rules for Cobol and another for PL/SQL. All
it sees is the SQL statement (or the handle of a previously PREPAREd SQL
statement now residing on the server) and a list of pointers to local variables.


http://techdocs.postgresql.org/guides/SetReturningFunctions

http://groups.google.com/groups?q=sql+select+multiple+rows&hl=en&lr=lang_en&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8&newwindow=1&safe=off&selm=CyM1I6.4Lr%40wtc35a.DaytonOH.NCR.COM&rnum=5

http://groups.google.com/groups?hl=en&lr=lang_en&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8&newwindow=1&safe=off&threadm=7gnr4i%2465k%241%40oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net&rnum=14&prev=/groups%3Fq%3Dsql%2Bimplicit%2Bcursor%26start%3D10%26hl%3Den%26lr%3Dlang_en%26ie%3DUTF-8%26oe%3DUTF-8%26newwindow%3D1%26safe%3Doff%26selm%3D7gnr4i%252465k%25241%2540oak.prod.itd.earthlink.net%26rnum%3D14

http://groups.google.com/groups?q=sql+implicit+cursor&start=10&hl=en&lr=lang_en&ie=UTF-8&oe=UTF-8&newwindow=1&safe=off&selm=3198c481.14310277%40dcsun4&rnum=16

>That is, if you put a SELECT .. INTO .. in Fujitsu and more than one
>row satisfies the conditions you will get an SQL error.

Not necessarily. It depends on who wrote the SQL processor. 

>You started here some months ago making claims about what 'COBOL'
>would do (eg pointers) when they were extensions, non-standard, and
>were not part of Cobol.

How is this relevent? 

In that case I didn't claim they were standard Cobol. We all know pointers were
not in the 85 standard. I was pointing out that Fujitsu accepts the same pointer
syntax as IBM and Micro Focus. 

>Your claim for 'SELECT' may be for some specific extensions in some
>versions of some databases available in to some preprocessors.  But it
>ain't [ANSI] SQL.

You should write to Postgre and Oracle. They'll thank you for pointing out their
error.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306081208.4460337d@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> Not necessarily. It depends on who wrote the SQL processor. 
 
> You should write to Postgre and Oracle. They'll thank you for pointing out
> their error.

It is not an error to have an extension. It _is_ an error to claim (as
you did) that an extension in some systems in some versions can be
used regardless of which version is being used.

Your claim was: "_Fact:_ select _CAN_ return more than one row", the
only proviso was adding more variables.

Now you have reduced that with "it depends on who wrote the SQL
preprocessor" and "it depends on having particular DBs" and "it
depends on having specific versions".

In fact with Fujitsu I don't use _any_ preprocessor, Fujitsu processes
the SQL during compile and accesses whatever database using ODBC.  So
this _also_ depends on having ODBC that supports it too.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T00:01:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee3a230.214426368@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306081208.4460337d@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>used regardless of which version is being used.

It is NOT an extension to SQL. Implicit cursor is in the standard. 

>Your claim was: "_Fact:_ select _CAN_ return more than one row", the
>only proviso was adding more variables.

Yes. It works on most modern databases. 

>Now you have reduced that with "it depends on who wrote the SQL
>preprocessor" and "it depends on having particular DBs" and "it
>depends on having specific versions".

Not the preprocessor, the execution time processor. 

>In fact with Fujitsu I don't use _any_ preprocessor, Fujitsu processes
>the SQL during compile and accesses whatever database using ODBC.  So
>this _also_ depends on having ODBC that supports it too.

Cobol compilers and preprocessors don't 'process' SQL at compile time. They
don't compile it. They syntax check it and store it away somewhere. IBM binds it
into the plan, like a stored procedure; most others simply store it in the
program as a literal. At execution time, they feed SQL source, just as you wrote
it minus host variables, to the execution time processor (perhaps via ODBC).  

The first time the database server sees your SQL, he compiles it some internal
code which he caches. Subsequent calls with the same SQL source use the cached
code rather than recompiling every time. 

ODBC support for array fetches is in the Microsoft ODBC spec 3.x and above.
Assuming your database is relational and running multi-tier (ODBC term for 'on
another machine'), it's a non-issue because the client-side ODBC is just a relay
who passes your SQL source on the database's execution time processor. Query
resolution intelligence resides on the server, not in the client's ODBC. And
ODBC doesn't know nor care whether it's being called by Cobol or some gee-whiz
OO language. If the server returns six fields and you provided six host variable
pointers, ODBC is happy. 

Database whizkids get performance by sucking in arrays of data with one
transaction rather than saying fetch, fetch, fetch. DB2 (UDB) has supported it
for years. See SQLFetchScroll here:

http://www-3.ibm.com/cgi-bin/db2www/data/db2/udb/winos2unix/support/document.d2w/report?fn=db2l053.htm#Header_87
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** jarl@mimer.com (Jarl Hermansson)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T14:10:45-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2aaa8682.0306081310.50c40c5b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net>...
> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
> 
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> returning a single row. 

The ISO/ANSI SQL Standard (SQL-99) doesn't describe anything like
that. The result set of a 'SELECT INTO' must consist of exactly one
row.

--- Start SQL-99
14.5 <select statement: single row>
Function: Retrieve values from a specified row of a table.
Format:
<select statement: single row> ::=
    SELECT [ <set quantifier> ] <select list>
      INTO <select target list>
        <table expression>

<select target list> ::=
    <target specification> [ { <comma> <target specification> }... ]

[---snip---]

1) Let Q be the result of <query specification> S.
2)a) If the cardinality of Q is greater than 1 (one), then an
exception condition is raised: cardinality violation. It is
implementation-dependent whether or not SQL-data values are assigned
to the targets identified by the <select target list>.
b) If Q is empty, then no SQL-data values are assigned to any targets
identified by the <select target list>, and a completion condition is
raised: no data.
c) Otherwise, values in the row of Q are assigned to their
corresponding targets.
--- END SQL-99

As you can see above, there's no way to retrieve more than one row
without using a cursor.

The SQL/PSM standard (i.e 'stored procedures') doesn't specify any way
for procedures to return a result-set. All data is returned using
parameters.

An SQL/PSM function returns one single value.


Regards,
Jarl
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T00:01:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee3cb81.4406183@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net> <2aaa8682.0306081310.50c40c5b@posting.google.com>`

```
The statement description sounds left over from the days of single-tier.
I don't dispute the standard says that, but it makes no sense in today's
environment. A server cannot put data INTO a client's memory. The INTO 
clause is removed before the SELECT is sent to the server. The server sends the
result back through the network. It doesn't know whether the requestor is a
COBOL program with an INTO, or another database server doing a join.
 
Those selects are returning more than one row. Granted, they don't use INTO but
the servers don't know that. The servers return entire tables and let the caller
figure out what to do with the data. 

unload to "file" select * from table1@serverx

select col1, col2, 'Y' as col3
  from table1@server1 into temp xxxx
insert into table2@server2 (col1, col2, col3)
  select * from xxxx


Did you read the reference on Set Returning Functions? How is that possible
under the ANSI standard?

http://techdocs.postgresql.org/guides/SetReturningFunctions

Why does ODBC support array fetch if SQL can't do it?

and
