_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T11:22:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee5a3d7.125341542@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net> <2aaa8682.0306081310.50c40c5b@posting.google.com> <3ee3cb81.4406183@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306091214.54f525f9@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>'fetch all'.

SQL doesn't have one set of rules for Python and another for COBOL. If Python
can 'fetch all', so can COBOL .. unless the COBOL compiler front-ends calls with
its own library. 

All fetches are through a cursor. If you don't specify one explicitly, as in the
case of a simple SELECT, you get an implicit cursor. 

Fetch sees only a list of pointers to data areas. It cannot see source code; it
does not know whether you're fetching into an array or discrete fields.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T14:03:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306101303.7ac2c83e@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net> <2aaa8682.0306081310.50c40c5b@posting.google.com> <3ee3cb81.4406183@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306091214.54f525f9@posting.google.com> <3ee5a3d7.125341542@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> >FETCH can do INTO multiple rows. I can do this in Python. But there is
> >no difficulty with FETCH because the _explicit_ cursor can be used
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> with its own library. 

Exactly, but this is the 'rule' for FETCH not SELECT .. INTO.

> All fetches are through a cursor. If you don't specify one explicitly, as in
> the case of a simple SELECT, you get an implicit cursor. 

Do you ?  Where does it say this ?  Or is this just in some versions
of some embedded preprocessors ?

> Fetch sees only a list of pointers to data areas. It cannot see source code;
> it does not know whether you're fetching into an array or discrete fields.

But then the programmer can enquire about how many results there are
in the data set via the cursor and can arrange beforehand enough space
to do a fetchall into.  If enough space is not arranged then the
programmer can restrict how many are fetched with each call.

It would be a very foolish programmer who would do a FETCH ALL without
checking how many that is to be.

As you say, the SQL cannot know whether fetching a large number of
records into a pointer will trash the program.  With a cursor and
using FETCH the programmer can ensure this doesn't happen.

Doing a SELECT .. INTO there is no mechanism to prevent the program
being trashed - except by ensuring that only one row is returned and
giving an error for the programmer to deal with in other cases.

Now it may be that certian systems and versions can do non-standard
multiple SELECT .. INTO, but your "it does not know ... array or
discrete fields" must be wrong in those cases.

Your original assertion was:

""Little known fact: select can return more than one row, provided
there are at
least as many host variables as the number of results.""

It seems that you have contradicted yourself with:

> Fetch sees only a list of pointers to data areas. It cannot see source code;
> it does not know whether you're fetching into an array or discrete fields.

It seems that if your 'little known fact' was true then SELECT INTO
can trash your program.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** jarl@mimer.com (Jarl Hermansson)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T06:19:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2aaa8682.0306100519.3faa2cb7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net> <2aaa8682.0306081310.50c40c5b@posting.google.com> <3ee3cb81.4406183@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote in message news:<3ee3cb81.4406183@news.optonline.net>...
 
> select col1, col2, 'Y' as col3
>   from table1@server1 into temp xxxx
> insert into table2@server2 (col1, col2, col3)
>   select * from xxxx

Now I see what you mean with SELECT ... INTO. That's quite different
from the SQL standard's <select statement: single row>.

What you are talking about is not a part of the SQL standard.

If xxxx in you statement stands for some kind of temporary table, your
statement is quite similar to the SQL standard's:

INSERT INTO xxxx SELECT col1,col2 FROM table1;

Though the current SQL standard (SQL-99) doesn't contain temporary
tables. They are introduced in SQL-2003.


> Did you read the reference on Set Returning Functions? How is that possible
> under the ANSI standard?
> 
> http://techdocs.postgresql.org/guides/SetReturningFunctions

I took a glance at it. Those Set Returning Functions is not a part of
the SQL/PSM standard. I agree they are quite useful, and other vendors
have similar implementations, like Mimer SQL's result-set procedures.


> Why does ODBC support array fetch if SQL can't do it?

ODBC is an interface, just like ADO, JDBC and embedded SQL.

ODBC itself doesn't know much about SQL. It just passes queries (and
data) from the application to the server and retreives data from the
server.

When reading a multi-row result-set (typically generated by a SELECT
statement), ODBC has support for reading several rows for each FETCH
(i.e. array fetch). This means that data for several rows in the
result-set are copied from the network buffer to some kind of data
area in the application.

However, array fetch doesn't guarantee that several rows in the
result-set are grouped together into one network package. How this is
done is not specified by ODBC (or SQL).

Its also possible that several rows in the result-set are sent in the
same network package, even though array fetch isn't specified. In this
case one FETCH must be performed for each row in the result-set.


/Jarl
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T19:27:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306101827.783b2010@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net> <2aaa8682.0306081310.50c40c5b@posting.google.com> <3ee3cb81.4406183@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

> The statement description sounds left over from the days of single-tier.

What has 'single-tier' to do with it ?    The implication is that the
database code would be compiled into the application, when was this
done with serious SQL  DBMS ?

> I don't dispute the standard says that, but it makes no sense in today's
> environment. A server cannot put data INTO a client's memory. The INTO 
> clause is removed before the SELECT is sent to the server. The server sends the
> result back through the network. It doesn't know whether the requestor is a
> COBOL program with an INTO, or another database server doing a join.

There is an interface library linked into the program, this controls
what is sent to the server and what is put back into the program. 
Just because this library and the server are separated by a network
connection does not affect how the library deals with the application
code.

> Those selects are returning more than one row. Granted, they don't use INTO but
> the servers don't know that. The servers return entire tables and let the caller
> figure out what to do with the data. 

I doubt that the SELECT "returns entire tables".  The server holds the
SELECT result and allows the application to FETCH data as required. 
It seems to me that a SELECT INTO would have the server return one row
or an error if more.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T16:40:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee754fe.10400846@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net> <2aaa8682.0306081310.50c40c5b@posting.google.com> <3ee3cb81.4406183@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306101827.783b2010@posting.google.com>`

```
riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

>robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>done with serious SQL  DBMS ?

I did not imply the database was linked in, you inferred that. I did imply that
the whole SQL statement, including FROM followed by a list of pointers, was
passed intact to the SQL engine. Then the SQL engine plopped data directly into
the caller's memory, following the rules of SQL, specifically the one quoted
here -- one row on a singleton select. 

>> I don't dispute the standard says that, but it makes no sense in today's
>> environment. A server cannot put data INTO a client's memory. The INTO 
>> clause is removed before the SELECT is sent to the server. The server sends
the
>> result back through the network. It doesn't know whether the requestor is a
>> COBOL program with an INTO, or another database server doing a join.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>code.

Yes it does. Under single-tier, the interface library was just a relay. Under
multi-tier, the interface must process the INTO portion of the statement. 

>> Those selects are returning more than one row. Granted, they don't use INTO
but
>> the servers don't know that. The servers return entire tables and let the
caller
>> figure out what to do with the data. 
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>or an error if more.

Suppose you run a utility which puts up a command line. You type SELECT * from
table, and see the entire table on your screen. Do you think the utility
analyzed the statement, set up a cursor, and then did a bunch of fetches? No, it
simply sent the SELECT to SQL and displayed what came back in response. When a
COBOL program issues a SELECT, the same thing happens -- the entire database
comes back through the pipe. The interface can store the data into host
variables, if there are enough of them, or it can return a SQL error.
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote

database server from the application.

> the whole SQL statement, including FROM followed by a list of pointers, was
> passed intact to the SQL engine.

> >> The INTO clause is removed before the SELECT is sent to the server. 

> Then the SQL engine plopped data directly into the caller's memory, 

That's not what you said here:
 
> >> A server cannot put data INTO a client's memory. 

> Yes it does. Under single-tier, the interface library was just a relay. Under

for these ? Which brands and versions did it one way and which the
other ?  Or is this _ALL_ brands and versions _ALWAYS_ worked in these
ways and I just missed the 'single tier' option.

>  caller
> >> figure out what to do with the data.

> COBOL program issues a SELECT, the same thing happens -- the entire database
> comes back through the pipe.

> The interface can store the data into host
> variables, if there are enough of them, or it can return a SQL error.

to the area then please do tell me how it knows 'there are enough' ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T14:02:28-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306121302.73549497@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <3ee328cc.183345260@news.optonline.net> <2aaa8682.0306081310.50c40c5b@posting.google.com> <3ee3cb81.4406183@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306101827.783b2010@posting.google.com> <3ee754fe.10400846@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306111450.1b673336@posting.google.com> <3ee856df.76427564@news.optonline.net>`

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> >> I did not imply the database was linked in, you inferred that. 
> 
> Single-tier means the database code can address the application's memory.

And on protected memory systems and (such as Unix), how does it do
that unless it is 'linked in' ?

You're just making this up.

> I said that about multi-tier ..

Is this a variety of 'I meant ..' ?

> ..and that about single-tier.

> You are juxtaposing statements taken out of context to make it appear that I'm
> contradiciting myself. 

Different database systems have different ways of working and this may
change in different versions and with different options.

You make, once again, vast sweeping generalisations about what every
DMBS does when it is used in 'single-tier' or 'multi-tier' as if all
DBMS's work exactly the same way except they all changed to all
working another way when the application and the server were put on
different machines.

Now it _may_ be that some DBMS somewhere, in some versions, running on
non-protected memory, did just dump rows into the application memory. 
Some DBMS's indeed were able to be linked into an application to
produce a single executable - here is another recent one: picoSQL.

This linking of course forced single-tier.

However most sunstantial DBMS systems ran as a server or deamon with
just an interface library required to access the server.  If this
interface used a pipe or queue then it restricted the usage to single
tier, if it used the TCP/IP socket then it could be multi-tier.

The way the library interfaced to the application was (or could be)
independent of how it communicated to the server.

Different DBMS's did these in different ways, yet you want to have
them all work one way or all another way: simplistic, superficial,
arbitrary, uninformed, clueless.  How else could your arguments be
described ?

> A 'discussion' under these conditions will not produce
> information useful to others.

Well so far you haven't done so.

Your 'SELECT INTO' can get more than one row was:

   Untrue for most DBMSs and most versions (given there are >40).
   Unqualified as to whether it was usable by anyone.
   Unexplained how one stopped this corrupting the program.
   Unexplained about _why_ it is useful.
```

```
On Tue, 10 Jun 2003 03:24:42 -0400 Joe Zitzelberger
<joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

:>In article <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com>,
:> riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:

:>> robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 
 
:>> > >""Use the SELECT statement if retrieving the data for only one row.""

:>> > >Of course, RW, you could always try your 'but I meant xyz DBS only' or
:>> > >even 'ignore the documentation - version x does something different
:>> > >from what it says'.
 
:>> > I don't know about Postre. There is no way Fujitsu can speak for all the SQL
:>> > processors who will process the request.
 
:>> There is an ANSI standard for SQL. This is what defines what SQL is or
:>> is not. Fujitsu speaks for what it does and for what the standard
:>> requires.
 
:>> That is, if you put a SELECT .. INTO .. in Fujitsu and more than one
:>> row satisfies the conditions you will get an SQL error.
 
:>> You started here some months ago making claims about what 'COBOL'
:>> would do (eg pointers) when they were extensions, non-standard, and
:>> were not part of Cobol.
 
:>> Your claim for 'SELECT' may be for some specific extensions in some
:>> versions of some databases available in to some preprocessors.  But it
:>> ain't [ANSI] SQL.

:>If "SELECT" is only able to return one row, why does the "SELECT 
:>DISTINCT" form, which says specifically return one and only one row, 
:>exist?

SELECT INTO, not just select.

DISTINCT is a qualifier that can be used on a SELECT INTO as well.

And, finally, SELECT DISTINCT eliminates duplicates. It will return more than
one row if the columns selected are not duplicates.

:>DB2 provides a SQL state (23xxx? 42xxx? can't remember now) that 
:>qualifies an attempted select with a result set containing more than a 
:>single row result.

:>I believe distinct is part of the ANSI standard SQL, but I need to check 
:>that.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3ee5e5af_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-5B2D04.03244110062003@corp.supernews.com>`

```

"Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:joe_zitzelberger-5B2D04.03244110062003@corp.supernews.com...
> In article <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com>,
>  riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard) wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Joe, this just shows how loony some of these threads get.

No-one is suggesting that SELECT can only return one row. Look at the post I
made some days ago in this thread and you'll see I mentioned that SELECT
returns a group of rows (dynaset).

The point is that a SINGLETON SELECT returns one row. Robert mentioned that
it was a well kept secret that a singleton select will return more rows if
you provide the host variables to accommodate it, and there ARE more rows
qualified by the Query.

That is nonsense although I didn't want to say so at the time, because if it
returns more than one row, then by definition it cannot be a Singleton
select.

There is also a lot of rubbish being talked about all access being by
cursor. It is if you define one and subsequently fetch it. It ISN'T if you
do a singleton select. I don't accept that this uses an "implicit cursor".
SELECT always fetches a dynaset. If you index into the dynaset you have a
cursor. If the dynaset contains one row, you have a singleton select and no
sniff of a cursor.

SELECT DISTINCT ensures there are no duplicated rows in the dynaset, not
that there will only be one row in it.


> DB2 provides a SQL state (23xxx? 42xxx? can't remember now) that
> qualifies an attempted select with a result set containing more than a
> single row result.
>
Yes, it does. 23000 is about referential integrity if memory serves me (I
haven't checked it) but there is a code that tells you your alleged
singleton select returned more than one row. (This is an error. On your
part, not the system's...)

> I believe distinct is part of the ANSI standard SQL, but I need to check
> that.

Pete.
```

```
robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote 

> That was in the Good Old Days, when systems were single-tier i.e. the database
> resided on the same (only) machine. 

While it may be that there was only one machine, that was not
necessarily a result of any limitation in how database systems worked.
Most DBMSs that I worked with were server based and the interface was
trivially different where that sent messages over the network rather
than between local programs.

Certainly by the time that SQL was introduced generally the DBMSs were
networked.

The 'Good Old Days' were IDMS and IMS.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T22:19:18+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee85406_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-5B2D04.03244110062003@corp.supernews.com> <3ee5e5af_6@news.athenanews.com> <3ee75b3e.12000700@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ee75b3e.12000700@news.optonline.net...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
> >There is also a lot of rubbish being talked about all access being by
> >cursor. It is if you define one and subsequently fetch it. It ISN'T if
you
> >do a singleton select. I don't accept that this uses an "implicit
cursor".
> >SELECT always fetches a dynaset. If you index into the dynaset you have a
> >cursor. If the dynaset contains one row, you have a singleton select and
no
> >sniff of a cursor.
>
> You didn't read the references, which explain implicit cursors.
>
My point was that the "cursor" happens AFTER the data has been retrieved. It
is simply a pointer into a dynaset, maintained by the RDB software.

I didn't read the references because I really don't care...<G> I have been
using relational databases since 1983 when the first IBM courses in
(Reading) England became available for DB2.

As far as using ESQL in COBOL goes I have no problems at all. (I understand
enough Relational set theory to be able to design and build databases that
work pretty effectively, too...)

But, the last place I worked woke me up to the REAL use of RDB. ESQL from
COBOL is primitive compared to what the new breed are doing with SQL Server,
ORACLE and such. I had a team of programmers who had never seen COBOL, but
used VB, Perl, and some C.  Very little coding was actually done in these
languages because everything was "quick build" with visual environments.
However, most of the real stuff was done with stored procedures in SQL
Server, and XML. I looked through some of these procedures and was blown
away.

It made me realise how stupid threads like this are.

It is like jungle people fighting tribal battles about how best to make
fire, while the evolutionally favoured have left the jungle and are using
lasers and arc welders...

> >> DB2 provides a SQL state (23xxx? 42xxx? can't remember now) that
> >> qualifies an attempted select with a result set containing more than a
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> That was in the Good Old Days, when systems were single-tier i.e. the
database
> resided on the same (only) machine. The standards language quoted here was
> undoubtedly left over from V1.0, when that was the normal case.
>

Maybe. I wouldn't have thought the number of tiers or whether it was
accessing a distributed database would have any bearing on this, but I can't
remember the last time I received a non-zero SQLSTATE so I don't know a lot
about error conditions <G>. This isn't because I am smart; it is because I
realise my limitations and act accordingly. I use what I know works. I don't
want to be a laser surgeon or arc welder, I'm happy by the old campfire...

> We're programming databases with precepts developed 30 years ago.
>

You speak for yourself... Besides, 30 years ago we were all using IMS...

I at least have moved on from rubbing sticks together to using a gas
lighter.... But I'm still a long way from central heating...(at least I know
it exists...<G>)

Pete.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: create procedure

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-13T00:31:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee872fd_7@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bbi9mn$9qgnm$1@ID-165476.news.dfncis.de> <bbim5n02ei9@enews1.newsguy.com> <92qpdv44uk2k7uca147mjv8082k89ht5ri@4ax.com> <3eddc62a_6@news.athenanews.com> <3edf05c6.562171320@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306052226.572ac44c@posting.google.com> <3ee074c5.6159532@news.optonline.net> <217e491a.0306071251.4f1f3444@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-5B2D04.03244110062003@corp.supernews.com> <3ee5e5af_6@news.athenanews.com> <3ee75b3e.12000700@news.optonline.net> <3ee85406_5@news.athenanews.com> <A%ZFa.3034$87.1850492@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```

"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
news:A%ZFa.3034> >
> > It made me realise how stupid threads like this are.
>
> .. and why any team of programmers should always include some younger
folks
> who *don''t* already know "how to do that" and "what can't be done?"
>

Yes, absolutely.

Actually, after I posted that I read it again and I guess I was a bit hard
on us <G>.

It's just that these people were SO bright, loved their work, and, because
they didn't come through the same "mould" that I did, they saw things that I
couldn't. We had some fantastic debates about many things. They even looked
at how I would achieve the same result in COBOL and weren't too scathing
(although I think there may have been some politeness due to my grey hairs
and the fact that I signed their timesheets <G>). Certainly, they recognized
that underneath I was a programmer and had a real grasp on their problems
and could even help on occasion.

The experience was invaluable and it taught me so much. There were occasions
when they saw my arguments for certain approaches and adopted them.
Certainly, they respected my right to manage and I had no trouble with them.
I guess the thing I found hardest to swallow was that they could produce
results in minutes that would take days in COBOL.

Of course, everything they do is component based. They were amazed when they
found I was writing components in COBOL and we tried some of mine with some
of theirs just to see if it worked (it did).

For people who are used to COBOL shops it is hard to envisage.

You are 100% on the button Michael, that having a good mix of people,
including those who will question, is the best way to build a successful
development team. The next step is then to create an environment where it is
safe to question and even get it wrong occasionally. But it is also
important to realise that the whole way we do things is changing. Even these
guys have a limited future. It is certainly longer than a career COBOL
programmer, but not by more than a few years. I don't believe any of them
will be doing in 20 years what they are doing now.

Pete.
```

```
Hi,


We are looking for companies that have been using COBOL for at least ten
years, and have more than 100.000 lines of COBOL code.


We would like to have some references in this target group as we have
developed an automatic COBOL restructuring service, which reduces
maintenance costs by as much as 40%. (http://www.raincode.com/online)


As we would be very interested in the feedback of such companies on our
service, we are willing to grant them a free restructuring for 3 of their
oldest or most complex programs, so they can freely try out our service and
gain the substantial benefits as a result of this.


Please contact us if you are interested.


Debbie
sales@raincode.com
```

<a id="t-96df79b0b7"></a>
## Thread: COMP data types with GCOS8 — 10 msgs

### COMP data types with GCOS8

- **From:** paul.thomas@cgey.com (Thomas)
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T09:28:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367276c1.0306030828.41cd9be4@posting.google.com>`

```
Hello,
I work on a migration from COBOL BULL GCOS8 to IBM COBOL for z/OS
(MVS).
Could some Bull specialist tell me what are data types (internal
representation in memory) for following USAGE and PICTURE :
  S9(09) COMP
  COMP-2

On MVS 
  S9(09) COMP   is  
       data type : signed integer,
       internal representation :  Fullword Binary (32 bits),
       values : from ï¿½2 147 483 648 to +2 147 483 647.
  COMP-2 is 
       data type : real double precision (floating point),
       internal representation : 32 bits,
                IBM specific with mantissa and exponent,
       values : from ï¿½7.2 * 10**75 to +7.2 * 10**75.
  
Is it the same thing on DPS8 ?

Thanks
```

```
I found their web site at http://www.bull.com.  However, they referenced
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP data types with GCOS8

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T06:33:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDDD922.8080500@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<367276c1.0306030828.41cd9be4@posting.google.com> <3EDD5B44.5020003@Knology.net>`

```
LX-i wrote:
> Thomas wrote:
>  > Hello,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> reference manual should have the allocation listed in it.

Thomas,

Someone e-mailed me the definitions of these (thanks!), but I didn't see 
your name on the e-mail.  Here 'tis...

=-=-=-=-=

Hi Daniel,
I hope that this helps:

COMPUTATIONAL-2

Specifies a four-byte binary integer. Bits 1-8 of four successive bytes 
are treated as a contiguous 32-bit binary item. Bit 0 of each byte is 
ignored.

examples:

Decimal -> Octal

1234   ->  000 000 002 322

-1234   ->   377 377 373 056

=-=-=-=-=

 From this, taking what I know about COMP in the environment with which 
I'm most familiar, I believe you could infer that Pic S9(09) COMP would 
be 4 characters (which, I believe, is two words - 32 bits).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP data types with GCOS8

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T06:37:12-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDDD9E8.6010201@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<367276c1.0306030828.41cd9be4@posting.google.com> <3EDD5B44.5020003@Knology.net>`

```
Take 2...  (sent the previous post prematurely...)

More of the e-mail follows...

=-=-=-=-=

S9(09) COMPUTATIONAL

The item is byte-aligned (occupying an integral number of bytes) and 
right-justified. If the length of the item including the sign is odd, 
the COBOL compiler generates a halfbyte filler in the leading byte. If 
the item is signed, a minus (trailing) sign is represented by octal 15, 
a plus sign by octal 13.

examples:

COMPUTATIONAL  PICTURE   S9(5)

1234   ->   001 043 113

-1234   ->  001 043 115
COMPUTATIONAL  PICTURE   S9(6)

01234   ->  000 001 043 113

-1234   ->   000 001 043 115

=-=-=-=-=
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COMP data types with GCOS8

- **From:** paul.thomas@cgey.com (Thomas)
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T01:50:42-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367276c1.0306050050.4269bff8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<367276c1.0306030828.41cd9be4@posting.google.com> <3EDD5B44.5020003@Knology.net>`

```
Thanks, 
Your answer confirms that COMP-2 on GCOS8 is not the same thing that COMP-2 on 
IBM MVS.
On MVS we use hexadecimal representation,
with the same example :
 
  S9(9) COMP on MVS :
    1234=X'000004D2' 
   -1234=X'FFFFFB2E'
 
  COMP-2 on MVS :
     1234=X'434D200000000000' 
    -1234=X'C34D200000000000' 

N.B. : A little error in my initial question : 
COMP-2 on IBM MVS use 64 bits for internal representation.

Thomas
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP data types with GCOS8

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T10:45:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kbFDa.565$87.169742@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<367276c1.0306030828.41cd9be4@posting.google.com> <3EDD5B44.5020003@Knology.net> <367276c1.0306050050.4269bff8@posting.google.com>`

```
"Thomas" <paul.thomas@cgey.com> wrote in message
news:367276c1.0306050050.4269bff8@posting.google.com...
> Thanks,
> Your answer confirms that COMP-2 on GCOS8 is not the same thing that
COMP-2 on
> IBM MVS.
> On MVS we use hexadecimal representation,

On MVS COMP-1 and COMP-2 representations differ depending on the setting of
the FLOAT directive, which may be set to FLOAT(HEX) or FLOAT(IEEE). (and I
think FLOAT(NATIVE) is equivalent to FLOAT(HEX)).

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COMP data types with GCOS8

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** paul.thomas@cgey.com (Thomas)
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T08:10:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<367276c1.0306050710.77a670fb@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<367276c1.0306030828.41cd9be4@posting.google.com> <3EDD5B44.5020003@Knology.net> <367276c1.0306050050.4269bff8@posting.google.com> <kbFDa.565$87.169742@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
"Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message news:<kbFDa.565$87.169742@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>...
> On MVS COMP-1 and COMP-2 representations differ depending on the setting of
> the FLOAT directive, which may be set to FLOAT(HEX) or FLOAT(IEEE). (and I
> think FLOAT(NATIVE) is equivalent to FLOAT(HEX)).
> 
> MCM

Exact, but it depends of the compiler version (Cobol for OS/390 & VM
or COBOL for z/OS & VM), with COBOL for OS/390 & VM Version 2 Release
1, Program Number 5648-A25, the FLOAT compiler options does not
exists, and under OS/390 and VM, the NATIVE phrase of the USAGE clause
is treated as a comment.


The answer to my initial question is :

COBOL Data description 
BULL                nearest equivalent
GCOS8               in COBOL for OS/390     data type
----------------------------------------------------------
PIC S9(n) COMP      PIC S9(n) COMP-3        packed decimal
COMP-2              PIC S9(9) COMP          4 bytes binary integer
COMP-12             COMP-2             double precision floating point
```

<a id="t-03523d8168"></a>
## Thread: Mail and Collaboration Data Objects CDO — 5 msgs

### Mail and Collaboration Data Objects CDO

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T12:01:09+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3edd3a97_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Inspired by Howard Hinnman's recent sample of .NET code to use SMTPMail, I
started thinking about what those of us who DON'T have .NET can do about
handling e-mail from COBOL programs.

Howard's sample utilises a .NET Class and it is pretty straightforward to
simply invoke methods of this Class (as Howard showed so elegantly.)

However, I am currently doing some stuff where I need to e-mail a
confirmation to a customer who has made a Web Booking. As this code will be
running from a portal, there are a number of different Sellers who my code
must represent.

I need to do the following:

1. Ask the customer whether he will accept HTML or requires Plain Text.

2. If he will accept HTML, send him a nicely formatted document with the
appropriate seller's letterhead, containing the details of his transaction
with us. This should look just like a letter he might receive through the
mail.It will be produced  on-the-fly by a mail-merge in Word or a similar
process (I have a "home made" component that I use for building dynamic HTML
that would do this job with a lot less overhead; I'll decide after running
some tests...)

If he opts for plain text, dynamically generate a suitable message and send
it to him.

I started getting into how mail works and discovered a few things of
interest...

1. There are some limitations on the SMTPMail Class, both technical and
functional.  (It is intended for very quick low-level mail, using a local
SMTP Mail server and a MAPI client.)

2. There have been a number of implementations by MS of an evolving COM
technology that started off as an OLE Messaging service and is today an
advanced COM+ component, callable from all languages that support this
interface, whether you have .NET or not.... (It really saddens me that MS
pay lip service to COBOL support - both Fujitsu and MicroFocus have
partnership arrangements with MS, and MS is on record as saying it sees
COBOL as being an important contributor to .NET, yet I have never seen an
MSDN "How To" that gave sample COBOL code... Someone here was saying
recently that they would like more "How To" stuff with code samples
(presumably, in COBOL <G>)).

Anyway, this component is called "Collaboration Data Objects" (CDO). It's
previous incarnation was CDONTS. (CDO for NT Server), but, as implemented in
Win 2000 and XP it is called CDOSYS. I spent a few hours yesterday reading
about this stuff and came to the conclusion that it SHOULD be available from
COBOL (given that COBOL supports COM+ interfacing.)

I knocked up a Project and tested it. It immediately posted mail to me! I
was very surprised (I never expect things to work without hassle on the
first attempt <G>), so decided to push the envelope a bit... I spent a bit
more time finding out the events, properties, and methods of the CDO
component, and then got stuck into firing off Mail, in HTML and plain text,
with attachments, directly from my test project, via a  local SMTP server on
IIS, AND a remote SMTP server (my local ISP's) and flooding my inbox with
test mail... Great stuff!

All of this was achieved without .NET. Turns out the .NET Class simply
repackages CDO anyway.

If you think you might need this kind of facility, check your
..\Windows\System32\ directory for a .DLL called "CDOsys.DLL".

I won't rob you of the pleasure of discovering for yourself how to use it,
but if you get stuck, mail me privately.

For the less adventurous, I now have a comprehensive e-mail component that
can be used from the Windows desktop, or the Web, and can send AND receive
e-mails from within an application, as well as access the address book and
diary in Outlook (if you HAVE Outlook), but, unlike the SMTPMail Class, does
NOT require a MAPI client (like having Outlook or Exchange server installed)
in order to run.

Never would have got there if Howard hadn't posted his code sample. (Thanks
Howard...)

CLC definitely has value for some of us...<G>

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Mail and Collaboration Data Objects CDO

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T21:53:41+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eddc1f8_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3edd3a97_3@news.athenanews.com> <01c32a30$83cdb840$f1c2f943@chottel>`

```
No, Charles, I'm not that contrary. ("Oh, Yes you are!"..."Oh, No I'm
not!"...etc.)

I promise you it is a genuine example from work I am currently doing. The
main reason I posted it is because I wanted to thank Howard, but also
because there are DOZENS (possibly even HUNDREDS) of components that are not
"Generally documented" (You won't find any mention of them in Windows Help,
for example) within the WINDOWS OS. Some of these do REALLY sexy things, but
MSs view is that the average user will never need to use them and developers
can join MSDN if they want to know about it... (To be fair to MicroSoft,
information is freely available ONCE YOU KNOW WHAT TO SEARCH FOR!).

I s'pose we should be thankful that the COM interfaces to these components
are exposed at all...

Pete.

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c32a30$83cdb840$f1c2f943@chottel...

the
> > mail.It will be produced  on-the-fly by a mail-merge in Word or a
similar
> > process (I have a "home made" component that I use for building dynamic
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: 88 Level in working storage

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T22:47:27-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306032147.35da9aa7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<b5pubo$8jo$1@newsreader.mailgate.org> <3eOdnS-iCLi4zUCjXTWcpg@comcast.com>`

```
Howard wrote:

01  CTRL-FILE-STATUS        PIC X(02).
    88 CTL-STATUS-OK VALUE '00' '10' '97'.
    88 CTL-AT-END                       VALUE '10'.

I may have briefly mentioned this before, but file status should only
be checked in the first byte for success and not for "00".  Valid
'success' codes already include "02", "04" and "05".  Perhaps:

     88 CTL-Status-OK  VALUE "00" THRU "09" "10" "97".

Note that status '14' also exists and this may be a result that takes
the AT END branch of a READ.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 88 Level in working storage

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)

```
Hi Cap,

I think that Colin meant that the 88 names should have a specific
meaning, not merely a label. Note Howard's use of 88 names.
```

<a id="t-e114199d2d"></a>
## Thread: Old-and-New - some coding examples — 2 msgs

### Old-and-New - some coding examples

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T22:45:28-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbjq0r$86a$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I thought that I would start a new SERIES of threads showing "minimal" code
examples demonstrating some of the (relatively) small enhancements/features
in the 2002 COBOL Standard.  In these notes, I will show an example of how
something could be coded in an '85/'89/'91 Standard compiler (with all its
"required stuff") and how the same thing could be coded in the new Standard.
(I may also show some "old extension" stuff, such as return-code
processing).

I do *not* claim that my "old" style coding is the best (much less ONLY) way
that this stuff could be coded.  Feel free to post "alternatives" for the
old style as well as comments on the new.

It is my intention to start out with some of the things that address
"functionality" that I recall being raised within CLC in the past.  If
anyone has any specific requests for "demos" - let me know.

P.S.  As I do *not* have a 2002 conforming compiler, I can't guarantee that
I am not making syntax errors in the "new" examples - but I will compile the
"old" ones before posting.  Possibly one of the Micro Focus "ISO2000"
directive users or the Fujitsu/Siemens people will tell me of any errors
that they see/find.
```

- **References:** `<bbjq0r$86a$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`


<a id="t-3baed3aead"></a>
## Thread: Old-and-New - Debugging Lines — 6 msgs

### Old-and-New - Debugging Lines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T22:58:03-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbjqoe$p7l$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those who don't have a <good> interactive debugger, the use of
"debugging lines" has always been useful.  This feature is marked as
OBSOLETE in the 2002 Standard - so they will be gone in the next Standard.
The following shows what feature can be used (besides an interactive
debugger) to accomplish the old task.

Old Code:

       Identification Division.
        Program-Id. DebugConditional.
       Environment Division.
        Configuration Section.
         Source-Computer. AnyWhere
               with Debugging Mode
                .
       Procedure Division.
        Mainline.
      D    Perform While-Testing
           Perform Always-Do
           Display "   Shown"
           Stop Run
            .
        While-Testing.
           Display "This is only shown"
            .
        Always-Do.
      D    Display "   When Debugging Mode is specified"
           Display "While this is always"
            .

New Code (Minimal changes):

       Identification Division.
        Program-Id. DebugConditional.
       Environment Division.
        Configuration Section.
         Source-Computer. AnyWhere
        >>Define Debug-Mode  as "YES"
                .
       Procedure Division.
        Mainline.
         >>IF Debug-Mode = "YES"
           Perform While-Testing
         >>End
           Perform Always-Do
           Display "   Shown"
           Stop Run
            .
        While-Testing.
           Display "This is only shown"
            .
        Always-Do.
            >>IF Debug-Mode = "YES"
          Display "   When Debugging Mode is specified"
            >>End-IF
           Display "While this is always"
            .

New Code (A couple more valid changes):

  Program-Id. DebugConditional.
 >>Define Debug-Mode  as "YES"
   Procedure Division.
    Mainline.
  >>IF Debug-Mode = "YES"
           Perform While-Testing
  >>End
  Perform Always-Do
  Display "   Shown"
  Stop Run
            .
  While-Testing.      Display "This is only shown"
            .
  Always-Do.
  >>IF Debug-Mode = "YES"
          Display "   When Debugging Mode is specified"
    >>End-IF
           Display "While this is always"
            .
```

#### ↳ Re: Old-and-New - Debugging Lines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-03T23:21:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbjs57$17j$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bbjqoe$p7l$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I should have put in my original note that there are LOTS of other reasons
for using "conditional compilation" - and many other things that it can do.
Among other "purposes" that it can serve is

 - the ability to "single source" slight-variations of the same program -
such as the weekly, monthly, quarterly, and annual versions of a "report"
program

 - keeping multiple "maintenance levels" of a program all stored in a
"single source"

 - keeping variations of the same program for different customers
single-sourced

and many other purposes.
```

#### ↳ Re: Old-and-New - Debugging Lines

- **From:** Karl Kiesel <Karl.Kiesel@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T14:23:30+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDDE4C2.88778885@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<bbjqoe$p7l$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" schrieb:
> 
> New Code (A couple more valid changes):
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>   >>End

The '-If' got lost on the >>End-If; but let me highlight an other
feature: 
in the example, switching the Debug-Mode requires change of source and a
recompilation; COBOL 2002 enhances flexibility here: it is possible to
obtain values from the operating environment; if Debug-Mode were defined
as 
   >>Define Debug-Mode as PARAMETER
thus no more source changes, but simply recompilation with appropriate
values provided via operating environment!
In case no value was provided, error handling should be done too, which
results in something like:
   >>Define Debug-Mode as Parameter
   >>If Debug-Mode is NOT DEFINED
     >>Define Debug-Mode as "some-default-value"
   >>End-If

regards
K. Kiesel
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Old-and-New - Debugging Lines

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T12:32:08-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306041132.20ca4487@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bbjqoe$p7l$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3EDDE4C2.88778885@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
Karl Kiesel <Karl.Kiesel@fujitsu-siemens.com> wrote

> in the example, switching the Debug-Mode requires change of source and a
> recompilation; COBOL 2002 enhances flexibility here: it is possible to
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> values provided via operating environment!

That does have a slight disadvantage that inspecting the current
source code doesn't necessarily tell you whether the latest compile
has it on or off, a compiler listing should tell you, but I don't
usually keep them.

My own preference for 'debugging' lines is to use a run-time
parameter.  This can be set on or off in a configuration or control
file, or for interactive systems may be set by a flag on the login
name.  Usually my debug data goes out to a log file or otherwise to a
line sequential file or puts additinal information on screens or
popups.

This allows the live system to run without clutter, if the user finds
a problem then running the same version with the flag on may give more
details of what is happening.
```

#### ↳ Re: Old-and-New - Debugging Lines

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T17:56:11-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDE790B.4000108@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<bbjqoe$p7l$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Do you actually preface the lines with >>?  And I noticed you just have 
 >>End as opposed to >>End-If.  Is that an "oops", or is that really how 
it's done?

<example below>
William M. Klein wrote:
>   Program-Id. DebugConditional.
>  >>Define Debug-Mode  as "YES"
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>             .
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Old-and-New - Debugging Lines

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T18:39:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbm00a$78e$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bbjqoe$p7l$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net> <3EDE790B.4000108@Knology.net>`

```
Yes, ">>" is the beginning of ALL the new "compiler directives".

Yes,  ">>END" was an "oops"  and should be ">>END-IF"
```

<a id="t-06df067619"></a>
## Thread: Problems w/ CBL_* Routines On NFS Mounts — 3 this month / 11 total

### Problems w/ CBL_* Routines On NFS Mounts

- **From:** ctaliercio@yahoo.com (Chris)
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T06:07:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8cc07162.0306040507.260b94bd@posting.google.com>`

```
I am running MF Object Cobol 4.1.40 on an HP-UX 11i machine.

There is a directory on the UNIX machine which is an NFS mount to one
of the PC's on our network.

Using the CBL_* routines I can copy/move files TO the NFS directory
and also check if a file exists, but if I try to I can copy/move files
FROM the NFS directory, the run unit hangs.

Example:

These work:

CALL "CBL_CHECK_FILE_EXIST" USING NFS-FILENAME RETURNING STATUS-CODE
CALL "CBL_RENAME_FILE" USING LOCAL-FILENAME, NFS-FILENAME RETURNING
STATUS-CODE
CALL "CBL_COPY_FILE" USING LOCAL-FILENAME, NFS-FILENAME RETURNING
STATUS-CODE

These fail:

CALL "CBL_RENAME_FILE" USING NFS-FILENAME, LOCAL-FILENAME RETURNING
STATUS-CODE
CALL "CBL_COPY_FILE" USING NFS-FILENAME, LOCAL-FILENAME RETURNING
STATUS-CODE

From the UNIX prompt I can execute all of these operations without any
issue, so I don't think this is something at the UNIX level
(permissions, etc.).

Any suggestions?

Thanks in advance.

Chris
```

#### ↳ Re: Problems w/ CBL_* Routines On NFS Mounts

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@shaw.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T21:22:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDE5F77.FB2AD922@shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<8cc07162.0306040507.260b94bd@posting.google.com>`

```
Chris,

I don't do Unix, but I think a similar (?) problem came up here within
about the last two weeks, or possibly month. Do a Google search looking
for Simon Tobias from M/F - he answered the query.

I'm taking it as read that your sending and receiving filenames are
quoting full pathnames ? It probably wont do any good, but how about
giving CBL_CHANGE_DIR  a shot to make your sending directory the parent ?

Jimmy

Chris wrote:

> I am running MF Object Cobol 4.1.40 on an HP-UX 11i machine.
>
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…
> Chris
```

- **References:** `<8cc07162.0306040507.260b94bd@posting.google.com> <3EDE5F77.FB2AD922@shaw.ca>`

```
Jimmy,

I did a search of the newsgroup and came up empty, that's why I posted
the message. Just to be sure I searched again, this time looking for
Simon (who has been extermely helpful in the past) - but unfortunately
still could not come up with anything.

Yes - I am specifying full (explicit) path names - no environment
variables being used, so there shouldn't be any problem in that
regard.

Using your suggestion, I made sure I was in the same directory as the
file I was trying to move. The call to the "CBL_" routines do not hang
anymore, but rather fail immediately with a return code of 14605. And
just to be sure, I tested running the application as root so there
would be no permissions issues. I got the same results either way.

So - changing directories helped, in that the call does not hang
anymore, but it didn't fix the problem.

Thanks for the advice. I'll continue to look for solutions in the
newsgroup.

Thanks,
Chris
```

## Thread: Tool announcement: Grammar Recovery Kit 1.0 released — 1 msgs

### Tool announcement: Grammar Recovery Kit 1.0 released

- **From:** Ralf Laemmel <Ralf.Laemmel@cwi.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-06-04T13:51:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDDF972.9767940F@cwi.nl>`

```
Tool announcement: Grammar Recovery Kit 1.0 released

GRK --- Grammar Recovery Kit --- is a software toolkit for semi-automatic
grammar recovery. For example, using GRK one can build an authoritative
Cobol definition from IBM's definition. GRK is provided with a BSD-style
license. It is implemented in 99.99% SWI-Prolog and 0.01% gmake.

http://www.cs.vu.nl/grammars/grk/

Ralf Laemmel
CWI & VU Amsterdam
```

---

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujistu Cobol V5 and Unrecoverable Errors

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T06:38:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0306070538.12569e36@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns93912A90B9F0BgNeOkSePnAtMeXr@207.106.92.226> <2962265.1054813257@dbforums.com> <Xns9391D3C83E008gNeOkSePnAtMeXr@207.106.92.226> <b5b8d7c7.0306060040.de62c64@posting.google.com> <Xns93932819795D6gNeOkSePnAtMeXr@207.106.93.226>`

```
Glenn,

It wasn't clear to me (and maybe still isn't) that you want to just
suppress this, and not display the dialog.  I had assumed you wanted
to recover and have the code continue to run.

This is what I set with v6.1 and v7 - not sure it will work with v5
but I think it will.  All three are needed in the COBOL85.CBR.

@MessOutFile=fuj.lst
@WinCloseMsg=OFF
@CBR_JUSTINTIME_DEBUG=NO

In addition you should compile the programs using the NOTEST option
and OPTIMIZE.



Glenn E Kichline <gNeOk@SePnAtMeXr.XnXeXt> wrote in message news:<Xns93932819795D6gNeOkSePnAtMeXr@207.106.93.226>...
> pvieira@emporsoft.pt (Paulo Vieira) wrote in
> news:b5b8d7c7.0306060040.de62c64@posting.google.com: 
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> level messagebox. This happens on both 2000 and XP.
```

## Thread: Help Urgently needed with Printing from A Two-Level Table — 10 msgs

### Help Urgently needed with Printing from A Two-Level Table

- **From:** mikey@mailbox.co.za (Michael)
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T06:29:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5cddb7f2.0306050529.24493935@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi, in this program I need to accumulate the profits and losses for
each town and each quarter of the year in a 2-dimesional table and
then to print these results from the two-dimesional table.

I can't get the program to run, it gets stuck somewhere on the search.
If anyone could help me, i'd really appreciate it, it's quite
urgent...

Thanks,

Michael

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID.  OP203003.
       AUTHOR.      MICHAEL LORENC.
       DATE-WRITTEN.  MAY 2003.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.
           SELECT SALES-FILE  ASSIGN TO DISK 'H:\OP2QTR03.DAT'
                  ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
           SELECT PRINT-FILE  ASSIGN TO PRINTER 'CON'.
           
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  SALES-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 21 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS SALES-RECORD.
       
       01  SALES-RECORD.
           05  SALES-TOWN-CODE      PIC 9(4).
           05  SALES-QUARTER        PIC 9.
           05  SALES-AMOUNT         PIC S9(5)V99 SIGN TRAILING
SEPARATE.
           05  PURCHASE-AMOUNT      PIC S9(5)V99 SIGN TRAILING
SEPARATE.
           
       
       FD  PRINT-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS PRINT-LINE.
           
       01  PRINT-LINE               PIC X(80).               
       
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
     
       01  WAREHOUSE-VALUES.
           05  SALES OCCURS 14 TIMES
               INDEXED BY TOWNS-INDEX.
               10  QUARTERS OCCURS 5 TIMES
                   INDEXED BY QUARTER-INDEX.
                   15  PURCHASE      PIC S9999999V99.
       
       
       
            
       01  MONTH-NAMES.
       
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'JANUARY'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'FEBRUARY'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'MARCH'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'APRIL'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'MAY'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'JUNE'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'JULY'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'AUGUST'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'SEPTEMBER'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'OCTOBER'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'NOVEMBER'.
           05                    PIC X(9)   VALUE 'DECEMBER'.  
           
       01  MONTHS-TABLE REDEFINES MONTH-NAMES.   
           05  MONTHS OCCURS 12 TIMES
               INDEXED BY MONTH-INDEX.
               10  MONTH-NAME     PIC X(9).
               
       01  TOWN-NAMES.
       
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "CT  CAPE TOWN".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "BE  BELLVILLE".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "ST  STELLENBOSCH".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "PE  PORT ELIZABETH".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "EL  EASTLONDON".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "GHT GRAHAMSTOWN".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "DBN DURBAN".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE
"PTMBPIETERMARITZBURG".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "BLM BLOEMFONTEIN".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "JHB JOHANNESBURG".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "PTCHPOTCHEFSTROOM".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "POL POLOKWANE".
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE '    UNKNOWN'.       
           05                   PIC X(20) VALUE "QTT QUARTER TOTAL".
       
       01  TOWN-TABLE REDEFINES TOWN-NAMES.
           05  TOWN-VALUES OCCURS 14 TIMES INDEXED BY TOWN-INDEX.
               10  TOWN-CODE         PIC X(4).
               10  TOWN-NAME         PIC X(16).

       
       01  PROGRAM-SWITCHES-AND-COUNTERS.
       
           05  PAUSE                   PIC X.
           05  DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH     PIC X(3) VALUE 'YES'.
               88 NO-DATA-REMAINS               VALUE 'NO'.
           05  PAGE-COUNT              PIC 9(2) VALUE 0.
           
       01  WS-DATE.
           05   WS-YEAR                     PIC 99.
           05   WS-MONTH                    PIC 99.
           05   WS-DAY                      PIC 99.
       
           
       01  HEADING-LINE-ONE.
       
           05                          PIC X(27)  VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(26)  VALUE 
               'THE MCWIMPY BURGER COMPANY'.
           05                          PIC X(11)  VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(14)  VALUE
               'MAY 2003'.
               
       01  HEADING-LINE-TWO.
       
           05                          PIC X(23) VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(3)  VALUE '1ST'.
           05                          PIC X(9)  VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(3)  VALUE '2ND'.
           05                          PIC X(9)  VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(3)  VALUE '3RD'.
           05                          PIC X(9)  VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(3)  VALUE '4TH'.
           05                          PIC X(8)  VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(4)  VALUE 'TOWN'.
               
       01  HEADING-LINE-THREE.
       
           05                          PIC X(7)    VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(4)    VALUE 'TOWN'.
           05                          PIC X(10)   VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(7)    VALUE 'QUARTER'.
           05                          PIC X(5)    VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(7)    VALUE 'QUARTER'.
           05                          PIC X(5)    VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(7)    VALUE 'QUARTER'.
           05                          PIC X(5)    VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(7)    VALUE 'QUARTER'.
           05                          PIC X(6)    VALUE SPACES.
           05                          PIC X(5)    VALUE 'TOTAL'.
           

       
       01  DETAIL-LINE.
       
           05                          PIC X     VALUE SPACES.
           05 DET-TOWN-NAME            PIC X(16).
           05                          PIC X(3) VALUE SPACES.
           05 DET-FIRST OCCURS 5 TIMES INDEXED BY DETAIL-INDEX.
              10 DET-AMOUNT              PIC ZZZZZZZ9.99-.
              10 DET-AMOUNTX REDEFINES DET-AMOUNT
                                       PIC X(12).
           
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       PREPARE-EMPLOYEE.
           
           PERFORM INITIALIZATION.
           PERFORM PROCESS-RECORDS UNTILEND OF FILE    
           PERFORM TERMINATION.
           STOP RUN.
           
       INITIALIZATION.     
           OPEN INPUT    SALES-FILE
                OUTPUT   PRINT-FILE.
           ACCEPT WS-DATE FROM DATE.
           PERFORM WRITE-HEADING-LINES.
           
           READ SALES-FILE
                 AT END MOVE 'NO' TO DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH
           END-READ.
           
           
       TERMINATION.
       
           CLOSE SALES-FILE
                 PRINT-FILE.
       
       PROCESS-RECORDS.
           SUBTRACT PURCH  
           PERFORM SEARCH-TOWNS.
            READ SALES-FILE
                 AT END MOVE 'NO' TO DATA-REMAINS-SWITCH
           END-READ.
           
       SEARCH-TOWNS.
            
            SET TOWNS-INDEX TO 1.   
            SEARCH TOWN-VALUES
            AT END
                ADD SALES-AMOUNT TO SALES(13 QUARTER-INDEX)
                ADD SALES-AMOUNT TO SALES(13 5)
                ADD SALES-AMOUNT TO SALES(14 QUARTER-INDEX)
                ADD SALES-AMOUNT TO SALES(14 5)
            WHEN SALES-TOWN-CODE = TOWN-CODE (TOWN-INDEX)
                SET TOWNS-INDEX TO TOWN-INDEX
                SET QUARTER-INDEX TO SALES-QUARTER
                ADD SALES-AMOUNT TO SALES(TOWNS-INDEX QUARTER-INDEX)
                ADD SALES-AMOUNT TO SALES(TOWNS-INDEX 5)
                ADD SALES-AMOUNT TO SALES(14 QUARTER-INDEX)
                ADD SALES-AMOUNT TO SALES(14 5)
            END-SEARCH.
           
       PERORM PRINT-TOWN-TABLE VARYING TOWN-INDEX FROM 1 BY 1
                 UNTIL TOWN-INDEX > 14.
            
       PRINT-TOWN-TABLE.
           MOVE TOWN-NAME (TOWN-INDEX) TO DET-TOWN-NAME.
           SET TOWNS-INDEX TO TOWN-INDEX
           PERFORM PROCESS-QUARTER VARYING QUARTER-INDEX
               FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL QUARTER-INDEX > 5
            PERFORM WRITE-DETAIL-LINE.
            
       PROCESS-QUARTER.
           SET DETAIL-INDEX TO QUARTER-INDEX.
           IF SALES(TOWN-INDEX, QUARTER-INDEX ) = ZERO
              MOVE ALL '*' TO DET-AMOUNTX(DETAIL-INDEX)
           ELSE   
           MOVE SALES( TOWNS-INDEX QUARTER-INDEX)
                       TO DET-AMOUNT(DETAIL-INDEX).        
           
                  
       WRITE-HEADING-LINES.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING-LINE-ONE AFTER PAGE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING-LINE-TWO AFTER 2.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM HEADING-LINE-THREE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM SPACES.
              
       WRITE-DETAIL-LINE.
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Urgently needed with Printing from A Two-Level Table

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T00:10:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3edfd8fc.616248957@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<5cddb7f2.0306050529.24493935@posting.google.com>`

```
The error is obvious. You are initializing towns-index rather than town-index
before the search. 

What is:

   PERFORM PROCESS-RECORDS UNTILEND OF FILE    

That will not compile. It should be:

   perform process-records until no-data-remains

-----------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

>Hi, in this program I need to accumulate the profits and losses for
>each town and each quarter of the year in a 2-dimesional table and
…[235 more quoted lines elided]…
>           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help Urgently needed with Printing from A Two-Level Table

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T21:14:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0306052014.6cf67bfa@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<5cddb7f2.0306050529.24493935@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Mike,

Looks like you're hell bent in the wrong direction. This is the 2nd
control break problem youï¿½ve presented here. Success in handling these
problems start with understanding how to order your input file. That's
usually spelled S-O-R-T.

Creating a table of amounts by town /by quarter can lead to a
solution, but it can also lead to the kind of mess you got yourself
into.

So you have to create a report that produces a line of data for each
town on the Sales file. That line contains a total of sales for each
quarter and a yearly total. How would you order (sort) the Sales file
to accomplish that? Think CONTROL BREAKS and the answer should emerge.

As I've mentioned above, this is the 2nd control break problem you've
encountered in as many weeks. It behooves you to sit down and 
establish a thorough understanding of the topic before proceeding.
There will be many control breaks in your future, if youï¿½re lucky. <G>

BTW,I noticed some syntax errors in your code. You should try to
present cleanly compiled code. It prevents misunderstandings. You are
cut & pasting, I hope.
 
Regards, Jack.

> Hi, in this program I need to accumulate the profits and losses for
> each town and each quarter of the year in a 2-dimesional table and
…[235 more quoted lines elided]…
>            WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE.
```

```
Student code posted here uses upper case, prefixes on data and paragraph names,
and a period at the end of every statement.  It looks like COBOL from the 70s. 

"Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>| I think the instructor should get the F for imposing that methodology.
>
>I have a low opinion of instructors in general.
```

<a id="t-1c78793ad7"></a>
## Thread: IBM mainframers (only) — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM mainframers (only)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T16:07:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbobe9$137$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In answer to a thread in IBM-MAIN about IBM dropping support for its
("almost ancient" by now) ESA "architecture" (replaced first by OS/390 and
then z/OS), there was a reference to a PDF file that I found FASCINATING.
Although probably somewhere between "unintelligible" and totally
uninteresting to NON-IBM "mainframers" (of the "MVS-ish" persuasion), I
would hope those who are in or interested in this environment would look
at - and share within their organization, the PDF presentation at:

     http://docs.share.org/proceedings/sh100/s2412.pdf

The quote (related to the original IBM-MAIN issue) is on page 15 where it
says,

"In the release planned for September 2004, z/OS will no longer support the
ESA/390 architecture."

P.S.  Some of the presentation is fairly "nitty-gritty" for the "average
application programmer" - if such exists, but it still is worth going thru.
```

#### ↳ Why is that FASCINATING?

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attgloabl.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T15:08:38-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EDFBF66.3688F3BF@attgloabl.net>`
- **References:** `<bbobe9$137$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
You can't run z/OS on a S/360 or S/370 machine, either.  Seem to me that it's
just the normal course of events.  Hardware that is out of production
eventually goes out of support.

I don't think it means that application programs written for S/360 won't run,
do you?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why is that FASCINATING?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-05T17:29:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbog8v$l3q$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bbobe9$137$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3EDFBF66.3688F3BF@attgloabl.net>`

```
I found the ENTIRE presentation to be fascinating - not the "drop" of ESA
support.  (Things like the percentage of z/900 machines running z/OS and the
future of VSAM and DB2 enhancements and 64-bit virtual addressing.  All of
this has been "public" - but not something that I have seen "put together"
as in this presentation.)

As far as what the ESA drop means, I believe (but won't swear to it) that it
means that applications that REQUIRE a specific (earlier) ARCHLVL will have
problems.  I think that only impacts specific (authorized) instructions and
various control blocks.  However, as I have never "played" with such, I
don't know its full implications.

I *do* believe that "older" COBOL programs (even those compiled with OS/VS
COBOL) will continue to "run" and be supported if using the LE run-time
library.  OTOH, I might be mistaken on even this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why is that FASCINATING?

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T15:24:00+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,ibm.software.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<kyIEa.115210$cO3.8446080@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bbobe9$137$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <3EDFBF66.3688F3BF@attgloabl.net> <bbog8v$l3q$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bbog8v$l3q$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net...
| I found the ENTIRE presentation to be fascinating - not the "drop" of ESA
| support.  (Things like the percentage of z/900 machines running z/OS and
the
| future of VSAM and DB2 enhancements and 64-bit virtual addressing.  All of
| this has been "public" - but not something that I have seen "put together"
| as in this presentation.)

The ability to update VSAM files from multiple programs is interresting.
Recovery from a batch abend may present a problem though.
It will be interresting to see if anyone actually uses it.
```

```
I am using Server Express on Unix , what is the command to get the
program counter(PC) ? . One of my program is sending an error with
the PC = 1322.Please let me know ..........
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** "Greg Condon" <nospam@me.thanks>
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T11:02:37+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hx_Da.141280$3C2.4641547@news3.calgary.shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com> <2965052.1054849905@dbforums.com>`

```
For Server Express 2.2 I use:

   NOERRLIST REF RESEQ

This gives a full numbered program listing along with the PC x-ref at the
bottom.


Regards,

Greg
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T15:00:16-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306061400.317ca7d8@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com> <2968147.1054917002@dbforums.com>`

```
MFDEVP <member27090@dbforums.com> wrote 

> Thanks for the answer.But I am using this command and not getting any
> lst files.All this is generating is .idy and .gnt files.I too using the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> cob NOERRLIST REF RESEQ myprog.cbl

Further to previous post: the REF numbers refer to the PC codes in the
.int file.  To locate the problem you should delete all .gnt versions
and run the .int version then the REF listing will match.  If you need
to run the .gnt then you may need to also do an ASMLIST"name" and
SOURCEASM to be able to relate the PC codes from the .gnt.

Leastways, that is what I need to do with my MF which is not the same
as yours.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** "Greg Condon" <nospam@me.thanks>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T10:56:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RxjEa.146582$ro6.4300140@news2.calgary.shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com> <2968147.1054917002@dbforums.com>`

```
Hi Ronald -

My answer was the extra pieces I had to put in to my regular set of compile
options.

If you want a complete sample command-line to create a x-ref (using .int
code):

    cob -i -C"LIST COPYLIST NOERRLIST REF RESEQ" PROG.CBL >> PROG.LST

    -i   = Generate .int code
    -C = Compiler directives


For example (on SE2.2 on Unixware) this is the output generated by the above
command-line:

* Micro Focus Server Express         V2.2 revision 000 07-Jun-03 03:53 Page
1
* PROG.CBL
     1 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
     2 78  78-FIRST-NUMBER       VALUE 1.
     3 78  78-SECOND-NUMBER      VALUE 2.
     4 01  THE-SUM               PIC 9.
     5 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
     6   DISPLAY "Quick demo of XREF directive"
     7   DISPLAY "First number is  : " 78-FIRST-NUMBER
     8   DISPLAY "Second number is : " 78-SECOND-NUMBER
     9   COMPUTE THE-SUM = 78-FIRST-NUMBER + 78-SECOND-NUMBER
    10   DISPLAY "The sum is : " THE-SUM
    11   STOP RUN
    12   .
* Micro Focus Server Express         V2.2 revision 000 Compiler
* Copyright (C) 1984-2002 Micro Focus International Ltd. URN
RXCSC/AA0/00000*
                                                     REF GNR-166060000AD
* Total Messages:     0
* Data:         404     Code:         168
*
 Address   Line No   Address   Line No   Address   Line No   Address   Line
No
00000024         6  00000044         7  0000005F         8  0000007A
9
00000085        10  0000009A        11



Cheers!

Greg
```

```
Thanks for the info.

Greg, Previously, I was using the Micro Focus Cobol,on that I was
getting
Program Counter next to each line of code when I generate the
listing .On the left hand side the line number and on the right hand
side the PC.

For example ..

01   INDENTIFICATION DIVISION  00
02   PROGRAM                             100.

According your example , the server express is providing at the bottom.

So I need to consider the Address will be my program counter , right ?

If that is the case , I have program counter 1313 from the error , now I
generated the listing, my program came up with addresses , which is not
showing this number(1313).It has 1300 and 1318.That means I need to
think about the code between the addresses of 1300 and 1318 in order to
resolve the problem.

Please clarify this.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Micro Focus Cobol Error Messages

- **From:** "Greg Condon" <nospam@me.thanks>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T09:06:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s6YEa.183533$Vi5.5112391@news1.calgary.shaw.ca>`
- **References:** `<2753633.1050006128@dbforums.com> <2972815.1055028842@dbforums.com>`

```
> If that is the case , I have program counter 1313 from the error , now I
> generated the listing, my program came up with addresses , which is not
> showing this number(1313).It has 1300 and 1318.That means I need to
> think about the code between the addresses of 1300 and 1318 in order to
> resolve the problem.

Yes - that is correct.

In my experience the program counter does not usually provide an exact match
to a line on the XREF listing.  I assume this is because the XREF shows the
starting line number of the code generated for the statement.

We find this XREF _extremely_ handy when a user says "my session went
*poof* - what happened!".  At least you have a great place to start.

FYI another nice feature of the RTS is the ability to trap run-time errors
(see CBL_ERROR_PROC).  You can hook into this and do whatever you like with
the info.  We wrote a utility to log the error details to a sequential file.



Regards,

Greg
```

```
I don't use WS as a prefix.
I do use the high level qualifier to group variables.
i.e. IND-INDICATORS
CNT-COUNTERS  etc.

I can use the text editor to tell me what I want to know.
(ISPF eXclude and Find are powerful tools)

When you work on very large programs, it seems that everything is WS.
WS becomes an unorganized garbage heap.

If your standards make life easier for you, they are valid.
```
