###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T01:52:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net>`

```

"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net...
| Harley wrote:
| > "LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| > | preceded by WS-.  When I first took COBOL, our instructor said it was
a
| > | good practice, and then our shop here has style rules enforcing the
same.
| >
| > P.S. How much real life experience did your instructor have.
|
| He had about 8 years, and this is a standard in our shop.  :)
|

I have seen some standards using WS010- WS020- etc. to make it easier to
find things in a listing.

I used WS- in the past, but when I started using text editors, Xedit, and
ISPF Edit, I stopped using listings to review programs. Although I am not
above editing a listing.
(I have REXX ISPF macros that let me look at a copy book from the program I
am editing)

My personal feeling is that WS- isn't really necessary.
To me, "what it is" is more important than "where it is".
Although WS-SUB, and WS-SUB1 conform to your standard, they really tell me
nothing about what they are. (Real world junk I found in a program written
by a programmer who was really hot on using WS-).
Data naming is a key to writing maintainable programs.

I have run benchmarks using reads into working-storage vs. using the FD
data, and found that using the FD is faster. (OS/390).
If I wanted to change processing from Working-storage to the FD, it would be
more difficult if everything in working-storage was prefaced by WS-.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T22:03:11-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
Harley <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

[snip]

>My personal feeling is that WS- isn't really necessary.
>To me, "what it is" is more important than "where it is".

[snip]

>I have run benchmarks using reads into working-storage vs. using the FD
>data, and found that using the FD is faster. (OS/390).

Well, that makes sense, in a way... a READ INTO contains an implicit MOVE,
as I recall.  But more importantly... I was taught, lo, those many years
ago, that Thou Shalt Not Perform Arithmetic Operations On/In File Buffers.  
The reasons for this may have vanished with Autocoder or 1401 emulation...  
but since the reason for this Thou Shalt Not had to to with
unintelligible, difficult-to-reproduce errors that would put garbage data
into things like bank-accounts and inventory-counts it was considered Good
Practise.

('Truncate a first name?  No real loss... truncate a bank-balance?  Find a 
new job.')

With that in mind - and the assumption that *anyone* who would preface 
something in the FD or LINKAGE with WS- (or WK-) would be found out and 
fired, *really* fast - then doing your math on WS-prefaced variables 
allows one to conclude reasonably that said variables are, in fact, in the 
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

>If I wanted to change processing from Working-storage to the FD, it would be
>more difficult if everything in working-storage was prefaced by WS-.

This can be seen as another Good Reason... as one would *not* want to 
'change processing from Working-storage to the FD' since Thou Shalt Not 
Perform Arithmetic Operations On/In File Buffers.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T03:17:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32ca3$58710880$a491f243@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
<attaboy Charlie good snip>

Early on in my career I once tried to increase the size of a variable
length record in an FD not realizing it was an input buffer. I managed to
wipe out the length field in the adjoining record and I did learn how to
locate a buffer in a memory dump!


> This can be seen as another Good Reason... as one would *not* want to 
> 'change processing from Working-storage to the FD' since Thou Shalt Not 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T20:23:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc2qbe$i4e$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c32ca3$58710880$a491f243@chottel> <etNEa.115627$cO3.8476656@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <01c32e07$c552e9e0$12ccf943@chottel> <3EE4E6B1.10305@XXXX.optonline.net>`

```

On  9-Jun-2003, Liam Devlin <LiamD@XXXX.optonline.net> wrote:

> > Making mistakes seems to me to be both a part of being human and learning.
> > Many people don't try things because they fear making mistakes. Being
> > perfect is overrated IMO.
>
> Not to mention impossible to achieve.

Even so - in programming we need to figure the costs and benefits of closer and
closer approximations of perfection.   Giving the users enough to do the job now
is valuable.

Part of our job is to make things fail gracefully.  Like the lines on a
sidewalk, we want failure to occur on our terms.  Is this "giving in"?   No, it
is making realistic goals.

A hockey team that waits for the perfect shot doesn't win.  It needs to take the
imperfect shots and try to get the rebound.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T14:49:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc26pj$9io$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com> <01c32ca3$58710880$a491f243@chottel> <etNEa.115627$cO3.8476656@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

On  8-Jun-2003, "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> You are in the minority, if you work in the mainframe environment.
> Most programmers avoid variable length records like the plague.

20 years ago, variable length records were more common in mainframe
environments.  Then we moved to header and trailer records for a while, and now
are into using databases.   Time has indicated which are easier and safer to
work with.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T14:13:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com...
| In article <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
| Harley <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
| ago, that Thou Shalt Not Perform Arithmetic Operations On/In File Buffers.

That's the problem with some standards, although they had validity based on
real world concerns, time marches on, but the standard remains.

Standards should be reviewed periodicly, and changed when the rational for
the standard is no longer pertinent.

| The reasons for this may have vanished with Autocoder or 1401 emulation...
| but since the reason for this Thou Shalt Not had to to with
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| new job.')

We got some programmers from a bank that was taken over.
They couldn't add 1 to a counter without checking for overflow.
The problem was, that when they got an overflow condition they moved zero to
the field, displayed a message, and moved on.
They WOULD have wiped out a bank balance.
The standard they followed was, to say the least, incomplete.


|
| With that in mind - and the assumption that *anyone* who would preface
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| >If I wanted to change processing from Working-storage to the FD, it would
be
| >more difficult if everything in working-storage was prefaced by WS-.
|
| This can be seen as another Good Reason... as one would *not* want to
| 'change processing from Working-storage to the FD' since Thou Shalt Not
| Perform Arithmetic Operations On/In File Buffers.

Why Not?

And who said the data in the buffer was being updated?
It could be input to a report program (read only).
```

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Message-ID:** `<bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com> <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
Harley <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>the standard is no longer pertinent.

That was never contested, at least by me... STRING/UNSTRING, for instance, 
or INSPECT were Strictly Forbidden in CICS when using IKFCBL00... then 
along came IGYCRCTL and lo, doors were opened!

>
>| The reasons for this may have vanished with Autocoder or 1401 emulation...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>The standard they followed was, to say the least, incomplete.

... and the bank they were working for was out of business.  What a 
coincidence!

>
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Why Not?

With all due respect, old boy... this was stated above, in the section you 
quoted, clearly and unambiguously.

>
>And who said the data in the buffer was being updated?
>It could be input to a report program (read only).

If it is read-only then there's no need to perform manipulation in the 
buffer, especially since the next READ wipes out what's in there.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T17:20:14+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com> <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com...
<<big snip >>|
| >
| >And who said the data in the buffer was being updated?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
|

What you say pertains to buffers, also pertains to the Linkage Section.
If you update an item in the Linkage Section, and your field definition is
different, you may wipe out other data.
In any event, you will corrupt data in the calling program.
You aren't saying that we shouldn't use called programs because of a
potential problem.

There is no current reason to avoid using the FD data.
You can update data there with less risk than you have updating Linkage
Section data.

The reason Working-Storage was preferred was because you used fewer base
registers, and avoided having to reload registers. Your program would run
faster.
A program with more than a few files should still use Working-Storage, but
simple programs can use the FD, and updating data in the FD is not a
problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T22:42:43-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbu7r3$33v$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com> <i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
Harley <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com...
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>What you say pertains to buffers, also pertains to the Linkage Section.

What I said pertained only to buffers; how one chooses to apply it is 
one's own concern.

>If you update an item in the Linkage Section, and your field definition is
>different, you may wipe out other data.

If you code badly errors may result... this is news?

>In any event, you will corrupt data in the calling program.
>You aren't saying that we shouldn't use called programs because of a
>potential problem.

No, I am not saying that at all.  I said, clearly and unambiguously, that 
'I was taught, lo, those many years ago, that Thou Shalt Not Perform 
Arithmetic Operations On/In File Buffers' and I fail to see how my stating 
something in this manner is so subtle that it causes difficulties to 
arise.

>
>There is no current reason to avoid using the FD data.

I did not say that there was or there was not... I related what I was 
taught lo, thos many years ago.

>You can update data there with less risk than you have updating Linkage
>Section data.

This is an interesting assertion... might you be able to document it, 
somehow?

>
>The reason Working-Storage was preferred was because you used fewer base
>registers, and avoided having to reload registers.

That is not the reason I was taught, the reason was... you know, that one 
you edited out when I pointed out to you that you had already quoted it.

>Your program would run
>faster.
>A program with more than a few files should still use Working-Storage, but
>simple programs can use the FD, and updating data in the FD is not a
>problem.

Not according to what I was taught... but, as I said several times 
already, I was taught that many years ago and things might have changed.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T21:31:05+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee302b3_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com> <i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbu7r3$33v$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bbu7r3$33v$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
> Harley <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
<snip>

> >A program with more than a few files should still use Working-Storage,
but
> >simple programs can use the FD, and updating data in the FD is not a
> >problem.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I was taught the same dogma Doc when I first started working on IBM
mainframes around 1969.

The reason we were given was that it was extremely difficult to see what was
in the buffer if the system crashed, as the registers could be clobbered and
there was nothing to point to the IO area in the dump.  Furthermore, even if
you DID happen to locate the buffer, it's contents could be suspect because
it may or may not have been purged before the crash. WS on the other hand
had Linkage Base locators that made it readily identifiable.

By the time I had learned BAL and started looking at what COBOL generated, I
realised it was fallacious. While it was true as far as it went, succeeding
releases of IBM OSs got better at preserving stuff and allowing you to
follow backward chains of control blocks through the dump. Besides, you
usually didn't need the data in the buffer in order to figure what was
wrong.

Dennis is correct in what he says, and today there is no reason why people
in IBM environments should still be using MOVE...INTO.  It simply duplicates
data and is an unnecessary overhead.

However, I know many (apart from yourself <G>) who do, (their reasons lost
in dim antiquity or never known...) and I guess if you feel more comfortable
doing it...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T22:15:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KzOEa.1958$87.1038847@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com> <i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbu7r3$33v$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee302b3_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3ee302b3_8@news.athenanews.com...
>
> The reason we were given [to not do arithmetic on file buffers ] was that
it was extremely difficult to see what was
> in the buffer if the system crashed, as the registers could be clobbered
and
> there was nothing to point to the IO area in the dump

Not to mention, you might not even be able to  READ an FD, as that storage
is officially inaccessible unless the file is open.

Yes, I know, earlier versons of IBM mainframe COBOL did not enforce this,
but IIRC about two years ago this came up here when someone starting getting
S0C4 (S0C3?) ("storage not accessible") errors shortly after changing
compiler versions or maybe it was going to Language Environment runtime...

MCM
```

```
> | S0C4 (S0C3?) ("storage not accessible") errors shortly after changing
> | compiler versions or maybe it was going to Language Environment
runtime...
> |
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do you remember if the resolution of the problem changed the program
output?


No problem to resolve. The doc always said the file buffers were
inaccessible unless the file was open. They just started meaning it.

MCM
```

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
> news:wZOEa.115723$cO3.8485872@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> inaccessible unless the file was open. They just started meaning it.

:)  We had a similar problem going from COBOL 74 to COBOL 85.  I think 
-every- background report had "Move Spaces to [Rec-Name]" as one of the 
first lines in the program.  On the Unisys mainframe, this generates a 
"Storage Limits Violation" (which is the most common way I've seen 
people generate this error).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Gerry Wolfe <gerry.wolfe@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T10:33:17-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0569ev8nsgm5fv6dbcm7qb6fi92kmm0fkb@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com> <i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbu7r3$33v$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee302b3_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
<<snip>>
>
>> >A program with more than a few files should still use Working-Storage,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Another reason for processing a file in WS rather than in FD was due
to addressing issues.

1.  Data elements in FD were not available until the file was opened
(I suspect the OPEN does a GETMAIN).
2. When writing to a file, after the WRITE statement was executed the
record that was just created was no longer available.  So, if (for
example) you just wanted to update some of the data fields and then
WRITE another record, you were SOL.  Prob'ly the WRITE statement
simply updated an address pointer to the next slot in the buffer.
3. After CLOSEing the file, reference to the data elements in FD also
resulted in addressing exceptions (CLOSE does a FREEMAIN?)

These comments go back to concerns that existed long time ago, maybe
(prob'ly?) this has changed.  Personally, I suffer from the "old dog /
new tricks" syndrome so still ALWAYS process records in WS.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T19:25:18+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ya5Fa.196142$ja4.10397582@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com> <i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbu7r3$33v$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ee302b3_8@news.athenanews.com> <0569ev8nsgm5fv6dbcm7qb6fi92kmm0fkb@4ax.com>`

```

"Gerry Wolfe" <gerry.wolfe@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:0569ev8nsgm5fv6dbcm7qb6fi92kmm0fkb@4ax.com...
| <<snip>>
| >
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
| >> already, I was taught that many years ago and things might have
changed.
| >>
| >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
| simply updated an address pointer to the next slot in the buffer.

It does.

| 3. After CLOSEing the file, reference to the data elements in FD also
| resulted in addressing exceptions (CLOSE does a FREEMAIN?)

You also did your last write prior to closing the file.

|
| These comments go back to concerns that existed long time ago, maybe
| (prob'ly?) this has changed.  Personally, I suffer from the "old dog /
| new tricks" syndrome so still ALWAYS process records in WS.

I'm an "old dog" myself.
I ran a benchmark out of curiosity, and found a difference in run time, and
CPU usage.
I wouldn't change something that works without justification.
Not every program needs to be as efficient as possible.
For the most part you never get a return on investment through run time
costs.
There has to be a compelling reason to tune a program, and they are usually
user driven, or the result of a totally incompetent programmer doing
something stupid.

As far as data being accessible,
My practice is to:
1. display a run time, and compile time message.
2. Open all files
3. Then start processing
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T20:59:53+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dtNEa.115626$cO3.8477494@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <OpmEa.113683$cO3.8329189@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbsu35$glg$1@panix1.panix.com> <i9pEa.190783$ja4.10166600@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbu7r3$33v$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
Doc,

I see what you were saying.

I may have misinterpreted your statements.

I was also taught things many years ago, and I regularly reevaluate them in
the light of changing technology.

I remember breaking a table into two parallel tables, one with the key, and
one with the data.
This was done to avoid paging during a search, since it has been years since
I've seen anything that indicate programs in the MVS environment are being
paged in or out, I've dropped the practice.

END of Top Post,
Noting else follows.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T10:28:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc2g4r$1fto$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bbrh4v$rvj$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>,
> Harley <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Practise.

Actually, the record area for a file is only "available" when the file is
OPEN.  The results of attempting to access the record area of the file when
it is closed are undefined, which can mean that the contents are undefined.
To allow access to the record area of a closed file with predictable/defined
results is an implementor extension.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T15:04:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE2455D.3050701@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
Harley wrote:
> I have seen some standards using WS010- WS020- etc. to make it easier to
> find things in a listing.

Each one was sequentially numbered by 10's?  Interesting...

> My personal feeling is that WS- isn't really necessary.
> To me, "what it is" is more important than "where it is".
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Data naming is a key to writing maintainable programs.

True - WS-X isn't that informative.  I actually like the Hungarian style 
naming convention (I believe that's what it's called - strName [or 
sName] for a string containing someone's name, intSSAN [or iSSAN] for an 
integer representing a social security number, etc.).  That way, you 
know the data type (which, together with a well-named variable, tells 
you the "what" as opposed to the "where".

I had actually thought about bringing that up at work, but we've been so 
busy coding, we haven't had much time to think about changing the 
"how's" (although, without violating any of our existing rules, I've 
managed to format some of my programs so that when we get the Hitachi OO 
compiler for the Unisys mainframe (something I hope to get soon), these 
programs -should- compile with sourceformat set to "free").

I guess WS-iSSAN would accomplish both things (what and where), but if 
you have too much prefix, you've got no room left for the name!  :)

> I have run benchmarks using reads into working-storage vs. using the FD
> data, and found that using the FD is faster. (OS/390).
> If I wanted to change processing from Working-storage to the FD, it would be
> more difficult if everything in working-storage was prefaced by WS-.

That's interesting.  In extended-mode Unisys (2200), the memory for the 
FD isn't allocated if a file isn't open.  So, if you try to access a 
variable in an FD and the file isn't open, you get a real nasty runtime 
error.  I haven't ever tested performance going between WS and FD 
variables, though...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T15:17:01+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<NrIEa.115205$cO3.8446140@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE2455D.3050701@Knology.net>`

```

"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3EE2455D.3050701@Knology.net...
| Harley wrote:
| > I have seen some standards using WS010- WS020- etc. to make it easier to
| > find things in a listing.
|
| Each one was sequentially numbered by 10's?  Interesting...

It was used to work from listings.
You avoided having to use the cross reference.

|
| > My personal feeling is that WS- isn't really necessary.
| > To me, "what it is" is more important than "where it is".
| > Although WS-SUB, and WS-SUB1 conform to your standard, they really tell
me
| > nothing about what they are. (Real world junk I found in a program
written
| > by a programmer who was really hot on using WS-).
| > Data naming is a key to writing maintainable programs.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
| you the "what" as opposed to the "where".

The down side to hungarian notation is that the type may change.
If you go from a short interger to integer, you have to change every program
to conform to the name change.
User defined types are supposed to make this type of change easier. It seems
to be self defeating to use hungarian notation.

|
| I had actually thought about bringing that up at work, but we've been so
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| programs -should- compile with sourceformat set to "free").

You might be better off having standard data name components.
i.e.
CNT Counter,
DT Date,
TM Time,
DTM Date Time,
SLS Sales,
PRCH Purchase,

This should allow you to construct consistent data names that can be
interpreted using your rosetta stone.
It's easier to scan your source library for a know consistent data name
component.
Scanning for PRCH should let me know which modules are involved in Purchase
processing.

My personal preference is to keep the lower order name consistent, and use a
high level qualifier to differentiate between multiple instances of the same
element.
i.e.
MOVE MSTR-PRCH-DT  to HLD-PRCH-DT.

|
| I guess WS-iSSAN would accomplish both things (what and where), but if
| you have too much prefix, you've got no room left for the name!  :)

See above.

|
| > I have run benchmarks using reads into working-storage vs. using the FD
| > data, and found that using the FD is faster. (OS/390).
| > If I wanted to change processing from Working-storage to the FD, it
would be
| > more difficult if everything in working-storage was prefaced by WS-.
|
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
|
If you haven't read the record, you may have garbage in Working storage
which could also cause an nasty abend.

My personal feeling is that we have allowed the lowest common denominator to
dictate our practices.
If an informed decision has to be made, avoid having to make it. That way
the village idiot won't make the wrong decision.

Performance isn't always an issue, and some times you will never recover the
cost of performance tuning.
BUT when you have a hundred people sitting around picking their noses,
instead of picking orders, you may want to see if performance can be
improved.
```

```
Harley wrote:
> The down side to hungarian notation is that the type may change.
> If you go from a short interger to integer, you have to change every program
> to conform to the name change.

You can have short integers in COBOL?  :)  I understand what you're 
saying, though...

> You might be better off having standard data name components.
> i.e.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> PRCH Purchase,

We do have "standard" abbreviations - NBR instead of NUM for "NUMBER", 
etc.  Of course, they were all declared "for future coding", which means 
that the older programs could still have non-conforming names.

> My personal feeling is that we have allowed the lowest common denominator to
> dictate our practices.
> If an informed decision has to be made, avoid having to make it. That way
> the village idiot won't make the wrong decision.

Heh - or they push the decisions off to the likes of me...  :)

> Performance isn't always an issue, and some times you will never recover the
> cost of performance tuning.
> BUT when you have a hundred people sitting around picking their noses,
> instead of picking orders, you may want to see if performance can be
> improved.

That's certainly not a problem right now - we've got more work than we 
really should be able to do.  We'll get it done, though - always have, 
always will.
```

```
> etc.  Of course, they were all declared "for future coding", which means
> that the older programs could still have non-conforming names.

This always seemed like a good idea - that worked until we buy someone else's
programs and integrate them with our system.   Someone else has different
abbreviations.

Such a standard that isn't followed isn't very useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T22:30:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32f9f$d0a844e0$bdcaf943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE2455D.3050701@Knology.net> <NrIEa.115205$cO3.8446140@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE5305B.8060402@Knology.net> <bc4pej$gvk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
We have "standard" abbreviations. They are only enforced for database field
names. I put it in quotes because the "standards" have changed ove the
years, sometimes radically. In grand bureacratic tradition fully spelled
out names have been rejected in favor of the abbreviations. Some archive
database field names are now different from the field names in the database
being archived. Arbitrary standards without common sense do not always
promote clarity.

No more comments below. 

Howard Brazee <howard@brazee.net> wrote in article
<bc4pej$gvk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>...
> 
> On  9-Jun-2003, LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
> 
> > We do have "standard" abbreviations - NBR instead of NUM for "NUMBER",
> > etc.  Of course, they were all declared "for future coding", which
means
> > that the older programs could still have non-conforming names.
> 
> This always seemed like a good idea - that worked until we buy someone
else's
> programs and integrate them with our system.   Someone else has different
> abbreviations.
> 
> Such a standard that isn't followed isn't very useful.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T19:55:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE7CF86.3020008@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE2455D.3050701@Knology.net> <NrIEa.115205$cO3.8446140@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE5305B.8060402@Knology.net> <bc4pej$gvk$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> On  9-Jun-2003, LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
>>We do have "standard" abbreviations - NBR instead of NUM for "NUMBER",
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> abbreviations.

When I first got here, there was a web page we could go to and "propose" 
a standard abbreviation.  When 50% + 1 had voted either "I love it" or 
"I hate it", the proposal was considered either adopted or dead 
(respectively).  You could also vote "I don't care" (for the chronically 
apathetic) if you didn't care one way or the other.

It was really interesting to see some of the things that were discussed. 
  One was "LN" for "Line Number".  I guess this person forgot that "LN" 
could reasonably be read as an abbreviation for "Line".  Both "NUM" and 
"NBR" were proposed as abbreviations for "Number", but the consensus was 
for "NBR", since "NUM" could also stand for "Numeric".  So, instead of 
LN, we have LN-NBR...  :)

> Such a standard that isn't followed isn't very useful.

We have electronic peer-reviews of our code before we check it in. 
There are a few us who aren't afraid to give a "No Go" to code that 
doesn't meet standards.  (I wonder why I'm not getting many peer reviews 
these days...  :> )
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T10:02:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc5303$8qm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE2455D.3050701@Knology.net> <NrIEa.115205$cO3.8446140@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE5305B.8060402@Knology.net>`

```

"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3EE5305B.8060402@Knology.net...

> You can have short integers in COBOL?  :)

ISO/IEC 1989:2002 page 361, 13.16.58, USAGE clause shows that the current
standard added signed and unsigned varieties of the USAGEs BINARY-CHAR,
BINARY-SHORT, BINARY-LONG and BINARY-DOUBLE.  The formats aren't mandated,
but the *minimum* ranges each usage must be able to cover are (equivalent to
8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit and 64-bit capacities respectively).  This standard
also adds FLOAT-SHORT, FLOAT-LONG and FLOAT-EXTENDED.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: A style question (WS)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T15:59:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc5grs$bf3$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ED1453A.6030409@Knology.net> <Z3PDa.111676$cO3.8134619@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE139B1.4060104@Knology.net> <qzbEa.113297$cO3.8271629@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE2455D.3050701@Knology.net> <NrIEa.115205$cO3.8446140@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3EE5305B.8060402@Knology.net> <bc5303$8qm$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
However, it also states (on page 696) in the "Processor Dependent" list,

"5) The usages FLOAT-SHORT, FLOAT-LONG, and FLOAT-EXTENDED are dependent
upon the availability of a suitable computer architecture for floating-point
data formats.

6) The USAGE PACKED-DECIMAL clause is dependent upon the availability of a
suitable computer architecture for the packed decimal data format."

The "processor dependent list must *not* be confused with the  "Implementor
Defined" list which appears earlier in the 2002 Standard.  For a
"description" of what "processor dependent" does (and does not mean), see
page 4, which states (in part),

"The decision of whether to claim support for a processor-dependent language
element is within an implementor's discretion. Factors that may be
considered include, but are not limited to, hardware capability, software
capability, and market positioning of the processor."
```

## Thread: EasyTRIEVE and FBA (on OS/390) — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### EasyTRIEVE and FBA (on OS/390)

- **From:** chuck_leviton@yahoo.com (charles leviton)
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T09:00:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d280a253.0306060800.797da7ea@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
Since I can't find an Easytrieve forum and all the Easytrieve
programmers I know are also Cobol programmers, I hope this forum is
appropriate :-)

I realise there have been any number of discussions about FBA and
COBOL and ADV/NOADV.

My question is does Easytrieve handle FBA the same as COBOL?  With
COBOL my DD would have RECL of 133 but in my program it would be 132
and COBOL would know to put the printer control character, right? 
(Assuming I used the ADV compiler option and did the ADVANCING thing)

Does Easytrieve work the same?  Would my FILE clause in EASYTRIEVE be 
FILE OUTFILE FBA(132 0)?

Thanks
```

```
Hello Chuck,

It's ages since I last used Easytrieve, so I think the fastest answer
would be from trying it yourself till you find the way that works.

That said, I am not sure why you are trying to do this anyway. 
Reports are specified separately from files with what must be a set
default DCB, which I am sure can be over-ridden, though I don't know
how.  I think all reports normally go to the ddname SYSPRINT for which
a DCB does not need to be specified in either the program or the JCL.

Cheers Robert

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

```

- **From:** Harry Carter <hpcarter@attbi.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T14:50:16+00:00

- **References:** `<d280a253.0306060800.797da7ea@posting.google.com> <fcd09c56.0306070516.529fa3c0@posting.google.com>`

```
On 7 Jun 2003 06:16:56 -0700, robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert
Jones) wrote:

>That said, I am not sure why you are trying to do this anyway. 
>Reports are specified separately from files with what must be a set
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Thanks

At my shop, we output our EZT reports to file and then genner them to
Sarview, etc.

Basically, EZT has a default line length (which i don't think is
necessarily 132 - depends on how the shop sets it up). I always
specify a line length on the linesize parm of the Report definition. I
also specify as part of the report parms which file it goes to. You
also have to in the file defintion speciy Printer so that EZT knows to
associate your file defintion with a report.
```

#### ↳ Re: EasyTRIEVE and FBA (on OS/390)

- **From:** Harry Carter <hpcarter@attbi.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T14:46:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mfu3evopnh6vr9pcjhbsi1bhbmm9v14524@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306060800.797da7ea@posting.google.com>`

```
On 6 Jun 2003 09:00:33 -0700, chuck_leviton@yahoo.com (charles
leviton) wrote:

>Hi,
>Since I can't find an Easytrieve forum and all the Easytrieve
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>FILE OUTFILE FBA(132 0)?

Easytrieve works like Cobol (with the ADV option) so you report would
have a line defintion of 132 and your Easytrieve File in the
easytrieve file section would say 133 and hence your JCL would specify
a LRECL=133.
```

<a id="t-d6c2397778"></a>
## Thread: Case Statement in DB2 — 1 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

_Thread started [2003-03](2003-03.md#t-d6c2397778)._

#### ↳ Re: Case Statement in DB2

- **From:** leger <member30867@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T19:57:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2969151.1054929459@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<20030327223829.08824.00000043@mb-fc.aol.com>`

```

This is a little out of date for your question but here is one example
on a case statement in DB2

SELECT
    CASE CDL.AD_VAL_SVC_ENB_IR
       WHEN ' ' THEN 'SPACE'
       WHEN '0' THEN '0'
       WHEN '1' THEN '1'
       WHEN '3' THEN '3'
    END                                    AS "AD_VAL_IR"
   ,COUNT(*)                            AS "UOW3.5.15"

FROM
        D168AA.TCMSCUS  AS  CUS
   JOIN D168AA.TCDLTBL  AS  CDL
     ON CUS.CDL_NR = CDL.CDL_NR

This will subsitute the word 'space' for a char of ' '.

Bill
```

## Thread: RW Exposed as Goverment Hoax — 13 msgs

### RW Exposed as Goverment Hoax

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T01:37:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32c95$51b0db00$56c2f943@chottel>`

```
Star Trek, the episode where the ship is being run by a computer whose
circuits have been empressed with human engrams. Spock to McCoy: "It would
be interesting to empress your engrams upon a computer, the resulting
torrent of illogic would be most entertaining".

My spys, a err ah, I mean researchers, have determined that DARPA has
actually tried this. Yes, RW is actually a PC based automaton. The honchos
at DARPA thought CLC would be the ideal place to put RW through his/her/its
Turing test paces. With typical government efficiency they failed to
research CLC beforehand.  Little did they anticipate the bulldog tenacity
of Docdwarf, Richard and others. Reliable sources have informed me that the
combined debate skills of CLC have often placed the RW automation into an
advanced state of denial leading to a complete catatonic state. This has
had an extremely adverse effect on the budget for the project. DARPA was
forced to buy a top of the line IBM mainframe with the maximum real memory
possible. This was used to mutlitask a classified number of Eliza programs
to bring the RW automaton back from the fringes of sanity. One DARPA
researcher was heard to say that it was a miracle that IBM had developed
such a powerful 64 bit architecture just in time for their application
needs.
```

```
>loyal minion to do it.

There are some things that We do by hand, just as there are some places 
where We go by foot.

>
>Say hi and pet KILLER for me.

In return she says 'whurfle'... no walks around the lake today, the 
monsoons have returned to Maryland.

DD
```

```
first thing I thoughtof was Cosby's "Noah" sketch.

Another of my favourite lines from this is when the neighbour asks him to
get it out of his driveway. "What's it for?" "I can't tell you. How long can
you tread water?"

Best of the lot is when he gets really annoyed with God and is about to tear
it all to bits when the thunder booms and the rain starts... "This is not a
shower, right?" "OK God, You an' me...".

Never hear these old Cosby routines any more. There was an LP on vinyl
called "Bill Cosby at 200 Miles per Hour" in which he sends up cars in
general and the Shelby Cobra in particular. Can't get it now. (Well not in
NZ anyway). I hope we don't have to wait for him to die before they release
a collection on CD...

Pete.
```

## Thread: OT: Problem with MS Internet Explorer 5.5 — 27 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web) · [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT: Problem with MS Internet Explorer 5.5

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T04:24:47+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c32cac$b879e860$6995f243@chottel>`

```
Environment:

Pentium 133 Mhz, Windows 95 (pre FAT32 version)

Symptoms: 

Error message on screen said Windows could not read from the C: drive. Ran
Norton disk doctor which found some cross-linked files in directory
C;\Windows\System. Error message went away.

Problem:

Internet Explorer 5.5 no longer displays anything in it's main window.  It
looks like it reads the web page files but nothing is displayed. View ->
Source shows the HTML source in Notpad put IE is not displaying anything.
Some pages seem to have script errors. Error window Title bars mention
"script error" but the rest of the error window is also blank.

Attempts at solution:

Tried to reinstall IE 5.5, but Microsoft is not longer supporting it and
the files are no longer available.

Could not install IE 6.x because my operating system is too old.

Restore C:\ProgramFiles\Plus! and all of its subdirectories (this is where
ie 5.5 is located on my machine) from backup tape. This did not fix the
problem. I believe some .dll in C:\Windows\System is broken but I don't
know which one. I considered restoring C:\Windows\System but was afraid
this might break more than it would fix.

As a temporary workaround installed Netscape Communicator 4.5

Does anyone have any suggestions on what I might try next? I am finding it
pretty inconvenient to not have IE 5,5.  In particular Howard Hinnmans
NetCOBOL for .NET CDs will not work under Netscape Communicator. It starts
up but mentions an error in a script initialization file ( does not give
the file name in the error message). Thanks in advance.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Problem with MS Internet Explorer 5.5

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T05:13:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0306070413.491c0f49@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01c32cac$b879e860$6995f243@chottel>`

```
I am not sure whether it works for Windows 95, but you could try
re-installing Windows without reformatting your hard disk.  Under
Windows 98SE this is a way of repairing problems with Windows without
having to reinstall all your programs and data.  Your help system and
documentation should indicate if this is allowable, if not try asking
Microsoft or a Windows forum.  You might still have quite a bit of
work to do to re-establish program settings, defaults etc.

Another way that should definitely work is to reinstall Windows from a
re-formatted hard disk, obviously back up everything first.  This
would have the advantage of also thoroughly checking your hard disk
for incipient failure, which might anyway be the underlying cause of
the problem in the first place.  While long-winded, the process of
reinstalling anew might clear out a lot of clutter that can accumulate
from discarded programs that never uninstalled properly as was often
the case.  If you went this far, you might think it worthwhile trying
to get Windows 98SE, to upgrade your system at the same time, but
Microsoft no longer sells it, so it might be hard to come by.  The
problem with this route, is that you still need a fresh copy of the IE
5.5 installation files.

Another way, if it is available under Windows 95, could be to use the
repair facility for IE, there is such a feature in Windows 98 under
system tools, but it might be somewhere else on W95.

Another way, try to beg/borrow a copy of IE 5.5 from a friend with old
computer magazine freebie disk.  I can't remember when IE 5.5 was
superceded, but you might even be able to order an appropriate back
issue from a Computer Magazine company.

Another possibility is to try another browser, such as a more recent
(but not necessarily bang up-to-date) version of Netscape, Mozilla,
etc.  Netscape has/had lots of old versions on its website when last I
looked a few months ago.

Hope this helps,

Robert

> 
…[35 more quoted lines elided]…
> the file name in the error message). Thanks in advance.
```

```
1. Better to buy a new hard drive first. In our experience, a hard drive
lasts about three years in continuous service. 40 Gig drives are available
for < $80. If you want a new computer, I'm going to let you in on the
world's best kept secret: Good, fast computers cheap: wait for it now....
walmart.com. No joke. Extreme example: 1.1 GHz, 10 GigHD, < $200 (model
SYSMAR550).

2. Forget about Win98; it's at least 2 generations behind. Install XP with
NTFS instead of FAT32 (NTFS is self-healing).

> Another possibility is to try another browser, such as a more recent
```

#### ↳ Re: Problem with MS Internet Explorer 5.5

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T01:28:16+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee1e8cf_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<01c32cac$b879e860$6995f243@chottel>`

```
Charles,

I read this and my heart goes out to you... you are having a very hard time.

There is no easy fix that I can think of. Your best bet is to upgrade the
system. (Yeah, I know...).

It sounds like the disk could be suspect (how old is the system?).

I'd be tempted to replace the lot, given that you've had it for 5 years or
more. If you don't want to go that far, you should certainly upgrade the OS.

I ran a pentium 133 for a couple of years under Windows 98 and had very few
problems with it. This might be cheaper than going up to XP which would
prefer a more powerful processor anyway.

It should be possible to get a Win98 upgrade for something pretty reasonable
now. Make sure you go to SE (Second Edition) and don't under any
circumstances touch ME...

In closing, this was a really good example of how to post problems here. All
the symptoms described and the errors obtained.

Hope you get it solved.

Pete.
```

```
> >

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Hell Jerry, I hope you don't have kids...<G>.

Sure I do. Same as with avoiding those 'child proof' caps on medicines: I
want my kids to have all the opportunities growing up as I did.

As for the tires - I live in a city where walking is faster than driving -
not that anybody would actually WALK anywhere in Houston - so the chance of
a driving disaster is negligible.

Yet I do walk to work! I'm like the Chinese grocer who lives above his store
(Clinton said much the same thing about the White House but I resist quoting
him <spit>). Installing a door between the two halves of a duplex allows me
to live on one side and work on the other. One must be careful, however, to
not appear in the workplace wearing a Teddy after the staff has arrived.

>
> Tyres and brakes are something I never stint on. I tend to drive fairly
hard
> (NOT in built up areas; there is plenty of space here in NZ and, outside
the
> urban centres there is little traffic) and used to drive competitively
back
> in my reckless youth. With my luck, if I'm going to die in a car, it'll
> probably be steering failure <G>...

Reminds one of the story: "I want to die peacefully in my sleep like my
grandfather; not in fear and screaming like the passengers in his car."
```

```
quoting

> to live on one side and work on the other. One must be careful, however,
to
> not appear in the workplace wearing a Teddy after the staff has arrived.

ROFL!

(So now the truth is out about your cross dressing...<G>) Or could it be
that you are actually a woman and I have assumed all this time you are a
man... Thinking about it "JerryMouse" is sexless... hmmmm.)

> >
> > Tyres and brakes are something I never stint on. I tend to drive fairly
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Yes I saw that on a bumper sticker while visiting the States. It cracked me
up.

I've never been to Houston but I have a soft spot for the place. Once, years
ago as a student pilot I was not too sure exactly where I was...(yeah,
frightening to think the skies over Texas have people like that in them
isn't it <G>?) I had a VOR but I didn't have the confidence to be sure it
was the right one and that I was interpreting the output correctly. Dialled
up Houston in a very anxious state (not quite panic but definitely
"worried"). A relaxed, friendly, Texan told me to squawk a certain frequency
(I had no idea what the Hell he was talking about but he explained it
calmly...). Then he said "OK I have you..." He told me my altitude, heading,
and speed (which I already knew; that wasn't the problem...) then he said
(shades of MicroSoft): "So where would you like to go today?" I could've
kissed him. He gave me a flight level and a heading and I was "home" in
Dallas before my instructor had time to call out S&R. I think of Houston
with affection to this day.

Pete.
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ [OT] Flying stories. Was: Re: Problem with MS Internet Explorer 5.5

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T20:26:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<-6ScnY_VZ-naf36jXTWJkA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<01c32cac$b879e860$6995f243@chottel> <3ee1e8cf_2@news.athenanews.com> <01c32d17$c477f740$4ccaf943@chottel> <7Y-dnUxAAO32un-jXTWcpg@giganews.com> <3ee284d4$1_7@news.athenanews.com> <fK-dnZO4MvpOuX6jXTWJkw@giganews.com> <3ee3bde3_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
>
> I've never been to Houston but I have a soft spot for the place. Once,
years
> ago as a student pilot I was not too sure exactly where I was...(yeah,
> frightening to think the skies over Texas have people like that in them
> isn't it <G>?) I had a VOR but I didn't have the confidence to be sure it
> was the right one and that I was interpreting the output correctly.
Dialled
> up Houston in a very anxious state (not quite panic but definitely
> "worried"). A relaxed, friendly, Texan told me to squawk a certain
frequency
> (I had no idea what the Hell he was talking about but he explained it
> calmly...). Then he said "OK I have you..." He told me my altitude,
heading,
> and speed (which I already knew; that wasn't the problem...) then he said
> (shades of MicroSoft): "So where would you like to go today?" I could've
> kissed him. He gave me a flight level and a heading and I was "home" in
> Dallas before my instructor had time to call out S&R. I think of Houston
> with affection to this day.

Yep. The Houston tower and approach control folk are a pretty laid back
bunch.

You'll appreciate this (at 3 o'clock in the afternoon):

Pilot: "Houston Hobby, Cessna 324 inbound from Shreveport with Charlie."
Tower: "Cessna 324, squawk 300 and ident."
Pilot: "Squawking"
(pause)
Tower: "Cessna 324 I have you. You are 110 miles SSE of Houston. Recommend
you do a 180 and come to 045 degrees."
(pause)
Pilot: "You mean I missed the whole fuckin' city?"
(Houston is the 2nd or 3rd largest city in the nation in land area - it's
not like you don't notice if from the air.)

And my moment:

Me: "Hobby tower, Cessna 77 turning final for 10."
Tower: "Cessna 77, traffic is a 737 at 3 o'clock on short final for 80. Step
it up."
Me (looking right) (aw, shit!) "Cessna 77 roger."

(These little Cessna 150's do, like, 50mph on final. A Southwest Airlines
Boeing 737, flaps down, both engines breathing fire and smoke, weighing a
quarter-million pounds, fill up the entire window, and do, oh, 200mph.)

A few summers ago, a (female) student pilot rented a plane from her flying
school near Galveston Bay after work to drill a few holes in the sky. After
about an hour, she headed home. Oops! Fog.

She radioed her instructor who told her to head for the practice area and
he'd be up to get her. About 20 minutes later, he appeared (in another
plane, obviously) and told her to follow him, they were going to Hobby.
Thirty minutes later, as they approached Hobby, it too, was below VFR
minimums.

"Ok," said the instructor, "we're going to Houston Intercontinental." Twenty
minutes later (after sunset by now):

Instructor: "Approach Control, Cessna ABC, flight of two request approach."
TAC: "Cessna ABC, this field is commercial only."
Instructor: "We're low on fuel and declaring an emergency."
TAC: "Roger, Cessna ABC, contact tower on xxx. You are cleared for straight
in approach for runway 35 Left."

The next day (after long talks with the FAA), the two flew their planes back
to LaPorte, Texas where the lady had her log book signed off:

1. 1 landing and 1 take-off at a controlled field.
2. 1 cross-country trip of 50 miles or more
3. 1 night landing
4. 1 emergency landing.

I don't even think the school charged her for the plane rental.
```

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
> and don't under any circumstances touch ME...

Gee, Pete, you didn't have to get so personal...  I don't think he could 
reach you from where he is...  ;)

(Although I must agree with you about Windows ME - we had that here and 
had nothing but frustrations.  When we got our latest computer, it came 
with XP home, which was fine until I tried to establish network shares. 
  You get a grand total of 2 choices - "Share this with others" and 
"Allow others to update files".  As an NT/W2K administrator, that was 
exceedingly frustrating ("What? No permissions to set?").)
```

```
believe there is a way round it but it is not immediately obvious. For me,
the best solution was an upgrade to XP Pro. I needed IIS anyway, and,
although there are some non-MS -approved ways to get your old Win 98 IIS
running under XP, for serious development, this is a better option.

Pete.
```

```
The only FAT32 version went to OEMs. It was not released to the public.

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Your ISP should have a copy. Many Microsoft CDs contain a copy.

>know which one. I considered restoring C:\Windows\System but was afraid
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Problem with MS Internet Explorer 5.5

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T05:38:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0306080438.44d9c31c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<01c32cac$b879e860$6995f243@chottel> <3ee207de.109368958@news.optonline.net> <01c32d17$54fd73e0$4ccaf943@chottel> <3ee24e23.127360195@news.optonline.net>`

```
> "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

With regard to Jerrymouse's comments, yes a new machine would be
desirable if possible, but if not, then I very much doubt if Windows
XP would work effectively on the old one, if at all.

RW's idea seems good and involves the least effort, but you don't know
whether the new version of these files have been corrupted or how many
of were corrupted.  Perhaps, if this doesn't work, you should just
replace them as he first suggested and re-install any programs added
since your last backup before you had problems.

There is also the possibility of some other DLLs used for relatively
infrequently used features of your other programs having been
corrupted as well.

I think a thorough check of your hard disk with Scandisk would be a
good idea before you do anything else, then see whether any other
programs are also having trouble before you decide what to do about
this problem.

Robert
```

<a id="t-3979baf0de"></a>
## Thread: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X? — 45 msgs

### Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

- **From:** emmanuel.ichbiah@bigfoot.com (Emmanuel)
- **Date:** 2003-06-06T23:18:50-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com>`

```
I have a field in a C record that is defined as char,
it is used to store any value between 0 and 255.

I have a corresponding mapping for cobol, with that field defined as PIC X.

Will it work?

Beginner's question, I know! :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T06:42:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0306070542.4f135ebd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com>`

```
What matters here is if you want to get at the value as a number.  

Yes - the CHAR goes in a PIC X fine.  If you want to get it as a
number though, how you do it depends on your compiler.

With Micro Focus define it as PIC X COMP-X.

With Realia PIC S9(2) COMP. and set the smallbyte directive.

With Fujitsu use PIC S9(2) COMP-4 and set BINARY(BYTE) directive. 
There may be side effects from these directives depending on how
properly your other binary fields are defined.


emmanuel.ichbiah@bigfoot.com (Emmanuel) wrote in message news:<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com>...
> I have a field in a C record that is defined as char,
> it is used to store any value between 0 and 255.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Beginner's question, I know! :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T16:38:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbtm0p$6fr$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com> <bfdfc3e8.0306070542.4f135ebd@posting.google.com>`

```
And as stated earlier, with any compiler with the ANSI/ISO Intrinsic
Functions, use the ORD intrinsic function (and subtract 1 - if you want a
value between 0 and 255 - rather than 1 and 256)
```

#### ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T08:58:25-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bbsr23$jle$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com>`

```
Yes it will work.  Look at the COBOL intrinsic functions "ORD" and "CHAR"
for additional information on handling this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-07T15:13:00-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE2474C.4040307@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com>`

```
Emmanuel wrote:
> I have a field in a C record that is defined as char,
> it is used to store any value between 0 and 255.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Beginner's question, I know! :-)

You can move 0 to 255 characters to a field defined as Pic X(255). 
However, you'll have spaces out the end.  Check out the 
Occurs...Depending On syntax.  According to my manual, you can have 0 in 
this, something like

01  MyTable.
     03  MyTable-Data       Occurs 0 to 255 Times
                              Depending on MySize
                                Pic X(01).
01  MySize                      Pic 9(03).

Then, if you're moving 50 characters into it, move 50 to MySize and move 
your text to MyTable.  It will then be 50 characters, with no spaces on 
the end.
```

```

> I have a field in a C record that is defined as char,
> it is used to store any value between 0 and 255.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T12:23:18+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee28250_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com> <3EE26A90.10408@Knology.net>`

```

"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3EE26A90.10408@Knology.net...
> Emmanuel wrote:
> > I have a field in a C record that is defined as char,
> > it is used to store any value between 0 and 255.
> >
> > I have a corresponding mapping for cobol, with that field defined as PIC
X.
> >
> > Will it work?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

It happens and most of us do it at some time <G>.

I was more concerned at you recommending OCCURS...DEPENDING (sometimes
referred to as ODO).

On most platforms this is a construct "best avoided" except when there is no
other way (and there usually is...).

It is ugly ( my opinion), prone to error (I have seen more than a couple of
cases where the count field gets clobbered by accident and the programmer
spends many hours looking for non-existent errors elsewhere), and involves
some very nasty overheads (at machine level).

Personally, I will only use it if the incoming data has been written in that
format. I would NEVER use it as a choice in WS.

If I had an application that required completely variable output, I would
change the design if possible, and, if not, use byte stream IO. (This would
have to be read by ODO or byte stream. The answer is not to get in that
situation ion the first place...<G>)

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T19:12:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee38053.205756024@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com> <3EE26A90.10408@Knology.net> <3ee28250_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
I agree that ODO isn't very useful in working-storage, where fields are
pre-allocated to their maximum size. But I find it very useful in dynamically
allocated structures such as lines of text or a list of words. Rather than
allocating 512 bytes for every line of text, I allocate its actual size. Rather
than allocating 30 bytes per word, thereby imposing a limit (as COBOL does), I
allocate the word's size. 



"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

>
>"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T10:35:52+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee3baa9_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com> <3EE26A90.10408@Knology.net> <3ee28250_4@news.athenanews.com> <3ee38053.205756024@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ee38053.205756024@news.optonline.net...
> I agree that ODO isn't very useful in working-storage, where fields are
> pre-allocated to their maximum size. But I find it very useful in
dynamically
> allocated structures such as lines of text or a list of words. Rather than
> allocating 512 bytes for every line of text, I allocate its actual size.
Rather
> than allocating 30 bytes per word, thereby imposing a limit (as COBOL
does), I
> allocate the word's size.
>

COBOL allocates the maximum size anyway. Unless you call API,  malloc, or
GETMAIN (for the mainframe environments)  to do true dynamic allocation,
WHEREVER you use OCCURS...DEPENDING, COBOL allocates the maximum size.

It is a pointless construct that lets people THINK they are being clever.

The only REAL value it has is for reading a variable length file, where
there are count fields on the records. Even then a maximum size buffer is
allocated by COBOL.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T11:45:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0306091045.62037dd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com> <3EE26A90.10408@Knology.net> <3ee28250_4@news.athenanews.com> <3ee38053.205756024@news.optonline.net> <3ee3baa9_6@news.athenanews.com>`

```
See notes below

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3ee3baa9_6@news.athenanews.com>...
> "Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
> news:3ee38053.205756024@news.optonline.net...
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

Two potential benefits of using ODO are when sorting and randomly
searching tables that do not always contain the same number of
elements.

When an ODO is a sending operand the compiler should use the defined
size according to the depending on variable to specify the amount of
data to be processed, such a feature can have its uses, though I admit
I haven't used it much if at all except when dealing with variable
length record files.

The rules for receiving operands are similar, but only when the
depending on variable is outside the ODO group that is referenced as a
receiving operand.

Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T11:10:15+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee51434_7@news.athenanews.com>`

```
news:fcd09c56.0306091045.62037dd@posting.google.com...
> See notes below
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You can get the same effect by initializing a table of maximum size to be
high values or whatever, then terminate your search on this value. This has
the added advantage that you can store the entries in sequence with a binary
chop when you load it, and there is no need to sort the table in memory.

(in 37 years I have NEVER sorted a table in memory...but then I'm not a
computer scientist <G>. I don't care about sort algorithms and Fibonnaci
series...)


> When an ODO is a sending operand the compiler should use the defined
> size according to the depending on variable to specify the amount of
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The overheads in this are unacceptable to me.

> The rules for receiving operands are similar, but only when the
> depending on variable is outside the ODO group that is referenced as a
> receiving operand.
>

You have still failed to demonstrate how this can ONLY be achieved by using
ODO.

It is a pointless and dangerous construct, best avoided.

Pete.

> Robert
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Sort table (was: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T14:42:40-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc5cbk$a0a$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com> <3EE26A90.10408@Knology.net> <3ee28250_4@news.athenanews.com> <3ee38053.205756024@news.optonline.net> <3ee3baa9_6@news.athenanews.com> <fcd09c56.0306091045.62037dd@posting.google.com> <3ee51434_7@news.athenanews.com> <bc4pop$h76$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bc4pop$h76$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On  9-Jun-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I've only done so a handful of times - but never for "computer science"
type
> reasons.

I *know* that I have used a "serial search" when I (sort-of) wanted to do a
binary sort (SEARCH ALL) - because a table was not already "in order".  To
me that is a major advantage of the 2002 Standard "table sort" feature.
Although such a COBOL language "table sort" may perform  (slightly - or not
at all) worse than a "hand-crafted" sort of a table, I have always
questioned the "maintainability" of the latter.  It will be interesting to
me, how much greater use of SEARCH ALL will occur when/if the "table" option
of the SORT statement becomes generally available.

Clearly run-time considerations need to be dealt with when using such
features.  If a table needs to be sorted ONCE in an application's logic but
SEARCHED *many* times, I would think that such would be beneficial.
However, if it is only SEARCHED a "few" times, the overhead of a SORT
(assuming the original input is NOT in sorted order) probably would not be
warranted.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort table (was: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T20:02:31+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc5dgn$pno$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<2dfb6ff9.0306062218.5b3bdf5c@posting.google.com> <3EE26A90.10408@Knology.net> <3ee28250_4@news.athenanews.com> <3ee38053.205756024@news.optonline.net> <3ee3baa9_6@news.athenanews.com> <fcd09c56.0306091045.62037dd@posting.google.com> <3ee51434_7@news.athenanews.com> <bc4pop$h76$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <bc5cbk$a0a$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I had to modify a program that already had a CoBOL sort in it.   It produced
detail lines under a header that needed to be sorted by subcode.  There aren't a
lot of detail lines per header, but there were LOTS of header lines.    Detail
lines were saved in a table and the header was created (with such data as
detail-record count).

A better design might be to design header and detail lines with a sort key for
an external sort when done writing.   But that wasn't an option for me.

Meanwhile I wanted to sort the detail lines.   So I wrote a table sort.   An OO
person would have created a table sort object.   I could have called an external
sort routine.   I wrote a simple comb sort (a bubble sort might have been
simpler, and probably about as fast for these small sorts - but adding one line
to turn it into a comb sort didn't cost much).   I added a comment that the next
compiler would probably have a table sort to replace that paragraph.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sort table (was: Is it possible to put any character (0 to 255) in a PIC X?

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
