<a id="t-3fd66bb2ea"></a>
## Thread: ODO's and "IBM mentality" — 7 msgs

### ODO's and "IBM mentality"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-08T22:23:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc0ujr$tje$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up on a different thread, I thought that I would point out two
IBM "extensions" that deal with ODO's (Occurs Depending On) structures.  I
know that Micro Focus has an emulation for these; I assume that Realia does,
and wouldn't be surprised if others do as well.  I am *not* (necessarily)
saying that these are "wonderful" constructs - but they are cases where
"existing" (and new) programs designed for IBM environments find ODO's
particularly useful.

"Feature" 1 - nested ODO.

 Consider a structure of the format:

  01 aRec.
       05 Num1    Pic 99.
       05 Num2    Pic 99.
       05 Level1 Occurs 1 to 99 times
                         depending on Num1.
             10  Elem1    Pic X.
             10  Level2  Occurs 1 to 50 times
                                Depending on Num2
                   15 Elem2   Pic XX.


This type of "structure" where the nested ODO "floats" within the containing
ODO is particularly "useful" for modeling some "real-world" structures.  In
the IBM extension, the maximum STORAGE may be allocated - but the references
are to the "user-friendly" (well at least friendly to me <G>) locations.


"Feature" 2 -  ODO with following data

 Consider a structure of the format:

  01 aRec.
       05  Num1   Pic 99.
       05  Level1 occurs 1 to 99 times
                         depending on Num1
             10  Elem1   Pic X.
       05  After-ODO    Pic X(10).

With this IBM extension, the "location" of the data-item After-ODO "floats"
within aRec depending on the CURRENT size of Num1.

    ***

It should be noted that BOTH of these structures are medium-error-prone for
those NOT used to them - but work "as expected" (and self-documenting) for
those who have "grown up" in an IBM COBOL environment.  OBVIOUSLY, such code
is *not* portable to "strictly" ANSI/ISO conforming compilers, but does
provide valid (?) functionality within a "strictly" IBM mainframe
environment.
```

#### ↳ Re: ODO's and "IBM mentality"

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T22:54:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ee467e1_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bc0ujr$tje$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I disagree vehemently, Bill.

Comments below...

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bc0ujr$tje$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
> As a follow-up on a different thread, I thought that I would point out two
> IBM "extensions" that deal with ODO's (Occurs Depending On) structures.  I
> know that Micro Focus has an emulation for these; I assume that Realia
does,
> and wouldn't be surprised if others do as well.  I am *not* (necessarily)
> saying that these are "wonderful" constructs - but they are cases where
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> This type of "structure" where the nested ODO "floats" within the
containing
> ODO is particularly "useful" for modeling some "real-world" structures.

OK, name one...

Then show how it couldn't be achieved WITHOUT ODO...

> In
> the IBM extension, the maximum STORAGE may be allocated - but the
references
> are to the "user-friendly" (well at least friendly to me <G>) locations.
>

So, you agree the maximum storage is allocated?

Rest my case.

>
> "Feature" 2 -  ODO with following data
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> With this IBM extension, the "location" of the data-item After-ODO
"floats"
> within aRec depending on the CURRENT size of Num1.
>

Yes, there WAS a time when we did this. After we realised the horrific code
it generates and recognised how error prone it is , most of us STOPPED doing
it 30 years ago! (And before Doc chimes in with "plural Majestatus est" I
mean "we" in the sense of the group of mainframe programmers I worked with
on various sites throughout the sixties and into the early 70s. By 1973 I
didn't know a single person who would endorse this data structure.)

Even the die-hards who insist on using ODO, for the most part, realise that
the ODO field MUST be the LAST field in the record.

If people are still doing this (and I believe your argument is predicated on
the fact that they are) then it's time SOMEONE pointed out to them the
dangers inherent in doing so and the fact that it isimply isn't NECESSARY.

>     ***
>
> It should be noted that BOTH of these structures are medium-error-prone
for
> those NOT used to them - but work "as expected" (and self-documenting) for
> those who have "grown up" in an IBM COBOL environment.

Bollocks, Bill!

They work "as expected" for anyone who uses them. It doesn't alter the fact
that they are clumsy and inefficient, as well as UNNECESSARY.



>  OBVIOUSLY, such code
> is *not* portable to "strictly" ANSI/ISO conforming compilers, but does
> provide valid (?) functionality within a "strictly" IBM mainframe
> environment.
>

Yes, the functionality is valid but so is GO TO and ALTER.  (These two
constructs are at least efficient, whatever else they may be...).

My point is they are UNNECESSARY. I defy you to show me an instance where
they can do something that standard COBOL definitions cannot.

(Actually, I could forgive them being unnecessary; in COBOL there is usually
more than one way to skin a cat... What REALLY pisses me off about them is
that they lure people into a sense of false security by appearing to be
"clever" when, in fact, all they are is DANGEROUS!.)

OK, I'm calm now...<G>

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ODO's and "IBM mentality"

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T15:17:03+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Px1Fa.195900$ja4.10382026@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bc0ujr$tje$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ee467e1_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3ee467e1_4@news.athenanews.com...


In general, I agree with your assessment.

I disagree on the issue of allocating storage.
We now have the storage for large tables.
I have changed some of my practices due to the fact that I no longer need to
be a storage miser (both in-core, and DASD).
If it's within reason, I avoid using redefines in records, especially when
numeric, and character data can occupy the same physical area of a record.
I will probably never again write a program to keep the last n records of a
randomly accessed file to improve I/O performance. I will just extract what
I have to keep and store it all in a table.
16 meg will hold over 200,000 80 byte records, enough to store most
transaction files in a table. I've seen programs that store 100 records in a
table, and use DASD to store any overflow. Now, there is no real need to do
so.
The net result is cleaner programs which address business processing without
the overhead and complexity of intermixed storage management.

WAR STORY: I got a chuckle, and the comment "Wow, I've never seen a picture
X eight million before" when I asked someone to review a series of programs.
```

```
Er...I don't have a position on this so I'm not sure what you are
disagreeing with <G>. I have no problem with people allocating storage,
whatever environment they are working in. I have a problem with them
thinking OCCURS...DEPENDING does this for them.

> We now have the storage for large tables.
> I have changed some of my practices due to the fact that I no longer need
to
> be a storage miser (both in-core, and DASD).
> If it's within reason, I avoid using redefines in records, especially when
> numeric, and character data can occupy the same physical area of a record.
> I will probably never again write a program to keep the last n records of
a
> randomly accessed file to improve I/O performance. I will just extract
what
> I have to keep and store it all in a table.
> 16 meg will hold over 200,000 80 byte records, enough to store most
> transaction files in a table. I've seen programs that store 100 records in
a
> table, and use DASD to store any overflow. Now, there is no real need to
do
> so.
> The net result is cleaner programs which address business processing
without
> the overhead and complexity of intermixed storage management.
>
> WAR STORY: I got a chuckle, and the comment "Wow, I've never seen a
picture
> X eight million before" when I asked someone to review a series of
programs.
>

While this may seem a little "excessive", if your environment supports it
and it makes sense, I would have no problem with people doing what you are
doing. It is no more difficult to understand and maintain than normal COBOL.
(In fact, for you at least, it IS normal COBOL...)

There is one point I would raise on this... Can you be sure that grabbing
huge areas of virtual memory will not have an overall debilitating effect on
other concurrent processes? If you can confidently say "No", then there can
be no objection to it.

Pete.
>
>
>
```

```
No, but it does allocate the maximum storage, and I thought that you thought
that it may be excessive.

|
| > We now have the storage for large tables.
| > I have changed some of my practices due to the fact that I no longer
need
| to
| > be a storage miser (both in-core, and DASD).
| > If it's within reason, I avoid using redefines in records, especially
when

record.

of
| a
| > randomly accessed file to improve I/O performance. I will just extract
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| > transaction files in a table. I've seen programs that store 100 records
in
| a
| > table, and use DASD to store any overflow. Now, there is no real need to
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
| doing. It is no more difficult to understand and maintain than normal
COBOL.
| (In fact, for you at least, it IS normal COBOL...)

It wasn't "normal" COBOL, it was a cross between applications, and systems
type processing. (the 8 meg thing)
I needed a huge buffer to store variable length records.
It was a learn as you go project.
I did display how much of the buffer was actually being used, planning to
cut it back after I got statistics.
After reviewing the statistics, I figured I had enough storage to handle a
worst case scenario.

|
| There is one point I would raise on this... Can you be sure that grabbing
| huge areas of virtual memory will not have an overall debilitating effect
on
| other concurrent processes? If you can confidently say "No", then there
can
| be no objection to it.

I haven't seen any instances of paging being done in the batch environment
in quite some time. I would assume, probably wrongly, that excessive storage
allocation would  cause some page swapping.

On a CICS project, I contacted the CICS systems programmer, said I would
need 3 meg of storage if I used Temporary Storage Queue processing. He said
not to worry it wasn't a big deal, go ahead and use Temp Storage.

I really don't know how much storage mainframes come with now, but from what
I've seen using a meg or 2 of storage isn't going to slow the system down.
People regularly increase the number of file buffers to speed up processing.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ODO's and "IBM mentality"

- **From:** Patrick Herring <"ph"@$pamblock.anweald.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T12:38:13+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc4fv6$80h$1@hermes.shef.ac.uk>`
- **References:** `<bc0ujr$tje$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ee467e1_4@news.athenanews.com>`

```
"Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
> 
> I disagree vehemently, Bill.

I agree and disagree.
 
> Comments below...
> 
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
> news:bc0ujr$tje$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
...
> > "Feature" 2 -  ODO with following data
> >
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> simply isn't NECESSARY.

ODO tables are useful, I've found, for variable-length linkage records,
particularly when the calling program is not COBOL and expects to deal
in variable length strings. You have to have a maximum length of course,
and know where the length is recorded so you can first move it to the
ODO number field, but if the called program walks off the end of the
memory set up by the calling program you get a proper exception not a
weird uninitialised storage bug, which is good. I'm not sure how that is
implemented - it implies that the linkage storage isn't addressable
until call time, which makes sense, but also that the COBOL run-unit
knows what storage has been allocated by the calling program, which may
not be in COBOL, which is a bit magical.

Another situation where I've seen ODO used well is in record formats for
variable-length record files - the particular case I recall had wildly
varying lengths and a /lot/ of space would have been wasted, enough to
significantly effect ftp times. But to actually save file space we had
to have just one ODO table and put it at the end, due to the maximum
size being compiled in as Peter points out, so those IBM extensions had
no point there.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ODO's and "IBM mentality"

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T12:44:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0pkFa.161$0v4.14030@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bc0ujr$tje$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ee467e1_4@news.athenanews.com> <bc4fv6$80h$1@hermes.shef.ac.uk>`

```

news:bc4fv6$80h$1@hermes.shef.ac.uk...
| "Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
| >

|
| Another situation where I've seen ODO used well is in record formats for
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
|

Another option is to use the RECORD IS VARYING DEPENDING ON, FD
specification.
A Working-Storage (or Linkage Section) variable is used to store the length.
of the record.
Reads update the length, writes use the length.
```

<a id="t-01da46495e"></a>
## Thread: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols" — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T17:29:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc31nh$rnb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
FYI,
  The *replacement* paper submitted for J4 consideration on "symbols" may be
viewed at:

    http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/03-0147.doc

It should be noted that *IF* this suggestion were followed, that there still
would be *no* symbol for the word "NOT" in relational conditions, e.g.

  If  A not < B
     could only be "changed" to symbols by "switching" the logic to
  If A >= B

While

  If Not A < B
    could be changed to
  If ! A < B  (or one of the other "suggested" symbols for NOT)
```

#### ↳ Re: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T16:37:32-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc35ns$1v0v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
news:bc31nh$rnb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net...

> It should be noted that *IF* this suggestion were followed, that there
still
> would be *no* symbol for the word "NOT" in relational conditions, e.g.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>   If ! A < B  (or one of the other "suggested" symbols for NOT)

Not sure why something like "If A not < B" couldn't be changed to "If A ! <
B", so long as there's a separator space between the "!" and the "<".  Could
you clarify?

    -Chuck Stevens
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T08:49:51-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc4nm3$6b1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bc31nh$rnb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <bc35ns$1v0v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
The "NOT" in the *relational* operators is a reserved word (part of) the
relational operator - it is NOT a logical operator. Robert's (current)
proposal *only* adds symbols for the LOGICAL operator "NOT".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T09:52:16-07:00

- **Message-ID:** `<bc52c0$85l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bc4nm3$6b1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
> The "NOT" in the *relational* operators is a reserved word (part of) the
> relational operator - it is NOT a logical operator. Robert's (current)
> proposal *only* adds symbols for the LOGICAL operator "NOT".

Thanks for the clarification.

... Which is why I prefer empowering REPLACE.  You pays your money, you
takes your choice, you get what you get.  You mess up, it's on you.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T22:06:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PDsFa.725$3o3.40038@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bc31nh$rnb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <bc35ns$1v0v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bc4nm3$6b1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bc52c0$85l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:bc52c0$85l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
|
| "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
|

The problem is, it's not on you.
It on everyone who has to maintain the system.
If this is done during compiler installation, either no decision will be
made, the right decision (what *I* want), or the wrong decision (NOT what
*I* want).
If there are 3 programmers working on a system, and they can each implement
their own preference, or change their preference in mid-stream, there is no
consistency.

Suppose they choose @, #, and %?

Could I choose BEAM for PERFORM , and ABOARD for TO.
MOVE MST-EMP-NAME TO PRT-EMP-NAME.
Becomes
BEAM MST-EMP-NAME ABOARD PRT-EMP-NAME.
Treckies will love it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T15:46:17-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc5n3p$msl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bc31nh$rnb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <bc35ns$1v0v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bc4nm3$6b1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bc52c0$85l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PDsFa.725$3o3.40038@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:PDsFa.725$3o3.40038@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...

> | ... Which is why I prefer empowering REPLACE.  You pays your money, you
> | takes your choice, you get what you get.  You mess up, it's on you.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It on everyone who has to maintain the system.

True enough.  I'm presuming, perhaps incorrectly, that anyone who would
construct such a REPLACE would be doing so with the permission of the
employer.  As far as I'm concerned, arbitrating issues of *style* is outside
the purview of the standard.  I can envision expanding the power of REPLACE
(through expanding the permissible information in pseudo-text-1) being a
useful enhancement in general.

> If this is done during compiler installation, either no decision will be
> made, the right decision (what *I* want), or the wrong decision (NOT what
> *I* want).

I wasn't expecting the choice to be made at compiler installation; I was
expecting it to be encoded -- either through COPY or directly -- in the
program.

> If there are 3 programmers working on a system, and they can each
implement
> their own preference, or change their preference in mid-stream, there is
no
> consistency.

Yup.

> Suppose they choose @, #, and %?

Suppose they do.  I can think of all sorts of ways to obscure code.  I
remember one time researching a bug that turned out to be somewhere in
twelve pages of the most gawdawful code, and right in the middle of it was
the comment "Yes, this is what you think it is!".  Took me three weeks to
figure out how to get there; turned out to be a compile-time optimization
for SUBTRACT X FROM X.  I ripped out the entire optimization and the problem
went away.

I also remember a COBOL cross-reference utility written in COBOL by a
Fortran programmer who had just discovered the joys of the ALTER verb.  I
submitted a patch to the plant and they rejected it on the grounds that
nobody knew enough about the utility to judge the patch; they discontinued
the product about a year later.

The authors of the standard can't be expected to prevent people from
shooting themselves in the foot.  It's up to the programmers to learn how to
use the weapon wisely.

> Could I choose BEAM for PERFORM , and ABOARD for TO.

Yup.  Sure could.  But that doesn't match your example:

> MOVE MST-EMP-NAME TO PRT-EMP-NAME.
> Becomes
> BEAM MST-EMP-NAME ABOARD PRT-EMP-NAME.

Not quite.  But you could code the latter, and have the compiler treat it as
equivalent to the former, right now, with "REPLACE == BEAM == BY == MOVE ==,
== ABOARD == BY == TO ==." so long as the REPLACE preceded the BEAM
statement.  With a little adjustment (to use COPY ... REPLACING instead of
REPLACE) I can do so with a '74 compiler of my acquaintance as well.  That's
the better part of thirty years worth of being able to do things like this.

What's your point?

> Treckies will love it.

Well, I guess I'm not a trekkie then!

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T13:16:46+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2ZFFa.1330$3o3.111558@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<bc31nh$rnb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <bc35ns$1v0v$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <bc4nm3$6b1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bc52c0$85l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <PDsFa.725$3o3.40038@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <bc5n3p$msl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```

"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote in message
news:bc5n3p$msl$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com...
|
| "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
| news:PDsFa.725$3o3.40038@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
|
| > | ... Which is why I prefer empowering REPLACE.  You pays your money,
you
| > | takes your choice, you get what you get.  You mess up, it's on you.
| > |
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
| employer.

Should be true, but too often it isn't.

|As far as I'm concerned, arbitrating issues of *style* is outside
| the purview of the standard.  I can envision expanding the power of
REPLACE
| (through expanding the permissible information in pseudo-text-1) being a
| useful enhancement in general.

It may be for some, but I won't tell anyone about it.
The trekkie thing was tongue in cheek, but someone will do it or something
similar if they know about it.
For the most part, it isn't used.
I will probably never use it myself.
COPY REPLACING seems to cover the situations where I would want to use a
facility like this.

I'm not saying that the facility isn't useful, it may be in some situations,
if the modifications you propose are adopted, I won't object.
|
| > If this is done during compiler installation, either no decision will be
| > made, the right decision (what *I* want), or the wrong decision (NOT
what
| > *I* want).
|
| I wasn't expecting the choice to be made at compiler installation; I was
| expecting it to be encoded -- either through COPY or directly -- in the
| program.

My main concern is using symbols for AND, OR, and NOT.
If they are fixed, no problem, but if they can be selected from a menu, it
would be better to specify them at the shop level.
Large scale shops move people around from application to application.
If the language modification is at the installation level there is little
concern, if it's at the program level then you could wind up with a mini
tower-of-babble.
I know what people should do, isn't what they do.
I still come across "cute" programs with paragraphs named BILL, or MONICA.

|
| > If there are 3 programmers working on a system, and they can each
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
| Suppose they do.  I can think of all sorts of ways to obscure code.

They wouldn't be doing it to obscure code.
Not all COBOL programmers know other languages.
Many that I have worked with know COBOL, and Easytrieve, and that's it.
To many, it's just a job, they have a book that they refer to, not a manual.
It wouldn't be intentional.

@ resembles the O in or.
# looks like a plus, use it for AND
they could choose & for NOT, because it looks like a knot.

| I
| remember one time researching a bug that turned out to be somewhere in
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| for SUBTRACT X FROM X.  I ripped out the entire optimization and the
problem
| went away.
|
| I also remember a COBOL cross-reference utility written in COBOL by a
| Fortran programmer who had just discovered the joys of the ALTER verb.

ROTLMAO. (sorry, I've seen similar)

|  I
| submitted a patch to the plant and they rejected it on the grounds that
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
| shooting themselves in the foot.  It's up to the programmers to learn how
to
| use the weapon wisely.

Sometimes big brother is watchin, sometimes not.
For the most part, nobody is watching, especially with non-technical
management.

|
| > Could I choose BEAM for PERFORM , and ABOARD for TO.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
| Not quite.  But you could code the latter, and have the compiler treat it
as
| equivalent to the former, right now, with "REPLACE == BEAM == BY == MOVE
==,
| == ABOARD == BY == TO ==." so long as the REPLACE preceded the BEAM
| statement.  With a little adjustment (to use COPY ... REPLACING instead of
| REPLACE) I can do so with a '74 compiler of my acquaintance as well.
That's
| the better part of thirty years worth of being able to do things like
this.

You're right. You can do it!
|
| What's your point?

The point is that if people can modify the language, someone will do the
trekkie thing, and, it will be blocked by management, right after the first
abuse is discovered. (Probably when someone has to modify the system 3 years
down the road.)

| > Treckies will love it.
|
| Well, I guess I'm not a trekkie then!

Do you have any where you work?
```

#### ↳ Re: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-09T18:48:11-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306091748.2e8bffb7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bc31nh$rnb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote 

> It should be noted that *IF* this suggestion were followed, that there still
> would be *no* symbol for the word "NOT" in relational conditions, e.g.

Nor for NOT in the syntax of many statements.

>   If ! A < B  (or one of the other "suggested" symbols for NOT)

Robert's 'proposal' is also ambiguous.  It is not clear whether the
'or' between symbols was meant to represent a choice to be made by the
J4 committee, or for one to be made by individual programmers.  Did he
mean "to add '/\' _or_ '&&' _or_ '^'" or did he mean "to add '/\'
_and_ '&&' _and_ '^'" as symbols for 'and' ?

Can Robert tell the difference ?

Or will these be left to the implementor so that some compilers allow
'/\' and some '&&'.

Is it intended that it be possible to write:

    IF ! ( ~ ( ï¿½ ( NOT ( A = 1 ))))

Does Robert think that random collections of squiggles from several
different places is an _aid_ to program maintenence.

Does he think that '/\' and '\/' will be 'welcomed by programmers ..
C++ and Java' ?  - I don't think so.

Or will '^' be welcomed by VB programmers - power of in an if ?

Will '|' be welcomed by C/Java - bitwise or ?

Will any programmer welcome ?:

    IF X = 1 && Y = 2 /\ Z = 3 ^ A = 4 \/ X = 2 || Z = 5 

But, of course, the proposal has nothing to do with _any_ of this.  It
is merely a stick to beat c.l.c. with:

    They don't want choice.
    The J4 just rejects change.
    They want to stay in the dark ages.
    You chose the wrong ones.
    It all _your_ fault that Cobol died, _I_ tried to change it.

Robert is just acting like a script kiddie flame baiter.  His
'argument' style is to:

    Take a stance through ignorence. 
    Ignore any and all rational arguments against him.
    Dismiss everything that doesn't agree with him.
    Claim clc is arguing because RW's superior 'intelligence'
         threatens them  !hah.
    Continue to argue until told to fuck off.
    Claim a 'win'.
    Go elsewhere and complain that c.l.c.'quibbles'.
```

#### ↳ Re: (replacement) Paper for J4 on "symbols"

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T21:21:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306112021.6f183986@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bc31nh$rnb$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote

>   The *replacement* paper submitted for J4 consideration on "symbols" may be
> viewed at:

RW> I feel these symbols would welcomed by younger programmers
RW> coming from other programming language cultures,
RW> especially C++ and Java.

I showed this proposal to a programmer who uses C++ and Java
extensively and he was really excited by the prospect
of working on Cobol - which he had only briefly looked at.

"""  --------------------------------------------------------
I recall looking at some COBOL code and being really confused
by the condition expressions.  While the comparison operators
were fine, the logic operators I just couldn't tell apart, I
mean who thought up using characters that were so easily
confused: 'a' and 'o' are almost the same and 'r' is just an
'n' with a leg cut off. 'd' and 't' are almost like mirror
images so it is very easy to mix up 'and' and 'not' if you
miss the 2 first characters being the other order.

I tried to learn some sort of trick to tell them apart and
thought 'd' was sort of like a '+' so that was anding, but
then 't' is more like a '+' so that didn't work.

Trying to write these was even worse, I would put 'art' and
'on' and 'rod' and 'nod' and they looked OK to me, but the
compiler rejected them.

Now when we get symbols for these there will no longer be
any impediment, I will rewrite all my existing programs from
C++ and Java into COBOL and will change over to that language
entirely.  Using ` ~ and ^ will just seem so natural and
friendly.

Thank you, Robert for looking after our interests so well.
""" ------------------------------------------------------
```

## Thread: COBOL FAQ — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### COBOL FAQ

- **From:** wmklein@ix.netcom.com (William M. Klein)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T08:58:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol,news.answers,comp.answers
- **Message-ID:** `<computer-lang/cobol-faq_1055235494@rtfm.mit.edu>`

```
Archive-name: computer-lang/cobol-faq
Posting-Frequency: twice a month
Last-Modified: 2002/05/06
Version: 3.03
URL: http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm
Reply-To: wmklein@ix.netcom.com


                            COBOL FAQ
                  (Frequently Asked Questions)

Last-Modified: Spring 2002

The full COBOL FAQ can be viewed at:

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

    wmklein <at> ix.netcom.com

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more
```

#### ↳ Re: copybook with values

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T20:37:02-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EL-dnWITXd8jGnujXTWcrg@comcast.com>`
- **References:** `<oVBka.6765$zc.242345@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
In article <oVBka.6765$zc.242345@news20.bellglobal.com>, paulhbliu@hotmail.com 
wrote:

>Here I have a stupid question for copybook structure
>Does any one know what's the meaning of filler in below copybook fragment
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>01850000

FILLER is used to indicate extra unused space and is ignored, but I
don't undertsand why someone placed the FILLER in this case within
the OCCURS portion; there are 50*975 characters of wasted space here! :-| 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: copybook with values

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-10T22:31:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306102131.4f7c8660@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<oVBka.6765$zc.242345@news20.bellglobal.com> <EL-dnWITXd8jGnujXTWcrg@comcast.com>`

```
weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote 

> >Will it be here ready for putting the ACCT-NUM information ?
> >
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> FILLER is used to indicate extra unused space and is ignored, 

FILLER is used to indicate unreferenced areas, not unused areas.

In this case the whole 1000 bytes can be accessed using
~-Output-Data[n] but only the first 25 bytes can be accessed as
specific parts of that by name.

> I don't undertsand why someone placed the FILLER in this case within
> the OCCURS portion; there are 50*975 characters of wasted space here! :-|

Not wasted.  The area may be used to store 50 1000 byte items.  To use
the whole 1000 bytes it needs to be moved out of here to, say, an FD
record.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: copybook with values

- **From:** weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous)
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T11:52:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc755f$t5f$2@news.utelfla.com>`
- **References:** `<oVBka.6765$zc.242345@news20.bellglobal.com> <EL-dnWITXd8jGnujXTWcrg@comcast.com> <217e491a.0306102131.4f7c8660@posting.google.com>`

```
In alt.cobol Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
: weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote 

:> >Will it be here ready for putting the ACCT-NUM information ?
:> >
:> >018100    07  :ACC:-OUTPUT-DATA OCCURS 50 TIMES.             01810000
:> >018200       15  :ACC:-ACCT-NUM-PREF                  PIC X(4).
:> >01820000
:> >018300       15  :ACC:-ACCT-NUM-ATT                   PIC X(1).
:> >01830000
:> >018400       15  :ACC:-ACCT-NUM                       PIC X(20).
:> >01840000
:> >018500       15  FILLER                               PIC X(975).
:> >01850000
:> 
:> FILLER is used to indicate extra unused space and is ignored, 
:
: FILLER is used to indicate unreferenced areas, not unused areas.
:
: In this case the whole 1000 bytes can be accessed using
: Output-Data[n] but only the first 25 bytes can be accessed as
: specific parts of that by name.

I think we're on the same page here. The 975 bytes are in the record
but ignored (unavailable). I am guessing one could circumvent this
with something like Out-put-Data[n](25:975), but that's being silly. :-)

:> I don't undertsand why someone placed the FILLER in this case within
:> the OCCURS portion; there are 50*975 characters of wasted space here! :-|

: Not wasted.  The area may be used to store 50 1000 byte items.  To use
: the whole 1000 bytes it needs to be moved out of here to, say, an FD
: record.

What I mean is 50 records with 975 extra bytes allocated seems mighty
wasteful to me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: copybook with values

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-11T12:43:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306111143.399ced5c@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<oVBka.6765$zc.242345@news20.bellglobal.com> <EL-dnWITXd8jGnujXTWcrg@comcast.com> <217e491a.0306102131.4f7c8660@posting.google.com> <bc755f$t5f$2@news.utelfla.com>`

```
weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote 

> : Not wasted.  The area may be used to store 50 1000 byte items.  To use
> : the whole 1000 bytes it needs to be moved out of here to, say, an FD
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> wasteful to me.

I'd covered that.  If a data file has 1000 byte records then a record
can be created in the FD area or WS and stored in the table. It needs
to be moved back to the record area for further processing and writing
to the file.  Accessing the first 25 bytes while it is still in the
table _may_ be because there is to be only one record for each account
code.  Search the table for an account code, if match found move
record into work area, if not create a new one with zero values. Add
new data, move [back] to table.

No waste at all.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: copybook with values

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T07:36:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EE873D9.5080102@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<oVBka.6765$zc.242345@news20.bellglobal.com> <EL-dnWITXd8jGnujXTWcrg@comcast.com> <217e491a.0306102131.4f7c8660@posting.google.com> <bc755f$t5f$2@news.utelfla.com>`

```
Ubiquitous wrote:
 > In alt.cobol Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
 > : weberm@polaris.net (Ubiquitous) wrote
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
 > :> the OCCURS portion; there are 50*975 characters of wasted space 
here! :-|
 >
 > : Not wasted.  The area may be used to store 50 1000 byte items.  To use
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
 > wasteful to me.

It could be that there -is- data in the remaining 975 characters, but
this is acting as sort of a "view" that allows the record to be read,
the first 3 items updated, and then replaced.  The FILLER would still
have the same value, and probably wouldn't be messed with.

(Just an idea...)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: copybook with values

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** jacksleight@hotmail.com (Jack Sleight)
- **Date:** 2003-06-15T11:46:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8a2d426e.0306151046.a57ffdd@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<oVBka.6765$zc.242345@news20.bellglobal.com> <EL-dnWITXd8jGnujXTWcrg@comcast.com> <217e491a.0306102131.4f7c8660@posting.google.com> <bc755f$t5f$2@news.utelfla.com> <3EE873D9.5080102@Knology.net>`

```
Large fillers are relatively common in situations where a file (and
its copybook) were originally created to process the full spectrum of
business components in the application.

Months or years later the data is used for some other purpose. The
pgmr decides not to bore the reader of the future with the gory
details of the original and creates a new copybook "fillering out" the
details.

Regards, Jack.


LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message news:<3EE873D9.5080102@Knology.net>...
> Ubiquitous wrote:
>  > In alt.cobol Richard <riplin@azonic.co.nz> wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> (Just an idea...)
```

```
> FILLER is used to indicate extra unused space and is ignored, but I

you should ask the author of this program for future extensions.

or

perhaps he has pressed the wrong key and has (095) misspelt as (975) :-)

Alfred
```

```
Peter Bogemans <member31043@dbforums.com> wrote 

> We have file status error 9/139 on a file.
> We move the file to another location and recopy it to the original , the
> programme work corretly again.

Are the programs that access the file:
     SCO programs on the same machine ?
     Running on another system using NFS ?     Running on Windows
accessing via SMB/VisionFS ?

SCO 5.0.6 is Open-Server (ex Xenix) are you sure the correct run-time
is used, and not SCO UnixWare (ex SVR5) ?

If Windows clients then ensure they are not doing delayed writes on
the network buffers.  ie stop them caching the network.
```

```
in my file the line with SYS-ATT-07 is: SYS-ATT-07 : CYAN on BLACK
maybe this information can help you.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mf-animator colors on xwindow

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T10:51:40+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc9ih5$oc6$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3ee78ff5$1@e-post.inode.at> <bcfdd616.0306112333.362d6a1b@posting.google.com> <3ee83dff@e-post.inode.at>`

```
Hi Wolf.

Take a look at Chapter 13 of the Programmer's Guide to Writing Programs. It
details the SYS-ATT attributes and their usage. I've pasted relevant text
below.

In addition, you should probably ensure that you're picking up the terminfo
binaries from under $COBDIR/terminfo, rather than the system versions. You
should set COBTERMINFO to $COBDIR/terminfo. See Chapter 15 of the User's
Guide for more details on this.

Hope this helps.

Simon.

      SYS-ATT-1 Reserved
      SYS-ATT-2 Reserved
      SYS-ATT-3 Menu-text
      SYS-ATT-4 Key-tops
      SYS-ATT-5 Tell-tales
      SYS-ATT-6 Lock-on
      SYS-ATT-7 Animated-text
      SYS-ATT-8 Error-messages
      SYS-ATT-9 Reserved
      SYS-ATT-10 Margins
      SYS-ATT-11 Normal-text
      SYS-ATT-12 Current-line
      SYS-ATT-13 Reserved
      SYS-ATT-14 Reserved
      SYS-ATT-15 Reserved
      SYS-ATT-16 Reserved


The following colors can be used for either foreground or background:

BLACK
BLUE
GREEN
CYAN
RED
MAGENTA
BROWN
LIGHT-GREY

and the following colors can be used in addition for foreground only:

DARK-GREY
LIGHT-BLUE
LIGHT-GREEN
LIGHT-CYAN
LIGHT-RED
LIGHT-MAGENTA
YELLOW
WHITE
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: mf-animator colors on xwindow

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-13T14:43:19+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcckgc$3ib$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<3ee78ff5$1@e-post.inode.at> <bcfdd616.0306112333.362d6a1b@posting.google.com> <3ee83dff@e-post.inode.at> <bc9ih5$oc6$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <bcamdf$4gs$1@news1.radix.net>`

```
Thomas Dickey wrote:

> how do the $COBDIR/terminfo entries differ from the system versions?

Hi Thomas.

Sometimes the OS-provided terminfo database doesn't include some mnemonics
essential to the operation of the COBOL product. The terminfo database
provided with Server Express includes all entries required for using COBOL,
though it may not necessarily work with non-COBOL applications (e.g. vi).

I've included a further snippet from the Server Express User's Guide, which
will hopefully help explain things further.

Simon.
16.1.1 System Terminfo
Your COBOL system can understand and use the standard UNIX System V terminfo
format and structure, although it ignores any non-standard UNIX
vendor-specific extensions. Some UNIX systems do not use the standard
terminfo format and structure and in this case your COBOL system uses the
alternative portable UNIX terminfo database described below.

The system terminfo database is located in /usr/lib/terminfo on most
platforms and is available for general use by any system application, a
typical example being the system editor vi. If a particular process requires
use of an alternative general terminfo database, then this can be selected
by specifying its location in the environment variable TERMINFO.

The terminal details are held in a terminfo database for general use by
non-COBOL applications. They can be made suitable for use by COBOL
applications but frequently there is a conflict. COBOL terminal I/O is
generally more sophisticated than that of most UNIX applications and has
more stringent minimal requirements of the terminfo database. The precise
meaning of some the terminal information can be ambiguous and sometimes the
terminfo database reflects an interpretation that differs from that made by
the COBOL system. In addition, the terminfo database is sometimes modified
to configure a non-COBOL application and so becomes unsuitable for use with
a COBOL application.

16.1.2 Portable Terminfo for COBOL
This COBOL system supports the use of a portable UNIX terminfo database and
provides one that includes details for a selection of terminals. The format
follows the UNIX specification but omits the UNIX vendor-specific portions.
The database can be used by COBOL applications as an adjunct or alternative
to the system databases. The system COBOL terminfo database is held in the
directory terminfo in the COBOL system directory $COBDIR. If a particular
COBOL process requires use of an alternative COBOL terminfo database, then
this can be selected by specifying its location in the environment variable
COBTERMINFO.
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

general format, but the order and length of the tables that are stored in the
terminfo database differ from those used in Solaris and IRIX64).  iirc, all
of them implement the capabilities listed in the X/Open documentation.

> non-COBOL applications. They can be made suitable for use by COBOL
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> a COBOL application.

> This COBOL system supports the use of a portable UNIX terminfo database and

> follows the UNIX specification but omits the UNIX vendor-specific portions.

such as the obsolete references to certain line-drawing characters & colors?

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-19c40e8742"></a>
## Thread: Issues with Non-COBOL characters and REPLACE / REPLACING — 1 msgs

### Issues with Non-COBOL characters and REPLACE / REPLACING

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T01:58:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bc98a0$78i$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
For those interested in the "history" (and implications) of allowing
non-COBOL characters in COPY REPLACING (and by extension REPLACE) syntax,
you might want to look at the section beginning at:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3mg10/4.7.1.5

For those not "familiar" with IBM compiler options, the distinction between
CMPR2 and NOCMPR2 is (sort-of) the difference between running their compiler
in '74 Standard (cmpr2) and '85 Standard (nocmpr2) modes.

The referenced sections includes some of the "migration" issues that
occurred when "new" characters (lower-case letters and colon) were ADDED to
the COBOL character set in the '85 Standard.  IBM's *current* recommendation
is simply,

"You should remove all non-COBOL characters from your source programs and
COPY libraries, and replace them with COBOL characters.
This removal of non-COBOL characters will protect you against new problems
in later releases of Enterprise COBOL. Future releases may assign meaning to
one of these characters (as with the colon) and results might be different."
```

<a id="t-19fa5550d6"></a>
## Thread: PULG: txt2pdf 6.6 — 1 msgs

### PULG: txt2pdf 6.6

- **From:** "Sanface Software" <sanface@sanface.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T09:10:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0454225cf7c7f05d5ce65f460ce09afe.93245@mygate.mailgate.org>`

```
We would like to announce txt2pdf 6.6 version. 
http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.html
txt2pdf is shareware; it is a very flexible and powerful Perl5 script
that converts text files to PDF format files, so you can use it in every
operating systems supported by Perl5, including MPE/iX, OpenVMS and
EPOC.
It's simple to design background like invoices, orders etc.
Here nice examples made using txt2pdf PRO
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/Purchase_Order.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/invoice.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/hfmus.pdf
http://www.sanface.com/pdf/heraldbill.pdf
If you prefer we also distribute executables for Windows, Linux,
Solaris, AIX, HP-UX, and Mac OS X. Inside the Windows version is Visual
txt2pdf, a VB GUI.
Remember to read the Hachette Filipacchi Media U.S. project at
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/?AURL=%2FArticles%2FV%2FV40603%2Easp

What's new in this version

It's possible to set the PDF version (default is 1.2). 
txt2pdf.vim update (vim plug-in to convert using vim text into pdf). 

Test txt2pdf 6.6!
You can find it at http://www.sanface.com/txt2pdf.htm
```

<a id="t-45a6004d32"></a>
## Thread: So, what's in a <text> word? — 7 msgs

### So, what's in a <text> word?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T15:45:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcaopa$1j1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
As a follow-up on the "symbols" thread, I thought that I would post the
"problem" (for upward compatibility) in introducing new symbols as
COBOL-defined "words" (e.g. as alternatives to AND, NOT, and OR)

Consider the following statement in an '85 Standard compiler that has an
EXTENSION allowing non-COBOL characters in pseudo-text

 Replace  ==!== by ==Not==
               ==!-True== by ==Never-True==.

 01 Switch-Field  Pic X.
      88  !-True  Value "N".
      88 Not-True  Value "n".

     ...
If !-True and !  A > B
     ...

Now, let's consider how this source code is "processed" when "!" is and is
not defined explicitly as a COBOL word (if such a change were made in the
future).

When "!" is *not* a COBOL reserved word (i.e. a defined symbol for "not")
the source gets converted to:

01 Switch-Field  Pic X.
      88  Never-True  Value "N".
      88 Not-True  Value "n".

     ...
If Never-True and !  A > B

but when/if  "!" *were* a COBOL reserved word (i.e. a defined symbol for
"not") the source gets converted to:

01 Switch-Field  Pic X.
      88  Not-True  Value "N".
      88 Not-True  Value "n".

     ...
If Not-True and !  A > B

        ***

Bottom-line:
   Be careful, VERY CAREFUL, when you consider adding new "symbol reserved
words".  Although this won't (shouldn't?) impact code developed for
"strictly conforming" compilers that do NOT allow non-COBOL characters in
pseudo-text, it will be POTENTIALLY disastrous to those programs developed
for environments with such an extension.

NOTE:
  This is *not* a "hypothetical" situation that has never occurred.  The
introduction of ":" as a text-word in the '85 Standard most certainly *did*
impact a variety of existing applications.

P.S.  As someone who has worked closely with compiler developers, changes to
"text-words" or anything else that impact COPY/REPLACING or REPLACE
"processing" are always frightening.  In many compilers, this is some of the
most "complex" and "exception driven" code.  Especially when it comes to
some of the rules that apply differently to REPLACE and COPY/REPLACE (such
as the ability to use "identifiers" instead of pseudo-text), changes in the
definitions need to be incredibly carefully considered - before
(unintentionally) impacting existing code and processing.
```

#### ↳ Re: So, what's in a <text> word?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T14:48:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcasf4$1aa9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bcaopa$1j1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:bcaopa$1j1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net...
>  Especially when it comes to
> some of the rules that apply differently to REPLACE and COPY/REPLACE (such
> as the ability to use "identifiers" instead of pseudo-text), changes in
the
> definitions need to be incredibly carefully considered - before
> (unintentionally) impacting existing code and processing.

REPLACE allows only pseudo-text to begin with, and though the use of "text"
(as distinct from "word") in COPY ... REPLACING is still supported, it's
marked as archaic in ISO/IEC 1989:2002.  I wouldn't recommend changing
*anything* beyond that which was necessary to allow some extra capabilities
in pseudo-text-1, and those would be very limited indeed.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> REPLACE allows only pseudo-text to begin with, and though the use of "text"
> (as distinct from "word") in COPY ... REPLACING is still supported, it's
> marked as archaic in ISO/IEC 1989:2002.  I wouldn't recommend changing

We store HTML in a database table.  One option we have is to pull the 
text back out and give it a .DOC extension, forcing Word to open it. 
However, when we store it, we convert ">" to "&gt;", etc.  It sure would 
be nice to say

Inspect WS-Traffic
   Replacing All ==>== by ==&gt;==
                 ==<== by ==&lt;==
                 ==&== by ==&amp;==

(and the reverse when we are pulling it back out)

However, -that- replacing doesn't support pseudo-text (and isn't, at 
least as I read it, in the 2002 standard as well).  I wonder if anyone 
else would like to do this without going character-by-character... 
Anyone got any tips?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: So, what's in a <text> word?

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-13T16:04:22-07:00

- **References:** `<bcaopa$1j1$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <bcasf4$1aa9$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3EEA5465.5030205@Knology.net>`

```
Just out of curiosity, have you taken a peek at the sort of object code an
even *moderately* complex INSPECT (or STRING or UNSTRING, for that matter)
statement tends to precipitate?   :-O

Actually, you can almost get away with it with three STRING ... DELIMITED BY
statements inside a single PERFORM loop, with maybe some pointer-bumping
logic in between.  The source code ought to be fairly straightforward, and
could well be pretty efficient.

The reverse would be a little tougher (and the multi-character search code
tends to be less efficient in my experience), but it still ought to be
doable (also using STRING ... DELIMITED BY, this time with something like
"&amp;" as the delimiter) in pretty much the same fashion.

    -Chuck Stevens


"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3EEA5465.5030205@Knology.net...
```

```
Chuck Stevens wrote:
> Just out of curiosity, have you taken a peek at the sort of object code an
> even *moderately* complex INSPECT (or STRING or UNSTRING, for that matter)
> statement tends to precipitate?   :-O

No, but I'm learning.  In my attempts to get a fixed-gate subsystem 
going on the 2200, I've written MASM and C over the last 2 days, in 
addition to 2 8-line COBOL programs.   And, I generated an error that 
someone with 20 years of experience with these things had never seen.  I 
always was above and beyond...  ;)

> Actually, you can almost get away with it with three STRING ... DELIMITED BY
> statements inside a single PERFORM loop, with maybe some pointer-bumping
> logic in between.  The source code ought to be fairly straightforward, and
> could well be pretty efficient.

What I've got right now is an Inspect...Tallying for All "<", All ">", 
etc.  If I don't find anything, I just move it.  If I do find something, 
I do something like

Perform Varying x from 1 by 1 Until x > in-length
     Evaluate In-char (x:1)
        When "<"
          Move "&lt;" to hold-text (y:4)
          Add 4 to y
...

This might be the most "maintainable" solution, as I (or whoever comes 
after me) can add special characters as they become problems.  :)  Plus, 
we don't put out many of these characters, so this logic isn't going to 
be executed all that often.  I can also reverse it pretty easily, except 
that I'd probably use a structure like Evaluate True... When text (y:4) 
= "&lt;"...
```

```
>
> What I've got right now is an Inspect...Tallying for All "<", All ">",
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

I like that. Here's a subroutine for inserting:

PROCEDURE DIVISION USING
   LINK-STRING
   LINK-STRING-LEN
   LINK-INSERT
   LINK-INSERT-LEN
   LINK-LOC.


*>  Move "Tail" of receiving field to new location

   MOVE LINK-STRING-LEN TO J.
   PERFORM VARYING I FROM BEG BY -1 UNTIL
      I < LINK-LOC
      MOVE LINK-STRING(I:1) TO LINK-STRING(J:1)
      SUBTRACT 1 FROM J
      IF J < 1
         MOVE 1 TO J
         END-IF
      END-PERFORM.

*> Move insert-string to area opened up

MOVE LINK-INSERT(1:LINK-INSERT-LEN) TO
   LINK-STRING(LINK-LOC:LINK-INSERT-LEN).

      GOBACK.
```

```
Hi:
   I got a data file from VMS7.1 system. I put it on HP-UX system via
Ftp. I will parse this file and put data into Oracle database.
This file was generated by COBOL program.
I also got it's FD declaration. When read this file using
java.io.fileInputStream read method. I did not know  how many bytes a
record was. Is there any delimited item for one record ?

following is file format definition.  

FD  LTH_FILE
    RECORD IS VARYING IN SIZE.

01  LTH_REC.
    05  LTH_KEY.
        10  LTH_LOT                              PIC X(11).
        10  LTH_DATE_TIME                        PIC 9(14).
        10  LTH_TRANS_COUNTER                    PIC 9(4).
        10  LTH_RECORD_TYPE                      PIC X.
            88  LTH_FILE_HEADER                  VALUE "A".
            88  LTH_DELETE_HISTORY               VALUE "B".
            88  LTH_BASIC_HISTORY                VALUE "C".
            88  LTH_ACTIVITY_HISTORY             VALUE "E".
            88  LTH_MOVE_IN_HISTORY              VALUE "F".
            88  LTH_DUMMY_BOM_HISTORY            VALUE "J".
            88  LTH_CREATE_CONVERT_HISTORY       VALUE "K".
            88  LTH_SHIP_HISTORY                 VALUE "L".
            88  LTH_MISC_HISTORY                 VALUE "M".
            88  LTH_ADLT_HISTORY_1               VALUE "N".
            88  LTH_ADLT_HISTORY_2               VALUE "O".
            88  LTH_HOLD_HISTORY                 VALUE "P".
            88  LTH_COMMENT_HISTORY              VALUE "Q".
            88  LTH_LOT_ITEM_HISTORY             VALUE "R".
            88  LTH_TRANS_ITEM_HISTORY           VALUE "S".
            88  LTH_SUBPRODUCT_HISTORY           VALUE "T".
            88  LTH_ATTRIBUTE_HISTORY            VALUE "U".
            88  LTH_LBRR_HISTORY                 VALUE "V".
    05  LTH_FILE_HEADER_DATA.
	  10 HEADER1	PIC X(8).
	  10 HEADER2	PIC X(8).
	  10 HEADER3	PIC X(8).
	  10 HEADER4	PIC X(8).
	  10 HEADER5	PIC X(8).
	  10 HEADER6	PIC X(8).
	  10 HEADER7	PIC X(8).
	  10 HEADER8	PIC X(8).
    05  FILL.
	  10 FILL1	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL2	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL3	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL4	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL5	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL6	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL7	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL8	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL9	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL10	PIC X(10).
	  10 FILL11	PIC X(12).
 **************************************************END
```

#### ↳ Re: How to extact one varying size data from cobol data into Java.

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-13T07:21:05-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<-yGdnQoXRO1EX3SjXTWcqg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<99eaa921.0306121807.5b66869a@posting.google.com>`

```

"Jung-hao Tai" <jhtai@winbond.com.tw> wrote in message
news:99eaa921.0306121807.5b66869a@posting.google.com...
> Hi:
>    I got a data file from VMS7.1 system. I put it on HP-UX system via
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>  **************************************************END

Doesn't look varying to me.

It IS possible that the record is terminated by a CR/LF representing
blank-filling.

It is also possible that the LTH-RECORD-TYPE provides a clue to the record
length ("A" implies "x" bytes, "B" implies "y" bytes, etc.), but this is a
really-weird way to do things.

In COBOL, as with other languages, text files have a standard delimiter (CR
or CR/LF). Absent that, there must be some explicit or implicit declaration
of the record length. Sometimes this declaration is explicit in that the
record length is carried as a data element within each record. Sometimes
this length declaration is implicit in that the record is presumed in
advance (by the program) to be a certain length.

This latter method SEEMS to be the case here.

Then, too, programmers can get tricky:
1. The first record (H) in the file is always 37 bytes.
2. This is followed by five 120 byte records (A).
3. Each of these is followed by 2 60-byte records (B) UNLESS the first
record has a "7" in byte 12. If "H" contains a "7" in byte 12, then each of
the "A" records is followed by 1 1200 byte record (C).

You see this kind of code written in the '80s by programmers having LSD
flashbacks from their '60s and '70s mis-spent youth.
```

#### ↳ Re: How to extact one varying size data from cobol data into Java.

```
There are several ways to do this:

1. Use the program to generate a single record file and a two record file
with special first char and last char. Then use a hex editor, you will find
the structure of the file. The file maybe in one of the structures: records
are separated by a special delimiter CR/LF, or a length field in first
several bytes. If you are lucky, you can find the structure right away. The
worst is the file format is system (compiler or OS) dependent.

2. A good way is write a COBOL program in the same system and write it out
as pure TEXT file with delimiter you know. This is a safe way, but you have
to do some Cobol programming.

By the way, if you want to do Cobol source code to Java conversion, check
http://www.corporola.com.

Good luck.


Thomas Li



"Jung-hao Tai" <jhtai@winbond.com.tw> wrote in message
news:99eaa921.0306121807.5b66869a@posting.google.com...
> Hi:
>    I got a data file from VMS7.1 system. I put it on HP-UX system via
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…
>  **************************************************END
```

<a id="t-026373d405"></a>
## Thread: Why waste so much brainpower on nits — 16 msgs

### Why waste so much brainpower on nits

- **From:** "Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-13T02:56:39+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<H3bGa.48120$nr.4352774@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
I must have gotten 50 emails recently on the "<> for not equal" issue.  What
a waste of brainpower!

There are far more important features needed in COBOL. As an example, there
are 3 competing proposals for an XML interface proposed to J4.  As it is, if
an XML proposal was already ready and approved it would not make it thru the
process to be approved until 2006.

Instead of complaining how long it takes to get a new COBOL Standard out,
how about contributing to the effort by actually considering and commenting
on the imporant features (and proposals) which are already on the list for
inclusion to the next standard!

J4 has a standing document (S-16) listing the proposed features, which
include features such as asynchronous processing, national character and
locale enhancements, date and time features, editing enhancements, file
processing enhancements, etc..   That document has recently been prioritized
in terms of feature importannce. If not already posted, maybe Bill Klein can
post a copy of that for you of you to see and consider.   If all you COBOL
experts really want to contribute in a meaningful way, spend time thinking
about those important features - not these nits.

There already is an acceptable way to do "not equal" - not so for many of
the proposed features, for which there are important business needs.  Put
all that great brainpower out there to use on important features for a
change.

Just my humble opinion.

Jeff Friedman
```

#### ↳ Re: Why waste so much brainpower on nits

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-12T22:21:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcbfv9$jhj$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<H3bGa.48120$nr.4352774@twister.southeast.rr.com>`

```
Top posting (Just because I often do <G>) - and in response to Jeff's note
below

The current version of the J4 document S-16 is (as I *have* mentioned
recently) at:
     http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/files/s16.doc

This document is "updated" relatively frequently (and I would expect it to
be updated after the "end-of-this month" WG4 meeting).  To *always* find the
most recent version, go to:
     http://www.cobolportal.com/j4/index.asp

and do a "find" on S-16 - which will include a link to the most current
version.

You may be interested in other documents at this site that reflect some of
the "current work" of J4.  Another useful site, is at:
    http://www.cobolstandards.com/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Why waste so much brainpower on nits

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-06-14T14:14:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0306141314.7b5df6b7@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<H3bGa.48120$nr.4352774@twister.southeast.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B959B8.08422313062003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message news:<joe_zitzelberger-B959B8.08422313062003@corp.supernews.com>...
> In article <H3bGa.48120$nr.4352774@twister.southeast.rr.com>,
>  "Schroeder" <jfriedman@nc.rr.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> What is the third one?

While not on the J4 radar and perhaps stretching the meaning of an
"interface"...here's a couple of more choices for consideration:

4th XML Interface for COBOL (when coding on .NET): The ADO.NET
"Dataset" Class which is part of the "System.Data" .NET Framework
namespace/class library.

5th XML Interface for COBOL (when coding on .NET): Either of the
feature-rich .NET Framework classes that are offered in the
"System.XML" .NET Framework namespace/class library.

regards.
```

```
> "interface"...here's a couple of more choices for consideration:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> regards.

Chris,
  Can you provide some examples of OO "syntax" or facilities that are *not*
in the 2002 COBOL Standard that would be required to INHERIT from these
classes and/or invoke their methods?  I am not saying that such missing
features don't exist, but I think (for me) the important thing to do in
"Standard COBOL" is to insure that it provides the facilities to "fit in"
with existing Class "structures" in different environments and NOT to supply
specific interfaces to operating-system (or similar proprietary)
environments.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why waste so much brainpower on nits

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-15T12:17:43-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bci9ql$q2k$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<H3bGa.48120$nr.4352774@twister.southeast.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B959B8.08422313062003@corp.supernews.com> <b11feff.0306141314.7b5df6b7@posting.google.com> <bcg65o$uvv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <01c332fd$cbf128c0$de8bf243@chottel>`

```
COBOL already allows CALLs to non-COBOL programs - and INVOKEs of non-COBOL
methods.  (It is implementor-defined which languages and what requirements
there may be).  With the 2002 Standard (and as an extension in several
existing compilers) the "CALL-CONVENTION" facility even enhances this.
```

```
Good start. Perhaps, having DOD specifiy a key word for all types, and
Use the one you need as the user. That's about how COBOL started.
Vendors would be asked to comply, and extensions could be created as
hardware requirements changed. From what I read, most have some ability
to sell to other customers who are using other methods now, so it should
not be a long time project to help everyone.

More ideas like this might make this group become a working group.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Why waste so much brainpower on nits

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-16T15:16:20+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3eed3b07_6@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<H3bGa.48120$nr.4352774@twister.southeast.rr.com> <joe_zitzelberger-B959B8.08422313062003@corp.supernews.com> <b11feff.0306141314.7b5df6b7@posting.google.com> <bcg65o$uvv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net> <01c332fd$cbf128c0$de8bf243@chottel> <bci9ql$q2k$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <01c33390$dc7b9660$4a93f243@chottel>`

```
You're absolutely right, Charles.

The Component Object Model (COM) has just such a standard that allows any
language to map its data.

That's one reason why components can be used from any language.

Without going into the full details:

VT_I4    =  4 byte binary.  (COBOL  pic s9(9) binary)
VT_CY  =  real number with 14 digits and 4 decimals. (COBOL  pic
s9(14)v9(4))

The above are a couple of examples.

When using COM with COBOL, conversion to the COM types is accomplished by
the compiler (Fujitsu), but I still like to do it explicitily so as to be
sure.

Here is an actual code snippet that demonstrates this...

*----------------------------------------------------------------
 set-visible-properties section.
*
* Set the External Properties (Custom Properties) of the Method
* from the internal fields above.
*
* The custom properties are defined using the OLE (COM) standard
* so, instead of having COBOL pictures, they have a cross-platform
* format. For example, The REAL number we have built is defined
* as "VT_R8", the currency field is "VT_CY", and so on...
*
* These COM formats can be mapped to COBOL pictures and the compiler
* knows how to do this...(In other languages the same formats are
* mapped to whatever is suitable for that language).
*
* To be on the safe side I am going to ensure that the custom
* properties are built from EXACTLY the right kind of COBOL picture,
* even though this is not strictly necessary...
*
 svp000.
     move dec-len to VT-I2          *> VT-I2 is a binary two byte field
(Mainframe halfword)
                                                  *>  Here's the definition:
01  VT-I2    pic s9(5) comp.
*
*  All the quoted fields below are  custom PROPERTIES of POW-SELF.
*  POW-SELF is the current instance of the "Get-Number" method of the
*  String2Num Object.
*
     move VT-I2 to "intDecimal" of POW-SELF
     move int-len to VT-I2
     move VT-I2 to "intInteger" of POW-SELF
     move num-fp to "realNum" of POW-SELF
     move num-currency to "realCurrency" of POW-SELF
     move dbl-integer    to "dblInteger" of POW-SELF
     move dbl-decimal    to "dblDecimal" of POW-SELF
     move string-integer to "strInteger" of POW-SELF
     if dec-len NOT = zero
        move string-decimal to "strDecimal" of POW-SELF
     else
        move spaces to "strDecimal" of POW-SELF
     end-if
     if is-a-number
        move POW-TRUE to "ItsANumber" of POW-SELF
     else
        move POW-FALSE to "ItsANumber" of POW-SELF
     end-if
*     invoke POW-SELF "ShowStuff" using input-string
     .
 svp999.
     exit.

The COM (previously OLE) standard, allows cross platform data definition and
is used by component builders all over the World.

comments on Bill's post below...

"Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in message
news:01c33390$dc7b9660$4a93f243@chottel...
> Yes, but it seems that many people have trouble with parameters. For
> example: on computer x what is the equivalent to data type y in language
z.
> Instead of everyone figuring all of this out for themselves, it might be
> nice to have a more standard approach.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Java has something called the Java Native Interface that you can use
to
> > > call non-Java programs from Java. Perhaps COBOL needs something like
> that?
> > >
JNI does a lot more besides allowing  non-Java to be called.

> > > William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in article
> > > <bcg65o$uvv$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>...
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > >   Can you provide some examples of OO "syntax" or facilities that
are
> > > *not*
> > > > in the 2002 COBOL Standard that would be required to INHERIT from
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > "Standard COBOL" is to insure that it provides the facilities to
"fit
> > in"
> > > > with existing Class "structures" in different environments and NOT
to
> > > supply
> > > > specific interfaces to operating-system (or similar proprietary)
> > > > environments.
> > > >

Yes, but you CAN'T do that unless it is OS specific. If I access MFC through
COBOL, I know that it can only work in a Windows environment.

A vendor MIGHT be able to provide access to other "non-COBOL" Class
libraries under the covers, but I believe it is a pipe dream and not worth
the effort.

The answer is to use the best tools for the job and not try and stretch
COBOL into a "one language wonder", as some here seem to be intent on doing.

Although I can invoke the Java Class libraries through COBOL, I don't. If I
want Metal windows (for example...) I'll write them in Java.

Now, I accept that that is my preference and other people will want to do
other things, but the question that has to be asked is this:

"Should we even try and make COBOL all things to all people, at this stage
of the game?"

I believe the answer has to be "No".
(If we were going to do this we should have done it 20 years ago...)

We will succeed in making it nothing to anybody.

A better chance of success is to focus on the strengths of COBOL and ensure
that those are enhanced and rejuvenated.

That way COBOL can maintain its place as a very useful language for
commercial application development (as long as commercial applications need
to be "developed").

Pete.
```

```
> > > > > call non-Java programs from Java. Perhaps COBOL needs something
like
> > > that?
> > > > >
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > > > While not on the J4 radar and perhaps stretching the meaning
of
> an

> > > > > > >
> > > > > > > 4th XML Interface for COBOL (when coding on .NET): The ADO.NET
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > > > > > 5th XML Interface for COBOL (when coding on .NET): Either of
the
> > > > > > > feature-rich .NET Framework classes that are offered in the

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

that
> > are
> > > > > *not*
> > > > > > in the 2002 COBOL Standard that would be required to INHERIT
from
> > > these
> > > > > > classes and/or invoke their methods?  I am not saying that such
> > > missing
> > > > > > features don't exist, but I think (for me) the important thing
to
> do

…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

If
```
