```
On 23-Jun-2003, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner) wrote:

> You raise an excellent point. When I see such dependencies, the first thought
> that comes to mind is "redesign". In a perfect world, there wouldn't be
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> If neither is practical, I document the dependency at both ends.

We automate such dependencies, but it can be useful to have charts anyway -
applications programmers don't use CA-7, and we do need to be aware of
dependencies and have some knowledge on how our changes can effect process flow.

I don't know that all dependencies can be redesigned away.   Sometimes we DO
need to make sure that close of business accounts are what are shown in the next
day's balance.    Business needs have dependencies which cannot be redesigned
away by programmers.
```

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>of the history of computers (mainframe and otherwise) is taken into account!

Yes, I have. Burroughs Large Systems wrote some 'one pass compilers'. The
architect told me it was a bad idea, in retrospect. He'd never again design a
one pass compiler. 

That doesn't directly address the question of intermediate code, but it's
symptomatic of myopic design. Suppose you're working for Burroughs and spitting
machine language for the one-and-only processor. Then the boss waves his hands
and says 'We've just been acquired by Unisys (or vice versa). How much trouble
(they always say 'how much trouble' rather than 'how fast can you') would it be
to emit code for another machine?' 

If you'd designed the compiler in two tiers, the addition would be relatively
painless. If you'd glommed it all together, you'd be in deep trouble. 

>> The separation between front- and back-end is obvious with Java, .Net and
>Micro
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>object code.

Cray was acquired by Silicon Graphics in 1996, then acquired by Tera Computer in
2000. The Tera/Cadence platform was quite a bit different from the IBM 390
platform that Cray initially emulated. I picture Cray compiler writers
scrambling rather than sitting content. 

DEC and Tandem have been acquired by Compaq. I picture their compiler writers
also under the gun.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T10:25:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcsrmn$1nnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bcd1ie$2qcv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeac912.236758765@news.optonline.net> <bcl0mm$27n2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeeaedd.207409527@news.optonline.net> <bcn8ql$p5o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eefb101.273503437@news.optonline.net> <bcps5k$2k7l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef08efb.330337761@news.optonline.net> <bcqeln$31j6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef0fceb.22750191@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ef0fceb.22750191@news.optonline.net...

> >Have you truly never encountered an environment in which the language
> >compilers are written to produce code for a particular machine?   I don't
> >think Unisys is uncommon in this regard, particularly when the entire
course
> >of the history of computers (mainframe and otherwise) is taken into
account!
>
> Yes, I have. Burroughs Large Systems wrote some 'one pass compilers'. The
> architect told me it was a bad idea, in retrospect. He'd never again
design a
> one pass compiler.

Burroughs Large Systems also wrote some "two-pass compilers" (for various
languages) in which the code-generation "pass" was aware of the underlying
language constructs.  Whether a given monolithic (or even modular)
executable program that functions to consume programmer's source code and
produce directly-executable object code goes through the source file once,
twice, or any number of times is irrelevant to whether the part of the
compiler that produces object code has knowledge or "memory" of what source
construct it is that the compiler's producing code for.   The number of
"passes" relates to the source images, not to the "passing" of any
intermediate structure (on disk or in memory).  Most often a "true" two-pass
compiler passes the source -- or some text-based massaging of the source -- 
twice in an effort to ensure that additional information developed by the
compiler is correctly coordinated with the source images for the listing;
any other information can be communicated from the parser to the rest of the
compilation through more direct means.

Based on my experience with both one- and multi-pass compilers, I would say
the most important criterion as to whether the compiler for a given language
should be one-pass or more-than-one-pass is the nature of the language being
compiled.  For languages in which everything "meaningful" about an element
of the program can be determined before it is necessary to generate code
associated with that element, there is no particular reason for the compiler
to have to process the entirety of the program first.  For example, either
some form of "forward-reference template" for an ALGOL or Pascal procedure,
or the procedure itself, must be encountered by the compiler before any
reference to that procedure may appear in the program.  Thus, I would place
both ALGOL and Pascal in the category of languages for which "one-pass
compilers" are adequate.  It is not necessary for the compiler to have
"seen" a future statement in an ALGOL or Pascal program before generating
object code for the current statement.  COBOL as a language is *less*
well-suited to generating object code as each statement is encountered; for
example, I personally think that, because of the way nested programs are
structured, there are compromises associated with generating object code for
a program upon encountering the end of that program.  I also think that
there are some reasonable optimizations that can take place in data mapping
and allocation that can only occur if the entirety of the code that accesses
the data has been "seen" before the final allocation process begins.

>
> That doesn't directly address the question of intermediate code, but it's
> symptomatic of myopic design. Suppose you're working for Burroughs and
spitting
> machine language for the one-and-only processor. Then the boss waves his
hands
> and says 'We've just been acquired by Unisys (or vice versa). How much
trouble
> (they always say 'how much trouble' rather than 'how fast can you') would
it be
> to emit code for another machine?'

Before that question is answered, one must also ask "Do they already have
the ability to emit code for their own machine?"  Using the B6900/U2200
analogy, if there's already a suite of COBOL products for the Univac 2200
that works just fine thank you very much, and a suite of COBOL products for
the B6900 that works just fine thank you very much, what's the incentive to
having one cross-compile for the other?   Is there a *business case* for
providing yet another environment on either, or both, systems?   Is there a
business case for suggesting to the existing users of either system that
they need to stop doing what they're doing, and spend lots and lots of money
to convert to another way of doing exactly what they're already doing?

> Cray was acquired by Silicon Graphics in 1996, then acquired by Tera
Computer in
> 2000. The Tera/Cadence platform was quite a bit different from the IBM 390
> platform that Cray initially emulated. I picture Cray compiler writers
> scrambling rather than sitting content.

I haven't kept up with the Cray saga; I was using it as an example of a
proprietary architecture, and the Fortran compiler as an example of a
compiler intended only to produce object code for a proprietary
architecture.

> DEC and Tandem have been acquired by Compaq. I picture their compiler
writers
> also under the gun.

Whatever you "picture" may or may not bear any relationship to reality.
Since a likely candidate for Someone Who Should Know Or Could Find Out is
readily available to you -- the HP representative on the J4 mailing list
which is posted on the J4 web site -- rather than engaging in idle
speculation you might want to query that individual to find out exactly how
much effort the various ex-DEC, ex-Tandem, ex-whichever-flavor-of-HP
compiler writers are expending in revising each of their compilers to
generate object code for any or all of the others, and if they are "under
the gun", exactly what sort of gun it is that they are under!  I don't know,
but I wouldn't be so rash as to speculate.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T19:15:22+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef4966f.19603578@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bcd1ie$2qcv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeac912.236758765@news.optonline.net> <bcl0mm$27n2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeeaedd.207409527@news.optonline.net> <bcn8ql$p5o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eefb101.273503437@news.optonline.net> <bcps5k$2k7l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef08efb.330337761@news.optonline.net> <bcqeln$31j6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef0fceb.22750191@news.optonline.net> <bcsrmn$1nnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
>news:3ef0fceb.22750191@news.optonline.net...

>Based on my experience with both one- and multi-pass compilers, I would say
>the most important criterion as to whether the compiler for a given language
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>the data has been "seen" before the final allocation process begins.

Forward references are easily handled by:
..Leaving a blank pointer in object code and fixing it up at the end (1.1 pass?)
..Getting the linker to do it 
..Letting the run-time guy do it. Variation: Microsoft's 'fixups' in .exe files.


I think a better reason for multi-pass is cleaner compiler architecture. I liken
it to application architecture. In the days of anemic computers, we made one
sequential pass through a 'master file' and crammed all processing into one,
often monolithic, program. Now we can afford to package logic more functionally.


The new generation of processors, notably Itanium, *requires* multiple passes ..
not on source code but on intermediate code. The hardware depends on compiler
optimization of predication, speculation (necessary for multi-threading), loop
unrolling, pipelining, inlining, etc. Intel, HP and others provide standard
components to optimize intermediate code and then produce machine language.
Intel's code generator is called ECG. 

In previous generations, the hardware guys did their own optimizations in
hardware. Now it has become too complicated, so they're relying on software to
do it for them.

>I haven't kept up with the Cray saga; I was using it as an example of a
>proprietary architecture, and the Fortran compiler as an example of a
>compiler intended only to produce object code for a proprietary
>architecture.

Proprietary architectures are going away. Consider:

..Tandem (/Compaq/HP) has announced they'e dropping proprietary processors in
favor of Itanium. 
..DEC (/Compaq/HP) dropped their proprietary VAX processors in favor of Alpha.
..HP has dropped their very successful PA processors and the HP-3000 line in
favor of Itanium. Why? Because they couldn't figure out how to make PA 64-bit.
They jumped in bed with Intel and messed up the Itanium 1 with their EPIC
concept, which had the compiler doing optimization rather than using separate
optimizers. Intel had to shove them out in order to get back on track with the
Itanium 2. 

>Whatever you "picture" may or may not bear any relationship to reality.
>Since a likely candidate for Someone Who Should Know Or Could Find Out is
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>but I wouldn't be so rash as to speculate.

I don't want to waste Mr. Murray's time asking for publically available
information (above).
```

```
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ef4966f.19603578@news.optonline.net...
> "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Based on my experience with both one- and multi-pass compilers, I would
say
> >the most important criterion as to whether the compiler for a given
language
> >should be one-pass or more-than-one-pass is the nature of the language
being
> >compiled.  For languages in which everything "meaningful" about an
element
> >of the program can be determined before it is necessary to generate code
> >associated with that element, there is no particular reason for the
compiler
> >to have to process the entirety of the program first.  For example,
either
> >some form of "forward-reference template" for an ALGOL or Pascal
procedure,
> >or the procedure itself, must be encountered by the compiler before any
> >reference to that procedure may appear in the program.  Thus, I would
place
> >both ALGOL and Pascal in the category of languages for which "one-pass
> >compilers" are adequate.  It is not necessary for the compiler to have
> >"seen" a future statement in an ALGOL or Pascal program before generating
> >object code for the current statement.  COBOL as a language is *less*
> >well-suited to generating object code as each statement is encountered;
for
> >example, I personally think that, because of the way nested programs are
> >structured, there are compromises associated with generating object code
for
> >a program upon encountering the end of that program.  I also think that
> >there are some reasonable optimizations that can take place in data
mapping
> >and allocation that can only occur if the entirety of the code that
accesses
> >the data has been "seen" before the final allocation process begins.
>
> Forward references are easily handled by:
> ..Leaving a blank pointer in object code and fixing it up at the end (1.1
pass?)
> ..Getting the linker to do it
> ..Letting the run-time guy do it. Variation: Microsoft's 'fixups' in .exe
files.

Forward references are but one of the reasons mentioned.
There are potential optimizations, what Mr Stevens calls
'data mapping and allocation', that cannot be achieved until
the procedure division has be analyzed.

The COBOL standard specifies, in the general rules, how
various syntax accomplishes some end. The implementor
is free to use any means to accomplish that end, 'as if' the
rules of COBOL were followed.

For example, a programmer may define an item as numeric
display within a group. During analysis of the procedure division,
it is determined that the item is used only as a subscript to a
table and that it has no other relation with the group in which
it is defined. (This may occur when 'subscripts-and-indexes' are
collected into one group.) Any of the following may occur:

-- the item is removed from the group
-- the item is changed to binary (or packed decimal)
-- the item is changed to an index or pointer
-- the item is placed on a stack
-- the item is deleted due to loop unrolling

To accomplish this, a two-pass compiler is required.

>
> I think a better reason for multi-pass is cleaner compiler architecture.

There can be no better reason than a requirement!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-22T23:55:36+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef6411a.45357894@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bcd1ie$2qcv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeac912.236758765@news.optonline.net> <bcl0mm$27n2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeeaedd.207409527@news.optonline.net> <bcn8ql$p5o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eefb101.273503437@news.optonline.net> <bcps5k$2k7l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef08efb.330337761@news.optonline.net> <bcqeln$31j6$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef0fceb.22750191@news.optonline.net> <bcsrmn$1nnr$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef4966f.19603578@news.optonline.net> <vfbnpoqak460bd@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Rick Smith" <ricksmith@mfi.net> wrote:

>
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[75 more quoted lines elided]…
>There can be no better reason than a requirement!

As I said in another posting, optimization is moving out of the compiler's
domain, thanks largely to the Intel Itanium. Optimizations once performed in
hardware now depend on the compiler. Now that hardware guys are in bed with us,
they want some control over optimization. 

Intel's compiler suite -- C++ and Fortran -- expect the compiler to produce
rough native code in ELF format (native code) and DWARF (symbol table). These
are Linux standards.  It then runs four optimizers on that code, probably with
two passes each, before running ECG to produce an executable. 

Microsoft wants the compiler to emit PE (formerly COFF) and STI.  Same deal,
multiple optimization passes. 

JVM and dot-net have their own standards for intermediate code, and move JIT
compiling (optimization + code generation) to the desktop. 

Yet another contender is EFI byte code (EBC), which works on multiple cpus.

The pattern emerging here is:

..Compiler --> intermediate code
..Optimizer --> optimized intermediate code
..Code generator --> native code

Even assembly language is being optimized. There is no more 'close to metal' as
there used to be. Assembly language is archaic on the Itanium. 

Micro Focus and Fujitsu offer compilers optimized for Itanium. I'd wager one or
both are using Intel's back end optimizers. HP has announced their intention to
use Intel back ends, and HP-UX is the only Itanium platform supported by Micro
Focus.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-18T12:46:23-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcqfig$p5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bcd1ie$2qcv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeac912.236758765@news.optonline.net> <bcl0mm$27n2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeeaedd.207409527@news.optonline.net> <bcn8ql$p5o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eefb101.273503437@news.optonline.net> <bcps5k$2k7l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef08efb.330337761@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ef08efb.330337761@news.optonline.net...

> >> If the compiler had been written in another language, the semantics
would
> >have
> >> been lost in translation.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Semantics of the SEARCH verb. If the front-end had turned it into a loop,
the
> back-end would have generated code for a loop.

As with Howard, I haven't a clue what you're trying to say here.

Nothing other than the compiler vendor's desires mandates whether a given
compiler produces an intermediate product that is then processed into object
code.  Nothing other than the compiler designer's lack of foresight
precludes the parsing process of the compiler from passing whatever
information the code generation process needs to enable it to make wise
decisions on efficient object code generation.  Nothing other than the
compiler vendor's choices and the marketplace needs that compiler is
intended to meet mandates whether the code generation process they use is
built into the compiler, compiled in as a separate procedure, or obtained,
purloined or co-opted from another party.

There's nothing whatever to prevent the compiler designer from informing a
code generation process provided by that same vendor that the code it's
generating is for the SEARCH verb.  If the code generation process has been
developed by the same folks that provided the parsing process, I'd be very
surprised if they didn't communicate well enough to ensure that the code
generation process was given enough information by the parser to generate
relatively optimal code!  Are you assuming that, generally speaking, COBOL
compilers produce code that's actually turned into object code by a program
built/developed/marketed by someone other than the compiler vendor?  I don't
think that's all that common.

       -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Alphabets

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T09:06:12+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef16667.49757663@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bcd1ie$2qcv$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeac912.236758765@news.optonline.net> <bcl0mm$27n2$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eeeaedd.207409527@news.optonline.net> <bcn8ql$p5o$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3eefb101.273503437@news.optonline.net> <bcps5k$2k7l$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com> <3ef08efb.330337761@news.optonline.net> <bcqfig$p5$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`

```
"Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com> wrote:

>There's nothing whatever to prevent the compiler designer from informing a
>code generation process provided by that same vendor that the code it's
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>think that's all that common.

Historically, it had not been common. But current compiler design, as seen in
JVM and .Net (and, I suspect, LE) are changing that precept.

Earlier, the separation between front-end and back-end was not so evident to the
end-user (programmer), but may have resulted from political dynamics within the
compiler producer. Noting it already had an INT->native translator for languages
X and Y, and a limited budget for the Z compiler, it would be tempting to reuse
it even though it's not optimized for Z.  Management sees the issue as technical
quibbling rather than strategic.
```

```
standards are of the shop in which you choose to work.
```

<a id="t-5fb2a4a69c"></a>
## Thread: ASSIGN TO PRINTER, Internal SORTing and .NET — 7 msgs

### ASSIGN TO PRINTER, Internal SORTing and .NET

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-06-14T15:11:56-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0306141411.3023c537@posting.google.com>`

```
I'd like to share the content from a converation that I had...

I friend asked me the other day:
 
"Is it true that Fujitsu's NetCOBOL for .NET does not support basic
features like ASSIGN TO PRINTER and Internal SORTing?"

My reply sounded something like this:

"Yes, this is true AND not true. 

True, IF you are trying to print or sort internally - WHILE using the
Legacy COBOL syntax approach, - those features are not supported in
pure COBOL .NET (managed) code.

Not True, however, IF you leverage the .NET Framework class library
for your printing and for internal sorting needs. Since Fujitsu's
NetCOBOL for .NET fully supports the .NET Framework class library, you
can use the .NET classes to accomplish your task."

I continued:

"The .NET "ARRAY" class - which is part of the "System"
namespace/class library - can be filled with data - conceptually much
like you would fill a traditional COBOL table. Then, you can use the
SORT method that is exposed by the ARRAY class.

Then, there is the .NET ARRAYLIST class - which is part of the
"System.Collections" namespace/class library. This class also exposes
a SORT method and can be filled with data (that you want to sort).

Both the ARRAY class and the ARRAYLIST classes offer many other
features (in addition to SORTing). Additonally, there are other
similiar feature-rich classes in the "System.Collections"
namespace/class library that can also be used for
"loading/retrieving/manipulating" table data and value/pair data.

As for printing, using pure .NET code, in COBOL or any other .NET
enabled language...you have the .NET PrintDocument class found in the
"System.Drawing.Printing" namespace/class library. Using the public
methods and properties exposed by this class, you can certainly
accomplish your printing - in your .NET Windows Application.

If you happen to be using a .NET Web Application, you can even add two
or three lines of DHTML (or even Javascript) code to invoke the Print
method from the Windows object - to accomplish your printing. Of
course, you can even leverage the Print button that is freely exposed
on your Browser window to print your Printer-Ready HTML display.

Lastly, for your advanced Printing/Reporting needs, with either
Windows or Web, you could take advantage of the .NET Crystal Reports
components - yet another .NET Class Library - that are bundled with
VS.NET.

So, using COBOL (or any other .NET enabled language) and the .NET
Framework classes, you can easily print and/or SORT internally.

- well, that is about it.

---------------------------------

<Marketing>

For those interested, I have authored a book that uses this .NET
centric view towards using COBOL (or VB.NET) to build Windows and Web
applications.

To find out more please visit either of the following 3 links:

http://www.amazon.com/exec/obidos/ASIN/1590590481/eclecticsoftw-20

http://www.eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com

http://apress.com/book/bookDisplay.html?bID=112

Alternate link page:
http://www.eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com/HTMLPage3.html

</Marketing>
```

#### ↳ Re: ASSIGN TO PRINTER, Internal SORTing and .NET

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-14T17:26:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcg7fh$t6n$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b11feff.0306141411.3023c537@posting.google.com>`

```
And the "general" answer is,

 No, Fujitsu's NetCOBOL for  .NET is *not* an ANSI/ISO '85 much less 2002
conforming compiler (either as far as OO or non-OO features go).  They don't
claim that it is - and they certainly do not claim that code developed for
this compiler is portable to any other ANSI/ISO conforming COBOL compiler
(unless you use a VERY limited sub-set of code).

If, on the other hand, your "goal" or design requirement is to create a .NET
*only* solution, then this is certainly a viable solution.  Of course, it is
ALSO possible to create (using Fujitsu COBOL NetCOBOL for Windows - or ANY
of the other Windows compiler) object code that can run as "not managed"
within the .NET environment.  Such code would have the advantage of being
more portable but the disadvantage of having no "native" access to anything
that makes .NET really language-independent and Windows-exploiting
(including no access to the *extensive* .NET class libraries)

P.S. Chris - do you know whether V2 of NetCOBOL for .NET still has all (or
many) of the native COBOL language restrictions that V1 did? (e.g. no
internal SORT statement, no direct output to printers, etc)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ASSIGN TO PRINTER, Internal SORTing and .NET

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-14T17:28:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcg7kj$omh$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<b11feff.0306141411.3023c537@posting.google.com> <bcg7fh$t6n$1@slb4.atl.mindspring.net>`

#### ↳ Re: Eztreve Help

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-16T19:32:24+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcl608$62n$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<3005313.1055716670@dbforums.com>`

```
If all files involved are in ascending order by their key values, and the number
of keys are identical -

FILE OLDMSTR        ...
        MASTER-KEY  ...
..
FILE TRANS      ...
     TRANS-KEY ...
...
FILE NEWMSTR ...
...
JOB INPUT (OLDMSTR KEY (MASTER-KEY) +
                   TRANS     KEY (TRANS-KEY))
...

IF DUPLICATE TRANS
	DISPLAY ...
	GO TO JOB
END-IF
...
IF MATCHED
	PERFORM MASTER-UPDATE
	PERFORM WRITE-NEW-MASTER
	GO TO JOB
END-IF
...
IF OLDMSTR
	PERFORM WRITE-NEW-MASTER
	GO TO JOB
END-IF
...
IF TRANS
	PERFORM MASTER-CREATE
	PERFORM WRITE-NEW-MASTER
	GO TO JOB
END-IF
...

IF[NOT] file-name
```

<a id="t-20cf51f5c7"></a>
## Thread: Linking / debugging with MicroFocus NetExpress and Oracle Pro*COBOL — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Databases and SQL`](topics.md#databases)_

### Linking / debugging with MicroFocus NetExpress and Oracle Pro*COBOL

- **From:** matthew.james@shinetech.com (Matthew James)
- **Date:** 2003-06-16T23:05:49-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f426830.0306162205.3324aec8@posting.google.com>`

```
I am trying to get NetExpress (on Windows) to work with Oracle
Pro*COBOL (Oracle 9i) and was wondering if anyone has had any success.
 In particular:

- I have a COBOL module which I can pre-compile successfully with
Pro*COBOL
- I can then compile that using NetExpress
- I can link an .EXE that includes this module and the procobol .lib
(orasql9.lib)
- The .EXE runs *but* I get the error:

Load Error : file 'ORASQL8'
Called program file not found

The link appears to be working correctly, but the entries don't seem
to be there - it's almost like the library and pre-compiler are not
consistent ...

On a related note - does anybody know how to use Oracle connectivity
in a debug (non-statically linked) environment, ie with the Animator. 
The old tricks of rebuilding the cobol runtime system to statically
link in the procobol library no longer seem to apply.

Thanks
Matthew James
```

#### ↳ Re: Linking / debugging with MicroFocus NetExpress and Oracle Pro*COBOL

- **From:** "Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-17T10:23:11+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcmmrh$66j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<1f426830.0306162205.3324aec8@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi Matthew.

This is actually a bug in the Oracle precompiler. It embeds a call to
ORASQLx , in order to make the entry points subsequently called available to
the program.

With Oracle 9, it should embed a call to ORASQL9. The workaround is --
within the Oracle bin directory -- to copy ORASQL9.DLL to ORASQL8.DLL. Your
application should then execute correctly.

You should be able to debug your app in int/gnt code also, without the need
to relink the run-time (on Win32 at least), as Pro*COBOL embeds the above
call into the user app. You should only need to relink the COBOL run-time on
UNIX platforms.

Simon.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Linking / debugging with MicroFocus NetExpress and Oracle Pro*COBOL

- **From:** matthew.james@shinetech.com (Matthew James)
- **Date:** 2003-06-17T16:21:02-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1f426830.0306171521.249fd225@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<1f426830.0306162205.3324aec8@posting.google.com> <bcmmrh$66j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
Thanks Simon

That little trick fixed both my statically and dynamically linked problems :-)

Matthew James

"Simon Tobias" <Simon.Tobias@nospam.microfocus.com> wrote in message news:<bcmmrh$66j$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>...
> Hi Matthew.
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Simon.
```

<a id="t-802130f949"></a>
## Thread: Unicode (UTF-16 and UTF-32) data types — 8 msgs

### Unicode (UTF-16 and UTF-32) data types

- **From:** Jarl <jarl@no.spam.at.all>
- **Date:** 2003-06-18T11:19:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Xns939E8885B10E3jarl@195.58.103.121>`

```
Sorry if this question is too silly. But since I do not know much about 
Cobol I don't know this (yet).

When writing database applications, host variables are used when 
sending/retreiving data from the database.

For regular Latin-1 character data, the typical Cobol declaration looks 
like PICTURE X(10).

But how are the host variables declared for national character data 
(Unicode), UTF-16 and UTF-32 encodings?

What I'm looking for is something like C's wchar_t.


TIA,
Jarl
```

#### ↳ Re: Unicode (UTF-16 and UTF-32) data types

- **From:** "Paul Barnett" <paul.barnett@nospam.microfocus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-18T15:04:56+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcprmg$gno$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns939E8885B10E3jarl@195.58.103.121>`

```
Hi Jarl,

It's not a silly question. In Net Express 4.0 (the only COBOL development
tool I know of that supports Unicode) it would be defined for UTF-16 like
this:-

01 mystring	pic n(20) usage national.

Moving a PIC X(20) to PIC N(20) would change the ASCII (or EBCDIC as
applicable) to UTF-16 for you.

www.microfocus.com


"Jarl" <jarl@no.spam.at.all> wrote in message
news:Xns939E8885B10E3jarl@195.58.103.121...
> Sorry if this question is too silly. But since I do not know much about
> Cobol I don't know this (yet).
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jarl
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Unicode (UTF-16 and UTF-32) data types

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-18T11:12:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bcq31u$13o$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns939E8885B10E3jarl@195.58.103.121> <bcprmg$gno$1@hyperion.microfocus.com>`

```
For IBM, you might want to check out:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.7.1

and

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/2.4.32

    ***

Although there are standard definitions in the 2002 ANSI/ISO COBOL Standard,
there is none in the '85 Standard.  Therefore, "we" can't give you a generic
answer - without knowing your compiler and O/S.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unicode (UTF-16 and UTF-32) data types

- **Message-ID:** `<Xns939F65A96E582jarl@195.58.103.121>`
- **References:** `<Xns939E8885B10E3jarl@195.58.103.121> <bcprmg$gno$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <bcq31u$13o$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

Bill,

What does the 2002 ANSI/ISO Standard say? Does it specify how to declare

Thanks,
Jarl
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unicode (UTF-16 and UTF-32) data types

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T19:51:54-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd2ujc$5aa$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<Xns939E8885B10E3jarl@195.58.103.121> <bcprmg$gno$1@hyperion.microfocus.com> <bcq31u$13o$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <Xns939F65A96E582jarl@195.58.103.121>`

```
I don't think that it handles (well) UTF-32, but it allows (doesn't require)
PIC N and USAGE NATIONAL for UTF-16.  If you have down-loaded the "free"
FDIS version of the 2002 Standard, you can look for UTF-16 to see where and
how that is handled.
```

#### ↳ Re: Unicode (UTF-16 and UTF-32) data types

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T02:24:26+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030618222426.08822.00001144@mb-m16.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<Xns939E8885B10E3jarl@195.58.103.121>`

```
Jarl writes ...

>Sorry if this question is too silly. But since I do not know much about 
>Cobol I don't know this (yet).
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Short answer for IBM Enterprise COBOL,

a) compile with NSYMBOL(NATIONAL)
b) declare data items with usage NATIONAL and a PIC clause that uses N in the
same way DISPLAY uses X
c) declare literals as n'....' or nx'...'


Hope this helps.

Kind regards,

-Steve Comstock
The Trainer's Friend, Inc.
http://www.trainersfriend.com
```

#### ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-18T17:58:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bjqdnXgtB7wyc22jXTWJhg@giganews.com>`

```

"Cali" <mktnc@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com...
> Would like to hear any good/bad stories about moving working systems
> from Cobol to Java. - cali

I don't think you're going to get any, inasmuch as the project is doomed and
any who worked on it are now long gone.

This question does come up from time to time. As best I can tell, all who
posted this question are now living under bridges, sucking meager sustenance
from small amphibians.

Let me blunt: The idea is insane - unless you need flames and hula girls
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java

- **From:** mktnc@yahoo.com (Cali)
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T01:01:39-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<646fff9e.0306190001.5cd8f7f5@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <bjqdnXgtB7wyc22jXTWJhg@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<bjqdnXgtB7wyc22jXTWJhg@giganews.com>...
> "Cali" <mktnc@yahoo.com> wrote in message

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> undulating on the A/R Aging Report.

Yes, I agree. I am not looking for flames but what happens when you
have a working system, say a credit card transaction processing system
that works day in and day out and now you are faced with moving 100
thousand + lines of working Cobol code that you may have a few
thousand man hours/years in writing/maintaing to Java. (IBM is
promoting WebSphere, the old hands are retiring, etc.).  I am actually
looking for war stories. What was gained and what was lost and what
about maintenance? Is Cobol easier to maintain or is Java? -cali
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T07:57:15-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZBKdnQwxYb-2LmyjXTWJiw@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <bjqdnXgtB7wyc22jXTWJhg@giganews.com> <646fff9e.0306190001.5cd8f7f5@posting.google.com>`

```

"Cali" <mktnc@yahoo.com>
> >
> > Let me blunt: The idea is insane - unless you need flames and hula girls
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> about maintenance? Is Cobol easier to maintain or is Java? -cali

OK, that's fair. You only have one insane person in your organization.

1. Easier to maintain? I don't know. In spite of bugs in COBOL programs, I
suggest it is easier to make a ghastly mistake in Java (and double-secret
ghastly mistakes are common in C). The COBOL language itself imposes a
syntactic and logical discipline that is sometimes lacking in other
languages, including Java.

2. Java programmers are not, in the main, business programmers. They don't
know an amortization table from a double declining-balance. So you have to
factor in the learning curve for financial business rules. The Java
programmers I've met (not many) tend to not be detail driven. While there
may be a greater labor pool for Java, the labor pool of Java programmers
willing to work on financial stuff is, well, small. Don't forget that credit
card transactions move at 2400 Baud over dial-up modems (well, 90% do
including 30,000 ATM machines). What Java programmer wants to work with
that? Yuk!

3. The keeper of the Java standard, Sun, is in, um, straits. You recall Sun
did a silly thing - they pissed on Micros~1's leg and Micros~1 has a long
memory. Further, Sun is finding it difficult to sell their medium-sized
hardware as Intel multi-processor chips invade Sun's market. The outlook,
then, for control of Java is not great.

4. One incentive for Java - write once, run anywhere - is not much of an
incentive for financial applications. The chances of a credit card
application running on an unsecured PC (or a refrigerator) is zero. And the
chance of Java not being able to run on ANY PC is, however, non-zero (see #3
vies-a-vies Micros~1).

5. I'm sure you've got a few Java-capable programmers; take one application
that no one cares if it gets screwed-up - say Master Card processing for
Bank of America or Just-In-Time inventory control - convert it to Java and
let it rip. Note the time, resources, etc. Then you'll have a specific case
for comparison. (Pay no attention to customer complaints - Philistines just
don't appreciate art.)

PS
Can Java even print a credit card monthly statement? Can it do it without
pop-ups?

PPS
Speaking of ATM machines, I see a company is selling a pad of pre-printed
ATM receipts. You put these in your pocket when you go bar hopping. When you
meet a pretty girl, you fumble around for something on which to write your
'phone number, pull out the receipt, scribble your number on the back and
give it to her.

The receipt is for a $400 cash withdrawal and shows your remaining balance
as:

     $317,254,117.36

You almost always get a call.
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T16:35:37+00:00

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:

> Don't forget that credit
>card transactions move at 2400 Baud over dial-up modems (well, 90% do
>including 30,000 ATM machines).

Actually, they move at 300 baud. The average dial-up credit card transaction
takes 20 seconds, of which 18 seconds is telco setup and modem connect. Connect
speed for 300 baud is the fastest. Data volume is so small that transmission
speed doesn't matter.
```

#### ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T13:06:58+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef10d28_1@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com>`

```
"Cali" <mktnc@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com...
> Would like to hear any good/bad stories about moving working systems
> from Cobol to Java. - cali

There is inherent risk in being a "dedicated follower of fashion".

If the systems are performing well but the concern is future maintainability
and the diminishing supply of COBOL skill, then that is a different problem
from converting the systems. There are several solutions to it, none of
which necessarily involve converting the code.

If you see conversion as an opportunity to revamp system functionality and
move to a more modern approach, then you are on firmer ground. In this case
it would be sensible to isolate and encapsulate existing functionality into
sharable "building blocks" for the new systems. As the source language these
components are in is irrelevant (they won't be maintained; they just do what
they do, analogous to Java Beans), it is feasible for at least some of them
to stay in COBOL. (Later on, if you need to change the functionality you
could replace the old COBOL component with a Java Bean. It is better to do
"conversion" on a modular basis, when it is necessary, than to strive for
complete conversion of everything, when it isn't.) If you REALLY want to
convert everything to Java, look at PerCOBOL, which compiles COBOL code into
Java. Even though this is an excellent tool, it can't re-create your systems
as OO, and it would not be my choice for "converting" hundreds of thousands
of lines of COBOL into "procedural Java". That really buys you very little.

I would recommend that you keep your existing systems running while you do
some pilots. Take a single (non-critical) system and look at encapsulating
the existing COBOL code into components. There are tools available to help
you do this. Your Java shop will be Object Oriented and your existing
systems are not. That's why you need to give a lot of thought to what can be
salvaged from the present (and how). That is also why it would be disastrous
to simply convert your existing code to Java. You will end up with a system
that simply degrades Java and needs to be rewritten anyway.

In today's environment, COBOL components can run alongside components in
other languages without problem.This can extend the life of this
functionality until it NEEDS to be replaced. You don't say what your
environment is (Mainframe or client server); there are different tools for
these environments.

If you need detailed advice and help with both planning and implementing a
major conversion (consultancy), contact me privately.

Pete.
```

```
were sadly mistaken.
```

```
Generally it's a bad idea unless you where going to do a major re-write anyway.

And it's possible that companies will (more and more) be forced to convert to
different technologies because of a lack of available development staff.
As we all know almost no education centers teach COBOL any more and the wide
spread attitude amoungst the younger generation is it is outdated and "uncool"
(so they wouldn't want to work as a COBOL programmer anyway!)

(I'm trying to buck the trend as an early 30's programmer in a COBOL shop where
I am usually 20+ years younger than most other developers :) )

Regards,

Ray Smith
```

```
expensive.

cheaper. MS Windows is cheapest.
Linux is free. Why Microsoft "invents" C#? Virtual machine code can run
anywhere.

unavoidable. Because the mainframe works and Cobol works 24 hours / 7 days.
Leave them there no matter the expense is. What happens if the new stuff
crash like Windows 95? The decisions are from old men. Old men like old
things and just want to keep their possition safe. That is the logic of our
world.


Thomas
```

- **From:** "Warren Simmons" <wsimmons5@cox.net>

```
As an OLD Man, I've seen the burden of failing to keep up with the new stuff
many
many times. At least 60 of my 79 years.

I believe I understand the thinking that goes into the bashing of 'not
keeping up with
the new stuff'.  In every thing we consume the arrival of new is what keeps
us going.
It's wonderful for the spending public.

If one thinks about where and how they get paid to do things, eventually
they will
discover that there are many things that are older and finer. Or older and
rusty.

Anyone working with systems of most any kind had better believe that there
is always
a better way. Yet, ever those who understand that, realize  the economic
pressures that
work to bring them about. Members of CODASYL (that I knew) did not think
that
COBOL would serve this long just as the Y2K problem was unexpected for some
time.

There are very few things that you can not use COBOL for, and most readers
here will
tell you to use the best tool for the problem.

Understanding the nature of the problem is hard for younger people who like
to use new
things. We live in a throw away society. CODASYL established a short range,
mid range,
and long range research group. At that time, I believe few really understood
that the
objectives were only partically achieved.

Even now, there are many things that COBOL could encompass not covered in
the latest
standard. We do not have a set of tools that can be used by them selves
without
using other tools that already exist in one form or another.

Perhaps the biggest contribution that COBOL has made is the documentation of
the
problems where everyone instintly new what they did, but could not define
them
unless they were proven by "simulations"  which highlighted the procedures
and modified
them to provide uniformity in the area. I know that my boss felt that over
time the
need for each computer user to define the same application over and over was
to
be replaced by vendor packages for the subject of such maturity that a
company
needing such and such could buy it off of the shelf.

From my view, much of that objective has happened, and the user doesn't care
how it was written UNLESS.... New features and new tools will continue to
arrive. People using these kinds of tools will make a dollar review. Forty
years
after the birth of COBOL that job is simpler in 2003. Knowone knows what
magical changes will make cost comparisons to decide on using something new
will occur. In ssome small part of the review today, conversion has become
quantifiable.

In how many years has C become C++ and C#. How soon will it take on a new
face. Why? In retirement I've tried to keep up with BASIC. Got to version 4
and decided the objective didn't seem clearcut. KISS.

Warren Simmons
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T14:27:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3l56fvkfj27hbvgaepi0sj5tcvar6h1vj6@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <8cwIa.23778$111.23694@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote:

>Not only Cobol programmers are difficult to find, but also the cost is
>expensive.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>anywhere.

Thomas:

I wish the world were this simple....but it's not.  Lowest price is
not necessarily the preferred criteria when determining the best
solution to a problem.

Yugos are cheap and Lamborghinis are expensive.  If your objective is
to go back and forth to the grocery store as cheaply as possible, buy
a Yugo.....if you are looking for high performance, speed and superior
engineering....the Yugo is NOT the best alternative.

My experience many, many, many times over is that you always get
EXACTLY what you pay for.  If it sounds too good to be true, it
usually is.

>For long run, migration of Cobol to Java or C# and downsizing mainframes are
>unavoidable. Because the mainframe works and Cobol works 24 hours / 7 days.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>world.

Thomas......Boeing 747's are over 30 years old and F-18 Super Hornets
are very new,  and very sexy technology.

According to your logic old is bad and new is good.

According to your logic, it would follow that the superior aircraft to
move 400 people from New York to Los Angeles would be the Super
Hornet.  After all.....it's new and 747's are old.  It could be
done....one person at a time and what a ride!!

But is it the best choice?  Of course not.  Just as it would be
foolish to use a Boeing 747 in a dogfight with French Mirage or
Russian MiG fighter aircraft.

Old is NOT necessarily bad and new is NOT necessaily good.  Old DOES
mean *proven technology* otherwise it would never have gotten old.

New DOES mean *unproven* technology.

Choose the right tool for the job.  It's naive to assume that new is
always better than old.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

```
Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote 

> Thomas......Boeing 747's are over 30 years old and F-18 Super Hornets
> are very new,  and very sexy technology.

I don't want to be _too_ picky but the F-18 may have not been a good
choice for the analogy:

"""The Navy however was implacably hostile to the winning
single-engine YF-16, and decided to adopt an aircraft developed from
the losing Northrop YF-17 Cobra. McDonnell Douglas was brought in as
prime contractor and the F/A-18 was born.

The first flight of the Northrop YF-17A Cobra (72-1569) was on June
9th 1974, first flight of the YF-18A Hornet came on November 9th 1978
(160775)"""

So the origin of the F-18 is also '30 years'. The 747-400 is much the
same relationship to the first 747 as the Super Hornet to its.

Time flies like an arrow, fruit flies like a banana.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java

- **From:** mktnc@yahoo.com (Cali)
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T01:41:12-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<646fff9e.0306210041.1272d5ae@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <8cwIa.23778$111.23694@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote in message news:<8cwIa.23778$111.23694@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>...
> Not only Cobol programmers are difficult to find, but also the cost is
> expensive.
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Ray Smith

Thomas

So what argument could those in power make to management to justify
the expense of replacing working systems? I can understand the loss of
talent being one of those arguments (a serious one at that). I
recently read about IBM touting their new "T-Rex" mainframe running
some absurd number of Linux instances. I remember working with a team
maintaining 200+ Quad RS6000 boxes and one day I was thinking whether
or not managing one big box would have been cheaper, easier, fewer
people, more practical than the racks upon racks of servers we had to
service. Given that, I'm not yet convinced downsizing mainframes is
unavoidable. -cali
```

##### ↳ ↳ Comparison between Cobol and Java

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T15:39:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com>`

```
How about we discuss the following topics:

    What Cobol can't do but Java can?  Nothing.
    What Java can't do but Cobol can?  Nothing.

    What Cobol  can do better than Java? Reporting, transaction processing,
experience
    What Java can do better than Cobol?  Web, OO, TCP/IP

    What is the problem with Cobol? Standard vs. Implementation
    What is the problem with Java? One standard but changes too fast

    What is the needs of business applications? Competition.

    I think if we find the problem, we can find solutions. Both Cobol and
Java are solutions.

    What is the best criteria to choose one? For short time period, Cobol.
For long run, Java.

    Is Cobol and Java comparable? No.


Thomas


"Ray Smith" <ray@rays-web.com> wrote in message
news:5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com...
> mktnc@yahoo.com (Cali) wrote in message
news:<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com>...
> > Would like to hear any good/bad stories about moving working systems
> > from Cobol to Java. - cali
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Generally it's a bad idea unless you where going to do a major re-write
anyway.
>
> And it's possible that companies will (more and more) be forced to convert
to
> different technologies because of a lack of available development staff.
> As we all know almost no education centers teach COBOL any more and the
wide
> spread attitude amoungst the younger generation is it is outdated and
"uncool"
> (so they wouldn't want to work as a COBOL programmer anyway!)
>
> (I'm trying to buck the trend as an early 30's programmer in a COBOL shop
where
> I am usually 20+ years younger than most other developers :) )
>
> Regards,
>
> Ray Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T17:15:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306201615.72bd6741@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote

>     What is the problem with Cobol? Standard vs. Implementation

>     What is the problem with Java? One standard but changes too fast

There isn't actually a standard for Java, it is 'what Sun says it is'.
 MS tried to make it 'what MS says it is', but failed so they made C#
instead.

>     What is the best criteria to choose one? For short time period, Cobol.
> For long run, Java.

40 years ago there were 3000 languages plus Cobol.  20 years ago there
were 300 languages plus Cobol.  In a few years time there may be 30
languages plus Cobol.

Whether Java survives or is just another 'language d'Jour' has yet to
be seen. It may be just another in the line of: Algol, Pascal, C, Ada,
Modula2, C++, where it reaches a peak early and eventually is mostly
replaced.  MS certainly is trying to get C# as the successor to Java
One advantage of Cobol over the others mentioned here is the ease of
training. The popular languages take their programmers from education
sites running courses.  This works as long as those courses run.  Thus
the slow demise of Algol, Pascal, etc.

Cobol training has traditionally been done in-house.  These
programmers are not 'computer scientists' or even 'career
programmers', usually just staff doing a job.
```

```
handling is perfectly adequate.  Maybe the comment has to do

My name is Dave.  And I like COBOL.
```

```
COBOL handles null-terminated strings just fine.

move 'some string' & x'0' to null-terminated-string

move spaces to fixed-length-string
unstring null-terminated-string delimited by low-value into fixed-length-string

>My name is Dave.  And I like COBOL.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T11:26:21-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c99d007f.0306231026.6112f362@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <joe_zitzelberger-D084DF.20244920062003@corp.supernews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0306210846.4b805dc3@posting.google.com>`

```
thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell) wrote in message news:<bfdfc3e8.0306210846.4b805dc3@posting.google.com>...
> One comment...
> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> What makes you think Java is better for strings?

I didn't say Cobol had poor string handling.  With
String/Unstring/Inspect and reference modification you can do pretty
much everything you need.  For most routine string operations, Cobol
is great.  It's only failing is fixed field sizes, but that is a
benefit in other parts of the language.

Java native strings make it very simple to do things that would be
very complex in Cobol.

Automatic sizing and resizing of strings earns Java lots of "better"
points.  So does the built in regex support in matching, replacing and
splitting.

Automatic conversion of variables to strings for comparison,
concatenation or display is nice as well -- Cobol* will do this for
Display, but not for comparison or concatenation.



*FTR, I only use IBM Cobol, I have no idea if Microfocus has
extensions for this.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T22:07:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd36ie$md3$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
I have to say I have very little experience with Java and over 20 years
experience with Cobol and I should probably keep my mouth shut but I'm
going to reply to this anyway.

> How about we discuss the following topics:
>
>     What Cobol can't do but Java can?  Nothing.
           Don't know.

>     What Java can't do but Cobol can?  Nothing.

           Disagree. Nothing is better at business logic than Cobol.
           On my last job I had a Cobol pgm that ran an internal sort, with
           assembler subroutines and several VSAM reads per sort record in
           in the input procedure. Total sorted volume was 600 million records.
           The whole thing completed in about 5 hours. I seriously doubt Java
           even running on the mainframe could match the performance of the
           Cobol program.

>
>     What Cobol  can do better than Java? Reporting, transaction processing,
> experience

           These are the bread and butter processes of any business application.
           Why convert them to an inferior platform just because it's the current
           "cool" thing?

>     What Java can do better than Cobol?  Web, OO, TCP/IP

           Are you sure about that? I had another project to develop a big web site
           using Cobol and NetExpress. We even did the file I-O using MicroFocus
           ISAM files. It ran at about warp 6. The users were very happy.
           Later, dumb management turned support over to another group who converted
           everything to Java and Oracle. It ran like a TURD.
>
>     What is the problem with Cobol? Standard vs. Implementation

           I don't understand what you mean? Every implementation pretty much
           follows the standard and then has extentions.

>     What is the problem with Java? One standard but changes too fast

           There is one stardard? Every time I turn around there's a new Java
           version that screws up already existing stuff.

>
>     What is the needs of business applications? Competition.

           A business program has requirements that Java programmers don't seem
           to get. "Cool" is NOT one of them.
           1. It must be CORRECT. Perform the processing accurately.
           2. It must be MAINTAINABLE. 'Nuff said. Cute code has no place in a
              business application.
           3. It must be EFFICIENT. Most Java I've seen, even on my home PC, runs
              like a TURD...see above.

>
>     I think if we find the problem, we can find solutions. Both Cobol and
> Java are solutions.

           But NOT for the same problem.

>
>     What is the best criteria to choose one? For short time period, Cobol.
> For long run, Java.

           Time will tell, but I don't think Java can replace Cobol in
           hard-core, high file I-O, complex business logic apps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T13:55:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote:

>How about we discuss the following topics:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>    What Java can do better than Cobol?  Web, OO, TCP/IP

Thomas....where have you been?  Virtually every COBOL compiler offers
Web and TCP/IP tools.  In addition, other third party COBOL compiler
vendors offer additional tools.  It is patently incorrect to suggest
that Java offers better capability than COBOL in this area.

Have you personally compared the Web and TCP/IP features of all the
COBOL compilers to those offered by Java?  If so, PLEASE enlighten us
with the details?

In addition, I know of two object-oriented COBOL compilers.  Have you
personally compared the point-by-point OO capabilities of Java to the
OO capabilities of these two compilers?  If so, PLEASE enlighten us
with the details!

It's easy to say "This is better than that."  It's quite another issue
to back up such a claim with factual data.

>    What is the problem with Cobol? Standard vs. Implementation
>    What is the problem with Java? One standard but changes too fast

So you are suggesting that Java is a moving target........corporate IT
managers don't like moving targets....they like stability.  They like
it when they aren't forced to re-write their core program source code
every three to six months.

>    What is the needs of business applications? Competition.

Not if it is an "in-house" application.  It doesn't need to compete
with any other application.  It merely needs to serve the requirements
of the corporation in the most cost-effective manner possible.

>    I think if we find the problem, we can find solutions. Both Cobol and
>Java are solutions.

I agree with this statement.  Use the correct language for the task at
hand.

>    What is the best criteria to choose one? For short time period, Cobol.
>For long run, Java.

Why?  From my observations, COBOL has already proven itself as a
long-term programming solution.  Java hasn't been around long enough
to prove anything.

>    Is Cobol and Java comparable? No.

You are absolutely correct........for business application
development, COBOL is far superior.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T16:49:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com>`

```
Virtually every COBOL compiler offers Web and TCP/IP tools.  In addition,
other third party COBOL compiler vendors offer additional tools.  It is
patently incorrect to suggest  that Java offers better capability than COBOL
in this area.


> Have you personally compared the Web and TCP/IP features of all the
> COBOL compilers to those offered by Java?  If so, PLEASE enlighten us
> with the details?
>

***************
The problem is the TCP/IP API which is not defined by standard where Java
defines TCP/IP API in its specification. Java can transmit Java program
itself  without recompilation and run on another platform. How can Cobol do
that?  Is there is anything like "applet" in Cobol world?
***************

> In addition, I know of two object-oriented COBOL compilers.  Have you
> personally compared the point-by-point OO capabilities of Java to the
> OO capabilities of these two compilers?  If so, PLEASE enlighten us
> with the details!
>
***************
I have read OO cobol specification from beginning to end. I think OO cobol
is the traditional Cobol with a new patch. They doesn't fit well. For
example, there is no OO library, no reflection, no way to define a class
without name. It is like C#. The syntax is too long for a compiler writer.
Nothing about binary format. You know both Java and .Net (including C#)
have defined binary format.
***************

> It's easy to say "This is better than that."  It's quite another issue
> to back up such a claim with factual data.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
***************
Sure. I don't like Java it changes too fast. But it get more and more
powerful and free.
Compare how many Java package you can download with Cobol.
The important thing they are all defined by a single party: Sun. I think it
is a standard like Microsoft in some sense.

***************

> >    What is the needs of business applications? Competition.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> >    What is the best criteria to choose one? For short time period,
Cobol.
> >For long run, Java.
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T09:22:15-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-FD7C89.09221524062003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <bd7i17$c0r$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bd7i17$c0r$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On 23-Jun-2003, "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote:
> 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> apples.

Mainframe Java is identical to all the other Javas.  That is the nice 
thing about them.  You can still create a socket, get its inputStream or 
outputStream and treat it just like a file.

I think the only way a Cobol could claim to have equal functionality in 
the networking tools is if you could dynamically define an FD for a 
network connection and use OPEN/READ/WRITE/CLOSE on it.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T12:05:23+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef7963b_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Thomas,

I've enjoyed your posts and I think you make some good points. In general I
don't disagree with your position re Java (I use it myself and enjoy it for
some tasks).

However, I simply MUST comment on some of this post ...<G>


"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote in message
news:7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com...
> Virtually every COBOL compiler offers Web and TCP/IP tools.  In addition,
> other third party COBOL compiler vendors offer additional tools.  It is
> patently incorrect to suggest  that Java offers better capability than
COBOL
> in this area.
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> itself  without recompilation and run on another platform. How can Cobol
do
> that?  Is there is anything like "applet" in Cobol world?
> ***************
>
Good point on Applets (which are extremely useful). However, .NET COBOL can
also be used as a scripting language. Have a look at:
http://www.aspalliance.com/aldotnet/examples/coboldotnet.aspx

I am not advocating that we all stop using Applets and move to COBOL; I am
simply saying that "modern COBOL" can achieve similar functionality.

The real solution is to use the best tools for the job. (I first advocated
this 6 years ago, in the face of fierce resistance <G>; I'm pleased to see
that today many people have realised the wisdom in maintaining an expanded
skill set and not just relying on COBOL as a "one language wonder".
Furthermore, more and more COBOL people are realising that Java is NOT hard
to learn (it is arguably easier than C++) and there are many benefits in
doing so. It is NOT a contest. Both languages complement each other.)

You mention that TCP/IP connectivity is not defined by standard, in COBOL. I
don't think that matters as long as I can access a TCP/IP Class library, or
COM component, or API from COBOL (and I can...).

> > In addition, I know of two object-oriented COBOL compilers.  Have you
> > personally compared the point-by-point OO capabilities of Java to the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> is the traditional Cobol with a new patch. They doesn't fit well.

Spooky! That was exactly my reaction when I first got into OO COBOL. It is
apparent that OO has been "bolted on" to COBOL. (I call it "Jake the Peg"
after a three legged man, invented by Australian comedian Rolf Harris...).
However, having used it for a few years now, and being much more familiar
with it, I think we should acknowledge that this was a major feat of
Software Engineering. The people who pushed for this and got it implemented,
deserve full credit. It is more verbose than comparable OO languages like
Java, but verbosity has always been a strength (as well as a disadvantage)
of COBOL. I am quite happy using OO COBOL to build components and manage
components written in other languages.


> For
> example, there is no OO library, no reflection, no way to define a class
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> ***************

Fujitsu OO COBOL comes with an extensive Class Library.  I don't understand
why you  would want to define a nameless Class (except for reasons of
interface maybe?), but these are surely small things. No one is seriously
going to suggest writing a compiler in OO COBOL (not because you COULDN'T;
it is just not the best tool for that particular job...). Binary format
(BLOBs), while it may not be intrinsically defined as part of the language,
can be easily manipulated using OO COBOL and a Class Library or COM
component. I believe it is important to see COBOL (particularly OO COBOL) as
NOT just what the Standard defines, but also the extensions that come from
component and Class libraries, exactly as the power of other OO Languages
lies in these facilities. (Where would C++ be without MFC <G>?)

>
> > It's easy to say "This is better than that."  It's quite another issue
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> powerful and free.

I try not to get emotionally attached to ANY computer software or language
<G>, but I DO like java. Having made a living from COBOL for around 30
years, I can't help but be attached to COBOL also...<G>. (I would be a
shallow individual to abandon it now <G>). Besides, there is no need to. OO
COBOL is an extremely useful tool for developing modern distributed Business
applications, just as non-OO COBOL was for decades of centralized mainframe
development. Java does change but it is much more in touch with its user
base than COBOL, and Java Programmers feel like they can actually make a
difference and shape the future directions of the Language. This is largely
due to Sun not taking an authoritative approach and, instead, encouraging
input from the User Base and listening to and acting on it, where the
community agreed proposed changes were beneficial.

> Compare how many Java package you can download with Cobol.
> The important thing they are all defined by a single party: Sun. I think
it
> is a standard like Microsoft in some sense.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >    I think if we find the problem, we can find solutions. Both Cobol
and
> > >Java are solutions.
> >
> > I agree with this statement.  Use the correct language for the task at
> > hand.

As long as we still need to use "languages", this is definitely the best
approach...<G>.

> >
> > >    What is the best criteria to choose one? For short time period,
> Cobol.
> > >For long run, Java.
> >

Why choose ONE at all?


> > Why?  From my observations, COBOL has already proven itself as a
> > long-term programming solution.  Java hasn't been around long enough
> > to prove anything.
> >

Slightly unfair on Java... Many commercial users have found (and are
finding) value in it.


> > >    Is Cobol and Java comparable? No.
> >
> > You are absolutely correct........for business application
> > development, COBOL is far superior.
> >

A sweeping statement. We can't be sure that in EVERY single case this is
true. (Even if we'd LIKE to think so...) Each case needs to be looked at on
its merits, but, in general this is certainly true. Nevertheless, there are
commercial applications where I would use Java (a Web Presentation tier, for
example). It really depends on how OO the application is.

(Nevertheless, I am currently working on a Web application that is "driven"
by COBOL CGI/ASAPI. The whole area is so grey that it is probably best to
refrain from making blanket statements about it...).

The best tools for the job.

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Rolf Harris

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T17:02:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef84e77_4@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <3ef7963b_5@news.athenanews.com> <3EF7A64E.9000701@Knology.net>`

```
Rolf Harris is an Artist, in the true sense of the word.

Yes, he did write and perform "Tie Me Kangaroo Down, Sport" but this is just
the tip of the iceberg.

(You certainly didn't believe this song was written by a serious songwriter,
did you?

"So we tanned his died now he's dead, Fred
 And that's it, hangin' on the shed...")

I used "comedian" in the sense of someone who makes us laugh.(I have never
watched a Harris performance without laughing out loud...)

Inevitably he engenders a spirit of well-being and fun in his performances
(whether they are painting, playing music, or simply telling stories.

Actually, he is much deeper than many people realise. He hosted a UK TV
program on pet rescue and animal welfare that showed the sensitive animal
lover side of his nature.

"Jake the Peg" remains one of my alltime favourites and is very appropriate
for OO COBOL.

You can see the lyrics, background, and even listen to a clip at this
Website: http://www.listentome.net/music2.html

Enjoy!

Pete.

"LX-i" <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote in message
news:3EF7A64E.9000701@Knology.net...

Peg"

Harris...).

> Kangaroo Down, Sport", right?  (My kids are big Wiggles fans...)
```

```
era, not my grandchildren's era.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Rolf Harris

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EF8DB47.7060508@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <3ef7963b_5@news.athenanews.com> <3EF7A64E.9000701@Knology.net> <bd9m03$ks0$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
