Howard Brazee wrote:
> On 23-Jun-2003, LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net> wrote:
> 
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> era, not my grandchildren's era.

I've never heard that claim of the Wiggles.  :)  They do have a -lot- of 
original songs, but one of the Wiggles videos (the kid's favorite) is 
called "It's a Wiggly, Wiggly World", and features songs and "guest 
appearances" by many different folks.  Rolf Harris is on there, and says 
"I've written a little song called 'Tie Me Kangaroo Down, Sport'". 
There's also an adaptation of the Tim Finn song "Six Months in a Leaky 
Boat", and a guest spot by Slim Dusty (who is a really cool Australian 
country singer).

I knew TMKDS has been around a while, but from the looks of the video, 
so has Rolf Harris, so I found it believable...  :)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T15:07:44+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<okogfvoi3r7hloev4iei7bnci2sbjei39o@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
"Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com> wrote:

>***************
>The problem is the TCP/IP API which is not defined by standard where Java
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>***************

Whether the feature is native in the language specification or
available through add-ons to the language is immaterial.

The simple fact remains that the facilities are there to allow
programmer to accomplish the task quickly and easily.

You can mow the lawn with a power mower or a reel mower........they
are two very different tools.  But they both get the job done.  Some
prefer the power mower for speed and ease, but others prefer getting
more exercise and sending fewer pollutants into the atmosphere by
using a reel mower.  In both cases the lawn gets mowed.  That's what
really counts.

>>
>***************
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>***************

I commend you for your knowledge of OO........but, who declared that
the only method of program implementation is through Object Oriented
programming?

You are assuming that this is the only manner in which to accomplish
the desired task.  At the risk of repeating myself, again.......the
MOST important factor is that the BUSINESS application gets written in
a timely fashion and works as desired with reasonably maintainable
code.  The END RESULT is the most important issue......the method to
achive the desired result is of secondary importance.

Wine grapes can be crushed under human feet in a large vat or crushed
with a half-million dollar crushing machine.......but applying the new
technology doesn't improve the quality of the wine......it may allow
you to crush grapes faster.....but at a much greater up-front
investment.

In the end, the customer really doesn't care how the grapes were
crushed.....he or she only cares about the wine.

>> So you are suggesting that Java is a moving target........corporate IT
>> managers don't like moving targets....they like stability.  They like
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>***************

Being able to download 2 or 3 or 10 times as many Java packages as
COBOL packages is a moot point.  I'm not referring to silly little
utilities to animate a web site.....yes...those utilities SHOULD be
written in Java.  I'm talking about real industrial-strength business
applications which can process massive numbers of business
transactions.  Those programs are not ones that are downloaded from
the Internet.

And there is a big difference between a "standard" and an "edict."
Standards are established by a consensus of many diverse
parties........edicts are issued by a single entity.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

It seems to me that "who" is just about everybody who uses OO.    Since it

as well.

of the new paradigm almost universally condemn the old problems.

> with a half-million dollar crushing machine.

And I know people who condemn modern food machines.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T15:13:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef913ca_2@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <okogfvoi3r7hloev4iei7bnci2sbjei39o@4ax.com> <bd9qh0$p78$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Come on Howard, you didn't seriously expect me to let this one go, now, did
you <G>?

Comments below...

"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote in message
news:bd9qh0$p78$1@peabody.colorado.edu...
>
> On 24-Jun-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> doesn't work so well on mainframe environments, they tend to condemn
mainframes
> as well.
>
 OO works perfectly well in mainframe or any other environment.

Please show a post where I, as an OO advocate, have ever condemned
mainframes in general.(I worked on them for over 20 years, for Chrissake!
IBM,  ICL, Burroughs, Univac, CDC, NCR, and Honeywell)

The fact that more and more mainframe sites are moving to Java as a bridge
to OO, refutes your argument. Some sites think they are moving to Java as a
way to "modernize" their code; it isn't long before the capabilities of Java
(which is innately OO) kick in and they start rebuilding functions into
Objects... Pretty soon they have grasped the ideas of OO development, and
from there on it is just a matter of time before the Fortress crumbles and
disappears.

OO is NOT hardware dependent. It is dependent on looking at commercial
system development in a manner which is completely different from what has
traditionally been the case. People with decades of COBOL (unless they have
kept their minds open and are mentally agile) have major problems with it.
Youngsters, who have no such prejudice, take to it like ducks to water.
Inevitably, the ducklings will outlast the old parrots...

I do refer to "Fortress COBOL" which encompasses the "COBOL think" on many
mainframe sites. This includes slavish devotion to the Waterfall as a
development Methodology, the fixed idea that something which has worked for
decades MUST be right and there is no need to change it (or even review or
evaluate it, in the light of more modern practice and tools).

The simple fact is that this "Fortress" mentality is prevalent on mainframe
sites, rather than Client/Server/PC ones. It is propped up by a lack of
understanding of what is happening in the IT Marketplace (and, up until
fairly recently, a disdain for things PC), a desire to stay comfortably
ensconced in "what we know"  (this leads to seeing COBOL as a "one language
wonder") and fierce resistance to ANYTHING (methodology, tools or even "best
practice") that would affect the Staus Quo. (Hence my use of "Fortress").

However, not ALL mainframe sites suffer from it, and my problem is not with
mainframes per se, it is with the ancient Priests who recite parrot fashion
the "received wisdom" of the 60s and 70s, are lost in the dreamtime of
former glory when their word was Law, and cannot see the barbarians at the
gates. (Sorry, I get a bit carried away about this, because it really pisses
me off...<G>. For a lighter look, read my article at:
http://www.aboutlegacycoding.com/archives/v4/v40503.asp (more than 25,000
people read this article within 4 weeks of it being posted; there are a lot
of COBOL people who don't frequent CLC...)


> If the new paradigm is not ideally suited for the old problems, then
advocates
> of the new paradigm almost universally condemn the old problems.
>
Well, you did qualify it by saying "ALMOST universally" <G>.

I hope you are excluding me with that "almost", Howard. If you aren't, then
please quote where I have ever condemned a PROBLEM because it didn't fit a
PARADIGM. (The converse of this (condemning a PARADIGM because you can't see
how your PROBLEMS are covered by it) is exactly what the Fortress Priests
do, "almost universally" <G>).
>
> > You can mow the lawn with a power mower or a reel mower........they
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
And I know people who pay someone else to do the lawns...

(Watch for a significant growth in IT outsourcing over the next 5 years. It
is fuelled by the general public becoming more computer literate and being
quite capable of managing their own spreadsheets and databases. This leaves
the IT responsibility as being to provide consolidated corporate figures
from all the departments, via the network, and maintaining and managing the
Network infrastructure. Extend this idea, and IT gradually comes down to
"Network Maintenance" with little or no systems development (that will be
catered for by User departments with their own Office tools (spreadsheets,
databases, etc.) and  component based packages for existing accounting and
Business functions).

 The "Network Maintenance" can be easily and profitably outsourced, and it
will be.

Goodbye, IT department, technocrats, and empire...Down comes the castle and
IT goes to the collective farms where it should have been all along...<G>.
Power to the People! (...and not a drop of blood was spilled <G>)

>
> > Wine grapes can be crushed under human feet in a large vat or crushed
> > with a half-million dollar crushing machine.
>
> And I know people who condemn modern food machines.

Yep, but most of them drink the wine...

Bob is right on the button when he says the end result is what matters.

However, as IT people, it is hard to look beyond our own part in how the
result is achieved. IT has been the heart and essential core of Corporate
effectiveness, and it still is. The difference is that IT is no longer the
preserve of a few highly trained technocrats; it is becoming Public Domain.
Kids are dealing with computers from the age of 2. I know 11 year olds who
organise their schoolwork assignments and social life with Outlook!  By the
time these same kids enter the work force (say 10 years from now), they will
be more than capable of handling the IT requirement of the departments they
work in.

The World is changing (rapidly), but from the turrets of the Fortress one
just can't see it...

Pete.
```

```
I snipped a lot, just a few comments.

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3ef913ca_2@news.athenanews.com...
<<snip>>|
| The fact that more and more mainframe sites are moving to Java as a bridge
| to OO, refutes your argument. Some sites think they are moving to Java as
a
| way to "modernize" their code; it isn't long before the capabilities of
Java
| (which is innately OO) kick in and they start rebuilding functions into
| Objects... Pretty soon they have grasped the ideas of OO development, and
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| traditionally been the case. People with decades of COBOL (unless they
have
| kept their minds open and are mentally agile) have major problems with it.
| Youngsters, who have no such prejudice, take to it like ducks to water.
| Inevitably, the ducklings will outlast the old parrots...

The problem, poor word choice (I know), it that there is still no ONE
solution to a problem. Different people see the same thing differently.
I worked on 2 projects that used Yourdon/DeMarco methodology to model the
system.
In both cases I worked independently, and developed models that were
significanly different than those developed by others. (I was told to
continue in both cases.)
Similar situations should occur with OO, and OO is not bad-design proof.

Bad design will still complicate rather than simplify maintenance &
enhancement.
A poor design on a pilot project will impact future projects.

|
| I do refer to "Fortress COBOL" which encompasses the "COBOL think" on many
| mainframe sites. This includes slavish devotion to the Waterfall as a
| development Methodology, the fixed idea that something which has worked
for
| decades MUST be right and there is no need to change it (or even review or
| evaluate it, in the light of more modern practice and tools).

Practices and tools be damned.
What is the cost justification for change?
Bean Counters still rule.

|
| The simple fact is that this "Fortress" mentality is prevalent on
mainframe
| sites, rather than Client/Server/PC ones. It is propped up by a lack of
| understanding of what is happening in the IT Marketplace (and, up until
| fairly recently, a disdain for things PC), a desire to stay comfortably
| ensconced in "what we know"  (this leads to seeing COBOL as a "one
language
| wonder") and fierce resistance to ANYTHING (methodology, tools or even
"best
| practice") that would affect the Staus Quo. (Hence my use of "Fortress").

You need the right tool for the job.
Sometimes it's a mainframe Batch system written in COBOL.

|
<<snip>>
|
| (Watch for a significant growth in IT outsourcing over the next 5 years.
It
| is fuelled by the general public becoming more computer literate and being
| quite capable of managing their own spreadsheets and databases. This
leaves
| the IT responsibility as being to provide consolidated corporate figures
| from all the departments, via the network, and maintaining and managing
the
| Network infrastructure. Extend this idea, and IT gradually comes down to
| "Network Maintenance" with little or no systems development (that will be
| catered for by User departments with their own Office tools (spreadsheets,
| databases, etc.) and  component based packages for existing accounting and
| Business functions).

The only problem is performance.
The 25 hour end-of-day update is still unacceptable.
"High Priest" of one demonination or another will still be needed, until
"How to do it" is perfectly determined by the machine.

The user may design the format and content of the monthly statement, but
it's still a programmer who has to make it happen.

A single user will usually address their own problem, and ignore the needs
of other departments.
|
|  The "Network Maintenance" can be easily and profitably outsourced, and it
| will be.
|
| Goodbye, IT department, technocrats, and empire...Down comes the castle
and
| IT goes to the collective farms where it should have been all along...<G>.
| Power to the People! (...and not a drop of blood was spilled <G>)
|

May I have a hit?
Must be some good shit!

War Stories of managers who went their own way, and abandoned IT make the
rounds regularly.
They tried to cut costs, and increased them.
They tried to cut the implementation time for new features/functions, and
failed.
If they kept their job, they regretted the decision.

Executives make decisions, they don't implement them.
That's left to others.
```

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:JqnKa.21273$3o3.1650258@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
> I snipped a lot, just a few comments.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> | The fact that more and more mainframe sites are moving to Java as a
bridge
> | to OO, refutes your argument. Some sites think they are moving to Java
as
> a
> | way to "modernize" their code; it isn't long before the capabilities of
> Java
> | (which is innately OO) kick in and they start rebuilding functions into
> | Objects... Pretty soon they have grasped the ideas of OO development,
and
> | from there on it is just a matter of time before the Fortress crumbles
and
> | disappears.
> |
> | OO is NOT hardware dependent. It is dependent on looking at commercial
> | system development in a manner which is completely different from what
has
> | traditionally been the case. People with decades of COBOL (unless they
> have
> | kept their minds open and are mentally agile) have major problems with
it.
> | Youngsters, who have no such prejudice, take to it like ducks to water.
> | Inevitably, the ducklings will outlast the old parrots...
>
> The problem, poor word choice (I know), it that there is still no ONE
> solution to a problem.

On re-reading my post, I realise that it sounds as if I am advocating OO as
a panacea. I'm not, and it isn't.

I agree there are many ways to skin a cat. But the Waterfall isn't a panacea
either.

Yet on Fortress sites it is like "Holy Writ" carved deep in stone, to the
point where people don't even THINK any more. The response is simply: "Did
we get a sign off?" as if that is all that matters. I have been on sites
where they would have burned me at the stake as a Heretic for even
SUGGESTING that alternative approaches (not necessarily OO) could be
considered...

>Different people see the same thing differently.

Absolutely. That is why the power of persuasion (backed with actual
benchmarks and tests) is an essential weapon in any consultant's armoury.

> I worked on 2 projects that used Yourdon/DeMarco methodology to model the
> system.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I understand what you're saying and I don't disagree. However, if  both
paths achieve a good result, no harm has been done and the installation is
now better equipped with broader experience to evaluate which tool to use in
the future, on a project-by-project basis.

Despite everything I have said in this thread, I do not advocate the removal
of the Waterfall. There are times when it is appropriate (although I try and
avoid those projects, as it is definitely anathema to my nature to lock
users in to something with a signature, and I relish the challenge of
dealing with the unexpected that comes with RAD approaches.) I guess what
I'm really saying is that there can never be a CHOICE as long as ONE
methodology is followed slavishly.

I like RAD/OO. I like to see systems evolve through interaction rather than
be designed and frozen on day one. However, that is a personal preference
and I accept that others see things differently. As long as we both achieve
the desired result, no harm is done. (I'm prepared to bet the people on my
team will have more fun and job satisfaction than those on the "other" team,
but then, I WOULD say that <G>!)

> Bad design will still complicate rather than simplify maintenance &
> enhancement.

Yes. But it is also important to point out that "maintenance and
enhancement" are becoming much less important in the scheme of things than
they have been traditionally. I know it is difficult for COBOL people to
come to grips with this, after accepting for years that one of the primary
advantages of COBOL is ease of maintenance, but we are approaching the stage
of "throwaway" systems and components. The programming language is no longer
a real consideration. The Business functionality is what matters (actually,
that has always been the case, but sometimes we get so involved in our own
technoworld that we lose sight of it...). If the Business changes (and, as
we all know, it does, with amazing rapidity and regularity) there is a case
for simply adding new components to the package (or replacing existing ones,
rather than amending existing ones). Obviously, Object Oriented programming
lends itself to this approach much better than the conventional procedural
approach. (That is one reason why I am a "fan" of OO...).

> A poor design on a pilot project will impact future projects.

Absolutely! After fighting tooth and nail to get permission to do a pilot,
it would be tragic to blow the opportunity by not doing it properly.

I always manage pilot projects personally and maintain close involvement
with the Users and the programming teams (whatever my other duties may
require). If it fails it is MY fault; no one else's.... Of course, when it
succeeds it is a success for all...<G> .  So far, I've never had one fail.
The point is, special care MUST be taken with pilot projects. It is
important to recognise that what we are doing will be new to many of the
participants and they may need help and support grasping new
concepts which are often diametrically opposed to what they have been
brought up on.

>
> |
> | I do refer to "Fortress COBOL" which encompasses the "COBOL think" on
many
> | mainframe sites. This includes slavish devotion to the Waterfall as a
> | development Methodology, the fixed idea that something which has worked
> for
> | decades MUST be right and there is no need to change it (or even review
or
> | evaluate it, in the light of more modern practice and tools).
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Well, not always...<G>

Sometimes new approaches and tools can be slipped in to an existing budget.
The best way to do this is to get the support of the Users and ideally, get
them to share the cost. I do this by finding out what is MOST important to
them and promising it will be delivered.  ("If you could have ONE single
thing from IT, what would it be?" )  I then stake my career on delivering
it...<G>

(Sometimes it has been too close for comfort...On one occasion, when  a
Director baited me with the old "You consultants cost a fortune and give us
nothing..." I reacted angrily that I would give him his money back if the
goals were not achieved in the time I said... (As I left his office, I
realised I had been suckered, but there was nothing I could do about it. If
I reneged I would lose all credibility...) 9 months later the deadline was 2
days away and we were having mainframe problems with APF (the programmer
responsible was not happy with what were doing as it undermined his power
base and he had been covertly hostile right throughout the duration of the
project.) I had a one-on-one with him. He made the mistake of assuming I was
just another Manager who could be bullshitted with techspeak. At that point
I was merciless. I told him if the required module was not available and
functioning within the next 12 hours I would write it myself and he would
be looking for another job. He wasn't game to call my bluff (actually, I
wasn't bluffing and would've done it, but he didn't know that...). He came
up with the goods and I made a special point at the celebration party of
acknowledging his great contribution. The Director who had suckered me
attended the party and was gracious enough  to say how impressed he was, and
he picked up the tab for the celebration (it was several thousand pounds,
some of which was coming out of my personal pocket as I couldn't justify all
of it in my project budget <G>). I learned my lesson from this   (I would've
had to mortgage the house to pay the debt...ugh! I get cold shudders just
thinking about it...) and have never offered a money-back guarantee
since...however, there are occasions where I would refund if it was fair to
do so.

Bottom line: If you believe in what you are doing, never let Bean Counters
or financial considerations stop you...

> |
> | The simple fact is that this "Fortress" mentality is prevalent on
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> | practice") that would affect the Staus Quo. (Hence my use of
"Fortress").
>
> You need the right tool for the job.

We all agree on that (I hope...<G>)

> Sometimes it's a mainframe Batch system written in COBOL.
>

I have never suggested otherwise. In fact I see this particular use of COBOL
as extending the life of the Language.

> |
> <<snip>>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> | is fuelled by the general public becoming more computer literate and
being
> | quite capable of managing their own spreadsheets and databases. This
> leaves
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> | "Network Maintenance" with little or no systems development (that will
be
> | catered for by User departments with their own Office tools
(spreadsheets,
> | databases, etc.) and  component based packages for existing accounting
and
> | Business functions).
>
> The only problem is performance.
> The 25 hour end-of-day update is still unacceptable.

If everything is updated in real time there is no end-of-day update.
Spreadsheets can feed the central data repository via the Corporate Network,
as they are updated. Even if this didn't make sense, performance is becoming
a worry of the past.

Faced with a "25 hour end-of-day update" the company would simply spend
$60,000 and put in two more top-of-the-range servers. Input files would be
split, the system would employ load-levelling, and the problem goes away.

Performance is no longer an acceptable excuse for antiquated system design.


> "High Priest" of one demonination or another will still be needed, until
> "How to do it" is perfectly determined by the machine.
>

OR the User Departments...<G>

> The user may design the format and content of the monthly statement, but
> it's still a programmer who has to make it happen.
>

Nope. Only in the Fortress. Look around. See what small companies are doing
with accounting packages. Users are also getting much "smarter" in their
ability to use software.

> A single user will usually address their own problem, and ignore the needs
> of other departments.

If they are properly managed they won't. In fact, I have found just the
opposite to be true. I have seen User departments implement their own "IT
system" then extend it to accommodate other departments who expressed a
desire for the same figures.

In one case this "IT system" was based around the corporate data resource
provided by IT, but with information "massaged" and presented in a form
which was most acceptable to the User department(s). This was a problem for
some of my guys who felt that IT should control ALL the data, until I got
them to work WITH these departments and ensure that proper controls were in
place and best practices were being observed. We made it clear that we
reserved the right to change the format and nature of the "feeds" at any
time and the Users were OK with this. In other words, rather than forbid
them to develop their own systems, we made it clear that we would not be res
ponsible for such systems, but we would "help" them as long as they adhered
to best practices. It worked out fine for all concerned. We had some
problems with different figures appearing on some reports depending on
whether they came from IT or the Users <G>, but this was eventually resolved
by Joint workshops.

Bottom Line: IT is the "tail". The Business is the "dog". The tail does not
wag the dog. But neither tail nor dog are complete, devoid of each other...
(Sure, some dogs exist happily without a tail, but most dogs PREFER to have
a tail <G> (source for this: a TV program I watched recently where Vets are
querying the wisdom of docking dogs' tails...)

> |
> |  The "Network Maintenance" can be easily and profitably outsourced, and
it
> | will be.
> |
> | Goodbye, IT department, technocrats, and empire...Down comes the castle
> and
> | IT goes to the collective farms where it should have been all
along...<G>.
> | Power to the People! (...and not a drop of blood was spilled <G>)
> |
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I have long believed (ever since I first programmed a computer in 1965) that
the problem with computer technology is that it is not freely available to
EVERYBODY. Up until the 1980s it was necessary to have wizards who could
cavort and chant in front of a mysterious machine, surrounded by smoke and
mirrors and flashing lights, in order to unleash the power of the computer.

With the coming of the "Personal" Computer this has changed and many of the
population are "doing it for themselves". (look around some of the Google
newsgroups and see the number of people who are getting information that
will allow them to make their computer behave the way they want it to.
(These are NOT professional programmers who make a living out of this
stuff...))

This trend is spreading and looks like continuing to do so. Software is
getting smarter (and simpler, Thank God) and people are getting more
computer literate.

The logical extrapolation of this is that computer power will devolve to the
people (and away from the technocrats...).

There are currently around 400,000,000 people making several billion hits on
the Internet every day. They are not about to become "computer programmers".
Instead, they will require user friendly interfaces and simple software that
they can interact with and achieve a result. Like any Marketplace, they will
pay for the privelege. The figures involved are astronomical and even as you
read this, software houses are racing to meet the demand (I have friends in
some of them). The next generation of software will be even smarter and
simpler. Eventually, there will be no need for a "programmer" to act as
intermediary between a user and the technology.

My perception is not chemically altered, Dennis, and there is no "good shit"
required (sadly, some might say <G>).

Just take a ride outside the Fortress and verify what I say for yourself...


> War Stories of managers who went their own way, and abandoned IT make the
> rounds regularly.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

So are you saying that we are required to live in a world where nothing can
change? It will always be costly and time-consuming to implement  computer
systems and those who try are destined to fail? Even if you are right (and I
refute that you are, vehemently <G>) it isn't much of a picture for an IT
professional is it?

"Some people tried and failed, therefore it can't be done."

That's what they said about lace-making machines.

Yet, I would venture to suggest that, in 2003, any lace you have in your
place was not made by hand. (Maybe, as a piece of artwork, but not
functioning.)

> Executives make decisions, they don't implement them.
> That's left to others.
>
And if they can't manage it, they get replaced...

Pete.
```

into

| and
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

water.

as

panacea
| either.
|
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
| considered...

I'm with you on this.
I used to ask before doing something, now I don't.
The problem is that I'm often hampered by the capabilities of others.
"It will take too long", "We don't have time to do that".
And, according to others, I have done the impossible.
In one instance I took over a module that had been worked on for about 3
weeks, the programmer ALMOST had it working.
I was told not to rewrite it, so I rewrote it in a day and a half.
The project leader, who told me not to rewrite it, enthusiastically embraced
the rewrite.
(Fortunately, she had taken the original module home to review, her first
comment was "Can you follow this, I can't.)
In conjunction with the project, I wrote a regression test report, that
basically took an existing report did a cut and paste, and showed the
differences between the base, current production, and test data values. The
VP in charge of the department took one look and asked me to place the
program in production status. The concept was simple, but no one had thought
to do it before. When I showed the report at a project leaders meeting, one
person exclaimed "Do you know how much time this would have saved on my last
project".


<<snip>>
|
| > Bad design will still complicate rather than simplify maintenance &
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

stage

longer

(actually,

case

ones,
| rather than amending existing ones). Obviously, Object Oriented
programming
| lends itself to this approach much better than the conventional procedural
| approach. (That is one reason why I am a "fan" of OO...).

In some of the environments I've worked in "throwaway" won't be embraced any
time soon.
As I stated above, I don't ask, I just do it.
|
<<snip>>
| >
| > |
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| > | development Methodology, the fixed idea that something which has
worked
| > for

review

| > | evaluate it, in the light of more modern practice and tools).
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
| Sometimes new approaches and tools can be slipped in to an existing
budget.
| The best way to do this is to get the support of the Users and ideally,
get
| them to share the cost. I do this by finding out what is MOST important to
| them and promising it will be delivered.  ("If you could have ONE single
| thing from IT, what would it be?" )  I then stake my career on delivering
| it...<G>

I find that when I walk into a project that is behind schedule, or has an
absolute delivery date, I get a free hand.
An add-on to a system may provide the opportunity to try a new approach.

I work on the mainframe, and I can't implement OO without getting my ass in
a major sling.
But, I get some of the benefits of OO by using nested programs.
(I see you are familiar with AFP, so)
In one instance I developed a system, and methodology to be used with
composed AFP files.
The requirements involved resequencing print streams, and extracting data
from each document to feed application systems.
I built a set of nested program components that addressed the structure of
AFP files.
The methodology of nested programs was new to the other programmers in the
group, but they quickly caught on.
For the most part they are unaware of OO concepts, and to be honest I
haven't delved too deeply into OO myself, but nested programs can be a
feeble step in the OO direction.

We had gotten 2 outside bids on the AFP project, and although this was my
first encounter with AFP, I beat both bids handily.
|
| (Sometimes it has been too close for comfort...On one occasion, when  a

us

becoming
| a worry of the past.

I agree that machines are getting cheaper, bigger, faster, but the
bottleneck is still I/O.
I can still usually improve performance by improving I/O efficiency.
When we reach the stage where DASD is used to log the update activity to
solid state storage, we will have fewer performance problems, but I'm sure
someone will find a way to defeat solid state storage performance.
|
| Faced with a "25 hour end-of-day update" the company would simply spend
| $60,000 and put in two more top-of-the-range servers. Input files would be
| split, the system would employ load-levelling, and the problem goes away.

War Story: A division of a major corp. asked for a larger faster machine to
handle the workload.
The response was: "If you need a larger machine, forget about a bonus or
raise."
Poor system design was the cause of the problem.
Actually, they didn't have a system, just a collection of stand alone
programs.
Their major system had a 100,000,000 plus monthly transaction file that was
passed once for each report.

|
| Performance is no longer an acceptable excuse for antiquated system
design.
|
|
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
| Nope. Only in the Fortress. Look around. See what small companies are
doing
| with accounting packages. Users are also getting much "smarter" in their
| ability to use software.

Some of the places I've worked at process millions of customer statements a
month.
Mailing operations, and print operations have a major stake in how things
are done.
No matter how creative a user may be, they still have to touch base with
accounting, the print room, and the mail room.

|
| > A single user will usually address their own problem, and ignore the
needs
| > of other departments.
|
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| desire for the same figures.

I have no problem providing users with READ ONLY data that they can play
with, and share with others.

I don't doubt that there are power users out there.
I've worked with SOME senior management that fall into that category, it's a
pleasure working with them.
I also know that they delegate responsibilty, not out of ignorance, but
because there are only so many hours in a day.
Maybe centralized Systems and Programming will fall by the wayside, but
there will probably be someone who is delegated the resposibility of
touching all the bases, coordinating updates, and testing any modification
to a Business Operations system.


|
| > War Stories of managers who went their own way, and abandoned IT make
the
| > rounds regularly.
| > They tried to cut costs, and increased them.
| > They tried to cut the implementation time for new features/functions,
and
| > failed.
| > If they kept their job, they regretted the decision.

| change? It will always be costly and time-consuming to implement  computer

I
| refute that you are, vehemently <G>) it isn't much of a picture for an IT
| professional is it?
|
| "Some people tried and failed, therefore it can't be done."

I prefer "Some people tried and failed, therefore the next to try it will be
more cautious."

There is nothing wrong with making a mistake.
The problem that I've seen, is that some users don't have the big picture.
The big picture people are sometimes in the IT department, not that they're
smarter, they already made the mistake, and learned. It would be a shame to
discard their experience, and repeat past mistakes.

Maybe IT will shrink, and take on a consulting/QA role.
I may be wrong, probably am, but I don't think programmers will become
extinct, they may be part of a smaller, smarter group that protects Business
Operations from the best intentions of an ignorant user.
|
| That's what they said about lace-making machines.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
| And if they can't manage it, they get replaced...

Or they get an assistant who can.
Being a good loser on the golf course is sometimes an asset.
|
| Pete.
|
|
|

Harley <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

[snip]

>I used to ask before doing something, now I don't.

It was, I believe, Rear Admiral Grace W. Hopper - may she sleep with the 
angels! - who used to say something along the lines of 'it is easier to 
get forgiveness than permission'.

>The problem is that I'm often hampered by the capabilities of others.
>"It will take too long", "We don't have time to do that".

It is then time to inquire 'For what reason do you believe that there is 
not enough time to do it Right but there is enough time to do it Over?'

>And, according to others, I have done the impossible.
>In one instance I took over a module that had been worked on for about 3
>weeks, the programmer ALMOST had it working.
>I was told not to rewrite it, so I rewrote it in a day and a half.

It seems you have encountered a variation of 'Our approach has failed so 
far so you are obliged to continue it'... or 'But we *always* beat the 
dead horses that way here!'

My response is usually 'Hmmmm... I don't understand; how can you say that 
my approach will be any *less* successful than what has been tried so 
far?'

[snip]

>In conjunction with the project, I wrote a regression test report, that
>basically took an existing report did a cut and paste, and showed the
>differences between the base, current production, and test data values. The
>VP in charge of the department took one look and asked me to place the
>program in production status.

Ahhhh... a variation of the classic 'Do it once, do it forever' (or 
'Nothing is temporary, including this statement.')

>The concept was simple, but no one had thought
>to do it before. When I showed the report at a project leaders meeting, one
>person exclaimed "Do you know how much time this would have saved on my last
>project".

My response is 'The concerns of the business are good enough for me... and 
the business is not concerned with saving time for others, it is concerned 
with making money for it'sself.'

[snip]

>In some of the environments I've worked in "throwaway" won't be embraced any
>time soon.

See above about 'do it once, do it always'.

[snip]

>I find that when I walk into a project that is behind schedule, or has an
>absolute delivery date, I get a free hand.

... and that while Victory may have Many Fathers... guess whose DNA will 
be matched to Defeat?

[snip]

>War Story: A division of a major corp. asked for a larger faster machine to
>handle the workload.
>The response was: "If you need a larger machine, forget about a bonus or
>raise."
>Poor system design was the cause of the problem.

Doesn't matter what is 'the cause'... a fool blames his tools, true, but 
one does not bail out a flooded cellar with a teacup.

'Hey, why are the reports late?'

'Machine can't handle the load and here are the stats; I asked for an 
upgrade and got shot down.  *You* want to put your bonus/raise on the 
block?  Be my guest; if the Company does not see fit to spend its own 
money on equipment it needs to get data to people in a timely and 
efficient manner then I see no reason why I should see fit to do so, 
either.'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-27T15:18:17+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efbb7f8_9@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <okogfvoi3r7hloev4iei7bnci2sbjei39o@4ax.com> <bd9qh0$p78$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ef913ca_2@news.athenanews.com> <JqnKa.21273$3o3.1650258@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3efa44df_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <LMKKa.23247$3o3.1771695@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
I believe we have more points of agreement than disagreement...

Heavy snippage and quick comments follow...

"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
news:LMKKa.23247$3o3.1771695@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net...
>
<snip>
> I'm with you on this.
> I used to ask before doing something, now I don't.
>
It is very good that you have managed to establish enough credibility to be
able to do this. (Sadly, it implicitly says that your Management may be
somewhat lacking in perspective, leadership, and vision. Hope I'm wrong
about that...

> The problem is that I'm often hampered by the capabilities of others.
> "It will take too long", "We don't have time to do that".
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> The project leader, who told me not to rewrite it, enthusiastically
embraced
> the rewrite.
> (Fortunately, she had taken the original module home to review, her first
> comment was "Can you follow this, I can't.)

That is commendable, and I have experienced similar situations (from both
the Management and Shop Floor). The question I ask myself is: "What happened
to the Programmer who took three weeks and only succeeded in producing
incomprehensible code that almost worked?".

Again it comes down to Management. This person needed help. They should have
been assisted, retrained, or fired (as a last resort when the other two
approaches had failed...)

>
> <<snip>>
>
> In some of the environments I've worked in "throwaway" won't be embraced
any
> time soon.

Yes, I know. However, most of these are Fortress sites.

I don't want to be too hard on these sites. A lot of it comes from the
bitter experience of seeing new approaches come and go, and finally having
to go back to doing it in standard COBOL because it does the job and its
"what we know". But, if we are honest, the mainframe world in general has
been very insular up until quite recently. (I think it is changing a bit
now, but it is some years since I worked on a mainframe site and I only know
what I glean from conversations with friends who do work on them or manage
them.)

To many mainframe people things like XML, HTML, Java, JavaScript, (in fact,
almost anything Web Based) are regarded with suspicion and seen as a threat.
OO comes in the same category. It is these attitudes that perpetuate the
Fortress mentality.On the occasions where I have been able to spend some
one-on-one time with a good COBOL programmer I have found they have no real
problem with understanding OO (once you cut through the jargon and some of
the verbosity that surrounds OO COBOL) and they are delighted with the the
advantages it brings. I've seen the same with RAD as a development
Methodology. People love it, and the Users simply insist on having it for
the next project they finance. But to move the Fortress mentality takes more
than a few successful pilot projects and some "converted" individuals. It
requires a change in the Management approach and thinking also. Shops which
have a rigid "Control" approach will never get it. Places where words like
"mandatory" and "Standard practice"  are used every day will never attempt
it. (they will if they are bullied into it, and the promise of vast rewards
are made, but it is difficult to deliver exaggerated promises and,
personally, I won't write cheques with my mouth that I am not sure my brain
and my ability can cover <G>).

So, for people like me who want to embrace the future and are never quite
satisfied with the Status Quo (...I like the band,
and, by an odd coincidence, they are playing on a CD in my lounge as I write
this...<G>), the best we can hope for is to see the
old parrots replaced by the new ducklings... This process takes time but it
is inevitable.
It is kind of sad that with OO we have something of real value, and yet it
is tarred with the same brush as the previous failed approaches (by people
who have experienced these failed approaches...; as I mentioned before, the
newbies embrace it).


> As I stated above, I don't ask, I just do it.

That's fine, as long as you succeed... God forbid you get it wrong; they'll
hang you out to dry and the support you could and should expect from your
colleagues and managers will evaporate like high octane fuel on a hot
manifold...


> |
> <<snip>>
>
> The Bean Counters will love you, if you come in ahead of schedule, and
under
> budget.
>

Yes, I have a good relationship with most of them...<G> Even coming in on
schedule and under budget cuts a lot of ice and improves credibility...<G>

> |
> <<snip>>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> someone will find a way to defeat solid state storage performance.

I think it's (forgive the pun, but I can't resist...) in the pipeline....

>
> War Story: A division of a major corp. asked for a larger faster machine
to
> handle the workload.
> The response was: "If you need a larger machine, forget about a bonus or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Their major system had a 100,000,000 plus monthly transaction file that
was
> passed once for each report.
>

And they couldn't find enough cash to upgrade the hardware AND give bonuses
where due..?

See my observations above regarding Management on this site...

Without digressing into a tirade, let me just observe here that any
Management that believes it is protecting its shareholders by flogging its
staff and using worn out tools, is sadly mistaken. It makes NO economic
sense to work this way unless you are in it for the short haul. Successful
companies value their staff, give them the tools they need, and thereby
raise productivity, morale, and profit.

I don't know what your personal aspirations are Dennis, but it sounds to me
like you should be looking for a different "culture".

>
> Some of the places I've worked at process millions of customer statements
a
> month.

Yes, they do. And more and more of them (certainly in the UK) are
outsourcing this task to companies who specialise in doing it, can make the
investment in very large print run facilities, can guarantee security and
privacy, and overall do it for around one third of what it costs to do it in
house.

> Mailing operations, and print operations have a major stake in how things
> are done.
> No matter how creative a user may be, they still have to touch base with
> accounting, the print room, and the mail room.
>
You are right and I don't disagree. I would qualifty it by saying that it is
dependent to a large extent on the nature of the business. There is a cut
off point where it is NOT feasible to bill from the PC Network (unless you
are billing totally in real time, in which case the network expansion as the
business grows brings its own set of problems...). There is also a cut off
point where it makes no sense to maintain a high capacity printing system
in-house. Horses for courses.

>
> I have no problem providing users with READ ONLY data that they can play
> with, and share with others.
>
 There is no problem with them updating it, provided it is centrally shared
by all (including IT <G>). The answer is to work WITH smart users and,
rather than "control" them, be a party to what they are doing, (and why they
are doing it) and advise them on how best to do it. Sometimes we lose sight
of the fact that the Business are NOT the "enemy"... we are all on the same
team. If the business goes down there will be no IT. It is in everybody's
interest to work together.

> Maybe centralized Systems and Programming will fall by the wayside, but
> there will probably be someone who is delegated the resposibility of
> touching all the bases, coordinating updates, and testing any modification
> to a Business Operations system.

Yes. There will always be managers and bean counters...<G> (In Ben
Franklin's day the inevitablities were Death and Taxes... Most very wealthy
people avoid tax and are working on  avoiding death also, but these spectres
have been replaced by others...the eternal bean counter for one...<G>)

Overall it will be no bad thing if computer technology evolves to the point
where ANYONE can use it. We are not there yet, but in the 37 years I've
lived and breathed IT, I have seen it moving inexorably that way.

(And I've done everything I could to help it...developed programs that wrote
other programs so users could do their own thing without coming to IT,
developed code generators to handle specific tasks like on-line enquiry,
heuristic programs to do clever (but very boring for a Human) stuff that
freed people to do less tedious and onerous tasks...It has been fun <G>) You
can see it as "dumbing down", "de-skilling", "simplifying and
rationalizing", or however your personal perspective regards it, but the
fact is that computer technology is proliferating at a rate no-one would've
believed even 10 years ago. I believe it is a fair statement that
"centralized Systems and Programming will fall by the wayside". It is a
question of "When?" rather than "If?".

I don't believe "programming" will die out altogether for at least 50 years
(unless there is a quantum leap that nobody has foreseen...) but it will not
be a general skill employed in every corporation, as COBOL once was.
Procedural programming in the generally accepted COBOL sense, has a much
shorter expected life span. And the value placed on these skills will be
less and less, and the scope for them will only be in specialised companies
who market software and components. I see COBOL as not being in general use
(except for Batch Processing and some component building) by 2015.

(I know...I don't like it either but I have to call 'em as I see 'em... My
advice is "Accept that this prediction COULD be true and prepare
accordingly" in other words, deal with it. If you are a young person
starting your career in commercial computer programming, hit the books and
courses. The only defense is an expanded skill set. Learn OO. (I recommend
learning Java). The future belongs to Objects.)

>
> There is nothing wrong with making a mistake.
> The problem that I've seen, is that some users don't have the big picture.
> The big picture people are sometimes in the IT department, not that
they're
> smarter, they already made the mistake, and learned. It would be a shame
to
> discard their experience, and repeat past mistakes.
>
Repeating past mistakes is one definition of stupidity.

It is almost equally stupid not to attempt something, because of failures in
the past.

> Maybe IT will shrink, and take on a consulting/QA role.
> I may be wrong, probably am, but I don't think programmers will become
> extinct, they may be part of a smaller, smarter group that protects
Business
> Operations from the best intentions of an ignorant user.

That will certainly be the case for a period of a few years. After that, the
systems will protect themselves. What is currently a peopleware solution
will move to being a software one.

Pete.
```

```

On 26-Jun-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> To many mainframe people things like XML, HTML, Java, JavaScript, (in fact,
> almost anything Web Based) are regarded with suspicion and seen as a threat.

I haven't seen this.

> OO comes in the same category.

I have seen this.  Good OO requires a different way of thinking, and it is hard
to teach an old dog new tricks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Peter Lacey <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-27T21:58:31-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EFD0456.C0FF3DE5@mb.sympatico.ca>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <okogfvoi3r7hloev4iei7bnci2sbjei39o@4ax.com> <bd9qh0$p78$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ef913ca_2@news.athenanews.com> <JqnKa.21273$3o3.1650258@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3efa44df_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <LMKKa.23247$3o3.1771695@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3efbb7f8_9@news.athenanews.com> <bdhisa$21l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:

> On 26-Jun-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> to teach an old dog new tricks.

I'm not sure you guys are looking at this the right way.  I'm sure you don't
include yourselves among the "old dogs" and are quite willing to learn new things.
And I'd wager that most people would classify themselves the same way.

So what's the problem? IMHO it's the old saying that people don't mind change, they
mind being changed.  "Many mainframe people regard things like XML, HTML, Java,
JavaScript, (in fact,
almost anything Web Based) with suspicion and see them as a threat" because they
feel themselves being attacked and vilified because they don't presently know
them.  Some young Turk or a management type comes along and slags everything that's
been done up until that time; of course s/he'll be received negatively.  As well,
"Old hands" have seen the enthusiasms du jour come and go and usually go without a
trace.  (What have you heard about push technology lately?)  Is it any surprise
that they'll be wary?

It's a very curious profession, this: it's the only one I know of in which the more
experience you've had, the less you'll be believed.  (I call it "the neighbour's
kid" syndrome).

Cheers
PL
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-27T14:07:42+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdhj3e$222$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

Nope.

Sadly, there are too many non-technical, and technically weak people in
leadership roles.
They can't argue with results.

I work as a consultant, so I try to judge the response I'll get from other

|

| To many mainframe people things like XML, HTML, Java, JavaScript, (in
fact,
| almost anything Web Based) are regarded with suspicion and seen as a
|threat.

The Web based portions of systems are going OO, and there hasn't been much
resistance.
CICS 3270 terminal programming is a pain in the butt.
Most of the coding addresses the environment, not the business
functionality.

As far as XML is concerned, some of the resistance may be justified.
Would you want your personal information stored in XML?
Hackers still break in from time to time, why give them the keys to the
kingdom.
I think security becomes more critical with XML.
I have no problem with storing data in XML temporarily.
A lag in acceptance may be acceptable, since from what I've read, XML is in
a state of flux. I don't know the pace of change, but if it's changing
rapidly, I would wait a bit before implementing it in a critical
application.
As a matter of fact, I proposed a web based solution for a project I was
working on because it was simple, and not a critical system. Failure or
delay wouldn't impact the business financially. It would have been a good
learning experience for the people in the group I was in.

| OO comes in the same category. It is these attitudes that perpetuate the
| Fortress mentality.On the occasions where I have been able to spend some
| one-on-one time with a good COBOL programmer I have found they have no
real
| problem with understanding OO (once you cut through the jargon and some of
| the verbosity that surrounds OO COBOL) and they are delighted with the
| advantages it brings. I've seen the same with RAD as a development
| Methodology. People love it, and the Users simply insist on having it for

more

which
| have a rigid "Control" approach will never get it. Places where words like

| it. (they will if they are bullied into it, and the promise of vast
rewards
| are made, but it is difficult to deliver exaggerated promises and,
| personally, I won't write cheques with my mouth that I am not sure my
brain
| and my ability can cover <G>).

Management is changing, it isn't upper management that's the problem.
They are under pressure to keep pace with the competion.
Customer Service, and the Web compelled them to get their toes wet.

The real limiting factor is the availability of COMPETENT talent.
I've know some people who couldn't program in COBOL, and now they can't
program in Visual Basic.
Money draws people to new technology, but it's the people, and their talent
that determines how the technology tool is used.

|
| So, for people like me who want to embrace the future and are never quite

For the most part, it's because they think that something that takes me a
day or 2 will take a week or 2.
It's mostly being judged by the capability of others.

And I also propose the heresy that we should write COBOL programs that
address the testing process.
After all, they already have an inaccurate, time consuming manual process in
place.
If IT were a user department, and we looked at what they were doing, we
would be sitting in front of their door with System proposals in hand.

I left that culture a while ago.
That's why it's a War Story.
In my travels, I've been at 2 places where the entire staff was below par.
Usually there are a few people who have very limited capabilities, but when
the whole staff is like that, you question your perception.

Corporate was right.
They spent 3 man-months to improve the performance of a 3 Minute (Wall time)
Job that ran one a month. They did save a tenth of a CPU second.
The shop had extremely high transaction volumes, and they had no System
perspective. Everything was a stand alone program.
MERGE was foreign to them, they always used SORT.

You want a print stream with groups of 3 page, 2 page, 1 page documents
because the inserting machine runs like Maserati with that combination, no
problem. 3-2-1-3-2-1-3-2-1.

You may be right. 3270 CICS processing is taking a dive, but there are some
CICS TCP/IP applications that ain't gonna die soon. Then you have DB2 Stored
procedures, and finally plain and simple batch.
Any business with proprietary software that gives them an edge will want to
keep it, as long as it still provides them with an advantage.
There may be more of a market than you think, maybe 2018.

So far, I've worked in Assembler, COBOL, and PL/1.
At various points, I've been advised to learn: C, C++, Visual Basic, Pascal,
PowerBuilder, Delphi, and Java.
I'll take a poorly written COBOL program over a poorly written program in
any of the other languages listed.
(I can READ most of the languages listed.)

On 27-Jun-2003, "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

> | > I work on the mainframe, and I can't implement OO without getting my ass
> in
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Which disadvantages aren't you getting?

8^)

Some of the more arguable disadvantages.   For instance, when we really get into
reusing components, we have to lower your standards of how complete our testing
can be.   We don't fully test every option in every program using that
component.    This is both a good thing and a bad thing, as we find ways to make
this more limited testing be good enough.     Most mainframe shops don't have
such standards, and often let a widely called program exist with a known bug
because testing a change to standards would be so expensive.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-27T18:27:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efc8128.79458691@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <okogfvoi3r7hloev4iei7bnci2sbjei39o@4ax.com> <bd9qh0$p78$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ef913ca_2@news.athenanews.com> <JqnKa.21273$3o3.1650258@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3efa44df_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <LMKKa.23247$3o3.1771695@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>I work on the mainframe, and I can't implement OO without getting my ass in
>a major sling.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>feeble step in the OO direction.

I agree with this. Over the years, the size of my COBOL programs (compile units)
keeps getting smaller. Where others would write a single program, I'll write a
dozen or more callable programs. Whether they're nested or separately compiled
matters little. 

I just finished a project extracting data from reports. The difference from your
project is my reports were in thousands of unknown formats. On the front end, I
wrote a program for each level of deconstruction and reconstruction. The
document guy calls the line guy who calls the lexical guy ... Data bubbles
upward through the pipeline. 

document level -- the driver for everything below
  line level -- statistics
    lexical level -- all knowledge of vocabulary in one place
      word level -- a complex parser that handled ambiguities such as hyphens  
                         and words run together
        input level -- "read a record" with various line terminators and tab
                           expansions

During preanalysis, a number of programs handled configuration morphologies, one
program for each:

two up, like an old fashioned balance sheet
"two deep" i.e. right side of page below left side
all lines folded
description columns folded and hanging below primary line
rotated 90 degrees i.e. columns reading down rather than across
elided titles, sometimes multi-level, for example:
      HARDWARE
         Widget
             Metric
                left-handed thread     12
                right-handed thread   15
              SAE
         Be careful not to mistake category titles (e.g. HARDWARE) as 
         elided titles. Lexical analysis provided clues, as did statistics 
         (upper/lower case). 
         Sometimes the whole description is elided, for example:
         Widget             12   bin number 10
                                15   bin number 12
                                27

free format -- columns delimited by a single space
verbiage, legal and administrative stuff above and below report, 
          sometimes with numbers that coincide with report columns
          or supplementary tables which look like data
footnotes, written in a dozen notations, containing relevant information

Now that data for one report line is on a "line" (a linked list of words), the
analysis begins. 

Pattern analysis -- find the most frequently occurring formats, some reports use

      multiple formats e.g. one each for hardware, software and supplies
Header analysis -- column headings _may_ say what the column contains, 
       or may be misleading due to different tabbing standard (text vs. 
       spreadsheet)
Report typing -- figure out what kind of report this is, sometimes a header 
       states that, other times you 'just know' based on data being reported
Binding -- for each detail line, determine which format it resembles, then 
       bind each word to a column. If two words appear in a column of numbers, 
       there is a problem. 
Scaling -- figure out whether columns of numbers are in units or thousands, 
       sometimes the column header says, sometimes it's in a footnote, sometimes

       it's in text at the top ('all numbers in thousands except quantity'), 
       sometimes it just doesn't say, sometimes the header says but is wrong
Normalization -- if a column of numbers has some or all decimals or scientific 
       notation, figure out whether it contains percentages or numbers. 
       If numbers, transform them into whole numbers (without causing rounding
       errors).
Line classification -- get rid of subtotal lines which look like detail, 
       distinguished only by their description or lack thereof

Now that we have nice clean columns of numbers, Control procedures analyze the
result. Do columns of numbers add up to totals, which may be in administrative
heading rather than at the foot of the column, may be scaled differently, may be
slightly off due to premature rounding? Do lines crossfoot? Do numbers look
reasonable .. for that currency, report type, previous report (if any), external
reference (prices)?  If not, some lines may have been mangled. Change
assumptions, UNDO alterations and REITERATE the whole analysis. Keep trying
until you run out of things to try. 

Finally there are output programs, including several loggers and a program that
reports internals for debugging purposes. 

The application runs under a larger interactive system, thus it should finish
within 100 milliseconds. For extremely large reports with thousands of lines
(uncommon), one second is tolerable. 

The application ended up with 35 programs. Most were in the original design but
a few were patched on as experience grew. I *can* conceive writing such an
application as one monolithic program, but I know what would have happened. At
some point in its evolution it would have become too complex to comprehend.
Changes made to fix one problem would have broken something else. Reiterative
systems are especially vulnerable to that happening. Without high volume
regression testing, its success rate would have formed a plateau -- one step
backward for every step forward. With regression testing, it would have taken
increasingly longer to fix the mishaps than the original problems. That's what
happens to poorly structured designs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java) - (RW Warning

For those not familiar with comp.lang.cobol,
     Check google for the accuracy track-record of this poster (R. Wagner) -
and the <lack of> regard with which most of his comments are viewed by many
in this newsgroup before reading his post below.

If you DO read this note, you may want to think about the functionality
available with Report Writer (in Standard COBOL) and the use of "data-name"
rather than "literal" in many of its phrases for creating dynamic reports.
See the 2002 COBOL Standard  for even MORE functionality than that available
in the '85 Standard (where RW "used to be" an optional module).
```

Well, the waterfall does work.  And as for extreme programming and other hot,
fast ways, I have two comments:
- not all programmers are created equal
- you can't ever get away from screwed-up thinking

So if somebody makes a bad choice early in the development you'll be hobbled by
it forever, or until you can re-do it.  So, also, you're best off using the most
experienced programmers for the new methods because they are less likely to make
bad choices.

…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

This is akin to saying that there's nothing left to program.  Only true to an
extent.  And it will always be true that keeping in-house is the best way to
control the result.


Another Pete.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T14:36:57+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efa593c_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <okogfvoi3r7hloev4iei7bnci2sbjei39o@4ax.com> <bd9qh0$p78$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ef913ca_2@news.athenanews.com> <3EFA4AD6.F120319F@mb.sympatico.ca>`

```
Thanks for the response, Peter.

Comments below...

"Peter Lacey" <lacey@mb.sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:3EFA4AD6.F120319F@mb.sympatico.ca...
> "Peter E.C. Dashwood" wrote:
>
> >
> >
> > I do refer to "Fortress COBOL" which encompasses the "COBOL think" on
many
> > mainframe sites. This includes slavish devotion to the Waterfall as a
> > development Methodology, the fixed idea that something which has worked
for
> > decades MUST be right and there is no need to change it (or even review
or
> > evaluate it, in the light of more modern practice and tools).
> >
>
> Well, the waterfall does work.

I used it for 20 years. It NEVER worked... I don't recall a single project
in that time that came in on time and on budget, and was actually
satisfactory to the Users. We consistently delivered something like what
they wanted 6 months ago, (when the requirements analysis was completed and
received the mandatory sign-off).

You might say, "Well, Pete, if it didn't work, why did you keep using it?".

BECAUSE WE HAD NO OTHER OPTION!!!

That was the state of the art as far as IT development Methodology went. It
was received wisdom and "everybody knew" that that was the proper way for IT
to develop computer systems.

The ONLY time I can see a justification for the Waterfall is in a batch
environment, where the requirement is not dynamic and it makes sense to
spend a lot of time up front designing, rather than simply evolving a
dynamic system as requirements change.

> And as for extreme programming and other hot,
> fast ways, I have two comments:
> - not all programmers are created equal
> - you can't ever get away from screwed-up thinking
>

Totally agree with you on "Extreme Programming". I see it as "Extreme
Stupidity" which trades a short term gain for long term grief.

"not all programmers are created equal" - Fair enough. But all of us can
grow...
"you can't ever get away from screwed-up thinking" - Maybe. You can
certainly create an environment where it can be "contained". You can also
introduce checks and balances that will isolate it before it gets a chance
to be implemented. In a RAD project you can even go ahead and implement it,
then roll the whole lot back to the last "sane" position when you realise
the error of your ways. This can be achieved without severe impact on the
project, because it is designed to permit "What if" scenarios.

> So if somebody makes a bad choice early in the development you'll be
hobbled by
> it forever, or until you can re-do it.

Only in the Waterfall...(see above)

> So, also, you're best off using the most
> experienced programmers for the new methods because they are less likely
to make
> bad choices.
>

The selection of teams is a topic way beyond the scope of this discussion.
Suffice to say, I like teams to have a cross section of experience and to be
composed of Users and IT people working together. ALL members of the team,
from the most junior new recruit to the Senior Manager sponsoring the
exercise, have the same "value" to the project, and will be listened to and
treated with the same respect. (I strive to make it "safe" for people to say
what is on their minds and not be afraid of looking stupid. Sometimes the
most amazing ideas come from the most unlikely sources...). Using  only
experienced programmers for the new methods has the following drawbacks:

1. New knowledge will not be spread around the team. This has a bad effect
on morale.
2. Experienced programmers will approach problems the way they always have
and may miss much of what is valuable in the new approach.
3. Experienced programmers compare what they are learning to what they
already know (and invariably, love...), before they have really had a chance
to absorb the new methods. This leads to bias against the new approach
before it is even off the ground. Doubts and negativity at the early stages
rip like wildfire through a team and the result is demotivation and high
risk of failure.
4. If they have difficulty with the new approach they will blame the
approach. (It can't be them because they could have done it, using their
experience, in half the time...)

Fortunately, if you can hold it together long enough for them to see it
work, some of these people become the most ardent converts.

> >
> > (Watch for a significant growth in IT outsourcing over the next 5 years.
It
> > is fuelled by the general public becoming more computer literate and
being
> > quite capable of managing their own spreadsheets and databases. This
leaves
> > the IT responsibility as being to provide consolidated corporate figures
> > from all the departments, via the network, and maintaining and managing
the
> > Network infrastructure. Extend this idea, and IT gradually comes down to
> > "Network Maintenance" with little or no systems development (that will
be
> > catered for by User departments with their own Office tools
(spreadsheets,
> > databases, etc.) and  component based packages for existing accounting
and
> > Business functions).
>
> This is akin to saying that there's nothing left to program.

No it isn't. It is NOT that there is nothing left to program, it is just
that the WAY of doing it will be different. And the tools are getting more
powerful and the Users are getting more "computer literate". There are still
many and diverse requirements that need to be implemented.


> Only true to an
> extent.
Not arguing this as it misses the point entirely...

>And it will always be true that keeping in-house is the best way to
> control the result.
>
Be careful when you use "always"...<G>. Many corporations throughout the
World are voting against your proposition with their feet. And the people
they are outsourcing to are prepared to spend millions to reassure them
regarding, not only their control, but also their security and
confidentiality.
>
> Another Pete.
>
:-)   You can't get too much of a good thing <G>...

Pete.
```

> >
> > (Watch for a significant growth in IT outsourcing over the next 5 years. It

> 

Doesn't this invite the spectre of "end-user computing"? I can't begin
to describe the horrors of debugging end-user creations (spreadsheets,
querys, ...) only to prove to them that the source data is ok.
Unfortunately, while some end-users are capable of producing spiffy
charts and screens the vast majority are not familiar enough with the
data architecture to prevent themselves from making serious mistakes
in their apps.
```

Operations systems.

The problem isn't in the coding.  It is in the testing.  Does the person
in one part of the organization understand the implications on the rest
of the organization?  Does anyone know what the current system is
doing?  Does everyone agree as to what the business rules are?  I have
heard it said that at least 30 percent of all spread sheets are wrong.

correct, I shudder to think about the job actual using departments will
do.
