```

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message
news:3efbba8c_5@news.athenanews.com...
|
| "Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote in message
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
| > The problems will arise when they can modify the functionality of
Business
| > Operations systems.
| >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
|

I thought they did that now.
Every time I change something it's because a User or Client requested it
<G>.
I CAN understand their frustration, but I also know that at times I give
them what they want, not what they though they wanted. Personally, I try to
work in partnership with them, and often provide options they didn't know
existed.

Maybe what you predict will come to pass, but not until far more users are
familiar with ALL the options available to them. In the end, it's the
knowledge, creativity, imagination, and genius of the individual that
determines the form of the end product. (Wanna see my hammer/saw?)

I can see this thing going full circle.
You know, they disband IT, then create a group to control things because
anarchy isn't working.
I'm not opposed to Users having more power, BUT like it or not there will
still be a bureaucracy blocking implementation until all the i's are dotted
and t's crossed. Then there will have to be a group of specialists who dot
the i's and cross the t's, because it's easier that way. Paperwork,
paperwork, paperwork, it ain't going away.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-27T15:27:26+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efbba1a_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <okogfvoi3r7hloev4iei7bnci2sbjei39o@4ax.com> <bd9qh0$p78$1@peabody.colorado.edu> <3ef913ca_2@news.athenanews.com> <3EFA4AD6.F120319F@mb.sympatico.ca> <543a8ba1.0306260856.6517debe@posting.google.com>`

```

"Gord" <gordcrawshaw@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:543a8ba1.0306260856.6517debe@posting.google.com...
>
> Doesn't this invite the spectre of "end-user computing"?

Yes it does. Interesting that you see it as a spectre...I see it as a
desirable end point.


> I can't begin
> to describe the horrors of debugging end-user creations (spreadsheets,
> querys, ...) only to prove to them that the source data is ok.

Don't worry. Within 10 years you won't have to. They'll do it themselves.
After all, it is THEIR data...(maybe you forgot what the whole Business is
about ?)

> Unfortunately, while some end-users are capable of producing spiffy
> charts and screens the vast majority are not familiar enough with the
> data architecture to prevent themselves from making serious mistakes
> in their apps.

Yes, that is certainly true for the moment. But there is a rising generation
who are coming on and they are MUCH more computer literate. The "vast
majority" will become "the small minority" as time goes by.

As IT professionals it is hard to accept they we no longer have a monopoly
on expertise. Sure, we have experience and that is very useful, but the fact
is that Users are simply getting better trained, more familiar with
computing and much better at it than they were even 10 years ago.

On my last assignment I found there were more people with Computing Science
degrees in the Business than there were in the IT department. Some of them
were EXTREMELY capable.

I advocate working WITH them rather than squaring off for a battle...

Pete.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-27T17:36:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdgl6b$6r7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <3EFA4AD6.F120319F@mb.sympatico.ca> <543a8ba1.0306260856.6517debe@posting.google.com> <3efbba1a_8@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> Don't worry. Within 10 years you won't have to. They'll do it themselves.
> After all, it is THEIR data...(maybe you forgot what the whole Business is
> about ?)

Yes, but there are variations on who 'THEIR' are, depending on which other 
'THEIR's you talk to.

For example saleman may use data in various ways to try to maximise their 
income rather than maximising company profit (or other objective), and so 
sales management will try to filter the data.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: let's slag off OO... was:( Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java)

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-28T01:08:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efc423b_9@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <3EFA4AD6.F120319F@mb.sympatico.ca> <543a8ba1.0306260856.6517debe@posting.google.com> <3efbba1a_8@news.athenanews.com> <bdgl6b$6r7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

"Richard Plinston" <riplin@Azonic.co.nz> wrote in message
news:bdgl6b$6r7$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
> Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>
> > Don't worry. Within 10 years you won't have to. They'll do it
themselves.
> > After all, it is THEIR data...(maybe you forgot what the whole Business
is
> > about ?)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
That's an interesting point, Richard.

So salesman may "massage" sales data in order to maximise commissions?

I wonder exactly how they'd do that without actually falsifying it....?

The data in a company is the company's resource. It does NOT belong to the
IT department.

This is analogous to the case where the Bank takes �10 from your account to
cover a (usually snotty) letter they wrote you.

(Many people just roll over and accept this...not me. The last time it
happened to me was 20 years ago and they have never done it since... I
threatened them with a lawsuit involving "Theft as a Servant". Their job is
to administer my account, not help themselves to it...I believe that
nowadays they have fine print in the Agreement you sign when you open an
account that permits them to do this. It is still worth making a fuss
about.)

I understand your point that some corporate data must be shared between
departments and users, and it can be difficult to decide who actually "owns"
it. Nevertheless, this decision must be taken. Where shared data is
involved, anything that impacts it must be agreed between all stakeholders.
Yes, there are politics with various departments concerned about their
visibility, and personality clashes and petty rivalrys and squabbles, but it
has to be dealt with. This is where Senior Management MUST take decisions
regarding what area of the Business is responsible for what data, just as
they decide what areas of the Business are responsible for what Business
Functions.

This is where IT can assume the role of "honest broker" ensuring that the
"right" result is obtained. (That's "right" for the Business, not "right"
for IT...<G>). The role of IT is to ADMINISTER the corporate data resource,
NOT to own it or be responsible for its generation and utilization.

It is not your Bank Manager's job to consider how you earned your cash or
what you intend to do with it. His job is to make sure there is full
accountability for it while it is in his care, and to make sure it is
available to you in notes or instruments of the desired denomination when
you request it.

Data, and its derivative, information, are the lifeblood of the
Organization. (It is much too important to let IT decide who can have
it...<G>)

The responsibility for it needs careful consideration and should involve
empowerment from the highest level.

One thing is for sure...the IT department don't own it, any more than the
butcher owns the farm, or a single ethnic group within a Nation can "own"
the seafloor, or the mountains, or the lakes and rivers.

Bottom line: Because we deal with the collection and distribution of data
every day, does not give us the right to decide who will have it, or to
control the use of it, or to otherwise establish "ownership" of it.

Pete.
```

- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <7cGJa.1644$H9q1.499@news04.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <okogfvoi3r7hloev4iei7bnci2sbjei39o@4ax.com> <bd9qh0$p78$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
"Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

>
>On 24-Jun-2003, Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>as well.

Howard:

I believe that you have hit dead center on the bullseye with your
comment!  Great observation.

>If the new paradigm is not ideally suited for the old problems, then advocates
>of the new paradigm almost universally condemn the old problems.

Yup!

>> You can mow the lawn with a power mower or a reel mower........they
>> are two very different tools.  But they both get the job done.  Some
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>But then I know someone who condemns people for using power mowers.

Me too.  When I lived in town and my backyard was only about 2,000
square feet, I used a non-motorized push reel mower and it worked
great.  No gasoline, no pollutants and a little exercise.  Now I have
4 acres to mow and a friend wants me to use goats and a non-motorized
push reel mower to do the trimming to reduce the pollutants in the
air.

Zoning laws forbid me to have livestock, so I offered to pay him $10 a
week to cut the lawn with his non-motorized push reel mower.  I told
him he would be earning money and saving the evironment........he
turned me down.

>> Wine grapes can be crushed under human feet in a large vat or crushed
>> with a half-million dollar crushing machine.
>
>And I know people who condemn modern food machines.

That's exactly my point......."different strokes for different folks"
and "use the right tool for the task at hand and the one which best
suits your specific needs."




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T08:49:14-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-CC78F2.08491425062003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-84F29D.09272524062003@corp.supernews.com> <3ef915ef_2@news.athenanews.com>`

```
In article <3ef915ef_2@news.athenanews.com>,
 "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:

> "Joe Zitzelberger" <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message
> news:joe_zitzelberger-84F29D.09272524062003@corp.supernews.com...
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete.

I have a very ugly story about having to recompile some code because of  
an OS "upgrade".  The only case where a Java recompile was ever 
"necessary" that I know of.

I was taking a "Java & the Web" classlet as part of my M.S. program in 
1997, about the time the version 4.0 browsers came out.  I had developed 
several applets for use by web clients on my Mac (Netscape and IE 3 and 
4 both), and tested them on my  Windows NT (sun reference impl, IE4 was 
a bit late for NT) box and my ISPs SunOS box.  Same executable class 
files in all cases.

Things looked great, tested well everywhere, I thought I had all the 
bases covered.

At my scheduled appointment with the prof, she pulls up the new MS-IE on 
Win95 and my frames were hosed, my font color and background was horrid 
and unreadable.

The prof was not impressed.

Surprisingly, all that was required to fix the problem was a recompile 
on her box.

Messing with frame insets and colors was bush league in the grand scheme 
of Micro$oft shenanigans.  But it just shows the evil bastards* cannot 
be trusted to behave in a decently to their customers.

(and yes, I am making a MORAL judgement with the statement "evil 
bastards".  What they did to Stac (and many others) was blatent theft.  
What they did to Sun with Java (and many others) was the computer 
equivlent of scattering nails in the parking lot of the competition.  
And they haven't mad a moderate their behavior yet.)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T23:35:31-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-7A2889.23353125062003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-84F29D.09272524062003@corp.supernews.com> <3ef915ef_2@news.athenanews.com> <bdcc2s$ann$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`

```
In article <bdcc2s$ann$1@peabody.colorado.edu>,
 "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net> wrote:

> On 24-Jun-2003, "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote:
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> data.

A system I worked on used something called packed-nosign extenaively.  
Anything that was always guarenteed to have a certain sign had the sign 
shifted off to allow an extra digit.

 It was done in assembler, so it was quite easy to shift half a byte and 
or a sign on for fields that would always be positive.

This contraption saved half a byte per field, but cost oodles at runtime 
in SRP/OI executions.

To make a long story longer, the shop decided to convert to Cobol and 
everything had to be converted using the trick:

   5 a-field     Pic x(4)
   5 a-field-9 redefines a-field Pic s9(6)v9 comp-3.

   Move +0 to a-field-9
   Move old-packed-nosign-field to a-field
   Compute a-field-9 = a-field-9 / 10

Talk about a pain....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comparison between Cobol and Java

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T13:04:49+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jt6jfv0vl0friafa5paf96pf18ebh1fqbe@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com> <aVFIa.149963$3Sm.56640@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com> <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-84F29D.09272524062003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote:

>In article <421efvk62qhonane6g0194aaq7gu8urapd@4ax.com>,
> Bob Wolfe <rtwolfe@flexus.com> wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>recompiled?

Joe:

You are absolutely correct.  I guess I was thinking back to using Java
applets to display certain user interface functions within various
versions of MSIE and Netscape web browsers.

When we wrote our Java Client product (project was scrapped years
ago), we wrote Java applets to display user interface screens within a
web browser from a COBOL application.  We had to tweak the code
considerably to get it to display screens consistently in both major
web browsers.

We were very pleased with the results after a little bit of "code
wrangling" and then tested the applets on the next versions of both
browsers......much to our dismay, the screens displayed very
differently than they did in prior browser versions.  It seems that
attempting to support every version of both major browsers was going
to be a code maintenance nightmare.

We even tested the software using both Sun's JDK as well as Microsoft
J++.  Ironically, J++ worked more consistently in various versions of
Netscape than it did in Microsoft's own MSIE.

We changed direction and developed our Web Client product which uses
HTML and Javascript to display screens and achieved a much higher
degree of consistency across the various versions of both major web
browsers........it wasn't so much a Java applet problem as it was a
browser problem.




Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T10:47:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EF47E05.5050704@Knology.net>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <5654fff9.0306191552.231d3f69@posting.google.com>`

```
Ray Smith wrote:
> And it's possible that companies will (more and more) be forced to convert to
> different technologies because of a lack of available development staff.
> As we all know almost no education centers teach COBOL any more and the wide
> spread attitude amoungst the younger generation is it is outdated and "uncool"
> (so they wouldn't want to work as a COBOL programmer anyway!)

You know, that's the prevailing philosophy, but I'm finding that it's 
just not a belief that's grounded in reality.  I've created a "COBOL 
Tips & Tricks" section for the programmers in our shop, posting some of 
the things I've picked up here that no one else had taught me, and even 
the Java folks in our application have been reading them and saying 
"Man, I didn't know COBOL could do that!"

I think the trainers need to put a different spin on COBOL.  I hate 
using myself as an example (have you seen the Charlie's Angels 2 ads? 
Demi Moore - "I work for myself now"  Cameron Diaz - "Well, your boss 
sucks!"), but in USAF technical training, the only exposure we were 
given to COBOL was during a 3-day block on maintenance, and even then, 
we were told that COBOL was old, archaic, and not use much anymore, as 
the DoD had a mandate that everything will be written in Ada.

Imagine my surprise when I arrived to this assignment and learned that I 
was going to be learning COBOL!  I wasn't excited about it, but the 
first time I wrote a little program and made that big mainframe do what 
I wanted it to, I was hooked.  Through other training, I was taught good 
programming structure; how to comment, comment, comment; and other 
techniques that have made me, I'd venture to say, a pretty decent 
programmer.

Each language I've learned in addition to COBOL has improved my 
understanding not only of programming in general, but of COBOL in 
particular.  For example, I said, "I wish COBOL had a construct like 
Select Case in VB".  Then, I looked in the manual, and lo and behold, 
there's the Evaluate statement!  :)

> (I'm trying to buck the trend as an early 30's programmer in a COBOL shop where
> I am usually 20+ years younger than most other developers :) )

:)  I'm 29 (will be 30 in September).  I'm in the database shop (we also 
have an application side and a GUI side), and when I arrived, I was the 
oldest military in my chain of command, and the lowest-ranking.  5 1/2 
years later, I'm -still- the lowest ranking, but I'm also now the only 
military in the shop.  Everyone else in our shop is retired military, 
now either a DoD civil service employee or contractor.  They think I'm 
nuts to take my personal time to read a COBOL newsgroup ("Man, when I 
get out of here, the last thing I want to think about is COBOL!").  Oh 
well - guess they'll just keep writing the way they do, and I'll be set 
when my enlistment's up.  :)

It's good to know I'm not the only "kid" in here...
```

#### ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java ===>>>Small System ==>Java

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T17:11:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8D%Ja.8917$O31.6185@news02.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com>`

```
In my home office, I adopted the following solution:

    Server: An old PC 486
    Workstations: Win98/95, Win2000/Win NT with Disk drawers

    Cable modem  and a hub ===> 67$/month for 100M/S
    Domain name service===> 25$/year

    OS: Linux 8.0 server without X, with IP forwaring configured ===> free
    Applications: Web server, email server, application server(Tomcat),
Samba for directory service ===>free

    Development platform: free Java IDE on a Windows 2000.
    Test bed: Win98/95, Win2000/Win NT

Total cost:  950$ for first year and 825$ for following year.
            fixed: 125$ (not counting moden, NIC, hub and cable wire)
            variable: 825$/year


What happened?
    The Linux server is kept running at my basement. I don't need to have a
look at it if there is no power outage.
    I developed  everything in Java in Win 2000 using Java IDE and then copy
the binary Java code to my server.

What I can't do?
    So far, Nothing found.

I know my solution is not for BIG enterprise. It is shabby.

Imagine how many home we have on the earth? How many small business we do
have?

I downloaded everything: Linux, manual, guides and HowTo from Internet.

Back to serious business:  for Cobol, I can't find the IBM ADF manual and
SAS manual for IBM mainframe, can anybody help me?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Large Systems Cobol -> Java ===>>>Small System ==>Java

- **From:** Frank Swarbrick<Frank.Swarbrick@efirstbank.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T11:58:35-06:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bda3gf$qqn7p$1@ID-184804.news.dfncis.de>`
- **References:** `<646fff9e.0306181411.3c86b15d@posting.google.com> <8D%Ja.8917$O31.6185@news02.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Our shop (a bank) has an inhouse Internet Banking site that is written, as
far as I know, entirely in Java.  Well, other than the mainframe part <g>. 
But all the pieces that actually exist on the Internet Banking server are
written in Java, as far as I know.

Anyway, we had been using IBM's Host-On-Demand product as the interface
between the IB server and the mainframe.  We've recently developed a TCP/IP
'socket' interface to replace the Host-On-Demand (which basically uses 3270
'screen scraping'.)  Since I developed the mainframe side of the socket
interface I was a bit more involved than I had been.

What absolutely amazed me is that the programming and the bulk of the
testing of the Java stuff was done on a programmer's Windows NT workstation
(with, I believe, Websphere loaded on his workstation).  But when we went to
do stress testing it was done on a Sun Solaris server (which is what is used
in production).  *With no changes!*  The same Java code that runs on the
Windows NT box also runs on the Sun Solaris box.  Now I'm not sure if they
actually had to recompile the source or if they just loaded the Java
bytecode that was compiled under NT, but in either case I found it to be
absolutely amazing that it even worked at all.  (Actually, I just talked to
the guy in charge of the Solaris stuff and he, as far as I could understand
him, indicated that there is no re-compile needed on the Solaris system.)

So anyway, I guess this at least somewhat proves your point...

Interesting stuff.
Frank

--- 
Frank Swarbrick
Senior Developer/Analyst - Mainframe Applications Development
FirstBank Data Corporation

>>> Thomas A. Li<tli@corporola.com> 06/24/03 11:11AM >>>

```
To qualify use "OF". I think that will work. I guess you are actually
suggesting a new way to do it that's more 'readable'.  Dot qualifier
should only be used with objects/classes, so if you aren't using OO
Cobol I'm not sure what the big deal is. COBOL is archaic, but it's
still a damn good language. I find the unstring statement in COBOL
much easier to use than anything I've found in Java (so far). 

MOVE 'TOM' TO CUSTOMER-FIRST-NAME OF MYTEMP-CUSTOMER.

>01  MYTEMP-CUSTOMER.
>   02  CUSTOMER-FIRST-NAME   PIC X(10).
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

  http://www.cobolreport.com/faqs/cobolfaq.htm

If you have comments, corrects, deletions, or additions, please send
private email to:

Toward the top of the FAQ is a pointer to the appendix listing the most
recent significant updates and changes.

This FAQ answers such questions as (and addresses topics such as):

 - Where can I get a COBOL compiler? 
 - Is there a free COBOL compiler? 
 - Where can I contact... ?
 - What is happening with the draft of the next COBOL Standard and what
is in it? 
 - What about OO COBOL? 
 - Books about COBOL
 - COBOL Tools
 - What can/should I post in the COBOL newsgroups?
 - What about USAGE? COMP? Storage for data in xyz format? etc?
 - How do I get started with COBOL? Where can I get education?
Tutorials? Etc

and much, MUCH more

<a id="t-8577f5ab0f"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu Printing Problem — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu Printing Problem

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T09:26:50-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gvCdnUFVZO-0VWyjXTWJjA@giganews.com>`

```
1. In all our programs that print, we have:

SELECT PRINT-FILE ASSIGN TO PRTFILE

2. In the COBOL85.CBR file we have:

PRTFILE=DEFAULT.PRT

3. In the DEFAULT.PRT file we have:

PRTDIALG    N
FORMKIND    C
SETPRTDIALOG    3

The goal of the above is to popup the Windows Printer Dialog box to get the
user's choice of a printer.

The above works swell on a development machine. On a non-development machine
(i.e., the application as distributed to a customer), the requested dialog
box does not appear and all printout is directed to the Windows default
printer. We, of course, want the printer selection box available on the
deployed application.

The COBOL85.CBR and DEFAULT.PRT files are installed in the same folder, on
the customer's machine, as the executables.

If we can't get this to fly, we'll have to drop back to invoking the Windows
Common Dialog box manually. The problem with the common-dialog box technique
is that it takes the user's choice for a single job and sets that choice as
the system's default printer thereby confusing subsequent print jobs from
other applications.

We've queried Fujitsu on this issue, via their problem-reporting website,
but what with different time zones 'twixt here and Japan, and assorted other
confusions, it's possible someone here can provide the answer faster.

Thanks ever so much.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Printing Problem

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T18:01:58+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3021859.1056045718@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<gvCdnUFVZO-0VWyjXTWJjA@giganews.com>`

```

Hi Jerry

Do you need to choose a printer where to redirect the print job?

I have my own solution: create the DEFAULT.PRT directly from the
application, passing on file a line with PRTID item and the printer name
selected from the "PrinterName" property in the CMPRINT controls, like:

....
PRTID      Hp OfficeJet K80   (this value is on "Printername" property
                                          in the CMPRINT control)
...


Please, contact me at: softline2000@tin.it

I will send to you a little dialog i'm using to select where to direct a
print job: Preview, Printer or PDF file..


Regards

Gianni Spano
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Printing Problem

- **From:** cobolnotdead@hotmail.com (Kate Parker)
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T13:09:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<49973e5b.0306191209.587a6710@posting.google.com>`

```
Jerry, in my PowerCobol applications, I invoke the "SetPrinter" method
of the Fujitsu Print Control and then update print file's PRTDVR
option to point to the new value of the Print Control's "PrinterName"
property.

It's clunky, but it works and it does not change the Windows Default
Printer.  I also use unique/temporary *.PRT files for each
application.  If you want the code, please email me privately.

Thanks.
Kate Parker

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<gvCdnUFVZO-0VWyjXTWJjA@giganews.com>...

>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

Hi Jerry

That's exactly how i do in my applications using Powerform sheets

Using the "SetPrinter" method of CMPRINT control, your customers are
able to choose the printer where to send the print job.
Powercobol use a .PRT file with some default items and you can re-create
its default .prt file using your own..It is easy.

You need only to declare in the COBOL85.CBR file :
PRTFILE=MYDEFAULT.PRT or something like.

I have an example of printer selection dialog that you can add in
your application..(preview, printing and generate a PDF file using
PDF995 driver)
For your informations, PDF995 suite costs about $20.00 and it
captures the printing generating a pdf file that you can use for your
own purpose.
Go to www.pdf995.com for more details.

Please, send me the request privately, on
softline2000@tin.it
I will be happy to help you to resolve this easy problem.

Gianni
```

won't use the options in the .prt file just like in your situation. My guess
for now is that the powerform runtime can't find the correct .prt file,
perhaps due to a wrong search path setting or conflicting runtime
environment setting.

When I find something useful I'll post it.

Rik

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu Printing Problem

- **From:** thaneh@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T06:40:53-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bfdfc3e8.0306210540.6662e85b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<gvCdnUFVZO-0VWyjXTWJjA@giganews.com>`

```
For those who hate top posting - I apologize.

I'm not writing to defend Fujitsu - but I do have some comments.

Fujitsu support usually doesn't respond until they have a solution. 
I've not had anything that has gone unresolved.  Now, you can quibble
about the SPEED of resolution - I'll give you that - but why the
"Urgency" on this?

Please take my comments as CONSTRUCTIVE criticism of the situation -
no slight/flame/insult is intended.

Let me make an observation based on the below:

> The above works swell on a development machine. On a non-development machine
> (i.e., the application as distributed to a customer), the requested dialog

You sent this to a customer and it didn't work?  Uh - I'm a software
developer.  Since I suggest my software runs on Windows 95, Windows
98, Windows NT 4 (SP6), Windows 2000 and Windows XP - before it leaves
the door - or is even SCHEDULED for a release, it's tested on ALL of
these operating systems on machines without the development
environment.  Deploying untested software to the end users is
irresponsible.  Had this been tested in house - and EARLY on - the
problem would have been detected in time for a timely resolution -
even given the snails pace of Fujitsu support.

And it would have saved a stint in the Methadone clinic.

Nothing below but the original posting...

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message news:<gvCdnUFVZO-0VWyjXTWJjA@giganews.com>...
> 1. In all our programs that print, we have:
> 
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks ever so much.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu Printing Problem

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T12:55:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nCednbcqleWdAWmjXTWJkQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<gvCdnUFVZO-0VWyjXTWJjA@giganews.com> <bfdfc3e8.0306210540.6662e85b@posting.google.com>`

```

"Thane Hubbell" <thaneh@softwaresimple.com> wrote in message
news:bfdfc3e8.0306210540.6662e85b@posting.google.com...
> For those who hate top posting - I apologize.
>
> I'm not writing to defend Fujitsu - but I do have some comments.

And I'm going to respond to your comments and, in the process, tell you more
about this than you really wanted to know.

>
> Fujitsu support usually doesn't respond until they have a solution.
> I've not had anything that has gone unresolved.  Now, you can quibble
> about the SPEED of resolution - I'll give you that - but why the
> "Urgency" on this?

The "Urgency" was precipated by an impending production deadline.

>
> Please take my comments as CONSTRUCTIVE criticism of the situation -
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > The above works swell on a development machine. On a non-development
machine
> > (i.e., the application as distributed to a customer), the requested
dialog
>
> You sent this to a customer and it didn't work?

No. We did not send it to a customer. Perhaps my phrase "the application as
distributed to..." could have been more precise. We were testing the final
distribution on one of our three test machines (Win98, WinXP, and Win2K - of
our 600 customers, none has an NT machine). The problem appeared during this
final phase of testing. Stunned, we began installing the distribution on
other machines in our office. Analysis revealed the problem existed only on
non-development machines.


> Uh - I'm a software
> developer.

Me too. For over 40 years.

> Since I suggest my software runs on Windows 95, Windows
> 98, Windows NT 4 (SP6), Windows 2000 and Windows XP - before it leaves
> the door - or is even SCHEDULED for a release, it's tested on ALL of
> these operating systems on machines without the development
> environment.

So do we (well, almost all). I notice you don't test on WinME or WinSE. Tsk,
tsk. We have to reserve production time at an outside party way in advance.
While we can cancel a scheduled deployment, it costs. A delay of one day
means we lose our deposit and about two weeks to get back into the rotation.
We avoid a delay if we can; accept a delay if we must.

> Deploying untested software to the end users is
> irresponsible.

In general, yes. There are exceptions, such as a customer wanting a
modification and we let him be the beta tester.

> Had this been tested in house - and EARLY on - the
> problem would have been detected in time for a timely resolution -
> even given the snails pace of Fujitsu support.

We WERE testing the application "EARLY."

Everything had been tested on development machines - rigorously. We have a
70-page pre-flight check-list that had been followed. Twice.

We were testing the final build and giving ourselves six days to detect and
correct any problems the final build might turn up. The problem we hit is
roughly equivalent to a program working swell but failing when OPT is turned
on during the final production compile. It happens. We knew it could happen.
We had a contingency buffer built in.

Immediately upon confirming the nature of the problem, we fired off a
support query to Fujitsu (and to CLC). While waiting, we set about analyzing
the system for circumventions or alternatives. Two of the members of this
group (I could kiss them - well, one of them anyway - the other's an Italian
and would probably kiss me) privately emailed their techniques. An "Ah-ha"
or two later, we coded an alternative methodology. Once we had a
bullet-proof work-around, we decided to cancel the Fujitsu support request
and install the work-around on all the programs in the distribution that
needed it.

Note the statement in the DEFAULT.PRT file:

   "SETPRTDIALOG    3"


What makes this whole episode such a pisser is that this statement was
provided to us by Fujitsu as a result of an (much) earlier query. We were
pleased as punch about this simple trick (to temporarily assign a printer
for a single job). We had no reason to believe this trick would fail for a
distribution. It did.
```

<a id="t-ee525f1a3b"></a>
## Thread: net express promlem — 4 msgs

### net express promlem

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T14:32:58-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9rnIa.7811$9y3.753137@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
I have a customer with a net express problem that seems rather strange ...
perhaps someone has an idea of what to try.  The version is Version 3.0.

A cheque file is being printed with signatures.  The signatures are in BMP
form, and the BMP file is read in once at the start of the program.  It is 1
meg in size.

When the program runs under test (IE-under animation), it runs perfectly.
When the exact same INTS are run (no re-compile) with the exact same data in
production mode, but not under animation, it runs fine until about check
number 30 or so, at which time the disk goes berserk and the machine starts
thrashing. It appears that the swap file is running out of space.

Is there any reason that runnng under animation should use less memory than
production?

Donald
```

#### ↳ Re: net express promlem

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T19:15:56+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MZnIa.230$BM.184826@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<9rnIa.7811$9y3.753137@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
"Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:9rnIa.7811$9y3.753137@news20.bellglobal.com...
> I have a customer with a net express problem that seems rather strange ...
> perhaps someone has an idea of what to try.  The version is Version 3.0.
>
> A cheque file is being printed with signatures.  The signatures are in BMP
> form, and the BMP file is read in once at the start of the program.  It is
1
> meg in size.
>
> When the program runs under test (IE-under animation), it runs perfectly.
> When the exact same INTS are run (no re-compile) with the exact same data
in
> production mode, but not under animation, it runs fine until about check
> number 30 or so, at which time the disk goes berserk and the machine
starts
> thrashing. It appears that the swap file is running out of space.
>
> Is there any reason that runnng under animation should use less memory
than
> production?

Well, if the animated run is for fewer checks than the production, and the
code which displays the bitmap image does not properly release prior
allocations, yes.

I've seen this fairly often with Windows GDI: you forget to DeleteObject and
BINGO! you have what is often called a "resource leak", which only has
physical manifestations when you've leaked a lot.

I'm not familiar with NetExpress at all, but I'd check whatever code the
programmer does write to insure that "get" and "release" functions are
properly paired.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: net express promlem

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-19T15:29:33-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cgoIa.7874$9y3.756714@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```

BMP

is

> > meg in size.
> >
> > When the program runs under test (IE-under animation), it runs
perfectly.
> > When the exact same INTS are run (no re-compile) with the exact same
data
> in
> > production mode, but not under animation, it runs fine until about check
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> allocations, yes.

That was my first thought, and the first thing we checked was to grab the
complete set of production data, bring it in entirety over to the test
machine, and run it.  The problem did not exist.  We then checked
configurations on the two machines.  Identical.  The only confirmed
difference we have found is that the problem ONLY happens when not run in
test mode.

>
> I've seen this fairly often with Windows GDI: you forget to DeleteObject
and
> BINGO! you have what is often called a "resource leak", which only has
> physical manifestations when you've leaked a lot.
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: net express promlem

- **From:** chris.glazier@microfocus.com (Chris Glazier)
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T09:23:43-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<260cd566.0306200823.78dd7a0a@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<9rnIa.7811$9y3.753137@news20.bellglobal.com> <MZnIa.230$BM.184826@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com> <cgoIa.7874$9y3.756714@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Are you using the PC_PRINTER_LOAD_BITMAP, PC_PRINTER_WRITE_BITMAP
calls to print the bitmap on the check?

Do you also use PC_PRINTER_FREE_BITMAP when you are done with it?

Do you check the return-status after the calls, as a non-zero code
indicates an error.

If you are not using these calls, then what exactly are you using to
accomplish this task?

"Tex"

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>

```
Also sorted the win95 problem. The docs say it's supported, the user32.dll
export says it's not. I've now used GetModuleFileName which is supported
from win95 and winnt3.1 and up. Use GetModuleHandle to resolve the handle of
the current module.

Rik

<a id="t-00bb4eed92"></a>
## Thread: Identify IBM Load Module — 11 msgs

### Identify IBM Load Module

- **From:** joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T06:52:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c99d007f.0306200552.10b50347@posting.google.com>`

```
I am looking for a way to uniquely identify a load module generated by
an IBM compiler.  The program prolog area is pretty clear in the
assembler listing such that I can easily pull out the WHEN-COMPILED
timestamp.

Can anyone tell me how often this prolog area changes?  Does IBM
guarentee the layout or are we supposed to just pretend it does not
exist?

I would need to support load modules from Cobol for OS/390 v2.2 and
forward.

Any information is greatly appreciated.

Joe Zitzelberger
```

#### ↳ Re: Identify IBM Load Module

- **From:** Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T12:16:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<o7g6fvkqul92r5t75k7ltgq8bksjg9g1nv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<c99d007f.0306200552.10b50347@posting.google.com>`

```
On 20 Jun 2003 06:52:57 -0700 joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)
wrote:

:>I am looking for a way to uniquely identify a load module generated by
:>an IBM compiler.  The program prolog area is pretty clear in the
:>assembler listing such that I can easily pull out the WHEN-COMPILED
:>timestamp.

:>Can anyone tell me how often this prolog area changes?  Does IBM
:>guarentee the layout or are we supposed to just pretend it does not
:>exist?

First of all, load modules are created by the linkage edittor^W^W binder, not
by a compiler. Compilers create object decks.

At any rate, will this load module be in storage or be in a program library?
Do you need to know the state of the first object deck, or all of them?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Identify IBM Load Module

- **From:** Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T20:28:50-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<joe_zitzelberger-228ACB.20285020062003@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<c99d007f.0306200552.10b50347@posting.google.com> <o7g6fvkqul92r5t75k7ltgq8bksjg9g1nv@4ax.com>`

```
In article <o7g6fvkqul92r5t75k7ltgq8bksjg9g1nv@4ax.com>,
 Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote:

> On 20 Jun 2003 06:52:57 -0700 joe_zitzelberger@vsam.org (Joe Zitzelberger)
> wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Do you need to know the state of the first object deck, or all of them?

Only the main entry point as reported by LOAD or E.C. INQUIRE PROGRAM 
ENTRYPOINT.

I was going to grab the compile date/time stamp between PPA1 and PPA2 at 
offset $60 (? cant remember as I am at home now, but I think it was 60).  
But only if IBM has a history of keeping that in the same place through 
various versions of compilers.
```

In article <01c337f5$a7e62960$ec91f243@chottel>,
 "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote:

> Have your tried the AMBLIST utility? Perhaps I don't completely understand
> what you are looking for.
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> > 

I want to have a unique string of bytes that will change every time a 
module is recompiled.  This will be passed around at run time as part of 
an envelope containing application data to allow all players in a 
certain distributed system know when any one player is changing things 
without telling the other players.

Just a sanity check on source control procedures.
```

Every time it is compiled anywhere e.g. in development, test, etc. or just
every time it is compiled for production? We use a combination of program
name and version number. Or if you recompile a lot use program name,
version number, and the letters a-z. Start with 'a' and bump it to the next
letter for each compile.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Identify IBM Load Module

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Charles Hottel" <chottel@cpcug.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T05:44:32+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01c33a13$94782fc0$d2c2f943@chottel>`
- **References:** `<c99d007f.0306200552.10b50347@posting.google.com> <o7g6fvkqul92r5t75k7ltgq8bksjg9g1nv@4ax.com> <joe_zitzelberger-228ACB.20285020062003@corp.supernews.com> <01c337f5$a7e62960$ec91f243@chottel> <joe_zitzelberger-DAC709.21273123062003@corp.supernews.com> <01c33a0c$581cb200$e095f243@chottel> <01c33a0e$18e32b80$e095f243@chottel>`

```
Our programs have the following two lines at the front:

      * LCSVERSION TMPFPF,01A,01/10/92,11.33.12,RICHARD-CHERNIN        
0000001 
      * LCS PROGRAM MAIN MODULE                                        
0000002 

When we start work on a new version the program is copied from production
to development and the version is bumped up and the suffix is set to A. For
each update ( or comiple?) the suffix and date and time are updated. For
each compile the info from the first line is used to update a copy book
which is copied following WORKING-STORAGE SECTION. This is what the filled
in copy book looks like. If you want I can provide what it looks like
before it is updated.

01 core-mark.
    03 filler pic                         pic x(32) value 'working storage
starts here for '.
    03  this-lcs-pgm                  pic x(08) value 'tmpfpf01'.
    03  pgm-field-breakdown redefines this-lcs-pgm.
        05  lcs-pgm-id                 pic x(03).
        05  lcs-pgm-ident             pic x(03).
        05  lcs-pgm-vers              pic x(02).
    03  this-archie-version          pic x(05) value 'none'.
    03  update-number               pic x(05) value '000002'.
    03  date-lcs-updated             pic x(08) value '92/01/10'.
    03  time-lcs-updated             pic x(08)  value '11:33:12'.
    03  date-last-lcs-access        pic x(08) value '92/01/10'.
    03  programmer-lcs-updated pic x(15) value 'richard-chernin'.
    03 librarian-dsn                   pic x(44) value
'dvl.source.libmast'.

Maps and copy books have a different second line and are handled a little
differently.

Parts of this were implemented using Roscoe's RPF language and I think we
may have paid CA to make a modification (or provide an exit?) to Librarian.
These processes should be automated and required otherwise people will
forget to make the required updates. I hope this helps at least to give you
some possible ideas.


Charles Hottel <chottel@cpcug.org> wrote in article
<01c33a0e$18e32b80$e095f243@chottel>...

> > version number, and the letters a-z. Start with 'a' and bump it to the
…[32 more quoted lines elided]…

does
> > not
> > > > > > :>exist?
> > > > > >

edittor^W^W
> > > > binder, not

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

When using the "value" of a Working-Storage item to identify a "version" be
careful, be VERY careful about the "OPT" compiler option.  See:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/2.4.37.1
```

## Thread: cobrtl ISAM problem — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### cobrtl ISAM problem

- **From:** sabad387 <member31555@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-20T14:39:50+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3025626.1056119990@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi:

SOS please. Need help.

I have written a COBOL program using Indexed files. When I compile my
COBOL program on Tru64 unix, it compiles absolutely fine with no errors.
However when I run my executable, it throws an error saying

"cobrtl: severe: Indexed file IO used, but ISAM isn't installed".

Thanks
```

<a id="t-9728cba5ce"></a>
## Thread: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion) — 24 msgs

### Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T17:19:57-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Given the fact that Mr. Wagner has *not* gone away (as I had hoped that he
might - while searching for some new "unsuspecting" employer), the following
is my current suggestion to others (and what I plan on following for a
while - to see how this works).

1) *NOT* respond to any of his notes that are responding to others comments
on his "opinions" (usually not identified as opinions)

2) Only respond to notes from him when they are responses to "new" members
of this group who may not know about his track record.

3) Like DD's standard "do your own homework" note, I will post a generic

    "Check google for the accuracy track-record of this poster - and the
<lack> of regard with which his comments are viewed by many in this
newsgroup"

comments to his posts.

    ***

Others are certainly free to do whatever they think "best" (both for
visitors to C.L.C. and to the "ongoing health" of the NG, but it is my HOPE
that maybe this approach will work - where others have not).
```

#### ↳ Re: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-21T17:42:13-05:00

- **Message-ID:** `<FOCdnW5v0p_ZQmmjXTWJiA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

comments
> on his "opinions" (usually not identified as opinions)
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> visitors to C.L.C. and to the "ongoing health" of the NG, but it is my
HOPE
> that maybe this approach will work - where others have not).

Bill, I hang out on newsgroups that can get pretty violent (tx.guns,
news.admin.net-abuse.email, alt.garden.gnomes, etc.) and RW is the only
direct poster I've ever killfiled.

His arrogance would be expected (and mild at that) on a politics-oriented
group; here, it is overboard.

I've always thought that programmers are like dentists: scientifically and
professionally trained, dedicated, diligent... but also artists. And, while
programmers may wear three-piece suits, all the stereotypical
characteristics of a hippie living in a garret in Greenwich Village hold
too.

One of these attributes of an artist is defensiveness about his work. Did
Reuben think his work better than Van Gogh? You bet (but of course
everybody's work is better than Van Gogh's)!

Most of us are willing to consider better ways of doing things, handle
well-meaning criticism tolerably, and give credit where due. Some are not.
They get blocked.
```

In article <3ef52792.21212794@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>Your solution is juvenile. If you tried it on J4 or in business, you'd be
>expelled.  Reasonable people rebut the offender's errors with facts and logic ..
>you know: argumentation. 

It is my experience, Mr Wagner, that 'reasonable people' modify their
behaviors based on rebuttals.

As a purely hypothetical example consider Person X.  Person X makes an
assertion about a feature of a technology; when it is pointed out that
Person X is wrong s/he responds with 'Well, it was that way when I looked
at it (x) years ago.'

If Person X were 'reasonable' it might be concluded that in the future 
s/he would make sure the feature of the technology about which s/he would 
post was a current one.

>
>If you continue using this 'spamming' solution, I too have developed a
>poison-pill  riposte (how appropriate: re-post). The two combined will destroy
>CLC faster than you can say alt.scientology. The choice is yours. 

Mr Wagner, you are obviously ignorant of a Story from the Oldene Dayse:

A tyrant calls a holy man before him and says 'My hands are behind my
back.  In one of my hands there is a baby bird.  You claim your Deity is
omnipotent, omniscient and omnibenificent; as such have your Deity tell
you in which hand I hold the bird.  If you cannot tell me correctly then I
will kill the bird and its death will be placed on your head by your
Deity.'

The holy man replies 'No matter what my Deity tells me or I tell you... 
the bird's life is still in your hands.'

One might be able to learn interesting things about personal 
responsibility from this anecdote, Mr Wagner... assuming that you have the 
capability to learn such things then the choice to do so, it seems, is 
yours.

DD
```

use the same tactic. That's not responsible.

When such things have happened to me in the past, Mr Wagner - as those who 
have been here a while might be able to attest to - I have responded as I 
saw fit and not-responded as I saw fit.  When someone sent a series of 
emails to the abuse@ address of my accounts I let the people there deal 
with it, also.

>If I left, I'd be setting a precedent which would encourage others to

do so!'

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-22T18:09:11+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef5f087.24728239@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef52792.21212794@news.optonline.net> <bd4a07$662$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ef5bc40.11342622@news.optonline.net> <bd4gm6$ap4$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ef5bc40.11342622@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>expelled.  Reasonable people rebut the offender's errors with facts and
logic
>>..
>>>>you know: argumentation. 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>modifications and optimisations built into MySQL.

I couldn't find a description of how Google works today. I did find the
founders' paper describing its internal structure when it started in 1997.

http://www7.scu.edu.au/programme/fullpapers/1921/com1921.htm

Back then they weren't using any database. They were doing everything with C
programs and ISAM files!! The paper mentions response time between one and ten
seconds .. for single-threaded queries. Now, I estimate they process 1,000
queries per second during peak times (50M/day). Obviously, the technology
changed in the last seven years. 

Ironically, the founders complained about their competitors' secrecy: "Up until
now most search engine development has gone on at companies with little
publication of technical details. This causes search engine technology to remain
largely a black art and to be advertising oriented. With Google, we have a
strong goal to push more development and understanding into the academic realm."

Where is it?

>>>>If you continue using this 'spamming' solution, I too have developed a
>>>>poison-pill  riposte (how appropriate: re-post). The two combined will
destroy
>>>>CLC faster than you can say alt.scientology. The choice is yours. 
>>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>saw fit and not-responded as I saw fit. 

There is nothing to respond to. The boilerplate doesn't rebut any of the points
I made. It's just noise. 

> When someone sent a series of 
>emails to the abuse@ address of my accounts I let the people there deal 
>with it, also.

Complaining to ISPs doesn't work. There are many many ISPs who ignore abuse
complaints. Also, there's no way to block postings via Google. 

>>If I left, I'd be setting a precedent which would encourage others to
>>use the same tactic. That's not responsible. 
>
>Anyone who decides to use your actions as precedent for their own, Mr 
>Wagner, would, it seems, get *exactly* what they deserve.

That's unresponsive. If the spamming tactic silences me, the next unpopular
poster will be hectored out the same way.
```

In article <3ef5f087.24728239@news.optonline.net>,
Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
>docdwarf@panix.com wrote:
>

>http://www7.scu.edu.au/programme/fullpapers/1921/com1921.htm

That's nice... what comes next, appropriate documentation to show that 
things burn because they are filled with phlogiston?

[snip]

>>>>>If you continue using this 'spamming' solution, I too have developed a
>>>>>poison-pill  riposte (how appropriate: re-post). The two combined will destroy
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>I made. It's just noise. 

Reading what I wrote carefully, Mr Wagner, shows that I did not use 
'responding' as my only suggestion.  If something is 'just noise' then it 
mighe be obvious that it is such.

>
>> When someone sent a series of 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>complaints. Also, there's no way to block postings via Google. 

Complaining to my ISP worked in that the abuse@ guy (it was a male in all 
cases) checked the newsgroup and posted that he saw nothing wrong.  As for 
Google... if you want it to contain a record of your responding to 'just 
noise' then that is your concern.

>
>>>If I left, I'd be setting a precedent which would encourage others to
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>That's unresponsive.

If you are referring to my comment as being 'unresponsive' that is
incorrect; I am being quite quite responsive, Mr Wagner, as you posit your
actions as a precedent for those of others.

If you are referring to the fact that not everything on the UseNet is 
worthy of a response and that acknowledging this unworthiness might cause 
one to be unresponsive... my, my, you've come to such a *stunning* 
conclusion!

>If the spamming tactic silences me, the next unpopular
>poster will be hectored out the same way. 

There might possibly be a difference, Mr Wagner, between not responding to 
'just noise' and 'being silenced'; using someone else's actions as 
justification for your own can lead to what my Sainted Grandmother - may 
she sleep with the angels! - used to invoke as the 'Brooklyn Bridge 
Defense': 'So, someone else is jumping off the Brooklyn Bridge... you 
should, too?'

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-22T23:55:38+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef61d00.36114817@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef5bc40.11342622@news.optonline.net> <bd4gm6$ap4$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ef5f087.24728239@news.optonline.net> <bd4uh0$q8e$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>In article <3ef5f087.24728239@news.optonline.net>,
>Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>>expelled.  Reasonable people rebut the offender's errors with facts and
logic..
>>>>>>you know: argumentation. 
>>>>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>That happened months ago, when I started posting. Since then, I've modified
my
>>>>behavior to look things up. 
>>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>things burn because they are filled with phlogiston?

No, but I wouldn't explain fire as simple oxidation either. I'd explain that
fire is a complex chain reaction involving radical formation. I'd describe fire
as a low speed explosion. I'd support it by pointing out that Halon fire
extinguishers work by blocking radicals at the molecular level, not by depriving
the fire of fuel or air. 

Know-nothings would say, 'RW is full of crap. I learned that fire is oxidation
in elementary school. Everyone with common sense knows it's true.'

Those are the kind of 'debates' we have on CLC. 

>>>>>>If you continue using this 'spamming' solution, I too have developed a
>>>>>>poison-pill  riposte (how appropriate: re-post). The two combined will
destroy
>>>>>>CLC faster than you can say alt.scientology. The choice is yours. 
>>>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>What would you do if someone posted depreciatory spam after every one of
your
>>>>postings?
>>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>>There is nothing to respond to. The boilerplate doesn't rebut any of the
points
>>I made. It's just noise. 
>
>Reading what I wrote carefully, Mr Wagner, shows that I did not use 
>'responding' as my only suggestion.  If something is 'just noise' then it 
>might be obvious that it is such.

Some would see silence as complaisance. The best way of dealing with a
schoolyard bully is giving him a taste of his own medicine. I've found they
collapse quickly when confronted, keep pushing when not. 

>>> When someone sent a series of 
>>>emails to the abuse@ address of my accounts I let the people there deal 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>noise' then that is your concern.

I doubt mindspring (where I happen to have a backup account) would find nothing
wrong with WMK's badgering and persistently annoying posts. After they cancelled
his account, he'd move to a more liberal ISP. There are hundreds with POPs in
the Chicago area. Problem not solved. <shrug>

>>>>If I left, I'd be setting a precedent which would encourage others to
>>>>use the same tactic. That's not responsible. 
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>actions as a precedent for those of others.

Forgive my conversational syntax, with its incomplete sentences. 

>If you are referring to the fact that not everything on the UseNet is 
>worthy of a response and that acknowledging this unworthiness might cause 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>should, too?'

That's a poor analogy because everyone isn't jumping off the Brooklyn Bridge,
only WMK. Even if everyone were mechanically flaming my postings, you just
supported me. I'm not adopting their ideas no matter how widely held.
```

>The best way of dealing with a

You are not proposing to 'deal with a schoolyard bully', Mr Wagner, you

…[14 more quoted lines elided]…

>>>>>If I left, I'd be setting a precedent which would encourage others to

I do not support Brooklyn Bridge Defenses, Mr Wagner, and your offer to
behave in what you believe to be an idiotic manner because you conclude 
that someone else is behaving in what you believe to be an idiotic manner 
appears to be just that.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "Chuck Stevens" <charles.stevens@unisys.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T10:59:40-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd7f6d$30uk$1@si05.rsvl.unisys.com>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef5f087.24728239@news.optonline.net> <bd4uh0$q8e$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ef61d00.36114817@news.optonline.net> <bd5khj$qdt$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ef71a55.32261495@news.optonline.net>`

```

"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ef71a55.32261495@news.optonline.net...


> >>That's a poor analogy because everyone isn't jumping off the Brooklyn
Bridge,
> >>only WMK.
> >
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> I didn't notice that you emended your Sainted Grandmother's words. She
used to
> say everyone is jumping.

According to what I read, his Sainted Grandmother is reported as saying
"someone else" in the current thread, "the manager" in another, and only
used "everyone" in rejoinder to an assertion explicitly involving "everyone"
in a third case.   "Used to" implies habit; the facts contradict that.
"Occasionally, when context warranted", given this set of three examples, is
arguably more accurate.

Best evidence of what DD's Sainted Grandmother used to say, as reflected in
this forum, runs along the formula "<noun> is doing <x>?  Fine, <noun> is
jumping off theBrooklyn Bridge... you'll jump too?".   In this formula the
nouns match.

I would also suggest that DD is incontrovertibly better qualified to
describe what his Sainted Grandmother used to say, and how she modified that
saying to fit which circumstances, than Mr. Wagner is.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef61d00.36114817@news.optonline.net> <bd5khj$qdt$1@panix1.panix.com> <3ef71a55.32261495@news.optonline.net> <bd7fo0$ns2$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```
docdwarf@panix.com wrote:

>Interesting that you use a military metaphor as a dodge, Mr Wagner... and
>one that makes about as much sense in this context as 'We had to destroy
>the village in order to save it'.

The one who said that was a Marine, so I understood what he meant. A village is
a place in political space and time; it is not a collection of buildings.
Destroying the buildings enabled the political entity to survive, albeit in
ephemeral form for a short while. 

FWIW, it irritates me that the quotation is misattributed to 'an American
soldier', 'a public relations officer' and 'a high-ranking officer.' And
(dis)credit usually goes to the US Army. The statement was made by a jarhead,
probably a lieutenant platoon leader. I saw him make it on TV. 

Have you ever read B.H. Liddell-Hart, especially his "Strategy"?  He puts
emphasis on occupying and administering, as opposed to killing the enemy and
leaving as we did in VietNam. We FINALLY seem to have grasped his wisdom in
Afghanistan and Iraq. Liddell-Hart's heroes are Carl von Clausewitz, Erwin
Rommel and Robert E. Lee. Although the latter two were on the losing side, their
brilliance is worthy of study and emulation. 

>>>I do not support Brooklyn Bridge Defenses, Mr Wagner, and your offer to
>>>behave in what you believe to be an idiotic manner because you conclude 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>Most gracious of you to admit this, as well.

You changed my thinking on dealing with WMK. Ok, I won't spam his spams. I'll
first try sending in the inspectors (sysadmins), as we did in Iraq. If that
doesn't work ......
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-22T13:28:56-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xKScnWANcoD7aGijXTWJhA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef52792.21212794@news.optonline.net> <bd4a07$662$1@panix1.panix.com>`

```

<docdwarf@panix.com> wrote in message news:bd4a07$662$1@panix1.panix.com...
> In article <3ef52792.21212794@news.optonline.net>,
> Robert Wagner <robert@wagner.net> wrote:
> >Your solution is juvenile. If you tried it on J4 or in business, you'd be
> >expelled.  Reasonable people rebut the offender's errors with facts and
logic ..
> >you know: argumentation.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> at it (x) years ago.'

At a company I once worked for, a chap (whose job function was best
described as "Theoretical Mechanical Engineer") came to my office, sat down
dejectedly, and said: "Jerry, I've been fired."

"Why?" said I.

"Suppose you're a VP. You see something in a magazine. You ponder it awhile.
Then you burst into my office and say: "Drop everything and investigate
property 'X'."

And I say to the you, the Vice President of Research and Development: "I'll
make it my hourly duty. But what about 'Proposition 'Y'?"

And you, the VP, respond: "Hmm, yeah, let me think about it." And you go
back to your office and look up Proposition 'Y' which clearly holds that
property 'X' is impossible, the impossibility was reported by Georgia Normal
School in 1897, and has been confirmed experimentally and reported in
everything including "Meteorology Today" at least twelve times in the last
100 years. You'd feel pretty foolish. So, how do you keep from feeling
foolish at least once a week?"

I agreed with him and wished him well.

P.S.
Within a week, my friend was hired by the Franklin Institute in Philadelphia
at almost three times the salary.
```

## Thread: EVALUATE - odd behaviour — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### EVALUATE - odd behaviour

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T16:34:22+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef68056_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
I wondered if anyone might care to comment on the following EVALUATE...

It gives a compiler error at the point indicated, saying that a conditional
statement is found where an imperative statement is required. If I cut the
code to a separate paragraph/section and PERFORM it from the Evaluate it,
compiles and runs correctly.

I don't understand why EVALUATE in general actually doesn't allow a
conditional statement and was hoping someone who is up with the standards
could elucidate. If this behaviour is correct, why doesn't it grizzle about
the IF under the first WHEN?

Why have countless EVALUATEs all worked OK woth IF statements in them and
then this one decides to adhere tot he letter of the Law <G>?

Here's the code...


     evaluate TRUE
        when HV-BkgStatus  < 2
* User has cancelled out and left the DB hanging... Give them 30 minutes...
             if lnk-yyyymmdd NOT > today-yyyymmdd
                if lnk-yyyymmdd < today-yyyymmdd
                   move zero to getHOURS
                                getMINS
                end-if
                compute ticks-row = (getHOURS * 3600) + (getMINS * 60)
                if (ticks-row + 1800) < ticks-now
                   perform drop-it
                   add 1 to total-drop-s1
                   subtract 1 from total-rows-after
                end-if
             end-if
        when HV-BkgStatus = 2
* Check that this unconfirmed Booking has not exceeded the hours we hold
* such a Booking for...(User configurable)

At this point the Fujitsu 97 Compiler packs a sad and goes off in a
huff...<G>

If I cut the code from here:
             if holdForHours NOT = 9999
                add getHours to holdForHours
                divide holdForHours by 24
                          giving holdForDays remainder holdForHours
                end-divide
                if holdForDays NOT = zero
                   move lnk-yyyymmdd to digits-8
                   compute digits-8 = function date-of-integer
                        (function integer-of-date (digits-8) + holdForDays)
                   move digits-8 to lnk-yyyymmdd
                   move holdForHours to getHours
                   move zero to getMINS
                end-if
                if lnk-yyyymmdd < today-yyyymmdd
                   perform drop-it
                   add 1 to total-drop-s2
                   subtract 1 from total-rows-after

                else
                   compute ticks-row = (getHOURS * 3600) + (getMINS * 60)
                   if ticks-row NOT > ticks-now
                   perform drop-it
                   add 1 to total-drop-s2
                   subtract 1 from total-rows-after
                end-if
             end-if
...to here, and replace it with a PERFORM, the whole lot compiles and runs
perfectly...


        when HV-BkgStatus  = 3
* If it's after the departure date, delete it. (Time period is user
configurable...)
             continue
     end-evaluate

I'm simply too busy at the moment to investigate deeply or worry about this,
but it is interesting and I thought someone might see it immediately...

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE - odd behaviour

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-22T23:57:45-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd61f9$ptl$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3ef68056_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Can we say "indentation" ???  Look at the line with

    "if ticks-row NOT > ticks-now"

and try and find an END-IF for it ???

You may have as "deeply" nested IF statements as you want in the WHEN phrase
of an EVALUATE - but you need to make certain that they are ALL "made
imperative" with END-IF's.
```

#### ↳ Re: EVALUATE - odd behaviour

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@Azonic.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T20:02:33+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd6c6u$sdb$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3ef68056_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:

> It gives a compiler error at the point indicated, saying that a
> conditional statement is found where an imperative statement is required.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> about the IF under the first WHEN?

This was covered a few weeks ago here.  A conditional statement can be 
turned into an imperitive statement by having it correctly terminataed.

Conditional statement:

          IF ( x )
              do something

Imperitive statement:

          IF ( x )
              do something
          END-IF


> At this point the Fujitsu 97 Compiler packs a sad and goes off in a
> huff...<G>
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>                    if ticks-row NOT > ticks-now
                     ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^
                   
>                    perform drop-it
>                    add 1 to total-drop-s2
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> perfectly...


The statement has a missing END-IF - therefore conditional not imperitive.

Within an IF or a separate paragraph a conditional is OK.
```

```

time flat...<G>)

My face is red, but I deserve it.

I had totally missed the offending IF and that is why it wasn't indented. I
do normally indent carefully and use scope terminators always.

I looked at this for some thirty minutes before posting it...<G> Mea Culpa.

Good job all, and thanks again.

Pete.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: EVALUATE - odd behaviour. THANKS to all...

- **From:** Colin Campbell <cmcampb@attgloabl.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T09:34:06-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EF72BFE.5D01295D@attgloabl.net>`
- **References:** `<3ef68056_1@corp-news.newsgroups.com> <3ef6f31b_9@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter,
It seems as if there's a lesson here:
Believe your compiler!

Since it tells you that not all of the statements are imperative, you can
immediately zero in on the "IF's" as a starting point.  If those had not been
the offenders, you would start looking for things like "ON OVERFLOW", "ON SIZE
ERROR", etc.

Your workaround reinforces the fact that the compiler told you what you needed
to know.  It is obvious that the offending statement was in the chunk code that
that you moved into a PERFORM'ed procedure.

I don't use Fujitsu's compiler, but the IBM mainframe and VA COBOL compilers
give you a helpful little goodie by showing the subordination level on the left
side of the listing.  Within a procedure, all statements at the "top level" have
a blank in this column.  When you code an IF, EVALUATE, PERFORM, etc, the
subordinate statements are assigned a "1", and so on.  If you have that, you can
use it to help you see failures to indent.  But really, you didn't need to go
that far....
```

<a id="t-1ab2bf55f4"></a>
## Thread: Top Posting and not "trimming" (was: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion) — 30 this month / 70 total

### Top Posting and not "trimming" (was: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T13:10:24-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd7fqj$5r6$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <bb0efvcnlfhms027q4sc3309h3m5u72kc8@4ax.com>`

```
I have previously posted my view on "top posting", i.e. that in many (not
all) cases, I prefer it.  For those with a NG reader that allows reading
"threads" - this is a much better (IMHO) way of doing things than in the way
that made sense with "old" telnet readers.

As far as trimming goes,  my "canned" comment is intended to alert "new"
(and unfamiliar) readers - or those looking thru archives.  If, after
reading other posts from RW, they still want to read his comments, I think
they should be left in "without editing".  However, I do understand your
"concern".  Does your NG reader cause you "long delays" in bringing in all
these posts?  If not, you can be certain that any post with such a "top
post" need not be read any further than my "canned" comment.  If your NG
reader causes you significant delay in "bringing" in all my replies to his
notes, then let me know if there is something specific that I could add (at
the end of the subject or in the body of my text) to let you "killfile"
these posts without missing other "useful" ones (which I hope you think many
of mine are).

I am willing to be "flexible" about how to "mark" all of RW's posts - but I
do think that we (and I am glad to see at least one other CLC participant
has used my wording) need to make certain that no unsuspecting (new) reader
thinks that his posts are normally relevant, accurate, or in the "spirit" of
CLC.
```

William M. Klein <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

[snip]

>I am willing to be "flexible" about how to "mark" all of RW's posts - but I
>do think that we (and I am glad to see at least one other CLC participant

#### ↳ Re: Top Posting and not "trimming" (was: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T18:46:33+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd7hu9$c0l$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <bb0efvcnlfhms027q4sc3309h3m5u72kc8@4ax.com> <bd7fqj$5r6$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```

On 23-Jun-2003, "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

> I have previously posted my view on "top posting", i.e. that in many (not
> all) cases, I prefer it.  For those with a NG reader that allows reading
> "threads" - this is a much better (IMHO) way of doing things than in the way
> that made sense with "old" telnet readers.

When it is appropriate to have a long quote with minimal addition - then top
posting makes sense, so that readers can decide whether to page down - at least
when it is obvious that you have no new material added below.   It makes even
more sense when the nature of your top post makes it unlikely that a
conversation with layers of quotes will result.

Top posting, bottom posting, & middle posting should each be picked to make it
easy for the reader to follow the conversation.
```
