#### ↳ Re: Top Posting and not "trimming" (was: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T22:26:51+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef77dba.57709561@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <bb0efvcnlfhms027q4sc3309h3m5u72kc8@4ax.com> <bd7fqj$5r6$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>I have previously posted my view on "top posting", i.e. that in many (not
>all) cases, I prefer it.  For those with a NG reader that allows reading
>"threads" - this is a much better (IMHO) way of doing things than in the way
>that made sense with "old" telnet readers.

I agree in re top posting. 

>As far as trimming goes,  my "canned" comment is intended to alert "new"
>(and unfamiliar) readers - or those looking thru archives.  

Your canned comment is a form of ad hominem which has nothing to do with
protecting new readers. You are SPAMMING the newsgroup in an attempt to
discredit me.  Spamming is universally recognized as unacceptable behavior. For
instance: 

Excessive Multi-Posting (EMP) has the same meaning as the term "spam" usually
carries, but it is more accurate and self-explanatory. EMP means, essentially,
"too many separate copies of a substantively identical article."

"Substantively identical" means that the material in each article is
sufficiently similar to construe the same message. The signature is included in
the determination. These are examples of substantively identical articles:

byte-for-byte identical messages 
otherwise identical postings minimally customized for each group it appears in. 
advertising the same service. 
articles that consist solely of the same signature 
articles which consist of inclusions of other user's postings, but are otherwise
identical. 

> However, I do understand your
>"concern".  Does your NG reader cause you "long delays" in bringing in all
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>of mine are).

You don't understand UseNet basics. If the subject line is changed, you've
started a new thread. Thus your flame would not be juxtaposed to my article. 

Given that you're a spammer, you cannot explain it away by offering cosmetic
changes. You can make it go away by stopping your juvenile spamming. 

>I am willing to be "flexible" about how to "mark" all of RW's posts - but I
>do think that we (and I am glad to see at least one other CLC participant
>has used my wording) need to make certain that no unsuspecting (new) reader
>thinks that his posts are normally relevant, accurate, or in the "spirit" of
>CLC.

What is the spirit of CLC? Grouchy old men defending obsolete technology? I have
some experience maintaining an outstanding FAQ. I admit yours is good but hand
the FAQ to me and you'll see more COBOL than mainframe.
```

```
"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
news:3ef77dba.57709561@news.optonline.net...
> "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:
<snip>
> Your canned comment is a form of ad hominem which has nothing to do with

> discredit me.  Spamming is universally recognized as unacceptable
behavior. For
> instance:
>

If you read the following,

"For those not familiar with comp.lang.cobol,
     Check google for the accuracy track-record of this poster (R. Wagner) -
and the <lack of> regard with which most of his comments are viewed by many
in this newsgroup before reading his post below."

This does NOT qualify as an "ad hominem" nor does it discredit you UNLESS
you accecpt the fact that viewing most of your posts via "google" shows that
you are not worthy of credit.  It is also a statement of fact that many in
the newsgroup do not have much regard for you - which I think you have
already acknowledged in some of your other posts.

I do *not* state in my words what will happen when "newcomers" view your
posts via google.  However, in this note I will say that I suspect that many
(not all) who follow my advice will have the same (and this is "ad hominem")
poor opinion of your accuracy, consistency, logic, and ability to
participate in this NG in  manner consistent with its past and that desired
by many of its current participants - as I have.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Top Posting and not "trimming" (was: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
In article <xG6dnZs9dPN2m2SjXTWJhQ@giganews.com>,
JerryMouse <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
>
><docdwarf@panix.com>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>that someone would order three jelly-filled and a diet Coke.

Well, maybe these folks were filling a group order for the office... or 
something like that... and maybe I am the King of England, too!  I recall, 
long ago, seeing in a Reader's Digest something like 'Trying to lose 
weight only by switching to low-calorie beverages is like trying to 
lighten a falling airplane by emptying the ashtrays'.

>
>Self-delusion is instinctive.

Oh, I *cannot resist... are you sure you're being honest with yourself?  
That sounded almost...  reflexive.

(Freud said that the most profound statement of psychology he had ever 
heard was Nietzsche's 'Memory says 'I did that'.  Pride says 'I could not 
have done that'.  Eventually memory yields.')

DD
```

```
IMO, the "caffeine-free" diet beverages are better than the caffeinated 
ones.  Generally, the CF versions of off-brand diet sodas are usually 
better than their counterparts as well (which I found out during my 
college days - "Hmmm...  Coke is $1.49, Sam's Choice is $.69...")
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Diet vs. Regular

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** yukonmama@aol.com (YukonMama)
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T03:06:04+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<20030629230604.04179.00001255@mb-m28.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3EFB9A34.5010308@Netscape.net>`

```
There is nothing diet or caffeine free around here :).

Diet drinks leave a real bad taste in my mouth and give my daughter a very bad
headache (am told it's a reaction to the aspertame in most diet beverages).

Decafe coffee tastes like dirt from the backyard.  Decafe tea isn't too bad and
it's about the only decafe beverage I'll drink if the 'real thing' isn't
available.

A few weeks back one of the grocery chains in town was hawking their store
brand of various items using blind taste tests.  Of the 20 different products
they were demonstrating I was able to identify the name brand in all but 2 and
prefered the store brand version of one.  If the family isn't going to eat it,
it's expensive.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Diet vs. Regular

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T04:16:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3effb419.46861390@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3EFB9A34.5010308@Netscape.net> <20030629230604.04179.00001255@mb-m28.aol.com>`

```
Soft drinks are as much a 'delivery device' of sugar as cigarettes are a
'delivery device' of nicotine. Manufacturers take 12 oz. of water, add TEN
TEASPOONS of sugar and a little flavoring, then sell it to you for ten times
what it cost to produce. Would you take a glass of water, stir in ten teaspoons
of sugar and drink it down? If you would, you're headed for Type II diabetes in
midlife. 

Ingesting that much sugar is abusive. If you need hydration, drink water, it's
free. If you want more interest, drink flavored iced tea. 

>Diet drinks leave a real bad taste in my mouth and give my daughter a very bad
>headache (am told it's a reaction to the aspertame in most diet beverages).

99% of allergens are proteins. Aspertame is a protein. It's not only allergenic,
it's toxic to those who don't show allergic reactions.  It causes brain
confusion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: [OT] Diet vs. Regular

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <alistair@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T21:05:40+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
To correct an error, aspartame is not a protein. From the OED:

aspartame // n.
a very sweet low-calorie substance used as a sweetener instead of sugar 
or saccharin.
[chemical name 1-methyl N-l-aspartyl-l-phenylalanine, from aspartic 
acid]
```

```
Whatever your motives, and however familiar either of the two terms might be
to your target audience, amino acids are components of proteins, they aren't
themselves proteins.  It's about as accurate to refer to Aspartame as a
protein as it is to refer to it as a hydrocarbon.

    -Chuck Stevens
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Top Posting and not "trimming" (was: Handling the RW factor (my latest suggestion)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T15:01:34-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdaan3$dai$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bd2lmf$45n$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <bb0efvcnlfhms027q4sc3309h3m5u72kc8@4ax.com> <bd7fqj$5r6$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef77dba.57709561@news.optonline.net> <bd8i1u$utl$1@slb1.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef8a492.133201721@news.optonline.net>`

```
Please note that the following reply from RW has *nothing* to do with the
quoted text from me.

Whether *my* posts are "consistent" with CLC's past or not - is a separate
issue from whether RW's posts are.

Just on an "historical" note, since I have been a participant in CLC, there
HAS been one other "frequent poster" to CLC who generated as much disgust
(my words - not the groups) as RW does.  There was SIGNIFICANT "spam" (if by
that you mean "tailored" replies) to his posts.  This person used to make
posts that "basically" claimed that what was "good enough" for COBOL in 1974
was good enough for EVERYONE to use today.  Furthermore, the fact that he
could "easily" maintain his own code (in a "shop of 1") meant that his style
was the "best possible".  I only comment on this to reflect that (yet again)
RW's view of history (this time of CLC) is in error.

P.S.  It should be noted that SINCE the "issue" of spam was raised, every
note of mine in reply to RW's (usually irrelevant and/or inaccurate) posts
has been "tailored" to explicitly address the specific post of his.

I certainly DO hope that RW learns from his reception within CLC and

    Either
        Learns how to post "useful" posts to CLC
    or
        Stops posting in CLC

It is unfortunate that the LENGTH of time (and the number of posts - and
"irrational threads") that RW has generated has already diminished the
"traditional" resources in CLC.  Until he recognizes how LITTLE support he
receives from any participants and that the reason for this is HIS "style"
and "content" - I fear that CLC will be a "poorer" place for those actually
interested in COBOL.
```

```
"Ron" <ron@address.below> wrote in message
news:g8eefvst8tsa7fk6pa3cca4bbl52ruqfe1@4ax.com...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote:
> >Sun's a goner. Sell your stock. Quit your job (if you work for Sun). Name
a
> >vendor-defined language that lasted (PL-1? Pascal?).
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> of job openings.

I applied for a job once at a shop that used RPG. Mind you, I was fluent in
COBOL, FORTRAN, and BAL (this was a long time ago). Asked if I had ever
programmed in RPG, I, of course, answered honestly that I had not. I
volunteered: "But I have the afternoon available; I could learn it if you
want me to."

I didn't get the job. Snobs.

'Course RPG is pretty good for what it does: sort of an Easytrieve in a
coma.
```

### Re: Anyone know where a COBOL IBM Mainframe programmer can get a JOB?

- **From:** usa_programmer@yahoo.com (US Programmer)
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T15:04:57-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** nyc.jobs,alt.bestjobsusa,alt.cobol,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol

```
rseid13@aol.com (RSeid13) wrote in message news:<20030611142750.23612.00000316@mb-m27.aol.com>...
> I have over 20 years plus, working with COBOL IBM Mainframe systems.  I have
> six years working with NATURAL ADABAS Xpeditor.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I am looking for work in NY

Yes - you can very easily find a job in NY or any other state, just
follow this steps:

1. Apply fo India's citizenship 
2. Get a valid Indian passport 
3. Apply to any Indian company that sends programmers to USA utilizing
L-1 visa.

Here is an example from some Indian forum: 

---------------- 

Mainframe Openings-USA 

Hello Mainframe Professionals, 
This is Satish-HR Manager here from Jenesys Technologies Pvt Ltd. ,
Bangalore. We are an executive search cum software development firm
based in Bangalore,India. Currently we have openings for you in one of
the Fortune 100 companies ranging from major banking to insurance
sector companies. We would like to touch base with your for some
excellent openings with us.Positioned at the threshold of explosive
growth, we invite resume from Mainframes professionals for our major
international and national client ,with at least 2 years of genuine
experience on
COBOL,JCL,DB2,CICS,VSAM,TSO/ISPF 
either in telecom, insurance and banking sectors. 
Applicant need to have valid passport and sould be ready for any
overseas travel within a span of 30 days if sortlisted.
Please do revert with your detailed resume ASAP to 
hrsatish2k@yahoo.co.in   and also to satish@jenesystech.com 
Note: 
1] Please make it sure that candidates send their resumes in word
format to both above mentioned mail-ids.
2] Mention in subject line-Mainframe (years of exp) 

Regards, 
Satish 
Jenesys Technologies Pvt Ltd. 
#1,ist floor,9th main road, 
100 ft road,BTM layout 1st stage, 
bangalore 560076(INDIA) 
---------------------- 

And after you'll find a job don't forget to thank your president,
congresman and senator for taking a good care of American and Indian
people.

Good luck, 

USA_Programmer
```

```
They kept their houses in the suburbs.

|
| My boss was just lamenting yesterday that we have tons of jobs on our

relocate to India.
It looked like an H1B scam to me.
|
| Perhaps we will have to look to India to find people willing to fill
| those positions...

There's probably a shortage of available talent by now.
BUT, that won't stop them.
In NYC we have bunches of Russian immigrants with phony "COBOL & CICS
experience".
They go to a cram course in the US, and give phony Russian experience.
They seldom get found out.
They have experts they contact when they have a problem or question.
You often find a "team" of them working in close proximity, one is usually
the answer man.
We had to let 2 of them go on one project I was on, they produced nothing in
3 months. It took me 2 weeks, starting from scratch, to do their
assignments.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone know where a COBOL IBM Mainframe programmer can get a JOB?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T20:31:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** nyc.jobs,alt.bestjobsusa,alt.cobol,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef9fe16.221667155@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<20030611142750.23612.00000316@mb-m27.aol.com> <161d8525.0306231404.4b545de8@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-34C4B8.21074523062003@corp.supernews.com> <Ci6Ka.19946$3o3.1567371@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <joe_zitzelberger-2B57C7.08251825062003@corp.supernews.com> <sSkKa.20610$0v4.1678764@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>There was one job posted for Buffalo NY, that required a willingness to
>relocate to India.
>It looked like an H1B scam to me.

The company I worked for had legit jobs like that. There is a need for people
who understand US business practices AND technology. He acts as a middle-man
between $1/hr programmers and the US business. 

>In NYC we have bunches of Russian immigrants with phony "COBOL & CICS
>experience".
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>assignments.

How do Russians get hired? I'm finding it difficult to find a job in NYC with
legit experience. Problem is nearly all want more than COBOL and CICS; they want
experience with all the tools and applications they've acquired over the years.
For example, here are the requirements of one posted today:

IBM and Amdahl mainframe, MS/DOS, MVS/XA, Unix, Windows 95/98/NT/2000, SAS,
Cobol , JCL, DB2, 9Equus, Acxiom, GRMS/GRMSe. As well as Accounts Monitor,
Credit MIS GNA, QUE, CBAM, and MR B/S/Datamart.

For all that, it pays $25/hr .. in Manhattan. Looks like a support position. Who
has experience with all, or even most of, those relatively unknown products
(starting with 9Equus)? Nobody. The person who gets the job will probably
fabricate it. 
'I used to be a whiz with 8Equus. Is that close enough?'
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone know where a COBOL IBM Mainframe programmer can get a JOB?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T22:03:45+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** nyc.jobs,alt.bestjobsusa,alt.cobol,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VA.000000ed.00e0f537@ieee.org>`
- **References:** `<20030611142750.23612.00000316@mb-m27.aol.com> <161d8525.0306231404.4b545de8@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-34C4B8.21074523062003@corp.supernews.com> <Ci6Ka.19946$3o3.1567371@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <joe_zitzelberger-2B57C7.08251825062003@corp.supernews.com> <sSkKa.20610$0v4.1678764@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3ef9fe16.221667155@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert,

> The person who gets the job will probably fabricate it.

The outsourcing company I'm involved with claim CMM LEvel 5. An 
ex-chief architect before we got taken over, I did a lot of work 
looking at CMM, and I can ttest that NONE of the people I work with now 
know anything about it. Their procedures are the ones we had in place 
before they turned up (we were around 2.5 - 3), and they didn't know 
Cobol or Natural - our two languages. We had to train them in all of 
that, and after three years they are still at a level I'd expect from 
any local after two.three years. But local don't lie as much as these 
guys - we have to keep on top of them to make sure they'll actually 
repair a problem and not simply drop bad data out of the system and 
carry on.

And since all our work is claimed by them to be theirs, and our 
management accept it, morale is a bit shaky. So the systems fail. But 
since the failures are recovered quickly (by dropping rogue data) their 
statistics are fine. Our senior management love it. 

---

Doug

dwscott@ieee.org
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Anyone know where a COBOL IBM Mainframe programmer can get a JOB?

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T05:38:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** nyc.jobs,alt.bestjobsusa,alt.cobol,alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efa8672.256579929@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<20030611142750.23612.00000316@mb-m27.aol.com> <161d8525.0306231404.4b545de8@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-34C4B8.21074523062003@corp.supernews.com> <Ci6Ka.19946$3o3.1567371@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <joe_zitzelberger-2B57C7.08251825062003@corp.supernews.com> <sSkKa.20610$0v4.1678764@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3ef9fe16.221667155@news.optonline.net> <VA.000000ed.00e0f537@ieee.org>`

```
Doug Scott <dwscott@ieee.org> wrote:

>Robert,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>before they turned up (we were around 2.5 - 3),

When employees see the company successfully 'puff' its qualifications, it should
come as no surprise when they do the same. 

CMM is not a cookbook nor a standard methodology. There isn't a book saying
'this is how to do CMM' except in abstract terms (and with some examples). Each
company must define its own implementation, even its own terminology, then be
audited to insure all conceptual elements are in place. They cannot claim to be
CMM level 5 unless they have such a document and audit report. 

What often happens is they get the certification in one shop, then announce
'Tekmark (a name picked at random :) is now a CMM level 5 company', which
overlooks the fact they're not using it in 500 other shops. If naive clients
think that means they'll get CMM 5, it's not Tekmark's fault they jumped to
hasty generalizations. 

> and they didn't know 
>Cobol or Natural - our two languages. We had to train them in all of 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>carry on.

That's how it's done in the Big League. I worked in production support,
mercifully for only a month, for one of the Big Howevermany outsourcing
companies. We were supporting the Sears payroll system, which paid 400,000
active employees. If a program crashed due to bad input, they simply deleted the
offending record and reran. That meant somebody wouldn't get paid this week. I
can understand their tactical approach, but not their failure to write down the
employee number so the damage could be repaired gracefully, perhaps with a hand
check. 

At the time, Keane was the second most successful computer company, after
Microsoft, in stock value. $1K invested in the '80s IPO would have grown to
$250K in 1999. If investors reward their methodology to that extent, who are we
to quibble about details? Possibly we're wrong. 

>And since all our work is claimed by them to be theirs, and our 
>management accept it, morale is a bit shaky. So the systems fail. But 
>since the failures are recovered quickly (by dropping rogue data) their 
>statistics are fine. Our senior management love it. 

Many here say business (in Texas: biddness) requirements are supreme. Given that
senior management love it, they've spoken. Your marching orders are behave like
the outsourcers. Hup two three four.
```

```
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

| experience with all the tools and applications they've acquired over the
years.

That's the situation now.
During the "boom", employers weren't so picky.
They listed 3 or 4 core skills, and were willing to wing it on the
"exotics".

There are still a lot of plain vanilla mainframe applications out there that
require nothing more than COBOL, CICS, VSAM, and maybe DB2.
The problem is that with fewer dollars, many installations are putting their
money into Web development, especially for customer service.
They get personnel savings, and a customer satisfaction boost.
Once things get rolling again, and they want to offer more products, the
enhancement cycle will pick up.

| For example, here are the requirements of one posted today:
|
| IBM and Amdahl mainframe, MS/DOS, MVS/XA, Unix, Windows 95/98/NT/2000,
SAS,

position. Who

products
```

- **Message-ID:** `<3efa7a96.253543695@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<20030611142750.23612.00000316@mb-m27.aol.com> <161d8525.0306231404.4b545de8@posting.google.com> <joe_zitzelberger-34C4B8.21074523062003@corp.supernews.com> <Ci6Ka.19946$3o3.1567371@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <joe_zitzelberger-2B57C7.08251825062003@corp.supernews.com> <sSkKa.20610$0v4.1678764@bgtnsc04-news.ops.worldnet.att.net> <3ef9fe16.221667155@news.optonline.net> <KTpKa.21532$3o3.1663628@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`

```
"Harley" <dennis.harleyNoSpam@worldnet.att.net> wrote:

>
>"Robert Wagner" <robert@wagner.net> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Who do you think they were looking for?

Two speculations:
.. They wanted every team lead to be an American and it was a big project.
.. They wanted Indians, but had to show they sought the skillset in the US and
found it wasn't available. H1B visas require evidence you sought the skillset in
the US. It seems doubtful Indian citizinship would be considered a Skill, but to
the legal mind, who knows. 

>Would you be willing to relocate to India?

Any place would be better than Lon.Giland. What a horrid culture. I'll be glad
to move back to the United States. I feel like I'm in a foreign country.
Business practices here are discrepant with everywhere else in the US. They're
amateurish. 

>There are still a lot of plain vanilla mainframe applications out there that
>require nothing more than COBOL, CICS, VSAM, and maybe DB2.

That was true in ALL the shops where I worked. They asked for exotic skills
during the hiring process. Once settled in, the work was plain vanilla and I
never needed the exotic skills they thought so necessary. Ok, the CM system is a
little different. How long does it take to learn how to check out and check in a
program from changman? Fifteen minutes? So why did they ask for three years'
experience? Because some recruiter wanted to be safe. 

The problem here is people making hiring decisions don't know what the acronyms
stand for nor have a clue as to their relative importance. They think the safest
course is to demand experience with everything thay have. 'You have experience
with Cognos 7.5. Sorry, we're running 8.0. Good luck in your job search.'
```

## Thread: Contest for a Free Book — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Contest for a Free Book

- **From:** richardson@eclecticsoftwaresolutions.com (Chris Richardson)
- **Date:** 2003-06-23T15:50:59-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b11feff.0306231450.48c87bc7@posting.google.com>`

```
I was just made aware that there is a contest going on for a free copy
of my book (COBOL and Visual Basic on .NET: A Guide for the Reformed
Mainframe Programmer)

(If interested, see http://www.lemon-tree.co.uk/ )

Once you visit that particular lemon-tree website, scroll down to the
entry dated "Wednesday, 4th June 2003".

I know....I know....this is self promotion. I just thought it
worthwhile to mention this contest. Someone "is" going to receive a
free book - seems worthwhile to me <g> My apologies to anyone that may
```

```
Please respond, if at all, off-list to steve@trainersfriend.com.

We're trying to evaluate getting a FLEX-ES based z/OS 1.4 system in order to
run public classes. But mainframe training is really in the doldrums. So my
question is: does anyone perceive any training prospects at their company if we
were to offer our curriculua in Denver? Quick overview of topics [most current
versions covered in all topics]:

Assembler
UNIX System Services
TSO / CLIST / REXX / Dialog Manager
DB2
CICS
JCL
COBOL
PL/I
C
Java
Debugging
Language Environment (LE)

[for details, see http://www.trainersfriend.com/Topics.htm ]

get a feel if there's enough market to cover the costs of setting this up.
```

<a id="t-c41f3f5493"></a>
## Thread: MF: cobcancel active program — 5 msgs

### MF: cobcancel active program

- **From:** Wolf Grossi <Go2DevNull@magro-soft.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T09:40:30+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef8017a$1@e-post.inode.at>`

```
Hi all,

the MicroFocus-runtime is linked with a bunch of c-programs;
C is main.

the situation:
The C-program cobcall's a cobol-prog.
the cobol-prog is using a c-functions.
in the c-function an error occurs and processing of the called 
cobol-prog must be terminated - *within the c-function*.
A cobcancel causes error "177 Attempt to cancel active program".

What can be done to "de-activate" the cobol-program so that it can be 
cancelled?

Thanks for reading
Wolf
```

#### ↳ Re: MF: cobcancel active program

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T19:09:09+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef89f84.131907525@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ef8017a$1@e-post.inode.at>`

```
Wolf Grossi <Go2DevNull@magro-soft.com> wrote:

>Hi all,
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>cancelled?

Where will the C function return to? Its return address is inside the cobol
program it just cancelled. 

The clean way is return an error to the cobol program and have it do a goback. 

A dirty way is take a pointer to a local variable and walk the stack until you
find the cobol program's return address, memcpy it on top of your own and do a
return. If you're lucky, the cobol runtime will clean up the stack; if not,
you've created a stack leak.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF: cobcancel active program

- **From:** Wolf Grossi <Go2DevNull@magro-soft.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T15:18:49+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efaf3ca@e-post.inode.at>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<3ef89f84.131907525@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3ef8017a$1@e-post.inode.at> <3ef89f84.131907525@news.optonline.net>`

```
Robert Wagner wrote:
> Wolf Grossi <Go2DevNull@magro-soft.com> wrote:
> 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> program it just cancelled. 
Well, before cobcall, I do a setjmp(cobsavenv(&cobjmp)).
All cobol-errors are trapped using "cobcall("CBL_ERROR_PROC", 3, args);"
So when gettin a cobol error, I do a longjmp right *after* the cobcall.
I cannot change the cobol-code.

Thanks for your comment,
Wolf
```

#### ↳ Re: MF: cobcancel active program

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T14:54:36-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306241354.3900dcc3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ef8017a$1@e-post.inode.at>`

```
Wolf Grossi <Go2DevNull@magro-soft.com> wrote

> the MicroFocus-runtime is linked with a bunch of c-programs;
> C is main.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> A cobcancel causes error "177 Attempt to cancel active program".

I understand this to mean:

     +-------+
     |c-main |      +----------------+
     |call x ------>|Cobol program x |     +--------------+
     |??     |      |call y -------------> | C function y |
     +-------+      +----------------+     | cancel x ?   |
                                           +--------------+

If you had managed to cancel the cobol program then where were you
planning for the C function to go to next ?  Hint: a return() goes to
a newly dug black hole.
 
> What can be done to "de-activate" the cobol-program so that it can be 
> cancelled?

The only way is to have the C function return a value to the Cobol and
the Cobol then return to the C main so that the C main can cancel it.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF: cobcancel active program

- **From:** Wolf Grossi <Go2DevNull@magro-soft.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T16:01:15+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efafdbf$1@e-post.inode.at>`
- **In-Reply-To:** `<217e491a.0306241354.3900dcc3@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3ef8017a$1@e-post.inode.at> <217e491a.0306241354.3900dcc3@posting.google.com>`

```
Richard wrote:
> Wolf Grossi <Go2DevNull@magro-soft.com> wrote
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Almost correct; but before call x I do a setjmp...


>      +-------+
>      |c-main |      +----------------+
if (!setjmp(cobsavenv(&cobjmp)))

>      |call x ------>|Cobol program x |     +--------------+
>      |??     |      |call y -------------> | C function y |
>      +-------+      +----------------+     | cancel x ?   |
I do a longjmp to the pos right after the cobcall...
then cancel x - getting the 177 error

Thanks for your comments,
Wolf
```

- **From:** "Brian Freeburn" <cfreeburn3@adelphia.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T15:52:27+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%s_Ja.5094$Hw.4388760@news2.news.adelphia.net>`

```
Is there a way in a batch cobol program running on MVS to determine the
machine name or any other enviorment information?

I have a batch cobol program that I want to determine if it is running on
the development or production mainframe.  Any suggestions??
```

#### ↳ Re: Batch COBOL 0S-390 Question.

- **From:** "Howard Brazee" <howard@brazee.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T16:26:48+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bd9u48$snr$1@peabody.colorado.edu>`
- **References:** `<%s_Ja.5094$Hw.4388760@news2.news.adelphia.net>`

```

On 24-Jun-2003, "Brian Freeburn" <cfreeburn3@adelphia.net> wrote:

> Is there a way in a batch cobol program running on MVS to determine the
> machine name or any other enviorment information?
>
> I have a batch cobol program that I want to determine if it is running on
> the development or production mainframe.  Any suggestions??

Is the JCL identical in both cases?   I have called a program to determine which
database CV is running by reading the JCL.

Alternatively, you might put out a file that is different in each environment -
read that file to find out where you are.
```

### Build failed Fujitsu V7

- **From:** "TeckDesign" <francis@teckdesign.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T19:01:06+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fn0Ka.3619$4a7.2051200@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```
        We are trying to use Fujitsu Cobol version 7, but too many problems
are coming up. We try to rebuild the all project but it send the following
message "Build failed"  without an explanation or just it stops in the
middle of the rebuild.
        We notice that using "#include" in the file section the rebuild
fails, so we replace it by "copy" the rebuild doesn�t fail but appears too
many warnings and the application doesn�t work correctly.
How can solve it??

Thanks
JM
```

#### ↳ Re: Build failed Fujitsu V7

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T13:47:35-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aEOdnQ-5hdBaAWWjXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<fn0Ka.3619$4a7.2051200@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```

"TeckDesign" <francis@teckdesign.com> wrote in message
news:fn0Ka.3619$4a7.2051200@newsserver.ip.pt...
>         We are trying to use Fujitsu Cobol version 7, but too many
problems
> are coming up. We try to rebuild the all project but it send the following
> message "Build failed"  without an explanation or just it stops in the
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> How can solve it??

If this is the same 'bug' left over from V6, you must start a new project
then copy over all the modules from the crippled project to the new project
("save as" is insufficient).

You should use #INCLUDEs for the data division and COPY for the procedure
division.

You must also tie a red ribbon around your big toe (either side).

Only by doing these three things will you have any hope.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Build failed Fujitsu V7

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T13:39:12+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef8fdb8_8@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<fn0Ka.3619$4a7.2051200@newsserver.ip.pt> <aEOdnQ-5hdBaAWWjXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`

```
LOL!

Good stuff, Jerry <G>

This has to be one of the most frustrating errors in Fujitsu.

It has happened to me even when I didn't have INCLUDES or COPYs in the
source and it just leaves you with nothing.

"Build failed..."

"OK, gimme a clue..."

"Nope. Build failed..."

I found through (very) bitter experience that creating a new project seems
to work OK (provided you are sure that everything in the one that failed is
fine...). I have also fixed it on occasion by deleting the make file and
forcing it to rebuild all the project information (though I wouldn't
recommend this as a standard approach). Once you have encountered it and
corrected it, it seems to go away for ever <G> (Like it knows "Awww...that
one won't upset him anymore..."). I haven't seen it now for a couple of
years.

The ribbons on the toes is a novel solution. I'll try that one next
time...<G>

Pete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Build failed Fujitsu V7

- **From:** "R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T13:54:43+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdc2gq$rof$1@kostnix.uvt.nl>`
- **References:** `<fn0Ka.3619$4a7.2051200@newsserver.ip.pt> <aEOdnQ-5hdBaAWWjXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`

```
-. Remove the entire debug directory. It'll be restored when totally
rebuilding the project, just don't delete the .ppj file!

-. Got any event scripts that are totally commented out? I've found out that
when event are created (aka a script is made) and is totally commented out,
the builder misses the various DIVISION's and will give strange build
errors, or not an error at all.

-. Check Fujitsu knowledgebase, wasn't there a fix for this? Don't remember,
can check it though.

Good luck,

Rik
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Build failed Fujitsu V7

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T08:18:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jKGcnerZ3aWlPGSjXTWJhQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<fn0Ka.3619$4a7.2051200@newsserver.ip.pt> <aEOdnQ-5hdBaAWWjXTWJiQ@giganews.com> <bdc2gq$rof$1@kostnix.uvt.nl>`

```

"R. Hendriks" <R.Hendriks_1@uvt.nl>
>
> -. Check Fujitsu knowledgebase, wasn't there a fix for this? Don't
remember,
> can check it though.

----------

Bug Fix Report

1. Product
   COBOL97 and PowerCOBOL

2. SYMPTOMS
   The compiler returns a build error but no other message.

   This problem occurs when compiling source code from a precompiler such as
PowerCOBOL.
   If a source line containing a  # FILE/#LINE directive was in error, the
compiler erroneously reported
   the line number to be 0.

3. ACTION TO BE TAKEN
   To apply the Compiler fix:
    1) Unzip this file into a  temp directory,
    2) Locate the file "F3BH220.dll." in the COBOL folder. By default it
should be in
        "C:\Program Files\Fujitsu COBOL\COBOL",
    3) Copy the new "F3BH220.dll." from the temp directory to the COBOL
folder

-----------
Note: The report does not say to which versions of FJ the fix pertains.
```

#### ↳ Re: Build failed Fujitsu V7

- **From:** gmspano <member9303@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T20:52:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3043414.1056574322@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<fn0Ka.3619$4a7.2051200@newsserver.ip.pt>`

```

I agree with Jerry.

You need to open a new project running a new instance of Netcobol and
copy/paste all your forms and scripts.
I did this way a lot. There aren't other solutions..

The fix mentioned is for v6.1 and this patch was been created to resolve
a trouble i have had during a compilation.
Japan team didn't believe about my request, then i sent them a project
and they have been able to reproduce the error message.

There is another GREAT bug in Netcobol.
If you have a project that is using a custom control not longer
registered on your system, Netcobol doesn't allow to cut the control
from the form and you are unable to save your project without it.
It needs to create a new project copying/pasting forms and scripts from
the unavailable project...
Japan team has promised (some years ago) "In the next release the
problems will be resolved"...Nothing to do!! It is always the same...

Japan team think different from the rest of the world.
For your informations, they released a particular version for .NET that
is not the same for the rest of world, not only for the Japanese
language, but with a series of different features.

There is another consideration: when we see a new release of the
product, Japan is using the same version a year before. (???)

Gianni
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Build failed Fujitsu V7

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

- **Message-ID:** `<3efa28b6_2@corp-news.newsgroups.com>`

```

"gmspano" <member9303@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3043414.1056574322@dbforums.com...
>
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
This is NOT just a problem with NETCobol. The same thing happens in V6.

I found this to my cost a few months ago when I de-registered some
components I had written, with a view to replacing them.

I was staggered to find that any project that used them was no longer
savable. I even re-registered them but it made no difference. (To be fair,
the OLE ClassID had changed; nevertheless, the OLE ProgramID was the same).

As Gianni described, I had to create new projects and laboriously copy every
element from the old projects (including event code, not just controls) to
the new project. One of these projects took a full day of really boring
error-prone cut-and-pasting to recover. It's a nightmare...

I haven't investigated it fully (yet) because I've been too busy, but I
believe there HAS to be a way to amend the build information (or the system
registry) so the reference to the de-registered component is removed.

This is something that Fujitsu SHOULD fix and fix quickly.

>
> There is another consideration: when we see a new release of the
> product, Japan is using the same version a year before. (???)
>

That has been the case for a long time. There is nothing wrong with this. It
means the version we get is more stable and the price we pay for it is less
than the price the Japanese pay.

Pete.
```

```
How do you copy the "module" when you are not allowed to save it? Even a
Ctrl-C brings up:"You cannot save an uninstalled component.".

You CAN copy (cut and paste) the constituents of the module and that's what
I (and I think, Gianni also) had to do.

You have to do this one-by-one to the new project, taking care to EXCLUDE
the  de-registered component.

It is a real pain in the arse...

If it really is that easy, Jerry I'm going to sulk for a day or two...<G>

Pete.
```

#### ↳ Re: Build failed Fujitsu V7

```

I confirm what Pete says.

It's not so easy.  You need to copy and paste all the stuff step by
step, avoiding to include the "bad" control.
Sometime is possible to copy the entire module.

I'm using v6.1, but i know in the new v7 the problem still remains.

As Pete is suggesting, Fujitsu SHOULD FIX this problem, but thinking
about  the normal course to release a new version, probably we need to
wait another year to see some bugs fixed.

I heard from a friend that Fujitsu probably will stop the Netcobol for
Windows development, pointing their money and efforts on the .NET
environment...If that is true, i'm really surprised.
Netcobol for .Net doesn't provide any tool to help us to migrate "old"
Netcobol for Win projects, so we need to rebuild them from the scratch.

Powercobol (or Netcobol for Win) is a great product, and actually in the
market there aren't other Cobol company offering a similar product, but
Fujitsu needs to improve better its IDE, adding new features. I sent
them last year a list of these features, hoping to see them in the next
v8.0...(I hope!!).

Gianni
```

## Thread: Cobol development environment — 12 this month / 422 total

### Cobol development environment

- **From:** "Hans Jakschik" <Spectra.Soft@t-online.de>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T20:23:09+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
Hi!

I!m looking for a PC-environment which allows (for host-based-applications
i.g.IBM, BULL..)

- to edit a cobol-program
- to compile that program
- to link it
- and to execute it using

-- classical file-organizations (SEQ, REL, ISAM etc..)
-- RDBMS (DB2, Oracle..)

Which toolset (Compiler, RDMBS, GUI...) is adequate?
Price?

Further informations? Links?

Any helpful answer is kindly appreciated.

Hans Jakschik
```

```
Jeez, Fred, I dunno how much you are earning, but I wouldn't say Fujitsu was

It seems to me that there are two de facto COBOL environments for
Client/Server/PC development: (apart from IBM, which is another ball game),
MicroFocus and Fujitsu.

Neither is "cheap". Both are excellent. (MF is disadvantaged by charging for
its run time).
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol development environment

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T14:46:38-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bda9r3$ere$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
The following is *not* true for Bull (I don't know for them) but for doing
IBM mainframe (MVS primarily  - although VM and VSE are possible with "some
effort") - the leading vendors are:

 IBM itself, see (VisualAge COBOL):
    http://www-3.ibm.com/software/awdtools/cobol/va/

 Micro Focus (Mainframe Express):
    http://www.microfocus.com/products/mainframeexpress/

 CA (Advantage CA-Realia II Workbench):
    http://www3.ca.com/Solutions/Product.asp?ID=1475

All of the above have SQL (DB/2) emulations/functions, IMS, CICS, and
(except IBM) S/370 (S/390) Assembler and JCL options)

    ***

It is *possible* to use Fujitsu products for this, but that is *not* their
primary target (and they really have NOT kept up with IBM mainframe
developments after VS COBOL II).

Other ANSI/ISO conforming compilers would work with a LOT of effort (and for
limited mainframe development).  Also, some of the "C" and "Java" COBOL
products (e.g. Kobol, OpenCOBOL, PerCOBOL) actually have "ported" versions
to IBM Linux/Unix environments - but would NOT work for "normal" IBM
mainframe off-loading.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol development environment

- **From:** ray@rays-web.com (Ray Smith)
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T15:41:14-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5654fff9.0306301441.57926202@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <joe_zitzelberger-DB3A8F.08283425062003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote in message 

> MySQL makes a great RDBMS, TCO is $0.

The last I knew MySQL was GPL'd software with commercial licenses available.

So if your writing GPL'd code it's free, if your not writing GPL'd code I think
you'll find you'll need to buy a license (saying that it's many orders of
magnitude less expensive than some other RDBM's).

Just to be really "nit picky" the license costs may be 0 or reduced but that 
doesn't make the TCO $0.  

Ray Smith
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol development environment

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T16:45:01-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306301545.297b274@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com> <joe_zitzelberger-DB3A8F.08283425062003@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Joe Zitzelberger <joe_zitzelberger@nospam.com> wrote

> How about Borland's Codewrite Editor with shell scripts attached to menu 
> items to invoke your favorite command line Cobol compiler?

Yes, I have disliked all IDEs that I have tried.  I use variations on
XTREE (such as XTGOLD, ZTW) or Midnight Commander plus selected
editors such as Semware's TSE and Set Edit plus make.

Most editors today will allow compiles to be done from the menu bar,
but I prefer not to sit staring at a compile running in my edit
window, and get on with other stuff while a separate process works out
what to do and does it.
 
> TCO is less than $100 (like 99.99), but much less with a student license 
> I think.

Plenty of stuff can be found really cheap or free.

> MySQL makes a great RDBMS, TCO is $0.

MySQL has only recently added transactions with InnoDB which now make
it suitable for multi-user business applications. Its main strength
has always been very fast search and retrieval for (eg) Web sites, but
not so hot on multi-user updates as is required for business use. I
mainly use PostgreSQL which is equivalently priced and has been more
of a heavyweight solution.
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol development environment

- **From:** "Theo Charalambous" <westleylodge@optusnet.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-06-28T10:11:54+10:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efcdd47$0$31276$afc38c87@news.optusnet.com.au>`
- **References:** `<bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com>`

```
If you would like tospend money, nothing will beat MF. Here in Australia a
full workbench (we are talking having a"little" mainframe setup on your
computer),will set you back around $20,000AUD

For really dazzling,colorfull, MS-like GUI  applications that follow the MS
environment, and have seemless connectivity to MS development environments
(ie VB etc), Fujitsu may be a better solution.

However be carefull of the runtime and license schemes they offer. I had a
horrible experience with the Fujitsu licensing scheme, where they are not
willing to provide me with an activation key after I rebuilt my PC (in other
words money spent previously went down the drain).

VA COBOL is a good mainframe-like environment for the cost (It is by far the
less expensive product).

Here's what I would base my decision on...
If you are going to build an "inhouse" type application, I believe by far if
money is not an issue go Microfocus way.

If you want to build applications to market, I must admit Fujitsu should be
your choice

If you want to integrate or offload mainframe development to a PC in a
"cheaper" method then go VA COBOL

Kind Rgds

Theo


"Hans Jakschik" <Spectra.Soft@t-online.de> wrote in message
news:bda4ue$45j$04$1@news.t-online.com...
> Hi!
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
compiler for the COBOL language that runs on Linux and Windows.  It
costs as little as $59.95 per developer with no run time costs.  You
get all platforms for one price.  It will have SQL pre-processor
support later this summer that will include MySQL, PostgreSQL and
optionally Oracle, DB2 and ODBC interfaces.

We have just the compiler available for the Eclipse IDE on Linux for
just $39.95.
```

## Thread: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390) — 21 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** chuck_leviton@yahoo.com (charles leviton)
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T13:10:20-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
I am primarily a CoolGEN developer using COBOL mostly to write
routines that will read files and CALLing these from CoolGEN.

Recently we encountered a situation where while stepping thru some
CoolGEN code (ie running in trace mode) some variables lost their
value after an invocation of a COBOL subroutine.
More by accident than design we tracked this down to an INITIALIZE
stmt in the COBOL program that was acting on a variable defined in the
LINKAGE SECTION.  When we moved the variable to WS the problem went
away.

So my question is what effect does it have when you initialize a
variable that is in the linkage section?
Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T20:55:13+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef8b940.138496119@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com>`

```
chuck_leviton@yahoo.com (charles leviton) wrote:

>Hi,
>I am primarily a CoolGEN developer using COBOL mostly to write
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>variable that is in the linkage section.

Initialize clears the variable(s) to spaces if pic x, zero if pic 9, in either
section. Variables in working-storage are local to the COBOL program; variables
in linkage section are in the calling (CoolGEN) program's memory.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T17:43:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N_SdnfhppJ6ySWWjXTWJhg@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com>`

```

"charles leviton" <chuck_leviton@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
> I am primarily a CoolGEN developer using COBOL mostly to write
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks

The memory locations specified by the starting address + length of the
subprogram's named data item get set to blanks or zero. Suppose:

(calling program)
CALL 'A' USING X Y.  <== note two variables

(called program)
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING P Q R.  <== note subprogram expecting 3 variables
INITIALIZE P Q R.

You're screwed inasmuch as no starting address for "R" was passed by the
calling program (so the called program had to wing it). The  blanks/zeros
resulting from INITIALIZE could appear almost anywhere in memory (but
usually in the same place).

This is a fairly common mistake.

Note that SOME compilers catch this error at execution time.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** "Chuck Leviton" <Chuck_leviton@yahoo.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K86Ka.95484$nr.6272627@twister.southeast.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com> <N_SdnfhppJ6ySWWjXTWJhg@giganews.com>`

```
Thanks all!  Glad to know it is a fairly common mistake!
```

#### ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** LX-i <DanielJSNOSPAM@Knology.net>

```
charles leviton wrote:
> Hi,
> I am primarily a CoolGEN developer using COBOL mostly to write
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> variable that is in the linkage section?

Not to be smart, but you get exactly what describe above.  :)  Unless 
you specify otherwise, the variables you pass in the call are able to be 
changed by that called module.
```

#### ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-24T19:50:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdarko$t4$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com>`

```
Others have already replied, but in case another explanation would make this
any clearer ...

When you CALL a subprogram (in a  COBOL environment) and use the "default"
or explicitly state (in the CALLing program) that you are passing a
parameter "BY REFERENCE" - then the Linkage Section of the CALLed subprogram
just gets a "map" into the storage that is actually in the CALLing program.
Therefore, ANY (all) changes that you make in the subprogram to that Linkage
Section are ACTUALLY changing the field in the storage of the CALLing
program.

If you WANT to pass a parameter to a CALLed subprogram but do NOT want any
of the changes that occur in the subprogram to impact the storage of the
CALLing program, you should use the "BY CONTENT" phrase in the CALLing
program.  This will allow your subprogram to "get data" from the CALLing
program but prevent changes within the subprogram impacting the storage of
the CALLing program.

All of this is true whether you use INITIALIZE, MOVE, READ INTO or any other
"data changing" statement in the subprogram.

Contrary to the MIS-information in one of the replies to your question, the
INITIALIZE statement has several "options" that might impact what it does
and does not change (in any program - CALLing, or CALLed).  It is true that
BY DEFAULT it changes  *named* (not filler - not redefinition) alphanumeric
fields to spaces and numeric fields to zeroes.  However, there are lots of
other possibilities - depending upon exactly how you define the "receiving
field" and what features of INITIALIZE you use.

I don't think that any of this really impacts your "problem" - but did want
to correct the incomplete information posted elsewhere on INITIALIZE.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T13:19:54+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
Funny thing...

I read ALL the responses (before yours, Bill) and wondered why no-one
bothered to explain the use of the various calling methods. How is it
helpful to someone who is experiencing the problem described, if they are
not aware that it is ONLY "BY REFERENCE" that will manifest in this way?

As usual, the quality of Bill's post is superb and covers ALL the bases.
(Jerry's point about a missed parameter was also valuable).

There is nothing I could/would add to Bill's response.

Pete.
```

```
Correct as far as it goes, but I would make one comment. If the parameter
passed BY CONTENT is a POINTER then the copy of the pointer still points to
the original address which may then be changed.
```

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T02:14:18-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdbi7f$dvm$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com> <bdarko$t4$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef90fb4.160631331@news.optonline.net>`

```
Of course RW is wrong again (see below for his part, I am "top posting" my
full reply)

The BY phrase has been part of the INITIALIZE statement since it was
introduced in 1985.  The BY phrase allows any of a specific set of
field-types to be initialized to any "valid" sending field value.  Spaces
and zeroes are (and - for as long as INITIALIZE has been a part of Standard
COBOL) *only* the default.

It is true that the 2002 Standard introduces several NEW features that are
also useful - but learning what is Standard (and available in many/most
existing compilers) will help others who may be as inaccurate in their
knowledge of COBOL as RW is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T09:02:07+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3ef96368.182062710@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com> <bdarko$t4$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef90fb4.160631331@news.optonline.net> <bdbi7f$dvm$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>Of course RW is wrong again (see below for his part, I am "top posting" my
>full reply)
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL) *only* the default.

You're really clutching at straws to make me look stupid. This borders on
deceit. The "BY phrase" is part of the REPLACING clause I mentioned. The 85
syntax is:

INITIALIZE { identifier-1 } ...
THEN REPLACING category-name DATA BY identifier-2 or
literal-1 

>It is true that the 2002 Standard introduces several NEW features that are
>also useful - but learning what is Standard (and available in many/most
>existing compilers) will help others who may be as inaccurate in their
>knowledge of COBOL as RW is.

If the criterion is many/most existing compilers, specifically OS/390, why do
you confuse the issue by mentioning features which don't exist in that realm? Is
your goal to inform the original poster, or is it to make RW look stupid? We
both know the answer, as does everyone else who has followed the saga.

Your claim of 'fact-based' becomes increasing weaker with each posting. You're
now intentionally twisting words to support your thesis that I'm inaccurate.
Keep it up and I'll write a caution advising newcomers of your disingenuity ..
supported by Facts like this one and others to come, evinced by Google searches.


Wake up and stop being an asshole. The spamming and word-twisting is digging you
into a hole. Please return to your former dignified persona.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Rosa Fischer <Rosa.Fischer@fujitsu-siemens.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T08:01:02+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EFA8C1E.97A08988@fujitsu-siemens.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com> <bdarko$t4$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef90fb4.160631331@news.optonline.net> <bdbi7f$dvm$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef96368.182062710@news.optonline.net> <bdc81f$svs$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
> With the new standard I see another possibility to use an "INITIALIZE"
> statement for data defined in the LINKAGE SECTION, where the data definition
> is not used as parameter by the calling program:

working-storage section.
01 w pic x(100).

linkage section.
01 a pic x(100).
01 b based.
     02 b1 occurs 10 indexed by i pic x(10).

PROCEDURE DIVISION using a.
1st section.
1.
            set address of b to address of w.
* this means w is filled with spaces
            initialize b.

           set address of b to address of a.
* this means b is filled with spaces
           initialize b.

The new standard allows to define b in LINKAGE SECTION or in WORKING-STORAGE or
LOCAL-STORAGE SECTION.

Best wishes from old Europe
Rosa Fischer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T12:19:10-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdf9un$r4p$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com> <bdarko$t4$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef90fb4.160631331@news.optonline.net> <bdbi7f$dvm$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net> <3ef96368.182062710@news.optonline.net> <bdc81f$svs$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net> <3EFA8C1E.97A08988@fujitsu-siemens.com>`

```
To expand upon Rosa's commetns below.

In the 2002 Standard, it is legal (not just an extention) to have a VALUE
clause in the Linkage (or File) sections.  Along with the *new* INITIALIZE
TO VALUE feature, the code below can be expanded as follows. (I haven't
"compiled" this - so there may be some typos)


 01 w.   *>  pic x(100)
    05 W-Alpha Pic X(80) Value Low-Values.
    05  W-Num  Pic 9(20) Value 123.

 linkage section.
 01 a pic x(100).
    05  a-Alpha Pic X(80) Value High-Values.
    05  a-Num  Pic 9(20) Value 987.

 01 b based.
      02 b1 occurs 10 indexed by i pic x(10)
*>  The following is the new format of VALUE clauses for tables
           Value Quote from 1 to 9 Zeroes 10.

 PROCEDURE DIVISION using a.
 1st section.
 1.
             set address of b to address of w.
 *> The following will use the VALUE clauses under B to filling the datat
 *> for both the "mappings" at  B and W
            initialize b to Value.

            set address of b to address of a.
 *> The following will still use the mapping of "B" to initialize the
storage poiinted to
 *> at B and A
            initialize b to Value.
            Move A to W
*> the following will initialize both the mappings at A and B to the values
from A
           initialize A to Value.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initializing a Linkage Section variable (On OS/390)

- **From:** chuck_leviton@yahoo.com (charles leviton)
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T05:42:03-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d280a253.0306250442.5965d249@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<d280a253.0306241210.5baa00fc@posting.google.com> <bdarko$t4$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote in message news:<bdarko$t4$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>...
> Others have already replied, but in case another explanation would make this
> any clearer ...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
Thank you, Bill.  I can vaguely recollect from my C programming
classes in school the concept of putting an ampersand (or it might
have been an asterisk) before a variable name when using it as an
argument in a function call.  In this case the function would actually
change the value of the variable itself as opposed to changing the
value of a copy of the variable.  We called it "Call by Value" vs
"Call By reference"

I looked at the COBOL code generated by CoolGEN (something one should
avoid if you can!) and since it doesn't explicitly say CONTENT it must
be a CALL by REFERENCE.
```

```
Here's a couple of my favorites from the Bulwer-Lytton contest (Google) for
the worst opening lines to a novel:

"The countdown had stalled at "T" minus 69 seconds when Desiree, the first
female ape to go up in space, winked at me slyly and pouted her thick,
rubbery lips unmistakably - the first of many such advances during what
would prove to be the longest, and most memorable, space voyage of my
career."

"Sheriff Chameleotoptor sighed with an air of weary sadness, and then turned
to Doppelgutt and said, "The Senator must have really been on a bender this
time -- he left a party in Cleveland at 11:30 last night, and they found his
car this morning in the smokestack of a British aircraft carrier in the
Formosa Straits."

"Having already removed her dress, five petticoats, a bustier, a camisole, a
corset, three sets of pantaloons, a proto-bra, slip, hosiery, and garters,
Count Wessingame looked down at the mound of linen remaining and remarked,
"Good Lord, woman, are you nothing but skivvies?"

"In the early dawn, Lady Bartlett stood naked at the foot of the bed, her
fists clenched in rage as she looked down at the slimy, but satiated,
amphibian in her bed and screamed: "You lied!"
```

## Thread: Dynamic move-statement support... — 8 this month / 13 total

### Dynamic move-statement support...

- **From:** Fooer <member10382@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T14:29:23+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3041390.1056551668@dbforums.com>`

```

Hi all,

I�m pretty much a COBOL newbie and wondering if there is a support for
something like this in "standard" COBOL:

move "nameOfADeclaredVariable" to strDestination
move value to strDestination

Thanks in advance for your input!
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic move-statement support...

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T16:53:06+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<SrkKa.26354$bRt.20132@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<3041390.1056551668@dbforums.com>`

```
In Cobol, I haven't found any reflection mechanism to move
"nameOfADeclaredVariable" to strDestination,
but to move value to strDestination is pretty popular if you didn't specify
your variable as one of the compacked
formats. Cobol is charater-based. Everything except compacked is a string of
fixed/declared size without end marker \0.


Thomas
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic move-statement support...

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T23:06:17+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JVpKa.2631$BM.1541114@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`
- **References:** `<3041390.1056551668@dbforums.com> <Dh-cnbKd5MCNZGSjXTWJiA@giganews.com>`

```
"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:Dh-cnbKd5MCNZGSjXTWJiA@giganews.com...
>
> "Fooer" <member10382@dbforums.com> wrote in message
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> Saves a step.



Nope, what he is trying to do is build a COBOL statement to be executed
while the program is running.

As in..
 MOVE "WS-VARNAME" to X
MOVE "Z" to X
DISPLAY WS-VARNAME  <=== would show "Z"


Nope, cannot do that with any COBOL.

But you can use pointers to do pretty much the same thing...
  MOVE SPACE TO WS-VARNAME
  SET ADDRESS OF WS-VARNAME TO ADDRESS OF X
  MOVE "HELLO WORLD" TO X
  DISPLAY WS-VARNAME   << displays "HELLO WORLD"

(I might be off a little on the syntax, but that's close)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Dynamic move-statement support...

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.org>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T21:37:26-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bddm98$jh7$1@ngspool-d02.news.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3041390.1056551668@dbforums.com> <Dh-cnbKd5MCNZGSjXTWJiA@giganews.com> <JVpKa.2631$BM.1541114@newssrv26.news.prodigy.com>`

```
> Nope, cannot do that with any COBOL.
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

This does work but it should be noted both ws-varname and X
must be in the Linkage Section. You can't do this with
Working-Storage fields.

Over the years I've often thought this kind of thing would be
useful. Most recently I had an ODBC project where it FETCHed
a slug of columns into a buffer. If I could have formulated a
data-name from the fetched column names and used it as a "data-name
in a data-name" I could have avoided a gigantic 3 page evaluate.
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic move-statement support...

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T18:23:47-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bddaua$1kf$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3041390.1056551668@dbforums.com>`

```
Without knowing your "full requirements", it is hard to say how to do this
in COBOL (as there is no "natural" / "built-in" way to do it in COBOL - as
there is in many programming languages).  However, depending upon how many
"variables" you might want to populate, you MIGHT find the following
technique useful,

01  Lots-of-Variables.
     05  Named-Var1   Pic X(10).
     05  Named-Var2   Pic X(20).
     05  Named-Var3   Pic X.
           ...
     05  Named-VarN  Pic X(99).

01  strDestination    Pic X(9).
      88  When-Var1  Value "Named-Var1".
      88  When-Var2  Value "Named-Var2".
      88  When-Var3  Value "Named-Var3".
            ...
      88  When-VarN  Value "Named-VarN".

Move  "Named-Var1" to strDestination
Evaluate True
    When When-Var1
        move value to Named-Var2
    When When-Var2
        move value to Named-Var2
    When When-Var3
        move value to Named-Var3
            ...
    When When-VarN
        move value to Named-VarN
     When Other
         Display "unknown receiving field"
  End-Evaluate
```

#### ↳ Re: Dynamic move-statement support...

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T03:16:43+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<vAtKa.29185$bRt.11215@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`
- **References:** `<3041390.1056551668@dbforums.com>`

```
I don't think to move "nameOfADeclaredVariable" to strDestination is a good
question.
In any language, variable is referred by name. You only know the symbolic
name before compiling. At running time there is nothing for the name of a
variable, it is an address in memory. It is useless normally. Do you want to
use naming convention to simulate OO inheritance?

I think you are trying to know the type of an variable at running time.
Because there is no polymorphism, every Cobol variable has a unique type and
can be determined statically at compiling time. But for OO language (Java,
C++ and C++), it is quite different.

Thomas


"Fooer" <member10382@dbforums.com> wrote in message
news:3041390.1056551668@dbforums.com...
>
> Hi all,
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Posted via http://dbforums.com
```

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3046992.1056645767@dbforums.com>`

## Thread: ADF and SAS? — 2 msgs

### ADF and SAS?

- **From:** "Thomas A. Li" <tli@corporola.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T16:46:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<rlkKa.26352$bRt.8125@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
Where can I find the IBM ADF manual/specification and SAS
manual/specification
for IBM mainframe? Any help will be appreciated.


Thomas
```

#### ↳ Re: ADF and SAS?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-25T13:57:17-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdcrak$fu8$1@slb3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<rlkKa.26352$bRt.8125@news01.bloor.is.net.cable.rogers.com>`

```
ADF is a (fairly) ancient software (if you are talking about "Application
Development Facility").  It was an IMS "application generator".  It was
(eventually) replaced by CSP which eventually became "VisualAge Generator".

At:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/ezedes02/10.3.4.2

there is some 1994 (dated) material on

 "10.3.4.2 From IMSADF II Transaction Driver to Cross System Product
Application"

    ***

For SAS, you can start at:

    http://www.sas.com/

and go from there.
```

- **From:** kyaqub <member31937@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T02:37:52+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3044504.1056595072@dbforums.com>`

```

Greetings:

               Well I am new to cobol.  I am using the MSCobol 4.5
               compiler on the Win 2000 platform. I have written a
               simple cobol application to just display a message on the
               console. When I try to compile the program using the
               cobol.exe, it gives me the error:

 "Cobol Runtime Library Not Installed"

 Please help me in removing this problem.

 Regards
 Khalid
```

#### ↳ Re: MSCobol Compiler

- **From:** "Lorne Sunley" <lsunley@mb.sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T04:22:41+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JMPlV5b5VFnN-pn2-DuxLvouSzuz8@h24-79-79-72.wp.shawcable.net>`
- **References:** `<3044504.1056595072@dbforums.com>`

```
On Thu, 26 Jun 2003 02:37:52 UTC, kyaqub <member31937@dbforums.com> 
wrote:

> 
> Greetings:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Try using the FORCEDOS command to run the compiler. The MS 4.5 COBOL 
compiler is a "dual mode" DOS and OS/2 program. You have to force it 
run in DOS to get it to work on Windows 2000. Or, as an alternative, 
you can install OS/2 or eComStation http://www.ecomstation.com
```

- **From:** kyaqub <member31937@dbforums.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3044693.1056603136@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<3044504.1056595072@dbforums.com>`

```

Thanks Sunley:

Well I able to compile my cobol source file by the use of FORCEDOS
command. Now how I the obj file. So where to go from here? How do I
link this file and make an executable which can display my message in
the program.

 Regards
 Khalid
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MSCobol Compiler

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T12:30:29-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306261130.7b6c2e42@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3044504.1056595072@dbforums.com> <3044693.1056603136@dbforums.com>`

```
kyaqub <member31937@dbforums.com> wrote

> Well I able to compile my cobol source file by the use of FORCEDOS
> command. Now how I the obj file. So where to go from here? How do I
> link this file and make an executable which can display my message in
> the program.

  LINK program;

If you are using DISPLAY AT or screen section you may need to use:

  LINK program + adis+adiskey+adisint;

If you use indexed files then add +extfh.

Many more options exist for how these are built see 'System Reference' manual.
```

#### ↳ Re: MSCobol Compiler

```
Souhaibb wrote:

> I have encountered an error message in cobol as mentioned below.
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> of compilations.

Let me guess: some form of MicroFocus.

ADIS is the Accept/Display module required when using XOpen screen handling 
such as screen section or DISPLAY ... AT.

With my vesrions of MF I need to link in somehow the ADIS, ADISINIT and 
ADISKEYS objects.  See your System manual for exact details on how to do 
this for how you are building systems.

What did you mean by 'copied the ADIS file' ?  Copying isn't what is 
required.
```

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)

```
Souhaibb <member31957@dbforums.com> wrote

> This problem is related to MS Cobol 4.5.
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> still not able to execute the program.

You cannot execute a .OBJ file.  Copying the ADIS.OBJ files will not
work, you need to LINK them into your program or link them together to
make an ADIS.EXE.
 
> The below mentioned error still reamains.

> Can anybody suggest that how to connect / link the ADIS, ADISINIT etc.
> files to my program. ( I don't have the System manual)

LINK program+ADIS+ADISINIT+ADISKEYS;

How hard is that ?  What message are you getting ?
 
> In addition, please advice a way on how to use the LINE, COLUMN command
> in MS Cobol 4.5. As at this moment I cannot use this command either with
> Display or Accept Command.

Is it that you don't know how to write the source ?  Does the compiler
give you errors ?  Or is it that you can't use it because the running
says it can't find the ADIS.

We can't see your screen displays, although you may think they are
just gibberish they do actually mean something.

We also can't see your source code.  Post it if you don't know why the
compiler is giving errors.
```

```
mfcobol2002 <marcos_as@terra.com.br> wrote:

>
>Respected Gentlemen,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…

Marcos:

Our COBOL sp2 and Thin Client solutions provide you with COBOL-based
server applications and PC Windows client-side user interface display.

Because the COBOL application resides entirely on the server, the
method of data/file handling is completely up to you.  Screen data is
moved to working storage and our software manages the transfer of the
data and subsequent display on the client.

Servers supported include Windows, UNIX (all derivatives) and VAX/VMS.

Please see www.flexus.com/products.html

and

www.flexus.com/thinclient.html

for more information.



Bob Wolfe
~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
When replying by e-mail, make sure that you correct the e-mail address.
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```
