<a id="t-e205c562f7"></a>
## Thread: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL — 6 this month / 9 total

_Thread started [2002-06](2002-06.md#t-e205c562f7)._

#### ↳ Re: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL

- **From:** FIRESTARTER <member31992@dbforums.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T14:17:10+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3046046.1056637030@dbforums.com>`
- **References:** `<29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com>`

```

How do you do XOR in COBOL. I am trying to do CRC in COBOL for a project
I am working on which needs XOR, and on Mainframe it kinda tough as
there is no XOR function. If you can share the info with me it will be
really great.
Thanks.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T15:55:05+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efb0e14.291307423@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com> <3046046.1056637030@dbforums.com>`

```
FIRESTARTER <member31992@dbforums.com> wrote:

>
>How do you do XOR in COBOL. I am trying to do CRC in COBOL for a project
>I am working on which needs XOR, and on Mainframe it kinda tough as
>there is no XOR function. If you can share the info with me it will be
>really great.

* Compute c = a xor b
  01  unqualified-variables. 
       05  a.
             10  a-n    comp  pic 9(2).
       05  b. 
             10  b-n    comp  pic 9(2).
       05  c.
             10  c-n    comp  pic 9(2)..
       05  a-bit         comp  pic 9(2).
       05  b-bit         comp  pic 9(2)

     move zero to c-n
     perform 8 times
         divide 2 into a-n giving a-n remainder a-bit
         divide 2 into b-n giving b-n remainder b-bit
         if  b-bit = 1
             compute a-bit = 0 - a-bit
         end-if
         compute c-n = (c-n * 2) + a-bit
     end-perform.

Think of b as a mask or sieve. If the b bit is off, the a bit passes through
unchanged. If the b bit is on, the a bit is flipped.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T16:01:15+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efb1866.293949773@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com> <3046046.1056637030@dbforums.com>`

```
Whoops, my bit flip was wrong. Make that compute a-bit = a-bit - 1
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T11:57:33-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdf8m5$mq7$1@slb9.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com> <3046046.1056637030@dbforums.com>`

```
You don't say which "mainframe" - but assuming a current IBM mainframe
environment, have you looked at the LE "bit manipulation" callable services?

See:

   http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/CEEA3130/1.2

and the routines pointed to from there?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: GETTING THE ASCII VALUE FOR CHARACTERS(NUMERIC & ALPHABET) IN COBOL

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T12:42:41-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdfbaq$6n1$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<29b57f17.0206190124.3073f865@posting.google.com> <3046046.1056637030@dbforums.com>`

```
Your subject and your note don't seem (to me) to match.  If you are
interested in getting the decimal ordinal number (range of 1 to 256 - not 0
to 255) of any EBCDIC character in an EBCDIC environment, look at the ORD
intrinsic function.

If you are interested in converting an EBCDIC "string" to an ASCII string in
a (current) IBM mainframe, check out the sample code at:

 http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.6.6.4

See my other note, if what you really want is "bit-twiddling" capabilities
in IBM mainframes.
```

<a id="t-066e24aad9"></a>
## Thread: Help with C-ISAM & D-ISAM — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help with C-ISAM & D-ISAM

- **From:** "NO.bruceradtke.SPAM@NO.earthlink.net.SPAM" <bruceradtke@NO.earthlink.net.SPAM>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T14:34:57+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lwDKa.13363$C83.1293473@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Hello All,

What are C-ISAM and D-ISAM?

I've been asked to extract some data from a Microfocus database.  I'm not a
Cobol programmer and am trying to get up to speed on the file formats, etc.
I've seen C-ISAM and D-ISAM used in some old news postings.

How could I tell what format the MF application is using?  I don't have the
source code.   What is usually used by default?

I'm doing all of this on SCO Unix as well....


Thanks in advance.

Regards,
bruce
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with C-ISAM & D-ISAM

- **From:** riplin@Azonic.co.nz (Richard)
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T12:22:35-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<217e491a.0306261122.999a82b@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<lwDKa.13363$C83.1293473@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
"NO.bruceradtke.SPAM@NO.earthlink.net.SPAM"
<bruceradtke@NO.earthlink.net.SPAM> wrote

> What are C-ISAM and D-ISAM?

C-ISAM is an indexed file system used by Infomix database system and
also adopted by X-Open as a 'standard' implementation of indexed
files.

D-ISAM is a compatible system that was cheaper.
 
> I've been asked to extract some data from a Microfocus database.  I'm not a
> Cobol programmer and am trying to get up to speed on the file formats, etc.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'm doing all of this on SCO Unix as well....

It is quite possible that the MF system used C-ISAM compatible indexed
files on SCO Unix. This means that the files will be in two parts - a
data file containing fixed size records each with a cr/lf terminator
(or possibly lf only on some systems) and an index file that is
filename.idx.  The index can be ignored if you just want the read the
data records.  Read fixed length and ignore any records that do not
have cr/lf as the last two bytes.

The content of the record is determined by the application and is not
possible to know exactly without source code.  Inspection may allow
you to guess at much of it.
```

#### ↳ Re: Help with C-ISAM & D-ISAM

- **From:** "Jim Morcombe" <jim@byronics.com.au>
- **Date:** 2003-06-27T14:11:05+08:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdgmna$qcm$1@yeppa.connect.com.au>`
- **References:** `<lwDKa.13363$C83.1293473@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
Bruce

C-ISAM is a file indexing library developped by Informix.
D-ISAM is a clone of C-ISAM.

MicroFocus used a modified early version of C-ISAM to handle the indexing of
their "ISAM" files.

I don't think you have to use C-ISAM.

If you look in the back of the MicroFocus Cobol system reference, you will
see an appendix full
of file layouts.  You can just ignore the File Indexes and open the data
file as an ordinary C file and read it.

Jim


NO.bruceradtke.SPAM@NO.earthlink.net.SPAM
<bruceradtke@NO.earthlink.net.SPAM> wrote in message
news:lwDKa.13363$C83.1293473@newsread1.prod.itd.earthlink.net...
> Hello All,
>

a

<a id="t-27f7c11fc8"></a>
## Thread: Telon segloop — 2 msgs

### Telon segloop

- **From:** rao_pmp@yahoo.com (telonq)
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T10:46:25-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<b38aa307.0306260946.202d2201@posting.google.com>`

```
Hi,
Any idea how to create a segloop in telon with two rows per record?

like

1. xxxx yyy zzz
   123455 7890
2. xxxx yyy zzz
   123456 7865 

TIA
```

#### ↳ Re: Telon segloop

- **From:** "Don Leahy" <don.leahy@nospamwhatever.leacom.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T15:47:01-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M4IKa.4855$Fe3.816192@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<b38aa307.0306260946.202d2201@posting.google.com>`

```
"telonq" <rao_pmp@yahoo.com> wrote in message
news:b38aa307.0306260946.202d2201@posting.google.com...
> Hi,
> Any idea how to create a segloop in telon with two rows per record?
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> TIA

Try the following:

SEGLOOP OUTIN,TYPE=FILE,INCRE=(02,02,02,02)....etc

This INCREment definition will give you four groups of two lines per page.

HTH
```

## Thread: Java vs. COBOL II — 9 msgs

### Java vs. COBOL II

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-26T19:51:58-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Sc-cnS0x99qzCGajXTWJig@giganews.com>`

```
Here's one more slight difference.

A federal appeals court today (26 June) unanimously overturned a lower court
injunction requiring Micros~1 to distribute Sun's Java with WinXP. As a
result, Micros~1 will NOT distribute the latest version of JAVA.

The appeals court also upheld another injunction prohibiting Micros~1 from
infringing on Sun's copyrights by distributing Micros~1's version of Java.

Micros~1, therefore, will not distribute ANY version of Java with WinXP or
its successors.

Story at:
http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/articles/A36061-2003Jun26.html

Of course all this may change when the Sun/Micros~1 case gets to court
(maybe three years).

So, then, here's the difference between COBOL and Java: COBOL has a future.
```

> Micros~1, therefore, will not distribute ANY version of Java with WinXP or
> its successors.
> 
> So, then, here's the difference between COBOL and Java: COBOL has a
> future.

What version of Cobol is MS distributing then ?

In other news (www.news.com):

"""In recent months, Sun finally began to succeed in its yearslong effort 
to bypass Microsoft as a distributor of Java by getting PC makers to 
include Java with their products. Earlier in June, the top two PC 
makers--Dell Computer and Hewlett-Packard--signed deals to bundle Java on 
their desktop and laptop computers.

Dell and HP both said they made the move because they needed to ensure that 
customers wouldn't have any difficulties browsing Web sites that use Java.

Sun Chief Executive Scott McNealy said the HP and Dell deals have started 
"a very interesting domino effect," leading other PC manufacturers to 
consider a similar inclusion of Java."""
```

#### ↳ Re: Java vs. COBOL II

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-27T15:57:50+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efbc13c_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<Sc-cnS0x99qzCGajXTWJig@giganews.com>`

```

"JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
news:Sc-cnS0x99qzCGajXTWJig@giganews.com...
> Here's one more slight difference.
>
> A federal appeals court today (26 June) unanimously overturned a lower
court
> injunction requiring Micros~1 to distribute Sun's Java with WinXP. As a
> result, Micros~1 will NOT distribute the latest version of JAVA.

I was just getting to like XP (it really is VERY good) when I heard that MS
require you to buy another copy, if the Motherboard you are running it on
fries... So now I have to wonder what future there is for XP... Who's going
to buy an OS that is NOT transferrable when you have a disaster?  (Win2K,
apparently has no such restriction...)

Given there could well be a question over XP, I can't see Java being
disadvantaged by not being included with it. I'm running it (XP), and my
Browser (IE6) seems to be Java enabled... (I have never specifically
obtained a Java plugin for it, but it runs applets OK...)

>
> The appeals court also upheld another injunction prohibiting Micros~1 from
> infringing on Sun's copyrights by distributing Micros~1's version of Java.
>
Ah, Goodbye JScript... No big deal. I use JavaScript anyway (VBScript
occasionally) and so do most of the Web type people I correspond with.


> Micros~1, therefore, will not distribute ANY version of Java with WinXP or
> its successors.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sounds good to me. People who want Java  (and that would be most of the
World, Jerry...<G>) only have to download it and run it as a plugin. That
was what we did before MS incorporated it in the Browser.

This will certainly not affect the future of Java as you seem to think. It
is NOT just a language for writing Applets to animate your
Browser...(although that is probably the single most important thing that
brought it to prominence).

> So, then, here's the difference between COBOL and Java: COBOL has a
future.
>
ROFL!

A totally illogical conclusion (though I wish it were true...)

Java will continue with or without MS. Even if Sun went broke tomorrow, Java
would continue. You haven't grasped that there is a Java community who have
an interest in what they see as THEIR language. It is a model that COBOL can
only aspire to emulate... (and sadly, hasn't...)

The real point here is that we need both COBOL and Java. It isn't a contest.

Pete.
```

This material is NFR but otherwise unrestriced. It can be used for
demonstration, training, learning, development, or production within your
company. You just can't move it outside (sell or give away - or use SQL
Server on your web site).

You have to register as a "Microsoft Partner," but you don't have to sell
Micros~1 products. We don't. We are a "Developer" partner.

You could fry 10 motherboards before you'd have to call.
```

Pete,

With all due respect - there's just a ton of misinformation here. 
Please let me explain....

"Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz> wrote in message news:<3efbc13c_5@news.athenanews.com>...
> "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com> wrote in message
> news:Sc-cnS0x99qzCGajXTWJig@giganews.com...
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

XP allows a reinstall and reactivation.  In fact while MS doesn't
publish the rules, there are plenty of internet sites out there that
explain what has to change etc.  You actually can change several
components and not have it act up on you.  In addition, if there is a
problem, you can call MS and they will be happy to add another
activation to your queue if necessary.  This is not as draconian as it
might first appear.

Now re:Java - there are many ways that a JVM can make it into your
machine, and if it's there, Java will work fine - but I can tell from
your next comment that you may not know what is Java and what is not.

> >
> > The appeals court also upheld another injunction prohibiting Micros~1 from
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

JScript - AKA Java Script is not Java, is not owned by Sun and has
nothing to do with Sun.  It was invented by Netscape.  It's supported
by MS browswers (though they would like you to use VB Script instead),
and does not require a JVM or anything remotely Java related to run.


> 
> Sounds good to me. People who want Java  (and that would be most of the
> World, Jerry...<G>) only have to download it and run it as a plugin. That
> was what we did before MS incorporated it in the Browser.
>

Java is not a plugin.  If you want to talk true Netscape plugins - MS
disabled those in it's browser with IE 5.5 service pack 2.  Now you
need to use ActiveX controls.  Nothing whatsoever to do with Java or
the JVM though.
 
> A totally illogical conclusion (though I wish it were true...)
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> only aspire to emulate... (and sadly, hasn't...)

What are you saying?  Java is OWNED kit and kaboodle by Sun.  Unlike
COBOL which is not owned by anyone.  Sun can make Java 3 require a fee
if they desire - and Java is hardly "Free" - there are license fees
required under certain circumstances.  If things get really tight at
sun - they will use their Java stranglehold to get some more revenue. 
They OWN Java.  If Sun goes away - so does Java.

> 
> The real point here is that we need both COBOL and Java. It isn't a contest.
> 

That's true - it's not a contest.
```

a

MS

going

(Win2K,

…[13 more quoted lines elided]…

That I didn't know.

> Now re:Java - there are many ways that a JVM can make it into your
> machine, and if it's there, Java will work fine - but I can tell from

>
One of us certainly doesn't...<G>
> > >

from
> > > infringing on Sun's copyrights by distributing Micros~1's version of
Java.
> > >
> > Ah, Goodbye JScript... No big deal. I use JavaScript anyway (VBScript
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> nothing to do with Sun.  It was invented by Netscape.

You are wrong that JScript and JavaScript are one and the same. They're not,
and my post reflected that. JScript is the MS "answer" to JavaScript.

I never said JScript was owned by Sun (I know it isn't...). I understand it
to be a MS product and my post reflects that. I believe JScript is the MS
implementation of JavaScript, which was invented by Netscape as you say. I
am well aware of the difference between JavaScript and Java, having used
both extensively for a long time now. I also know that JScript is NOT
JavaScript, as you say it is. There is actually no connection and only a
passing similarity between JavaScript and Java. JScript and JavaScript are
very similar because JScript was designed to be.(The main differences are in
the area of security).

 Before declaring anybody wrong (espcially someone I have as much respect
for as I have for you, Thane) I did some homework. I refer you to the
following history (which corroborates my understanding):

====== quote from Jim Parrish's Web Site on the history of JavaScript
=====================

The year is 1995.

Netscape has just formed an alliance with Sun Microsystems. Java, a new
object-oriented language, is Sun's new baby and Netscape plans to support
not only embedding Java applets, small programs that can live on a web page,
but wishes the ability to script the applets as well. To accomplish this
goal they came up with LiveScript. This was Javascript's first incarnation.
It lasted all of about 6 months. In what some have called a pretty dumb
move, Netscape decided to capitalize on the name recognition which Sun's new
Java language was receiving. Thus LiveScript was renamed Javascript.

Of course, Javascript is neither the same as Java, nor vice-versa, however
the confusion inevitable with naming the two different languages so closely
has not really hampered their acceptance in the community at large. For one
real good discussion on what the difference is at a technical level, look no
farther than this document at Netscape - Javascript 1.5 Core Guide Details
of the Object Model.

We now come to a very important date, December 4, 1995, which is the date
Javascript was officially announced and released by the Netscape Sun
alliance.

Client-Side good, Server-Side bad
Javascript quickly gained acceptance among the web literati. Authors the
world over found they could manipulate webpages and do wonderous things to
astound users. Image flips, document verification and all sorts of magic
just waited to be exploited to amaze the customers. Javascript truly seemed
the stuff of legend.

On the other hand, a band of renegades attempted to make Javascript work on
the server-side. This did not go over so well. Other technologies, CGI, ASP,
JSP, PHP all combined to make Javascript, for all intent and purpose, fail
on the server-side. Yes, I know you can write ASP pages in Javascript ;) .
What I am talking about here is literal server-side Javascript.

Thus, while one could find Javascript used all over the client portion of a
website, one had to look quite hard to find it in use for the server and it
remains so to this day.

Yeah, it's the same, but better
Microsoft was none too happy about Netscape's success with the new language.
Let's face it, hard to run all the browsers in the world if you have to use
a Netscape controlled language to make it happen. So...they made their own
language.

Was it Javascript?

Was it like Javascript?

Nope.

It was VBScript.

WHAT???

No really, it's just like Javascript-only better. Or so we were led to
believe. However, truth be told VBScript has several BIG drawbacks. It does
not work on any browser other than Internet Explorer. It is based on Visual
Basic, which is a Microsoft language and that means it does not run on
anything but Windows (I know that is not true now, silly). Three, it comes
from Microsoft, which in some people's eyes is enough to doom it just by
default. This triad of doom secured VBScript's fate. In the present day,
VBScript is about to go away. Yes. Away. Forever. Well, hopefully. Perhaps.
Ah, right let's just move on shall we...

So, after that somewhat underwhelming debut, Microsoft came out with their
version of Javascript-JScript.

OK, let's try this again
JScript appeared on the web scene on July 16, 1996.

JScript is another version of Javascript. It is written from scratch by
Microsoft and is meant to be not only compatible with Netscape's version
(the original), but also to support the new standards put forth by the W3C.
Though it failed pretty badly at both of these things initially, it did a
darn good job considering and by now it is quite a decent language.

=============================== end quote ==================================


> It's supported
> by MS browswers (though they would like you to use VB Script instead),
> and does not require a JVM or anything remotely Java related to run.

Yes, I know that.
>
>
> >
> > Sounds good to me. People who want Java  (and that would be most of the
> > World, Jerry...<G>) only have to download it and run it as a plugin.
That
> > was what we did before MS incorporated it in the Browser.
> >
>
> Java is not a plugin.

Not now, it isn't. It was if you wanted to run Java 2 with IE 4. If MS drop
it, it probably will be again.

To run Java 2 applets under IE 4.4 it was necessary to download the Java
plug-in from Sun's site. If you tried to run an Applet on a Browser that
didn't have the plug-in, you were offered the chance to download it from
Sun's special site. This applied to both Netscape and IE. It was definitely
a plug-in and the site address was: http//java.sun.com/products/plug-in.

Java is not a plugin in the sense that you can download it free from Sun and
develop applications (and Beans since Java2) with it, without ever writing
an Applet or running anything in the Browser.

So far, you haven't told me anything I didn't know and you are wrong about
JScript. I think you misread my post, Thane.

>  If you want to talk true Netscape plugins - MS
> disabled those in it's browser with IE 5.5 service pack 2.  Now you
> need to use ActiveX controls.  Nothing whatsoever to do with Java or
> the JVM though.
>
OK. That I didn't know. I have no problem with using ActiveX.

> > A totally illogical conclusion (though I wish it were true...)
> >
> > Java will continue with or without MS. Even if Sun went broke tomorrow,
Java
> > would continue. You haven't grasped that there is a Java community who
have
> > an interest in what they see as THEIR language. It is a model that COBOL
can
> > only aspire to emulate... (and sadly, hasn't...)
>
> What are you saying?  Java is OWNED kit and kaboodle by Sun.

I am saying that, while Sun OWN Java, there is a very active community of
Java developers who would not let the language die simply because Sun went
broke or was taken over. The developers see it as THEIR language (even
though they know it is copyright to Sun) and they participate actively in
improving it. Sun are happy to let this happen and have never (to my
knowledge) enforced the fact that they actually own it. Instead they have
provided support to the developer network and encouraged the participation.
I still receive a monthly Java Developer Newsletter sponsored by Sun with
interesting code samples and articles about proposed changes to the
language. I have no such thing from Fujitsu, my COBOL vendor of choice...

It makes me sad that the current keepers of the COBOL standard have done
nothing similar either. Read what I said above. I haven't changed it.


 > Unlike
> COBOL which is not owned by anyone.  Sun can make Java 3 require a fee
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Total supposition on your part. There is no evidence to indicate that Java
will not continue to be free and there is no evidence that if Sun goes away,
so will Java. I don't believe that. I hope it is never put to the test, but
I don't believe the established Java base of over 10,000,000 programmers is
going to say..."OK, guess we better learn COBOL...." (or anything else...).
You can speculate all you like Thane, but unless you are actually involved
with Java you cannot begin to understand how passionate the user base are
about it.

Personally, I think Sun SHOULD charge for Java, especially for the
commmercial use of it.

> >
> > The real point here is that we need both COBOL and Java. It isn't a
contest.
> >
>
> That's true - it's not a contest.

Good, so we can agree on that then and stay friends...<G>

Pete.
```

About 16 months ago we released our Kobol compiler and IDE for Linux
and Windows, a sub $75 per developer product with no run time costs. 
We've had a tremendous amount of growth and positive reaction, for
which we are grateful, and we've listened to what our customers have
asked for, and one thing they asked for was the ability to use our

##### ↳ ↳ comp.lang.cobol.moderated

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-28T23:11:20+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3efe17bd.183559318@news.optonline.net>`
- **References:** `<3efd7cc8.143883985@news.optonline.net> <bdkqd3$bvf$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

>I have already received one suggestion that maybe it is time that CLC become
>"moderated" (at least until existing problems are resolved).  I plan on
>starting a new thread on this topic.

Hopefully my experience with a moderated group will be helpful.

Some years ago I was an active participant in rec.food.veg (RFV), a high-volume
forum about vegetarianism. One guy entered and pissed off everyone with
irrational arguments, many posts, unresponsiveness to rebuttal, dogmatism, etc.
He was Jai Maharaj, who was listed at the time in Top Twenty Netkooks. 

Animal rights activists, who call themselves Ethical Vegetarians and others
call The Religious Right, normally stand on the brink of intolerance.  Jai
pushed them over the edge. They lobbied for and eventually got
rec.food.veg.moderated. I say eventually because it took 3-4 weeks of USENET
bureaucracy -- writing a formal proposal, calling for votes, etc. 

Everyone should understand that the process does not "make CLC a moderated
group." It leaves the original CLC intact and adds an additional group named
CLC.moderated. Users may read and post to either or both.

What happened is a few of the goodie-goodies went off to RFV.moderated, where
they exchanged banal pleasantries and 'supported' each other by preaching to the
choir with no possibility of contradiction. The moderated group had no
substance. Its volume was less than 10% the original RFV, which kept going as
before. When I checked a year or two later, RFV.moderated was gone.

In my opinion the only appropriate reason for moderation is when the topic is so
abhorrent to the general population that the group will be overrun by hecklers.
Censoring reasonable opposition is not a valid reason. Silencing kooks is not
even a valid reason. USENET history shows that such attempts are inevitably
short-lived. Legitimate topics are:

..Religion -- atheism, witchcraft
..Quasi religion -- astrology, paranormal, metaphysics, new age
..Sexual preferences -- gay (nearly all are moderated), pedophelia
..Business models -- multi-level marketing
..Diseases associated with sex -- herpes
..Paranoia -- conspiracy theories
..Emotion-based legal/social -- abortion
..Borderline legality -- drugs, 2600

Physics and math attract more kooks than most people realize. They used to be
moderated, but are no longer. The only moderated programming forums are C, C++
and Perl. The unmoderated/moderated ratio of messages in C++ is 2:1, C is 267/5,
Perl is 20/3 (your newsserver's retention may vary). The latter two will be gone
soon. 

Without pontificating about the ethics of censorship, my argument is pragmatic:
it is unsuccessful unless the topic warrants it. COBOL is not such a topic.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.moderated

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-29T13:14:36+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5gltfv8931dcmkqpjta485fm0msskpbl3e@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3efd7cc8.143883985@news.optonline.net> <bdkqd3$bvf$1@slb0.atl.mindspring.net> <3efe17bd.183559318@news.optonline.net>`

```
On Sat, 28 Jun 2003 23:11:20 GMT, robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
wrote:
Snip
>
>Everyone should understand that the process does not "make CLC a moderated
>group." It leaves the original CLC intact and adds an additional group named
>CLC.moderated. Users may read and post to either or both.
>
Mr. Wagner. Please do not post incorrect information.
When changing a existing group to moderated the name does not change,
and it remains the same group.

Sometimes what is done is that a new group is created, because the
number of votes was not enough to "convert" the group or because the
persons wishing to proceed to a moderated group decided since
inception that they would have a new group, as it is a lot easier to
create a new group than it is to change an existing one (different set
of rules).
Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: comp.lang.cobol.moderated

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T03:32:44+00:00

## Thread: Should CLC become a moderated NG??? — 25 this month / 32 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help) · [`Meta: FAQs, group policy, charter`](topics.md#meta)_

### Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-28T14:34:07-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdkqjg$p2b$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I know that many of the past active and helpful participants in CLC have
already dropped out - since the recent rise of one (no longer new)
participant who has disrupted the "normal" useful and friendly ("debating is
NOT the purpose) nature of comp.lang.cobol.

The question has been raised to me in private email as to whether *I*
(personally) think that CLC should now become a MODERATED newsgroup.  I
certainly have MIXED feelings on this, but can see some advantages (both to
old and future participants) in such a change.

How do others feel about this (as an initial inquiry)?  The required steps
take a much more "official" set of actions, but before I/we consider it, I
did want to hear what others feel about this.
```

#### ↳ Re: Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>

```
Bill,

moderation of this Newsgroup will NOT stop people disrupting it if they are
Hell Bent on doing so.

I would need to see what benefits you think will be attained before I could
acquiese.

It also raises the question of who will do the moderating.

Another point in passing...

People have dropped out. That is regrettable, but it is the actions of those
people and not the group.

No one HAS to drop out any more than anyone HAS to stay in.

It is a personal choice.

The fact that people drop out is not a factor in deciding whether the group
should be changed.

I believe there is a real problem that needs to be addressed and I don't
think moderation is the way to address it.

However, if you have arguments that could persuade me (or anyone else)
otherwise, bring 'em on.

Pete.

#### ↳ Re: Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-28T20:25:55-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<I46cnXTq56W-3WOjXTWJiQ@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bdkqjg$p2b$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> I know that many of the past active and helpful participants in CLC
> have already dropped out - since the recent rise of one (no longer
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> consider it, I did want to hear what others feel about this.

For those who think invective and contention has risen to an unacceptable
level, I suggest they spend some time on groups where it is the norm. Any
political group, any group dealing with abortion, gays, guns, yard gnomes,
or (especially) spam.

This group is a haven of peace and sensibility compared.

Inasmuch as the bulk of vituperation stems from one person (or a group
operating under one name), it's easy enough to simply block that one person.
I, having more experience in detecting nuclear-level vanity than most,
blocked this pompous ass long ago.

So, for those disturbed by certain elements, try blocking these evil-doers
(as our president calls them) first. If the collective blood pressure does
not fall back to acceptable levels, we can sample other possibilities.

On mark against moderated groups: response time. Often the moderator (and
here I'm thinking of "misc.legal.moderated") is off in jail for drunk and
disorderly conduct so requests for guidance go unrequited for too long.
```

#### ↳ Re: Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-28T20:52:37-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3EFE4665.5020202@Netscape.net>`
- **References:** `<bdkqjg$p2b$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> How do others feel about this (as an initial inquiry)?  The required steps
> take a much more "official" set of actions, but before I/we consider it, I
> did want to hear what others feel about this.

In a word - no.

As a relative newcomer to this group, I've sat and watched goings-on 
here for several months now.  I've pissed some people off, no doubt, by 
my tendency for my fingers to, at times, outpace my brain.  However, 
through my time here, I've seen good in every regular poster here.

  - You - You absolutely -know- the COBOL standards (I think you 
probably have the 2002 and 1985 standards memorized ;> ), and usually 
take the time, when a question is asked, to explain the expected 
behavior or proper syntax.  You also provide references and links which 
I've found very instructive.

  - Robert Wagner - I believe his first post to me was a comment about 
how much he enjoyed Montgomery when he worked here.  Through the 
newsgroup and in private e-mail, he's shown me some really cool stuff. 
He has a tendency to make generalizations, which seems to annoy other 
posters here.  Sometimes he's flat wrong - but so are a lot of other 
regular posters, who don't catch the flak for it he does.  More on what 
I've seen of how people deal with him below...

  - Pete Dashwood - Called me a troll in his first post to me.  :) 
Since then, I've learned an incredible amount of information from him, 
such as how to access a database from PC-based COBOL, and making Windows 
applications with COBOL.  Now people at work don't understand how I can 
make a GUI Windows program in COBOL...

  - Richard Plinston - Defended my ignorance to Pete's response to my 
first post.  Although a lot of the sorting discussions were a bit over 
my head, one thing I gleaned from that discussion was looking at object 
code listings from a compile - something that I hadn't ever thought to 
do.  I'm still nowhere near proficient in assembler-level languages, but 
I'm learning.

  - DD (His Highness) - Made me think twice before ever using a plural 
pronoun, and making sure I have an antecedent for that pronoun.  Also, 
while his "cite?" responses can become irritating at times, they have 
encouraged me to go do some digging, which helped me learn.

  - Howard Brazee - He suggested the structure of placing the "And" or 
"Or" on the next line in front of second and subsequent conditions of an 
If statement.  Using this technique, I've actually fixed two problems in 
programs at work just by restructuring the If statement, and finding 
that once it was restructured, it wasn't right.

  - Joe Zitzelberger - He's encouraged me, letting me know it's okay to 
have this "youthful exuberance" that sometimes gets me into trouble. 
We've also had some great conversations via e-mail.

Now, I realize that this whole thing has been brought up by the 
urinatory olympiad that's been going on the past couple of weeks.  I 
mentioned above that he has a tendency toward making generalizations. 
   I think back to the comment he made about there being less than a 
dozen widely-used languages, because programmers voted by using them. 
-I- knew what he meant, even if maybe there are more than 12 - several 
people jumped on him over that comment, though.

One way to eliminate generalizations is to clarify - narrow the scope, 
if you will.  But, when he attempted to do that by saying "I meant 
compiled languages", he was accused of changing what he said!  It wasn't 
a change, it was a clarification (and made his number of 12 pretty close 
to true).  Now, whether it was right for him to exclude scripting 
languages is debatable, but it was -his- right to exclude them when 
clarifying what he meant.

I'm not saying that other don't have a right to be annoyed by him - and, 
judging from excepts that have been posted here, he made more than a 
little splash when he first arrived here.  But, even if every post he 
made were completely inaccurate from top to bottom (a feat I have yet to 
see him, or anyone else here accomplish), that alone would not diminish 
the "value" of this newsgroup.  It's difficult to do, but focusing on 
what he says, and not how he says it, would be a great first step.

I know for a time, periods/full-stops were a hotly debated topic.  But, 
the time came when those who were in it took a moment, stepped back, 
gained some perspective, and realized that the case could be made for 
either side, and it's up to each person to choose which case they 
believe is more convincing.  I think this particular go-around with RW 
has reached that point - time to step back and gain a little perspective.

It is -your- right to post whatever you'd like in here.  However, I must 
say I'm dismayed over your latest tactic.  I've tried to stay out of it 
(since I don't really have a dog in this fight), but this question, 
outside the scope of the debate, gives me a chance to say what's been on 
my mind.  I especially thought the last one (at least the last one I've 
received) about how he broke out a report reverse-engineering process 
into 35 components would be allowed to pass.  But rather that focus on a 
disagreement with something substantive in the message, you posted your 
little note, -and- something about checking out Report Writer (which, 
coincidentally, also bears the initials RW).  I'm not aware of how 
Report Writer can help you reverse-engineer reports.

In closing, there are a couple of little trite sayings that have come to 
mind while I've been watching this unfold.  The first is "It take at 
least 2 people to argue."  This means that if CLC is filled with debate, 
it can't all be emanating from a single poster.  Someone has to respond 
- that's how the tax debate between DD and me ended.  He got the last 
word, and that's not really how I would have liked it, but I saw that he 
wasn't going to convince me, and I wasn't going to convince him, so 
what's the use of using up the bandwidth?  :)

The second is "Never argue with a fool - observers may not know the 
difference."  I don't feel that RW is a fool, but that seems to be the 
opinion held by a lot of folks here.  Especially regarding your latest 
tactic with him - what do you think a newcomer is going to think when 
they see what you're doing?  I don't think it's going to enhance your 
credibility - which is a shame, as you're probably the most consistently 
credible person here.
```

My understanding of a moderators role is simply to determine whether a post
is on topic or not. The issue is not debating or accuracy etc but topic. As
most of RWs posts seem to be on topic I don't see how moderation would help
and choosing a moderator could  (IMO probably) result in further
polarization of the group.  In short I don't see what good would be
accomplished and I do see where further harm could result.

I believe more would be accomplished by simply ignoring him all or most of
the time.  It is not his fault if we cannot muster that much self control.

#### ↳ Re: Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** Frederico Fonseca <real-email-in-msg-spam@email.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-29T13:23:14+01:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8dhtfvgdcumh1pa0co5u7q36a8ivjrof2k@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<bdkqjg$p2b$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Sat, 28 Jun 2003 14:34:07 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

snip

>
>The question has been raised to me in private email as to whether *I*
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>did want to hear what others feel about this.

I would normally say no but due to the last evolution of posts I am
beginning to think it is a good idea.

For those that have problems with moderated groups, this is not just a
question of being on/off topic, or liking/disliking a person.
You may sometimes be completely on-topic, and still be asked to change
your post, or even be completely refused, by the moderator(s), and you
may be off-topic and still be allowed to post (if not within the group
prohibited subjects).

And there are other good things with moderated groups. For example one
can send the FAQ to any new poster, thus avoiding some of the less
instructive posts such as where to find a particular COBOL tool, or
"will you do my homework?".

Now that we are speaking of this subject please also be perfectly
assured that being a moderator is not something you can do lightly. It
requires lot of time and effort.
 Please see the following article for further info

ftp://rtfm.mit.edu/pub/usenet/news.groups/Pitfalls_of_Newsgroup_Moderation


Frederico Fonseca
ema il: frederico_fonseca at syssoft-int.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** "Peter E.C. Dashwood" <dashwood@enternet.co.nz>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T15:45:25+12:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3effb2d7_5@news.athenanews.com>`
- **References:** `<bdkqjg$p2b$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <8dhtfvgdcumh1pa0co5u7q36a8ivjrof2k@4ax.com>`

```
Frederico,

I understand your points but I have to balance them against the following:

1. The Internet in general is one of the last refuges for Free Speech in our
society.

2. You Don't HAVE to access it or participate in it if you don't like what
goes on in it. (Can't stand the heat? Don't go in the kitchen...)

3. Unmoderated Newsgroups provide a valuable "steam valve" for people to say
exactly what they think without having to modify it or tone it down. For the
most part, the participants are self-moderating and this has to be a better
solution than imposing Moderation. This forum has run effectively for over 5
years now and the current problem has only arisen because one individual has
set out to disrupt and destroy it for reasons of his own that are
incomprehensible to me.

4. I for one, cannot tolerate the idea that some other individual can "vet"
my posts and match them against a standard I may have no agreement with. It
is simply censorship where we allow another to decide what is acceptable to
us. I can't go along with that. (I resent it with movies but have little
choice; here on the Internet I do at least get to vote...)

Moderation in this forum (assuming you can get someone to do it...) would be
anathema to me and I would probably no longer post here. (Some will no doubt
see that as an excellent reason for getting Moderation as quickly as
possible...<G> Nevertheless, without closing my options, my feeling is that
I would not want to be part of a group were everything is censored for
"correctness".)

My vote therefore, has to be "NO" to Moderation.

As to what we do about Robert, well, maybe he'll change his mind and decide
there is no great kudos for him in destroying a group that has done him no
harm. (Yes, I do tend to see the bottle as half full, rather than half
empty...<G>). If that doesn't happen, it will be down to the particpants in
this forum to decide wheher they will let him simply ruin it, or they will
do what it takes to prevent him from doing so. In either of the above
scenarios, the group comes out better off.

Pete.

I
> >did want to hear what others feel about this.

…[24 more quoted lines elided]…

on too long and also out of office automatic messages. I would guess the

moderation. I just don't see much up side to it as it is easy to ignore
what is not of interest.

#### ↳ Re: Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-06-29T07:17:08-07:00

```
Top posting only

I think moderation of CLC would be undesirable and agree with many of
the other responses to this thread.  I agree that some of the threads
have gone on excessively and with vituperation as well as inaccuracy
in some cases.  I think that people (especially occasional visitors
who don't know the relative authoritativeness of participants) should
also bear in mind (and probably mostly do) that the last word on a
topic in a newsgroup isn't necessarily the right word and that
selective snipping of previous posts must be regarded with caution,
lest the meaning be distorted.

Robert

On Sat, 28 Jun 2003 14:34:07 -0500, "William M. Klein"
<wmklein@ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>I know that many of the past active and helpful participants in CLC have
>already dropped out - since the recent rise of one (no longer new)
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>did want to hear what others feel about this.

As has been pointed out by other posters, moderation would not
eliminate the "problem" CLC appears to be having.  But as someone else
said, I participate in newsgroups where the sort of posting you are
proposing to moderate is much more vitriolic and common.  The way it
is usually dealt with in those newsgroups is an easy to perform
procedure called, "IGNORE".

Given the amount of time and work it would take to make CLC a
moderated group and to actually moderate it, I feel it is just not
worth it.

So, I say, "NO".

Regards,

Steve

#### ↳ Re: Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** Steve Thompson <steve_nospam_t@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T12:11:21-04:00

- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.196a2b34c0dcde19989681@News.CIS.DFN.DE>`
- **References:** `<bdkqjg$p2b$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <bdkqjg$p2b$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net>, 
wmklein@ix.netcom.com says...
> I know that many of the past active and helpful participants in CLC have
> already dropped out - since the recent rise of one (no longer new)
> participant who has disrupted the "normal" useful and friendly ("debating is
> NOT the purpose) nature of comp.lang.cobol.
<snip>

Adding a new moderated group is probably the way to go. I've 
noticed the problems and this group is going the way 
alt.computer.consultants (acc). One or two trolls made it 
impossible to get anything accomplished along with the several 
bone headed body shops who demanded to post ads...

As for the argument that people chose to leave [CLC]...

Where there a war to breakout where I live, I would choose to 
leave ASAP. Would I come back? Perhaps, once I knew that peace 
reigned again.

Then there is the idea that should I go to a church where those 
there decided to stage a coup and change the doctrines, I would 
attempt to put a halt to it, but once it was obvious that I and 
others like me weren't being listened to... 

The people who want to argue about free speech keep forgetting 
that while they may speak freely, there is nothing that requires 
that I listen! There is nothing that requires that I take part 
in vain useless discussions in CLC. Therefore, I have chosen to 
come back now and then and see if anything useful MIGHT be going 
on.

Again, is what happened to acc. Once it became acc.Moderated the 
nonsense stopped for those who wanted sane, cogent discussions. 
For those who wanted to discuss things that were not part of the 
charter, they could always go back to acc.

Just my .5 cents worth.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Should CLC become a moderated NG???

- **From:** "JerryMouse" <nospam@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T21:32:48-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<LiGdndGqYeVHb52iXTWJhA@giganews.com>`
- **References:** `<bdkqjg$p2b$1@slb6.atl.mindspring.net> <MPG.196a2b34c0dcde19989681@News.CIS.DFN.DE> <3f00c195_3@news.athenanews.com>`

```
Peter E.C. Dashwood wrote:
>>
> As I have made several posts here about Free Speech I have to believe
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Let me share some thoughts on the true meaning of "Free Speech."

We all heard it said that free speech does not give you the right to shout
"FIRE" in a crowed theatre. This hoary maxim is wrong, for two reasons:

(1)You can't shout anything in a theatre, crowded or otherwise. If you do,
you'll likely be thrown out.

(2) If the government did enact a law prohibiting the shouting of "FIRE," or
"HOT TAMALES," or "BRAVO!" such a law would be struck down.

The First Amendment to the US Constitution says (and I quote): "Congress
shall make no law... abridging the freedom of speech..." *I* can make a
rule. YOU can make a rule (as in "Shut your mouth or I'll slap it shut!")
but no agency of GOVERNMENT can make such a rule or law.

Of course, recognizing that government can do as it damn well pleases, there
are hundreds of exceptions.
```

---

<a id="t-57748a0389"></a>
## Thread: OO - syntax and concepts - "old" and ISO 2002 Standard — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Object-oriented COBOL`](topics.md#oo) · [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

### OO - syntax and concepts - "old" and ISO 2002 Standard

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-28T14:52:29-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdkrp0$gql$1@slb5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I thought others in this NG might be interested in some information that I
provided to another COBOL group.

    ***

With NetExpress V4, Micro Focus now provides OO support that fully (or so
they claim) supports the syntax approved last year as an ISO COBOL Standard.

To see the differences between these two "syntaxes" - go to:


http://supportline.microfocus.com/supportline/documentation/books/nx40/oppubb.htm

and (find) read the chapter:

    "Chapter 12: Micro Focus OO COBOL Alternative Syntax"

If you aren't familiar with OO "concepts" in general - as used in COBOL, you
might also want to read the chapter,

     "Chapter 2: OO Programming Concepts"

in the same (online) documentation.
```

---

No as well.  I mentioned in an earlier post one of the
problems.  Unless you have a TEAM of moderators,
I don't see how anybody could have a life AND be a moderator.

Not to mention the time lag between posting a message (my point of view),
and seeing it show up from the public's point of view.


    Moderated groups can be usefull with a busy newgroup that
attracts a lot of spam.  In these cases, an automated filter can
do much of the work, and the moderator (human) only needs to
handle messages that the filter can not handle.

    I don't believe that this would be possible in CLC.

## Thread: Unique ID — 16 msgs

### Unique ID

- **From:** "Trill" <trillkev@alinto.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T07:55:12+02:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr>`

```
Hello,

I do not know how to solve this issue :
I have got a file with a lot of IDs in it.
Many values can be associated to each ID.
And each value can be associated to different IDs.

The goal is to define a kind of "technical unique ID" for all the IDs that
have at least one value in common.

For instance :

ID        Value
2            400
3            400
1            500
1            655
2            655
3            655
1            780
2            780
3            780
3            945
9            945
8            974
7            982
8            982

The IDs 1,2,3 and 9 should have a technical ID in common, as well as the IDs
7 and 8.

Thanks a lot for you help
Thomas
```

#### ↳ Re: Unique ID

- **From:** docdwarf@panix.com
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T08:11:02-04:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdp9cm$k1s$1@panix1.panix.com>`
- **References:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr>`

```

#### ↳ Re: Unique ID

- **From:** robert@jones0086.freeserve.co.uk (Robert Jones)
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T07:37:19-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fcd09c56.0306300637.fb4258@posting.google.com>`
- **References:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr>`

```
Top posting only

Hello Trill,

I think there are two possible ways of interpreting your requirement

a)  A technical id for each set of original ids with the same value,
where each original id could possibly have as many different technical
ids as it has values if those values also occur in other original ids.

If this is the case, the value is the technical id, but assuming you
only want it for individual ids when that value is duplicated across
several original ids, you must sort the file into the table by value
and work through it adding an indicator field to mark up those
elements where the same value is duplicated.  If you use an output
procedure with your sort, the processing of duplicate values can be
done in that output procedure.  The duplicates indicator then
indicates whether or not the value is relevant as a technical id.

b)  A technical id that indicates whether each original id has any
values that are shared with those of any other original ids, in other
words a duplicates indicator.

If this is the case, the same solution can be used, except that the
duplicates indicator is the technical id.

Good luck, Robert

"Trill" <trillkev@alinto.com> wrote in message news:<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr>...
> Hello,
> 
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thomas
```

#### ↳ Re: Unique ID

- **From:** robert@wagner.net (Robert Wagner)
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T14:42:02+00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3f0048b2.18581313@news.optonline.com>`
- **References:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr>`

```
1. Sort by Value-ID. Assign a TechID to each duplicated value. 
I changed 8-974 to 5-974 in order to demonstrate an entry that doesn't need a
TechID.

TechID   ID           Value
10          2 	   400
11          3            400
999        1            500
12          1            655
13          2            655
14          3            655
15          1            780
16          2            780
17          3            780
18          3            945
19          9            945
999        5            974      <- id is unique
20          7            982
21          8            982

2. Sort by ID-Value. For entries which don't have a TechID, assign the next
value if others for the ID had a TechID otherwise use ID as TechID.

TechID   ID           Value
12          1            655
15          1            780
22          1            500    <- next TechID
10          2            400
13          2            655
16          2            780
11          3            400
14          3            655
17          3            780
18          3            945
  5          5            974    <- TechID = ID
20          7            982
21          8            982
19          9            945

The result:

TechID   ID           Value
  5          5            974
10          2            400
11          3            400
12          1            655
13          2            655
14          3            655
15          1            780
16          2            780
17          3            780
18          3            945
19          9            945
20          7            982
21          8            982
22          1            500


--- history follows ---

```

- **From:** "Trill" <trillkev@alinto.com>

```
Yes, I apologize for not giving a clear explanation of the problem (which
maybe isnt a problem :-)
I am working on OS390 with COBOL2.

Let's not talk about IDs.

Let's say I have a file of thousands of customers.
Each customer may have some keys to find him more rapidly. For instance, his
phone, his email, the first two digits of his postal code, etc.
And each key can potentially be assigned to more than one customer.

A group of customers is defined when each of these customers have at least
one key in common. The problem is that there are different types of keys
(the email type, the phone type, etc.). So how can I find all the groups of
customers ??
You'll see that the problem occurs when the type of key changes. The file is
sorted on TYPE then VALUE then CUSTOMER#.

TYPE            VALUE                    CUSTOMER
EMAIL        tata@toto.com            2
EMAIL        tata@toto.com            3
EMAIL        toto@tata.com              1
PHONE        555-55555                1
PHONE        555-55555                2
PHONE        555-55555                3
POSTAL        54568                        1
POSTAL        54568                        2
POSTAL        54568                        3
POSTAL        56749                        6
etc.


I think there is no "easy and fast" way to do that....

Best regards,
Thomas

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unique ID

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T13:42:13-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nF_La.3790$Ec2.218575@news20.bellglobal.com>`
- **References:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <8a2d426e.0306300825.4324688@posting.google.com> <3f006d46$0$23988$626a54ce@news.free.fr>`

```
sure there is.  Make each key an isam key, and you can read any group by
type and or value. Your first read on a particular key type will get you the
first instance.  Successive reads will get you the rest. when the key
changes, you have completed a group.  You willneed one key that is unique
... you can make it a customer number.

Donald

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unique ID

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T13:21:13-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bdpv2v$3g6$1@slb2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <8a2d426e.0306300825.4324688@posting.google.com> <3f006d46$0$23988$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <nF_La.3790$Ec2.218575@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f007c5b$0$24004$626a54ce@news.free.fr>`

```
What you want (on OS/390) is to define your file as a VSAM KSDS file with
alternate record keys (with duplicates).  If you are not familiar with the
IDCAMS control cards to "DEFINE" such a file, contact someone "at your shop"
who can help you with this. You must have one UNIQUE key (as the primary
key) - probably, this would be your "customer number" or something like
this.

As far as the COBOL goes, your SELECT/ASSIGN will look SOMETHING like:

Select CustFile Assign to CUSTDDNAME
  Organization Indexed
  Access Dynamic      *>  this gives you the greatest flexibility - other
options are possible
    Record Key Cust-Num
    Alternate Record Key Cust-Type with Duplicates
    Alternate Record Key Cust-Value with Duplicates
   File Status   Cust-FS  Cust-FS2.

Then in your procedure dividision, you may use ANY of the keys (primary or
alternate) and "read next" thru the items that have the same (or greater)
values.

If any of this isn't clear, you might want to check out:


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.10.3.3

and for "defining" your file,


http://publibz.boulder.ibm.com/cgi-bin/bookmgr_OS390/BOOKS/igy3pg10/1.10.6.2


P.S.  *ALWAYS* remember to check your file status codes after every I/O
operation - including OPENS, READS, and CLOSES.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unique ID

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Donald Tees" <Donald_Tees@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T14:40:26-07:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <8a2d426e.0306300825.4324688@posting.google.com> <3f006d46$0$23988$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <nF_La.3790$Ec2.218575@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f007c5b$0$24004$626a54ce@news.free.fr>`

```
Perhaps I am not understanding the problem as well.
see below.

"Trill" <trillkev@alinto.com> wrote in message
news:3f007c5b$0$24004$626a54ce@news.free.fr...
> Hello Donald,
>
> Thanks for your answer, but I am not sure I understand well what brings
the
> ISAM key...
> Let's take my example :
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> A.....

You have, lets say, ten TYPES of key.  How many of any individual key exists
is unknown ... lets say type A is area code, and you may have 100's of
customers with a specific area code.

So you define each key TYPE as an Isam key, and have a known value for
"unknown".  So your file looks like

customer  area code    postal digits first letter of name
1               519               N2             S
2               416               N2             S
3               519               B5              T
4               311               --                *
5               ---                N2              -

Each of the types is a key, so if you want all customers in area code 519,
you start with area code 519 and read them in succession.  If you want all
the customers with unknown area codes, you start at three dashes and read
them.  You can do the same for any of the keys.

Is that not what you want?  Isam keys can be duplicates, though ONE of them
must be unique.  The customer number would be the unique key in the example
above.

Donald

- **References:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <8a2d426e.0306300825.4324688@posting.google.com> <3f006d46$0$23988$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <nF_La.3790$Ec2.218575@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f007c5b$0$24004$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <Xv%La.1718$eF3.225740@news20.bellglobal.com>`

```
Donald Tees wrote:
> Perhaps I am not understanding the problem as well.
> see below.
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> three dashes and read them.  You can do the same for any of the keys.

Friendly explaination: You START with key = area code and initial value =
519 then READ NEXT until the value changes (i.e., "520"). You need not read
the whole file. If you want only the records of red-heads, you will retrieve
only the records containing red-heads + 1.

An ISAM file may have multiple keys. To get what you want, you tell the ISAM
routine on which key it should recover records and the starting value for
that key. Like magic, ISAM returns the first record matching on the
specified key. Subsequent reads continue, alphabetically, on the specified
key.

>
> Is that not what you want?  Isam keys can be duplicates, though ONE
…[123 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>>>> Thomas
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Unique ID

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** LX-i <LXi0007@Netscape.net>
- **Date:** 2003-06-30T17:25:21-05:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3F00B8D1.50100@Netscape.net>`
- **References:** `<3effd0bb$0$5429$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <8a2d426e.0306300825.4324688@posting.google.com> <3f006d46$0$23988$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <nF_La.3790$Ec2.218575@news20.bellglobal.com> <3f007c5b$0$24004$626a54ce@news.free.fr> <Xv%La.1718$eF3.225740@news20.bellglobal.com> <1JWdnT69OuGoCZ2iXTWJiQ@giganews.com> <3f009e10$0$24011$626a54ce@news.free.fr>`

```
Trill wrote:
> Thanks a lot for all your answers and explanations, I think I have a better
> understanding now.
> I am going to try ISAM as soon as I can.

If you have access to a relational database, you might consider creating 
tables that mimic this file and dumping data to it.  Then, you can do 
things like

SELECT * FROM Customers
   WHERE Customer_ID IN
     (SELECT Customer_ID FROM Customers
       WHERE ZIP_Code LIKE '374%')

which will give you a "group" of people whose ZIP code begin with 374. 
If you know the value you're looking for, and the field it's in, this is 
a quick way to "group" customers together without actually having to 
assign an ID to a group.  This also allows customers to belong to more 
than one "group", depending on how it's defined.

(STARTing the ISAM file, as was suggested earlier in the thread, is 
definitely the way to go if you can't port the file to a table.  The 
advantage to the table would be when you want to combine conditions (ex. 
all males in the 374 ZIP code area).  You could define alternate keys 
for the ISAM file, but the SQL will process without having to change the 
table definition.  It may not be the fastest, if you're not hitting at 
least one primarily indexed field, but it will drop in very quickly.)
```
